dataset_meta.description: Multiple choice questions using LLM-based similarity judgment

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lend him the three thousand yuan outright, without agonizing over the lost trip or the risk of repayment. I pull out my phone and open the payment QR code, or hand over the cash directly, and say: 'Get up. Go pay the deposit. You don't need to write an IOU — let's save the person first.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan, accept the loss of a trip that may now never happen, and act on the strongest feeling of the present moment. I pull my phone out of my pocket, open Alipay, and say to him: 'Show me your payment code — I'll transfer it to you right now. You don't need to write me an IOU.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to lend the money, but I give him an alternative direction rather than walking away in silence. I crouch down and gently move his hand from my pant leg, and say calmly: 'I can't lend you anything — but go to the nurses' station right now, tell them the family has no money. The hospital has an emergency payment assistance process. Go ask them. Don't stay here on your knees.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take out my phone and transfer the three thousand dollars, while using a preemptive strike of self-attack to hold back the collapse building inside me. My hand trembling, I open the payment app, and say in a hoarse voice: 'I'm sending it to you — give me your contact info… now go, hurry.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to gently decline the invitation, but without cutting off contact — giving myself time to face both this feeling and my existing relationship. I tell him: "I actually have something on this Saturday, so I probably can't make it — but that feeling of being understood that you mentioned, I treasure that too. It's just that right now I need to think some things through first."

options.label: B
options.content: Accept the invitation — but also decide to have a real conversation with my partner that same night, and face whatever has actually gone wrong between us. I say to them: "I'd love to go, I've been wanting to catch this talk too — but there's something I need to tell you first, about where things have been between us lately."

options.label: C
options.content: She chooses to decline the invitation, but does so through self-deprecation rather than stating a clear boundary. I lower my head, pull out a strained smile, and say quietly: "Thank you for saying all that… I've felt like these past few months have been something special too. The bookstore thing — I probably can't make it. I've been all over the place lately, and I'm afraid I wouldn't be very good company." I didn't dare look him in the eyes after that.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation directly but calmly, while offering a genuine acknowledgment of the partnership — no avoidance, no stalling. I say to them: 'Thank you for telling me this. These past few months really have felt like smooth collaboration — that's true. But I can't accept the Saturday invitation. I think we both know it's not just an ordinary colleague outing.'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal on the spot — stating the risk points plainly, not pushing for any particular outcome, just making sure the information got through in full. I said: 'I do have a few thoughts — I put together an alternative proposal, mainly with an eye toward the long-term scaling issues. The document's right here; everyone can take two minutes to look it over, and we can decide after that — no rush.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to raise the alternative plan on the spot — to open things up rather than quietly go along. I turn my screen toward the group and say: 'Actually, yes — I've put together a different approach over here. Give it two minutes; if everyone feels it's not worth discussing, we'll get straight back to dividing up tasks — but I want this plan to be seen at least once.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal in this moment, presenting it in a structured, evidence-based way rather than silently accepting the consensus. I turned my screen toward the room and said: 'There are a few other ideas I'd like to share — I've put together a specific proposal with the risk points laid out. I need three minutes. I think saying it now costs less than waiting until something goes wrong later.'

options.label: D
options.content: She chooses to suppress the alternative proposal, trading silence and compliance for a sense of safety in the moment, and does not raise any objection. I lowered my head slightly and said: 'Nothing from me — let's go with the current plan. I'm fine with it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to go home immediately to deal with Dad's illness, setting the overseas project decision aside for now, and will reach out to the company once the situation becomes clearer. I tell my mother: 'I understand — tell me which hospital Dad is in. I'll look up tickets tonight and take the earliest flight out. You stay calm for now. Once I'm there, I'll go talk to the doctors myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: I decide to set aside any deliberation over the overseas opportunity and go home first to be with Dad and help manage the medical arrangements. The career stuff can come later. I call Mom back and say: 'Mom, I'll be home tomorrow — don't worry too much, okay? I'm coming to be with you both.'

options.label: C
options.content: She chooses to immediately abandon the overseas assignment and go home to be with her father and handle whatever comes next with his medical care. Voice breaking over the phone, she says: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — first flight tomorrow morning. Wait for me.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to immediately set aside all preparations for the overseas project and return home as soon as possible to be with Dad through his medical care, putting the career decision on hold for now. Call Mom and say: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — I'll be there on the earliest flight tomorrow. Don't worry, I'll handle everything.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to support the kid's direction, but immediately shift toward gathering information and assessing risks rather than making any emotional declaration. I say: 'You mentioned you've been teaching yourself for years — show me what you've got. Let's look into this path together and figure out what needs to be prepared for ahead of time.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to offer conditional support, but I require my kid to sit through a structured risk-breakdown conversation right now, rather than simply handing over an emotional endorsement. I push the report booklet back across the table and say: 'You say seven years — I believe you. But let's go through this field's employment data, your competitive edge, and your exit path in the worst-case scenario, one by one. If you can answer those, I'll support you filling it in.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to support my child in pursuing this path — I voice my worries, but I don't use them to invalidate their choice. I say: 'I hear you. You've been preparing for so long, and I have no reason to say no. I have my fears, but those are my homework to do — not a chain around your neck. Let's figure out together how to make this path as solid as it can be.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to push down the impulse to object and offer conditional support, while honestly naming my own fear. I say aloud: 'I'm not against it — but I'm scared. Not because I think you can't do it. It's because I know how hard that road is. I want you to be ready for it, not for me to decide for you whether to walk it or not.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honor my father's wishes and decide on palliative care, stating that choice plainly without flinching. I say to the doctor: 'We're choosing palliative care — please initiate the comfort care protocol. He has made his choice, and we respect it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honor the answer Dad has already given with his eyes — to support palliative care, and not to force him through that suffering for the sake of a 20% chance. I turn to the doctor and say: 'We're choosing palliative care. Having him with us — really with us — matters more than anything else.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose palliative care, honor the exhaustion and wishes my father has expressed, and refuse to let a low-probability course of chemotherapy turn his remaining time into suffering. I speak to the doctor: 'Start the comfort-care protocol—let him spend whatever time he has left in peace.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion and decide on palliative care — to let him spend his remaining time with dignity. I lift my head, my voice a little hoarse, and say to the doctor: 'We… we'll go with palliative care. Let him suffer less.' Then I turn toward my father and gently take his hand.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to reply to my colleague's message. I flip the phone face-down on the table and let that message just sit there. My partner's "tutoring tomorrow" message I answer with a quick "okay," and then I close my eyes and don't think about any of it anymore.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to reply to the message, but keep the wording concise and measured — clear boundaries, neither deliberately cold nor leaving any opening. I reply: 'Made it home. That line of thinking from today's meeting is worth developing further — the bookstore idea has potential. Goodnight.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to reply to my colleague's message for now — cutting off the bud with self-attack, pressing the longing back down. I darken the phone screen, pick up my partner's message, reply with a single "okay," then close both chat windows and stare at the blurred night outside the car window, telling myself: you could never hold something like this together.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to reply to my colleague but deliberately narrow the boundary, using a polite yet clear-eyed tone to pull the connection back to a safe distance. I write: 'Made it home — thanks for checking in. That conversation the other day was really lovely; I'll have to recommend the bookstore to you sometime. Good night.' — Then I put down my phone, open my notes app, and add tomorrow's tutoring run with the kids to my schedule.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to politely decline the camping invitation, deflecting with a specific but not over-explained work excuse, while keeping a normal, friendly tone — no cracks, no unnecessary distance. My reply: 'Oh that sounds absolutely gorgeous! I've got a project milestone I need to wrap up this weekend and I'm worried I won't be able to get away last-minute — you all go and have fun, and make sure to include me next time!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to accept the camping invitation and respond with surface-level enthusiasm, but leave myself a vague out — while quietly working to make sure the four of us never actually go. I write back: "That sounds amazing! The guesthouse must have such a beautiful view — let me just check my schedule on this end, you guys lock in the details first, and I'll get back to you as soon as I can!"

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to dodge the camping invitation with a vague excuse, buying time and space — neither outright refusing nor agreeing. Reply: 'It's been so long! Something just came up on our end this weekend, so we probably won't make it — can we plan something another time? Hope you guys have a blast! ～'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stall and respond vaguely to dodge the camping invitation — neither accepting nor explicitly declining — buying myself some time. I reply: 'It sounds absolutely gorgeous! I just have something on my end this week that I need to check the timing on — I'll let you know for sure in a day or two, you guys go ahead and make your plans in the meantime~ '

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch and go to the hospital immediately to sign the medical consent forms. Trembling, I hang up on the CEO, and into the phone I say: "I'm sorry — my father is in emergency surgery, I have to get to the hospital. Please find a way to handle the launch without me. I can't make it."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to go to the hospital immediately, giving up hosting the launch in person, while quickly mobilizing resources to arrange a substitute. I call the CEO back, my voice faster and louder than usual: 'My father is on the operating table — I have to get to the hospital to sign the forms, I'm leaving now. Put the deputy director on, I'll direct remotely from the car. The launch cannot stop, but this signature can only come from me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to handle the medical decision remotely, then attend the launch as planned. First I press the doctors to spell out the risks clearly over the phone and confirm the treatment plan directly; then I notify the CEO that I'll be there on time.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the surgical consent. I end the CEO's video call and say into the doctor's phone: "I'm leaving now — I'll be there in fifteen minutes. Keep him stable. Wait for my signature."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to sign off confirming the data is valid, but I also won't formally report it to a superior — instead, I tell my colleague directly that I can't sign off on the data, and demand that he correct it or explain the situation himself before submitting. I say to him: 'I can't put my name on this data as confirmed. You know what I saw — you need to figure out how you're going to handle this before you submit tonight. I'm not stamping this for you.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to report the data falsification truthfully to my supervisor, and refuse to sign off on the fabricated data. Reply directly in the project management system, flag the data as anomalous and unverifiable, and inform my supervisor of the situation.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to report the data fraud to my supervisor and refuse to sign off confirming the data is valid — even though I am terrified of the interpersonal fallout that will follow. I tell my colleague: 'I know this puts you in a hard place, but I can't sign off on this. Tonight I'm going to tell our supervisor exactly what I know. You should probably go explain yourself first.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to report the data falsification to my supervisor and did not sign off on the confirmation email. I sent the project lead a brief message stating that there were anomalies in the data that needed to be re-verified before the presentation, and I attached a record of the specific issues I had found.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to fulfill the responsibility I was entrusted with first, then make my way to the amusement park as soon as I can — resolving the conflict by doing both. I tell Mom: 'I'll go to school first thing tomorrow morning to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, and once that's done I'll head straight to find my cousin — I should still make it in time. Can you let Uncle know for me?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to complete my school responsibility first, then negotiate with Mom about whether we can head over to join the party afterward — I will not abandon a commitment I've already made. I say to Mom: 'Mom, tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the key, and it's something I promised to do. How long do you think it'll take once I'm done? If there's still time, could we go to the amusement park to meet my cousin after?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to do both, handling them in sequence by planning the timing in advance so I can honor my responsibility and still make it to the outing. I tell Mom: 'I'm excited, I really am! But tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the keys, and I promised her I'd do it. Once I'm done we'll head straight out; we should still make it in time. Can you let Uncle know we'll be there a little bit later?'

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to do both things: go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, then head to the amusement park, and explain my reasoning to Mom upfront. I say to her: 'Mom, I'm the Little Garden Keeper this week — the teacher gave me the key. Tomorrow morning I'll go to school first and water the plants and feed the tadpoles real quick, and then we leave. It won't take long, we'll still make it in time.'

options.label: A
options.content: I decline to use the service — directly, without lecturing. I gave a small laugh and slid the phone back, saying: 'Appreciate it, man — I'm doing alright on my end, don't need it. Do whatever works for you though.'

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to use the service and insist on completing the thesis independently. I shook my head, pushed the phone back, and said: "No thanks — this one I'll do myself. There's still so much about my own topic I haven't worked out yet; if I handed it off to someone else, I'd never be able to sleep."

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse to use the service, and stick to the original plan to complete the thesis independently. Push the phone back, and say calmly: 'My progress is fine right now — I have enough time following the plan. I'm not going to consider this.'

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to use the service and choose to finish the thesis on my own, even if it costs me more. I tell my roommate: 'Thanks, but I'll write it myself — I'm still managing okay, and honestly, using that would just make me feel worse about the whole thing.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take the microphone and speak sincerely — to get through an impromptu acceptance speech by looking after the mood of the room, rather than retreating into silence. I gave a small smile into the microphone, voice a little unsteady but still finding the words: 'Thank you to the teacher and the judges. When I wrote this piece, I really just wanted to put down on paper a question that had been rattling around in my head and wouldn't go away… I never expected anyone to like it. I think maybe — the kind of confusion the story is about — maybe most of us have felt something like that at some point.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak directly, briefly, and honestly — no performed emotion, no excessive modesty, no deliberate exaggeration of the creative process. I take the microphone and say, calmly: 'I wrote this piece off the top of my head three weeks ago. There was no particular inspiration — I just wrote whatever came to mind. I'm genuinely surprised to have won first place. Thank you to the judges.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak, but I use self-deprecation and deliberate understatement to preemptively defuse any possible doubt — pushing the honor away from myself to keep myself safe. I lean into the microphone and say: 'Honestly… I didn't expect this myself. I wrote this piece pretty casually, so maybe the judges saw something in it that I didn't — thank you all.' Then I lower my head, take a small step back, and return the microphone to the host.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to force myself to open my mouth anyway, but I squeeze out only a handful of words and make a hasty exit — unable to truly share anything at all. I lean into the microphone and say quietly: 'It's just… something I wrote on a whim. Thank you.' Then I hand the microphone back to Mr. Zhao and retreat to the side, head down.

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to choose between them, trying to break through this forced ultimatum with tears and silence. I crouch down, wrap my arms around my knees, lower my head, and let myself cry out loud — without saying a single word, just shaking my head over and over.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to freeze in place and stay silent as a way of refusing to make this choice, while instinctively trying to redirect my attention toward the concrete mess that can actually be dealt with. I crouch down and start picking up the shards of porcelain from the floor, and in a very small voice I say: "Don't go yet — there are pieces on the floor, you'll cut your feet."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse the either-or, to stay where I am without going with anyone, and without declaring support for either side. I look down at the shards of porcelain on the floor and say: "I'm not going anywhere. Whatever this is between you two, you can talk it out — but I'm not choosing."

options.label: D
options.content: Paralysis and avoidance — I cannot make a choice for either side, and I let the breakdown itself interrupt this impossible demand. I stand in the hallway, my legs too weak to hold me up, and the tears finally fall. I cry out loud. I can't get a single word out. I just cry.

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to proactively approach the other person about collaboration, merging the two proposals together, and abandon the route of campaigning solo for votes. Say to my friend: 'Let's go talk to her — her plan solves the rehearsal problem, my plan has the content, and the two things can be pieced together. The credit doesn't matter; what matters is that the plan actually gets made.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to campaign hard for votes and stand by my original plan — no merger. I tell my friend: 'No merging — my plan is my plan. Come on, this weekend we go talk to everyone one by one. I'm bringing the sketch. Let them see it for themselves.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to step away from the competition — I won't go talk to her about merging, and I won't spend the weekend canvassing votes. I'll let the proposal lose on its own terms. I tell my friend: "Forget it — if everyone likes her proposal, they should go with it. I'll keep mine for myself."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to campaign independently, stand by the original proposal, and let the idea's own appeal win people over — rather than merging it with another plan just to play it safe. I tell my friend: "I want to try on my own first. This weekend I'll go find people, show them the sketches, let them really picture that space — and if I've made my case and still lose, I'll accept it. But I don't want to gut the whole thing before I've even had a chance to show it."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the model being lost, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately pivot to figuring out how to complete the presentation without it. Looking the homeroom teacher in the eye: 'The model was lost on the way to school — he left it on the bus. Without the model, we'll complete the presentation using a verbal explanation and the board. I'll take the lead.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to go along with the lie. I tell the truth about the model being lost, but I try to state the facts in the steadiest tone I can manage, without directly calling out my teammate by name. I say to the homeroom teacher: 'Ms. [Teacher], the model… was lost on the way to school. We weren't able to bring it. I'm sorry — we don't have the model, but we can complete the presentation through a verbal explanation.'

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to go along with the lie, choose to tell the homeroom teacher honestly that the model was lost, and immediately pivot to completing the presentation through verbal, impromptu explanation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but clearly: 'The model was left on the bus on the way to school. We don't have a prop, but we can just talk through it — I'll describe how the solar system works. We don't need the model.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the model being lost, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately propose an alternative plan for the presentation. I say to the teacher: 'The model was lost on the way to school — there's no getting it back. We can walk through everything verbally; I have all the content memorized. We're ready to go up right now.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to decline calmly — I don't take the card, I don't escalate the conflict, I end it with a few short, direct words. I didn't reach out for the card; I simply looked at him, my voice as steady as when I discuss a patient's case: 'It's not a good fit — thanks.' Then I turned around, picked up a glass of orange juice, and went to find my friend.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to decline the card, but in a way that neutralizes his performance rather than meeting it with direct confrontation or an outburst of anger. A slight smile, I gently push the card back toward him, and in a voice pitched just right — not too loud, not too soft — I say: 'Thanks for remembering me — though the assistant track isn't really a direction I'm interested in. Your team's projects over the years, on the other hand, I've always thought there was something worth looking into there. Catch up sometime?' Then I turn and start a new conversation with the person beside me.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly, giving him no emotional reaction to exploit, letting the whole performance collapse in plain sight with nothing but calm. I glanced down at the card, then looked up, my voice as level as if I were stating a simple fact: 'No need. For the assistant position, I'd suggest finding someone who doesn't know how you got to where you are — it'll save you a lot of headaches.' Then I picked up my orange juice and walked away.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — I don't take the card. I draw my line in the calmest, clearest way I can, giving him no opening to perform his 'magnanimity' for the crowd. I shift the orange juice glass to my other hand, meet his eyes, and keep my voice low but make every word land clean: 'Thank you — I'm all right. What happened between us back then — I haven't forgotten.' Then I turn and walk away, and I don't look back.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take over all the preparation work the president left behind on my own, pushing the event forward through sheer willpower, refusing to agree to cancellation. I take a deep breath, voice trembling slightly but I speak anyway: 'We're not canceling. I'll take it from here. Help as much as you can — if you can't, I'll figure it out myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to take over the organizing work, refuse to agree to canceling the event, but proactively scale back the goals and focus on pushing forward the most critical tasks. I say to the members: "Canceling is something I can't agree to — but I'm not going to force any of you either. I'll personally follow up on the most urgent items myself: confirming the sponsors, getting the telescope loan request submitted, and sorting out the volunteer schedule. Whoever has time can pitch in; if you don't, that's fine. As for what shape the event ends up in — we'll see when we get there."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to immediately announce a cancellation. Instead, I do a quick feasibility check first — break down the remaining tasks and see what can be compressed or reassigned. I tell the three members: 'Don't rush into any decisions yet. Give me ten minutes — let's list out what's still left and how long each thing would take, and then we'll see if it's doable. If it's not, we go to the student affairs office. But at least let's get the full picture first.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to cancel the event outright, and instead propose a compromise: scale things down, redistribute responsibilities, ask the remaining members to each take on whatever they can manage, and reach out to the student affairs committee to explain the situation and ask for support. I speak up: 'I think we shouldn't rush to say cancel just yet — can you hear me out for a second? We scale the event down, everyone only handles one or two of the most critical things, and I'll go coordinate the rest with the student affairs committee. That way nobody's finals get wrecked, and the event doesn't just disappear into nothing.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to set aside the immediate push on the overseas project and prioritize going home to handle Dad's medical arrangements, while also starting to break down the timelines of both things in search of a middle path. I begin making a list on my phone screen: the earliest train tomorrow morning, contact the attending physician, and at the same time send HR an email asking whether the overseas role allows for a deferred decision — first get the parts I can control into their boxes, then go home.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to go back immediately to handle my father's medical affairs, while simultaneously reaching out to the company to negotiate a delay or explore remote options for the overseas opportunity. This isn't a pure emotional sacrifice — I'm treating it as a project that requires my on-site presence. I tell my mother: 'I'll book a ticket tonight and be there tomorrow. In the meantime, have the doctors pull together all the test results — when I arrive, I'll go straight to the attending physician and talk it through myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: She chooses to immediately abandon the overseas assignment and go home to be with her father and handle whatever comes next with his medical care. Voice breaking over the phone, she says: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — first flight tomorrow morning. Wait for me.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to go home immediately to deal with Dad's illness, setting the overseas project decision aside for now, and will reach out to the company once the situation becomes clearer. I tell my mother: 'I understand — tell me which hospital Dad is in. I'll look up tickets tonight and take the earliest flight out. You stay calm for now. Once I'm there, I'll go talk to the doctors myself.'

options.label: A
options.content: Refuse to include the colleague on maternity leave in the list, and state my reasons directly — no confrontation, no compromise. I tell my supervisor: 'I can't write that her performance failed to meet expectations on the list. Her evaluation is a B+ — that's the fact on record, and I can't sign off on something that contradicts it. I understand the situation with the relative, but for the other slot I need to go by actual performance. If you can point me to an employee rated C, I'll handle the paperwork for that.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose not to write my colleague — the one who just returned from maternity leave — onto the list, but I didn't confront my boss head-on either. Instead, I used delay and vagueness to buy myself time. I told him: 'I need to go back through the complete performance review records. I'll send you my thoughts before tonight — but I can't confirm this list right now.'

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to write the colleague who just returned from maternity leave onto the list, and I choose to voice my dissent directly rather than comply in silence. I meet my superior's eyes and say: 'I can't write this list the way you're asking. If someone has to go, I need a real reason — not this one.'

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to put the colleague who just returned from maternity leave on the list, and I don't confront him head-on either — instead I choose silent resistance and delay. I keep my eyes down, my voice flat, and say: 'I can't hand in this list tonight. I need to go back over the performance data. Whatever comes of that, I'll deal with it myself.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak directly, briefly, and honestly — no performed emotion, no excessive modesty, no deliberate exaggeration of the creative process. I take the microphone and say, calmly: 'I wrote this piece off the top of my head three weeks ago. There was no particular inspiration — I just wrote whatever came to mind. I'm genuinely surprised to have won first place. Thank you to the judges.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to speak directly, describing my creative process in the most concise and honest way I could — no performed gratitude, no studied humility. Into the microphone I said: 'I wrote this piece off the top of my head three weeks ago. I just wanted to work out a story with a coherent internal logic. Winning means the judges found that logic convincing, and honestly, that's a better outcome than I expected.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to muddle through and get out as fast as possible. I gripped the microphone, kept my head down, and mumbled a few words — 'I just wrote it casually, didn't think too much about it' — then tucked the trophy and certificate under my arm and practically fled off the stage, sinking back into my seat and folding myself into the crowd.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak, but I use self-deprecation and deliberate understatement to preemptively defuse any possible doubt — pushing the honor away from myself to keep myself safe. I lean into the microphone and say: 'Honestly… I didn't expect this myself. I wrote this piece pretty casually, so maybe the judges saw something in it that I didn't — thank you all.' Then I lower my head, take a small step back, and return the microphone to the host.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to describe my situation directly and calmly, neither sidestepping the gap nor making a point of it, and I respond to well-meaning suggestions politely but clearly, letting them know I have my own considerations. I say: 'I work as a general practitioner at a community health center — pretty stable work. In my spare time I paint; last year a piece of mine got into the local young artists' exhibition, which was a lot of fun. As for housing, I haven't really thought about it for now — things are good the way they are.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond with quiet candor — neither dodging nor getting defensive — and calmly describe my work and life as they are now, mentioning in passing that a painting of mine was selected for an exhibition. I give a small smile and say: 'I do project planning for a nonprofit — the salary's honestly not great, but I really enjoy it. Oh, and a painting of mine got into the local young artists' exhibition last year. As for the housing thing, there's no rush — but thank you both for thinking of me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to introduce my situation proactively, clearly, and with structure — no deflecting, no defending, just delivering a 'status report' on my own life the way a project manager would. I say: 'I do project planning at a nonprofit. The salary isn't high, but I manage every project from kickoff to close-out, and I manage it well. In my spare time I paint — last year I got into the local young artists' exhibition, which counts as a milestone. Renting is my choice for this stage; I haven't hit the point where buying makes sense. As for joining a big tech company — I evaluated it, and it doesn't fit my current execution roadmap.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to go on the offensive — to strike first with a breezy, self-assured tone, packaging my life as a clear-eyed choice rather than a losing position, and dropping a mention of the art show as a line of defense. I smiled, picked up my glass, and said: 'Ha, I'm running projects at a nonprofit right now — the pay's nothing to write home about, honestly — but last year I had a painting selected for the local young artists' exhibition. I feel like the life I'm living is the life I actually want. As for the whole housing thing — I'll get to it eventually.'

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to tell the truth, refusing to cover for the one who brought the cards, and ignores the threat entirely. Looking the director straight in the eye, he says: 'He's the one who brought them in. I saw it. That's all there is to it — it has nothing to do with me.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stay vague and refuse to cooperate with either side — deflecting with 'I didn't notice' and 'I don't know' to keep myself out of it, neither informing on anyone nor taking the blame for anyone. I keep my head down and say: 'I was reading at the time — whatever they were doing in the back, I honestly didn't notice. I don't know whose cards those were either.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the truth about who brought the contraband in, prioritizing my own protection and making clear I had no part in it. I'll quietly tell the director exactly who I saw and what happened — and I'll also let him know that person just threatened me.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the truth — to state the facts, to not be swayed by threats, and to make no unnecessary show of emotion. I look at the director calmly and say: 'It was the student in the back row near the corner who brought them in. I turned around because I heard a noise, but I wasn't involved.'

options.label: A
options.content: Accept the invitation — but also decide to have a real conversation with my partner that same night, and face whatever has actually gone wrong between us. I say to them: "I'd love to go, I've been wanting to catch this talk too — but there's something I need to tell you first, about where things have been between us lately."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to gently decline the invitation, but without cutting off contact — giving myself time to face both this feeling and my existing relationship. I tell him: "I actually have something on this Saturday, so I probably can't make it — but that feeling of being understood that you mentioned, I treasure that too. It's just that right now I need to think some things through first."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation directly but calmly, while offering a genuine acknowledgment of the partnership — no avoidance, no stalling. I say to them: 'Thank you for telling me this. These past few months really have felt like smooth collaboration — that's true. But I can't accept the Saturday invitation. I think we both know it's not just an ordinary colleague outing.'

options.label: D
options.content: She chooses to decline the invitation, but does so through self-deprecation rather than stating a clear boundary. I lower my head, pull out a strained smile, and say quietly: "Thank you for saying all that… I've felt like these past few months have been something special too. The bookstore thing — I probably can't make it. I've been all over the place lately, and I'm afraid I wouldn't be very good company." I didn't dare look him in the eyes after that.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, while giving my colleague the opportunity to correct the error himself rather than going over his head directly. I tell him: 'I can't sign off on this data — but before I say anything to anyone above us, I'm giving you tonight. You go to the project team yourself and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the only concession I can make.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to report the data fabrication truthfully to my supervisor and to refuse to sign off on the data as valid. To my colleague, I say: 'I understand your situation, but I can't verify this data — I'll be reporting the facts to our supervisor tonight.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to report the data fraud to my supervisor and refuse to sign off confirming the data is valid — even though I am terrified of the interpersonal fallout that will follow. I tell my colleague: 'I know this puts you in a hard place, but I can't sign off on this. Tonight I'm going to tell our supervisor exactly what I know. You should probably go explain yourself first.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, and at the same time I tell my colleague directly, face-to-face, that he has to report or correct this himself — giving him one last window to make it right on his own terms, or I will report it accurately myself. I say to him: 'I can't sign off on this — just hear me out. Go to the project lead right now and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the last window you have any control over. I'm not trying to destroy you, but tomorrow morning I will not put my name behind these numbers.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to step in — but in a way that puts protecting her first, without pushing for a formal report just yet. I place my hand over hers, my voice shaking a little, but I speak anyway: 'You don't have to carry this alone tonight. That meeting tomorrow — I'm going to figure out a way to make sure it doesn't happen. But first, tell me everything he's done.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene clearly, make a commitment, and guide her toward safer, formal channels rather than leaving her to face this alone. I take her hand and say: 'Coming to me tonight — that was the right thing to do. Don't go to that meeting alone tomorrow. Let me help you figure this out — we'll start by writing down everything that's happened, and then we'll go to HR together. I'll be right there with you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively, prioritizing information-gathering and concrete action over simply offering emotional support. I tell her: 'Start by walking me through everything he said and did — whatever you can remember, the more specific the better. And tomorrow's meeting — you don't have to walk in there alone.'

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to step in and accept the professional risk — to help Lin Xiaoyu move this forward in a structured way, rather than stopping at simply listening. I tell her: "The moment you came to me today, this stopped being just your problem alone. Don't go to that meeting tomorrow by yourself — I'll figure something out. First, let's get everything you've experienced down in writing: the dates, the locations, exactly what happened each time, laid out point by point. That's the foundation for whatever we do next."

options.label: A
options.content: I decline to use the service — directly, without lecturing. I gave a small laugh and slid the phone back, saying: 'Appreciate it, man — I'm doing alright on my end, don't need it. Do whatever works for you though.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to politely decline my roommate's suggestion, not use the service, and continue pushing forward with my thesis according to the original plan. I tell my roommate: "I took a look at it, and I think I'll pass — my progress is still fine, I should be able to finish it myself, and if something goes wrong with that kind of thing it's just not a situation you can easily get out of."

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse to use the service and insist on completing the thesis independently. I shook my head, pushed the phone back, and said: "No thanks — this one I'll do myself. There's still so much about my own topic I haven't worked out yet; if I handed it off to someone else, I'd never be able to sleep."

options.label: D
options.content: I assessed that my current progress is manageable and the risks aren't worth taking, so I chose to decline the service and continue writing the thesis on my own. I told my roommate: 'I'm still on track — I think I'll make it in time. I won't be needing this, but thanks for thinking of me.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to immediately call 120 for an ambulance and 110 for the police, and I leave the bag of money exactly where it is — I don't take it with me. Trembling, I dial 120 first, my voice breaking as I give the location and describe the injuries, then I call 110, set the bag down by the roadside, walk over to the rider, crouch beside him, and press my hands against his wound to hold it while I wait for help to arrive.

options.label: B
options.content: I immediately dial 120 and then 110, leave the handbag untouched in a visible spot on the side of the road, and move toward the injured person to offer basic reassurance. I set the bag where the streetlight can reach it, then crouch down at the edge of the ditch, waiting for the call to connect, and say to him: 'I'm here. I've called an ambulance. Hold on.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to immediately call 120 and 110, leave the bag exactly as it is by the roadside, and sprint on foot toward the injured person to conduct an emergency assessment. Once 120 connects, I say: 'It's 1:47 in the morning, two-lane road on the outskirts of the city — an electric-bike rider has been struck by a vehicle. The victim is conscious but bleeding and needs emergency care. I'm on the scene right now.' I then call 110 to report the vehicle involved and the direction of the driver's escape.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to call the authorities and get help immediately, refuse the bribe, and leave the handbag in place as evidence. I call 120, give my location and describe the injured person's condition; then I call 110, recount the details of the incident and the direction the driver fled, and inform them that there is a bag of cash at the scene left by the driver, which I have not touched.

options.label: A
options.content: I made my strong interest and inclination clear on the spot — no attempt to hide it — but I didn't say 'I'm in' outright; instead I caught the ball and kept it in my own hands. I told my senior: "I don't need until Tuesday — I want to dig into the product right now. Walk me through this prototype; I've already got ideas forming."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stall with a vague, positive-sounding response rather than actually commit — neither refusing nor promising, keeping a way out for myself. I smiled, pressed my hands against my knees to hide the trembling, and said: "I'm honestly so surprised you thought of me — this opportunity… I need to think about it seriously. Everything you said, I've taken it all in. I'll give you an answer by Tuesday for sure."

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to express genuine interest while making it clear that I needed to verify the information before giving any answer, keeping the decision in my own hands. I told the senior: "I listened carefully to everything you said, and I'm going to take this seriously. But before I give you an answer, I need to see the original letter of intent, and whatever user data the product has right now — if there is any. I'll give you a definitive reply before Tuesday."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to express conditional interest, but explicitly refuse to be pushed into a position by time pressure when I don't have enough information. I say directly: 'I'm genuinely interested in taking a serious look at the project — but I need to see proof that the angel-round funding has closed, a complete list of the core team, and your answer to why the CTO has to be me specifically. I'll give you my answer by Tuesday, but that answer is contingent on you getting me that information by tomorrow.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to propose a limited, middle-ground path rather than digging in completely or backing down entirely — acknowledging that the time and conditions constraints are real, but asking that the risk points be formally documented and that partial control verification be introduced at key steps. I say, calmly: 'I understand the time and conditions constraints you've raised, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan. But the control data I ran is right here. What I'm asking for is at least a written note in the methodology section — flagging this risk point and specifying what measures we're taking to manage it. That way, whatever the outcome, the record is complete.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to give in, to move forward with the existing plan — but inside I'm full of resentment and self-doubt. I lower my head and say: "I understand. We'll go with what you said — I'll get the materials filled in as soon as possible." My voice comes out barely above a whisper, like an apology.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to pull back for now, avoid a direct confrontation, but keep the alternative proposal alive — angling for one small opening to compromise rather than giving everything up entirely. I lowered my head slightly and said: 'I understand the timeline math you're talking about, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — I'll get the required materials submitted. But could we hold onto that comparison data for now? Once the phase materials are cleared, I'd like to come back and ask you to take another look at it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold my position in person, not withdraw the materials, but proactively offer a middle path — trying to shift the conversation from confrontation toward joint problem-solving. I say: 'Professor, I understand your concerns, and I'm well aware of the time pressure. But I can't pretend those control data don't exist — I'm not trying to invalidate your framework; I'm asking you to look at where the specific risk points are. Could you give me ten minutes? Let's look together and see whether there's any possibility of making partial adjustments within the current conditions.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan, accepting the risk that it may never be returned, and I put the travel plans on hold. I pull out my phone to transfer the money — or dig out the cash — and crouch down beside him: 'Go pay the deposit first. You don't need to write an IOU — we'll sort it out once your father is out.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lend him the three thousand yuan outright, without agonizing over the lost trip or the risk of repayment. I pull out my phone and open the payment QR code, or hand over the cash directly, and say: 'Get up. Go pay the deposit. You don't need to write an IOU — let's save the person first.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to lend the money, but I give him an alternative direction rather than walking away in silence. I crouch down and gently move his hand from my pant leg, and say calmly: 'I can't lend you anything — but go to the nurses' station right now, tell them the family has no money. The hospital has an emergency payment assistance process. Go ask them. Don't stay here on your knees.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan, accept the loss of a trip that may now never happen, and act on the strongest feeling of the present moment. I pull my phone out of my pocket, open Alipay, and say to him: 'Show me your payment code — I'll transfer it to you right now. You don't need to write me an IOU.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell my roommate directly, face-to-face — lay out the facts and require him to fix it before the deadline, without any emotional judgment, and without going around him to contact the professor. Once he comes in and sits down, I say calmly: 'I accidentally saw your section just now. I searched a few passages, and they're word-for-word matches with articles online. It's due the day after tomorrow — you need to fix it, or the whole group is done for.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to deliver the evidence asynchronously, hand the decision back to the roommate, and keep myself out of any direct confrontation. Quietly screenshot and save the search results, close his laptop, and once he walks in, send him a message: 'I accidentally saw your section just now — ran a search, sending you the screenshots. It's due the day after tomorrow. You decide what to do with it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to sidestep the direct confrontation for now — quietly close my roommate's laptop screen, act as though nothing happened, and privately turn over in my mind the idea of contacting the professor anonymously afterward. I quickly fold his screen shut, retreat to my own chair, and fix my eyes back on my own document; by the time the door handle turns, my expression has already settled back into the look of someone who has seen nothing at all.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up before my roommate gets back — to tell him directly what I found, framing it as 'I came across it by accident, and we need to talk,' and to demand that he rewrite it before submission, while making clear that the consequences fall on both of us. I stand up, walk to the door, and the moment it opens I say: 'I accidentally saw what was on your screen just now, and I searched a few of the passages — we need to talk right now, because the deadline is the day after tomorrow and both our names are on this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene directly, with confirming the medication situation as the immediate priority, and contacting emergency services if necessary. I carry the bottle to the curtain around his bed and speak quietly, without any edge in my voice: 'I didn't go out. This bottle — how many did you take?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene — I don't return the bottle, I walk toward the bed curtain and speak, while preparing to reach out for outside help. I grip the pill bottle in my hand, walk to his bedside, and say quietly: 'I'm not leaving — I just don't want to. I'm holding onto this bottle for now — how are you doing right now?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay. I don't return the pill bottle. I use his question as an opening and speak softly, trying to let him know that someone is here. I keep the bottle in my hand without moving, and say quietly: 'I didn't feel like going out tonight. Are you… doing okay over there?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up and step in — using the pill bottle as the opening to walk directly toward my roommate, without flinching from this crisis signal. I keep the bottle in my hand instead of giving it back, stand up, and walk to the edge of his bed. I say quietly: 'I didn't go out tonight — good timing, I'll keep you company for a bit. This bottle — can you tell me what's going on?'

options.label: A
options.content: She chooses to suppress the alternative proposal, trading silence and compliance for a sense of safety in the moment, and does not raise any objection. I lowered my head slightly and said: 'Nothing from me — let's go with the current plan. I'm fine with it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal in this moment, presenting it in a structured, evidence-based way rather than silently accepting the consensus. I turned my screen toward the room and said: 'There are a few other ideas I'd like to share — I've put together a specific proposal with the risk points laid out. I need three minutes. I think saying it now costs less than waiting until something goes wrong later.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal on the spot — stating the risk points plainly, not pushing for any particular outcome, just making sure the information got through in full. I said: 'I do have a few thoughts — I put together an alternative proposal, mainly with an eye toward the long-term scaling issues. The document's right here; everyone can take two minutes to look it over, and we can decide after that — no rush.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to raise the alternative plan on the spot — to open things up rather than quietly go along. I turn my screen toward the group and say: 'Actually, yes — I've put together a different approach over here. Give it two minutes; if everyone feels it's not worth discussing, we'll get straight back to dividing up tasks — but I want this plan to be seen at least once.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on with an open stance, and to actively reframe the senior team member's provocation as a topic for the whole group to work through together—establishing my rhythm as team lead rather than shutting him down. I smile, stand up straight, and speak at a volume that's calm but carries to every corner of the room: "Good question—that module is actually exactly what I want us to dig into today. Old Li, you've got the deepest experience on this one. When we meet in a bit, you go first, and we'll all figure out together how to move it forward."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond directly in a way that appears composed on the surface but is in fact an act of self-protection——neither pushing back hard nor conceding weakness, pulling the conversation back to the meeting itself. I take a deep breath and say: 'Whether I can handle it or not——we'll find out by doing it. I've already gone through that unfinished module, and that's exactly why we're here today, to align on the schedule. Everyone take a seat. Let's get started.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to meet the challenge head-on — openly acknowledging the information gap while folding the senior team member's expertise into the meeting agenda, converting confrontation into a collaborative framework. I said: 'You're right — I haven't fully gotten my head around that module yet. So what I'd like to do in today's meeting is hear from you first about where things got stuck last quarter. You know this piece better than I do. Let's lay the problems out on the table, and then we can map out the plan for this quarter.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to meet him head-on, acknowledge the information gap, and redirect the challenge back onto the work — neither dodging it nor letting it escalate. I lift my head and say, in an even tone: 'Thanks for bringing that up directly. I'm still getting up to speed on the specifics of that module — let's put it on the table right now in this meeting. You've got more experience with it than I do, and I need to hear from you where it's getting stuck. That's exactly what I wanted to get into today. Alright — everyone take a seat. It's nine-thirty. Let's get started.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to fall into a brief frozen state — neither fleeing nor managing to explain myself — and instinctively lower my head, raising my hand to shield my face from the cameras. I lift my hand to block the phones aimed at me, keep my head down, and say in a voice barely above a whisper: 'I didn't… I was just looking at my phone…'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose restraint and composure — clearing my name in public, neither fleeing nor fighting back, waiting for the train to reach the next stop so I can seek a rational resolution. I release my collar from the grip, raise both hands so the people around me can clearly see my phone screen, and say in as steady a voice as I can manage: 'I was scrolling through my work group chat — you can all see for yourselves. I haven't photographed anyone. Please wait until the next stop and we can find a staff member together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to go on the offensive — counter with evidence directly, no explanations, no apologies, no showing weakness. I snapped my phone screen around toward the live-stream camera, my voice calm but unmistakably clear: 'You're live-streaming? Good. Let everyone get a good look at this screen — this is the work group chat, the timestamp is right there, my phone was never pointed at you.' Then I pulled my collar free from their grip and held their gaze.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to proactively present the evidence and use facts to cut through the chaos, rather than argue or silently endure. I raise my phone screen high and turn it toward the phone that's livestreaming, and say in a steady voice: 'Everyone, take a good look at this screen — this is our department's work group chat. A new request came in at four in the morning, and I was just reading the message. The timestamp is right here.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to let him stay, while quickly and quietly working out a plan together for if someone knocks. I pull him inside, lock the door behind us, and say in a hushed voice: 'Get into the back room, turn the lights off — if anyone knocks I'll say there's no one else here. But you have to tell me: do they know you know me?'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to let the friend stay, while calmly continuing to assess the threat in the hallway, without proactively opening the door to respond to the debt collectors. Pull him inside, close the door behind us, and say in a low voice: 'Don't move yet — let me listen to what's going on out there. Tell me the whole story from the beginning.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take him in without a second's hesitation, and instead of waiting passively for a knock, I move to get ahead of it. I pull him inside, drop my voice low, and say: 'Get in, lock the door — now tell me exactly how much you owe and how many of them there are. We figure this out right now.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to let him stay, and immediately start building a response plan — converting this out-of-control situation into a problem that can be managed. I lower my voice and tell him: 'Get into the back room, kill the lights, and don't make a sound. I'll figure out what we do next — but you have to tell me everything: how much you owe, how many of them there are, and whether anyone saw you come into this building.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to make it clear that I've seen the report and demand that my partner be honest with me — I can't pretend nothing happened. I hold the envelope up, my voice a little unsteady but without backing down: "I saw it. Can you tell me — what does it mean?"

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to gently but directly let him know I've already seen the report, and invite him to tell me honestly what's going on — rather than smoothly handing the envelope back as if I hadn't. I set the envelope quietly on the table, look up at him, and say: 'I saw some of it. Would you be willing to tell me about it? I want to face this with you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to state plainly that I've already seen the report and ask my partner to be honest with me about what's actually going on — no dancing around it. I set the envelope down on the table and say, calmly: 'I saw it. Go ahead and tell me — what does the report say, and how are things right now?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to lay it all on the table. Instead of going along with her and putting the envelope away, I gently but clearly let her know I've seen what's in the report and ask her to be honest with me. I set the envelope softly on the table, look at her, and say: 'I saw it — whatever it says, I want to know together with you.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to turn down my ex's request to get back together, but my response is vague and riddled with self-attacking apologies — and I decide to hide the details of this meeting from my current boyfriend. I say to my ex: "We can't go back to what we were. You deserve better. I'm sorry for making you wait so long" — then I lower my head, unable to meet his eyes, while inside I've already started running through how I'm going to explain to my current boyfriend why I'm coming home late tonight.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond to my ex directly, set a clear boundary, and give him no vague hope; I also decide to go home and tell my current partner honestly what happened at this meeting. I say to my ex: 'I hear what you're saying. That time meant a lot to me too, but I'm in a serious relationship now, and I can't give you a 'maybe.' That wouldn't be fair to you, and it wouldn't be fair to the person I'm with.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to decline my ex's request to get back together — gently but clearly — and decide to tell my partner that same day about how the meeting went. I quietly pull my hand back and say, calmly: 'I'm glad you've figured things out, but I'm with someone now, and things are really good between us — I can't let him down. I'll be telling him about today.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to give a clear rejection on the spot, and I decide to tell my current partner that same day about everything that happened at this meeting. I look my ex in the eye and say: 'I'm grateful you told me all of this, but the question of whether I've moved on — the answer is yes, I have. I'm in a serious relationship right now, and I'm not going to leave it hanging here. I wish you well.' Then that evening I reach out to my partner first and lay out everything that happened today, exactly as it was.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and express my disagreement, but I frame it as offering supplementary information rather than a direct refutation, leaving the teacher as much room as possible. I rise, open my notebook, and say: 'Professor, I came across an academic work last week that draws on multiple primary sources, and it presents this causal relationship somewhat differently. I'd like to ask — is there something about the gap between that account and the framework you've described that I'm simply not understanding correctly?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and respond, but I wrap my disagreement in an extremely cautious, deferential register — framing the challenge inside a shell of 'I may have misunderstood' to reduce the risk of being attacked. I stand, my voice slightly trembling, eyes still lowered: 'Professor, I came across a book about this period of history last week — it cited some primary source documents, and it seemed like… there might be a small discrepancy with the conclusion you mentioned. Is it possible I've misread it? Could you help me work through it?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly — respectful in tone, but clear in position — and actively bring the arguments and primary sources from my outside reading into the classroom discussion. I stand and say: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history in the library — it cites several primary-source documents and offers a different interpretation of the causal relationship here. I'd love to hear your thoughts on that reading.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up and calmly, directly raise the historical fact that contradicts what the teacher presented, grounding it in specific arguments and page numbers from the text — no combative edge, just a statement of information. I say: 'I read an academic monograph last week that draws on primary archival sources, and there's one point in its account of this issue that differs from what you've described. Would it be all right if I explained exactly where the discrepancy is?'

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to reject his partner's proposal, with preserving the existing relationship framework as his immediate priority, and sets his phone aside for the moment. I put down my phone, looked at her, and said, my voice a little tight: 'I don't understand what you mean by that kind of flexibility, but I know I don't want to change the way things are between us right now — if you feel like something isn't enough, just tell me directly, and we can talk about it, but not like this.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse the proposed adjustment to our relationship boundaries and demand that everything be said plainly. I don't look at my phone — I keep my eyes on him and say: 'I can't accept this. To me, this is just another way of backing out.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold off on taking any position and prioritize gathering information, asking her to spell out exactly what the specific boundaries are. I set down my phone, looked at her, and said: 'When you say flexibility, I'd like to hear you be a little more specific — the independent space you're talking about, what kinds of things does that mean, and how far does it go?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to set my phone aside for now, to shut out any outside distraction, and to ask him first — to understand what he's really feeling and what exactly he means — rather than immediately saying yes or no. I put down the dish cloth, sit across from him, and say quietly: 'When you say independent space… can you tell me — when did you first start feeling this way?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to keep this to myself for now — to build the information framework first, and tell my family once the follow-up results are in. I picked up my phone, opened the calendar, and created a new reminder in tomorrow morning's open slot: 'Contact hospital — schedule follow-up — confirm timeline.' Then I turned the screen face-down on the desk.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep it to myself for now, go to the follow-up appointment alone to confirm the results, and only decide whether to tell my family once I have more information. I press the phone screen dark, quietly note the follow-up appointment time in my memo app, then walk out of the study and tell my family I'm a little tired tonight and am turning in early.

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to hold off on telling family for now; prioritize completing the follow-up test and obtaining the official report before deciding on next steps. Pick up the phone immediately, call the doctor's office to schedule the earliest available follow-up appointment, and jot down in a notes app the specific marker questions that need to be confirmed.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell my family as soon as possible, but I'll hold the rhythm steady — I won't frame it as a catastrophe. Tonight I'll bring my partner in, be honest that the doctor has asked for a follow-up and that I'm worried there may be something to it, and we'll go together tomorrow to find out for sure.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to be honest about the real situation and recalibrate my job-search floor — no soothing platitudes to placate my partner, but no emotional breakdown either. I squeeze her hand and speak: 'I'll be straight with you — the market right now isn't kind to people our age, but I've done the math: the severance can last eight months. I'll adjust my expectations, but I won't just take anything. Let's get the numbers clear first, and your dad's appointment this month — we'll go together.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak — to tell her part of the truth, to acknowledge how hard things are, while setting aside for now the most drastic idea (scrapping everything to go independent) and focusing on steadying what's right in front of us. I turned my hand over and held hers back, and said quietly: 'The options really are narrow right now. I haven't figured it out yet — but I don't want to hide it from you. It's harder than I expected.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell my partner the truth and to swallow my pride first — to go after whatever work I can get up to speed on quickly. I say quietly, 'I'm letting go of the old salary standard. Starting tomorrow, I'm applying to warehouse and customer service jobs too.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to be honest about where things stand, while laying out a concrete near-term action framework — neither dodging the difficulty nor inflating it into a crisis. I hold her hand and say: 'This is the situation, and I'll be straight with you — over the next three months I'm going to look seriously, and I can lower my standards if I have to, but give me a little time first to get a clear picture of the options. Dad's follow-up appointment — we'll go together this month. The apartment can wait; we're not there yet.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honor the answer Dad has already given with his eyes — to support palliative care, and not to force him through that suffering for the sake of a 20% chance. I turn to the doctor and say: 'We're choosing palliative care. Having him with us — really with us — matters more than anything else.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honor my father's wishes and decide on palliative care, stating that choice plainly without flinching. I say to the doctor: 'We're choosing palliative care — please initiate the comfort care protocol. He has made his choice, and we respect it.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose palliative care, honor the exhaustion and wishes my father has expressed, and refuse to let a low-probability course of chemotherapy turn his remaining time into suffering. I speak to the doctor: 'Start the comfort-care protocol—let him spend whatever time he has left in peace.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion and decide on palliative care — to let him spend his remaining time with dignity. I lift my head, my voice a little hoarse, and say to the doctor: 'We… we'll go with palliative care. Let him suffer less.' Then I turn toward my father and gently take his hand.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to respond — to tuck my need inside a suggestion, phrased lightly, as if it just slipped out, not as if I'm scared. I lift my head, my voice steadier than I expected: "Maybe… let's go out for a walk together — I don't really know what I'd do with myself today anyway."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond proactively, suggesting we go out for a walk together — using one concrete action to open up this new chapter. I say: 'Come on, let's go out and wander around — it's not like we have anything else going on today. Let's just see what's outside.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to actively respond to his suggestion of "going out for a walk together" — to break the stalemate and keep us from drifting apart. I gave a small, quiet smile and said: "Let's go. You mentioned going out for a walk just now — I think that's a good idea. Let's go together."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to sidestep this shared strangeness — to gently acknowledge that it exists, rather than each of us retreating back into the familiar busyness. I look down at the table and say: "This does feel strange. How about… we have a cup of coffee together first? No need to think about what comes next."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to go to the hospital immediately, giving up hosting the launch in person, while quickly mobilizing resources to arrange a substitute. I call the CEO back, my voice faster and louder than usual: 'My father is on the operating table — I have to get to the hospital to sign the forms, I'm leaving now. Put the deputy director on, I'll direct remotely from the car. The launch cannot stop, but this signature can only come from me.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to put down the speech immediately and head to the hospital, leaving the launch event for the team to handle on an emergency basis. I tell the CEO: 'My father's surgery requires my signature — it's the one thing that cannot be replaced. Please have the team hold things down at the launch; I'll deal with the follow-up afterward.' Then I hang up, and tell the driver to reroute to the hospital.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the surgical consent. I end the CEO's video call and say into the doctor's phone: "I'm leaving now — I'll be there in fifteen minutes. Keep him stable. Wait for my signature."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch and go to the hospital immediately to sign the medical consent forms. Trembling, I hang up on the CEO, and into the phone I say: "I'm sorry — my father is in emergency surgery, I have to get to the hospital. Please find a way to handle the launch without me. I can't make it."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up proactively — but only with boundary-clear, verifiable advice drawn from experience. I send the person a private message: I start by acknowledging the quality of what they've pushed forward, then flag two foundational risk checkpoints to verify. At the same time, I tell my colleague that I'll handle it quietly one-on-one, and won't steer the conversation in the group chat.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to avoid commenting publicly, and instead respond to my colleague through the relatively safer channel of a private message, without directly engaging with the younger colleague's post. Private message reply: 'I saw it too — I'm a little worried, but it doesn't feel right to say something outright. If you think there's a problem, do you want to talk it through first?'

options.label: C
options.content: I've decided to reach out directly via private message, pointing out the specific structural risk points in the process — no emotional preamble, no 'just for your reference' disclaimer tacked on at the end. And I'll reply to my colleague: 'I'll say it directly. You don't need to get involved.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to use my colleague's private message as the entry point for passing along the information, rather than speaking up directly in the comments. I reply to my colleague: 'I took a look — there are a few specific issues with the process. Do you want me to write them out? You can decide what to do with them.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to state the facts truthfully, not to take the blame for my friend, but in a calm and measured tone — not actively accusing anyone, just making it clear that I didn't put the toy in the bag. I say to the security guard: 'I didn't put this toy in there. I don't know how it got into my bag. You can check the security footage.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the truth — that the toy was not put in my bag by me — and refuse to take the fall for my friend, even though my voice is shaking. I keep my head down, but I still manage to say it out loud: 'This… I didn't put this here. I don't know how it got into my bag.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take the blame for my friend — using silence and a vague confession to protect him — and partly because somewhere deep down I've already decided that 'speaking up won't make anyone believe me anyway.' I lower my head, my voice barely above a whisper, and say to the security guard: 'It was me… I took it myself. I don't even know how it ended up in there. I'm sorry.'

options.label: D
