options.content: I choose to tell the truth, refuse to take the fall for my friend, and state the facts directly to the security guard. I speak calmly: 'I didn't put that toy in there — he did. I don't know when he put it in, and I didn't take anything.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to answer the call, but keep it vague — I don't mention the abnormal results for now, and plan to tell her everything honestly after the follow-up. I press accept and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Hey, I just got the results — a few things need a follow-up check, the doctor said it's pretty common. I've already made the appointment. Don't worry — when you're back Friday we'll talk it through properly.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to answer the video call and share the information honestly but precisely, separating the facts from any conclusions. I pick up and say, calmly: 'The report came back. Two markers are elevated — the doctor recommends a follow-up, and I'm already arranging it. There's nothing more to say until those results are in. You don't need to come back.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I gloss over everything — I downplay it with "it's fine, a couple of indicators came back slightly elevated, the doctor said to just keep an eye on it" — and I don't breathe a word about the line that says "recommend follow-up as soon as possible to rule out the possibility of early pathological changes." I pick up, manage to arrange my face into something that looks reasonably calm, and say: "Just got out — overall it's fine, a couple of minor indicators were a little high, the doctor said it's probably just been too hectic lately, told me to watch my diet a bit. Nothing serious."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I keep things vague for now — I'm not going to drop the worst of it on her yet. Once I pick up, I tell her: two of my markers came back elevated, the doctor wants me to get a follow-up as soon as possible, and the moment those results are in, you'll be the first to know.

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to delay full cooperation and seek legal counsel immediately — selectively acknowledging the basic facts while refusing to provide any information about team members. To the investigator: 'I understand what you're asking. I did see that document — that much is true. But without a lawyer present, I won't be providing any further information. That's the more responsible approach for both of us. I need 24 hours. I'll make good use of them.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while immediately seeking legal protection, and refuse to provide any substantive information before a lawyer is present. Say aloud: 'I need to contact my lawyer first — until then, I'm not in a position to make any statement about the specifics. This isn't a refusal to cooperate; it's my basic right.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose conditional cooperation: make it explicitly clear that a legal counsel must be present before any formal statement can be given, and in the meantime provide no information about team members or internal materials. I speak, my voice steadier than I expected: 'I understand the necessity of this investigation, and I am willing to cooperate. However, without legal counsel present, I am not in a position to make any formal statement on specific matters. Please allow me 24 hours — I will prepare a response with the assistance of a lawyer.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while seeking legal support, and refrain from making statements about others when the information isn't clear. Say aloud: 'I need to consult legal counsel first — I'll give you a response within 24 hours. As for the circumstances of my own signature, I can clarify that right now: at the time, I was simply asked by my superior to review a budget document. I was not involved in any decision-making.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to complete my school responsibility first, then negotiate with Mom about whether we can head over to join the party afterward — I will not abandon a commitment I've already made. I say to Mom: 'Mom, tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the key, and it's something I promised to do. How long do you think it'll take once I'm done? If there's still time, could we go to the amusement park to meet my cousin after?'

options.label: B
options.content: I decide to do both things: go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, then head to the amusement park, and explain my reasoning to Mom upfront. I say to her: 'Mom, I'm the Little Garden Keeper this week — the teacher gave me the key. Tomorrow morning I'll go to school first and water the plants and feed the tadpoles real quick, and then we leave. It won't take long, we'll still make it in time.'

options.label: C
options.content: I decide to do both things, by arranging my time in advance to fit everything in — go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, then head to the amusement park for my cousin's birthday party. I say to Mom: 'Mom, tomorrow morning I'll go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — it won't take long — and then we can go to the amusement park. That way neither one gets missed, okay?'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to do both — go to school first to finish my task, then head to the birthday outing, arranging the timing so the two wouldn't conflict. I told Mom: 'I'm not unhappy — I was thinking I'd head to school first thing in the morning to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, leave around seven-thirty, be back by eight, and then we can all go to the amusement park together at nine. There's plenty of time.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to dodge the camping invitation with a vague excuse, buying time and space — neither outright refusing nor agreeing. Reply: 'It's been so long! Something just came up on our end this weekend, so we probably won't make it — can we plan something another time? Hope you guys have a blast! ～'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a personal excuse, without revealing anything about the secret, and use a brief, vague reason to quietly remove myself from the situation. Reply: 'I haven't been feeling great lately — I probably won't be able to make it this weekend. Hope you all have a great time, let's plan something again soon!'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to politely decline the camping invitation, deflecting with a specific but not over-explained work excuse, while keeping a normal, friendly tone — no cracks, no unnecessary distance. My reply: 'Oh that sounds absolutely gorgeous! I've got a project milestone I need to wrap up this weekend and I'm worried I won't be able to get away last-minute — you all go and have fun, and make sure to include me next time!'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to politely decline the camping invitation, using a simple, honest but non-detailed reason to bow out — no lying, no explaining, no revealing anything. I reply: 'We actually have something going on this weekend, so we won't be able to make it — next time for sure! Hope you all have a great time.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to support the kid's direction, but immediately shift toward gathering information and assessing risks rather than making any emotional declaration. I say: 'You mentioned you've been teaching yourself for years — show me what you've got. Let's look into this path together and figure out what needs to be prepared for ahead of time.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to support my child in pursuing this path — I voice my worries, but I don't use them to invalidate their choice. I say: 'I hear you. You've been preparing for so long, and I have no reason to say no. I have my fears, but those are my homework to do — not a chain around your neck. Let's figure out together how to make this path as solid as it can be.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to object outright, and instead ask that both of us first lay this path out in full and see it clearly before any decision is made. I say: 'Leave that booklet here with me. I'll read through it carefully tonight. We'll talk again tomorrow — I have questions I want to ask you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to push down the impulse to object and offer conditional support, while honestly naming my own fear. I say aloud: 'I'm not against it — but I'm scared. Not because I think you can't do it. It's because I know how hard that road is. I want you to be ready for it, not for me to decide for you whether to walk it or not.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honestly share my genuine assessment of the academic opportunity, while bringing the question of impact on our family into the decision-making framework, and proposing that we map out the specific variables together before drawing any conclusions. I say to him: 'I really am thinking seriously about this. The salary, the pace, the effect on our life — those are all real questions. We can lay them out one by one and look at them together. There's no rush to decide right now, but I want to get the full picture first before I write back to my advisor.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honestly express my assessment of the academic opportunity and to formally reply to my advisor accepting the invitation. I'll say: 'I've thought it through. This opportunity is the right call logically, and I'm going to take it. As for the life logistics — I'll handle them. But they're not a reason to walk away from this.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to honestly express my longing for this academic opportunity, while framing the decision as a project that needs to be evaluated together — not as an emotional impulse. I turn to them and say: 'You're right, I need to think about this seriously. I want to start by laying out all the variables — the salary gap, the adjustment period, the actual impact on our lives — and go through them together, and then I'll reply to my advisor. But I need you to know: that email reminded me of something I thought I'd let go of, and I don't want to close the door on it before I've figured out what that means.'

options.label: D
options.content: Honestly express how strongly I want this academic opportunity, acknowledge the uncertainty, but make it clear that I want to seriously consider accepting the invitation — and that same evening, reply to my advisor's email with an initial expression of intent. I tell him: "I know this will change a lot of things, and I'm scared too. But when I found that notebook last night, I realized I haven't really been alive for a long time — I want to reply to that email, and we can figure out the rest together."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to accept the overall direction while raising specific risk nodes on the spot, pushing for a transition buffer and written confirmation — rather than silently complying or confronting him head-on. I flip open the proposal, press my finger on the first timeline node, and keep my voice steady but unyielding: 'I accept the overall direction, but there are three risk points here that need to be confirmed before execution — if we don't get them written into the transition plan this week, I can't guarantee a problem won't affect the launch date, and I need you to give me a written record of the decision basis.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to raise the specific risk points on the spot, grounding everything in operational facts — not challenging the overall direction, but pushing for adjustments on the nodes that have real problems. I open the proposal and point to two of the timeline entries: 'I have no objection to the big-picture direction — but here and here, the transition period is going to hit concrete problems at this pace. I can walk you through exactly what they are. Do you want to hear it?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to step up and engage directly, raising specific objections on the spot and working to embed my own judgment and buffer space within the execution framework — rather than offering surface compliance. I flip the proposal open, press my index finger against one of the timeline milestones, and say: 'I have an issue to raise here — not a question of whether it can be changed, but if this milestone goes the way you're describing, I can tell you right now exactly what gaps will open up in the transition period. Let's hear how you want to handle that.'

options.label: D
options.content: Accept the overall direction on the surface, while fighting for a voice at the implementation level — trading 'I'll oversee the transition' for real room to preserve the process details that matter. I speak up: "Fine, I have no issue with the overall direction. But if I'm the one overseeing the transition, then I need to break this down according to my own understanding of how these workflows operate. I'll hold the pace you want — but when it comes to the specifics of what gets changed, I need you to give me the space to do that."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse the either-or, to stay where I am without going with anyone, and without declaring support for either side. I look down at the shards of porcelain on the floor and say: "I'm not going anywhere. Whatever this is between you two, you can talk it out — but I'm not choosing."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stay, expressing through the rigid stillness of my body a despair that cannot make any choice, while instinctively leaning toward the 'stay' side. I drew my shoulders in tight, my feet didn't move, and in a voice barely above a whisper I said: "I'm not leaving… I'm not going anywhere…" Then I crouched down, covered my face, and cried out loud.

options.label: C
options.content: Paralysis and avoidance — I cannot make a choice for either side, and I let the breakdown itself interrupt this impossible demand. I stand in the hallway, my legs too weak to hold me up, and the tears finally fall. I cry out loud. I can't get a single word out. I just cry.

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to make the choice either of them is demanding, and in a calm, direct way I hand the decision back to the adults. I speak up: 'This is between the two of you — it's not my business. I'm not going anywhere and I'm not staying for either of you. You decide.' Then I turn around, walk back to my room, and close the door.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea in front of the class, while remaining open to the speech competition proposal without rushing to commit. I stand up and say evenly: "Put the elephant on one side of the seesaw, then keep adding people to the other side until it balances — remember how many people that is, then weigh one person, and multiply. That tells you how much the elephant weighs." When I finish, I sit back down and tell the teacher: "The competition — I'll think it over and let you know."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond in a vague, retreating way — neither outright refusing the teacher nor actually standing up to perform — trying to shrink myself back into the crowd. I keep my head down and say in a very small voice: "Teacher… what I said just now wasn't very clear, it probably wasn't that good…" and give a small shake of my head, without standing up.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up and share my idea in front of everyone; the suggestion about the speech competition is set aside for now, neither accepted nor explicitly refused. I rise from my chair, my voice a little rushed at first but growing steadier as I go: 'It's just… you put an elephant on one end, then you stack people onto the other end one by one, and you count how many people it takes — because you can measure each person's weight ahead of time —'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea concisely, but politely decline the invitation to join the speech competition. I'll say: 'Teacher, I can walk through this method, but I'd rather not join the speech competition — I don't think it's necessary.' Then I'll lay out the logic of the seesaw weighing method in the most straightforward way I can, without dragging it out, and without caring about the skeptical looks.

options.label: A
options.content: He politely declines the invitation, tells the other kid to head back first, and stays to look a little longer before deciding anything — neither diving into research nor leaving right away. "You guys go ahead, I'll come check it out in a bit."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to soften the moment gently — neither flatly refusing the invitation nor immediately giving up my observation — trying to buy myself a little time. I stay crouched where I am, look up, and give him a small smile: "Go ahead and warm up without me — I just need two more minutes. This bug is seriously weird, I've never seen anything like it — I'll be right there!"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation and go play soccer, but at the same time I turn this strange bug into a 'surprise' I bring to the field — merging both things into one. I say to the kid: 'Let's go, let's go — but look at this first. Have you ever seen a bug like this? Its back is green and it actually glows! We'll come back and study it after the game — come on, let's go!'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to politely decline the invitation and stay to keep observing the insect and the plant. Say to the kid: 'You guys go ahead — I'm watching this bug. Never seen one like it before, I want to look a little longer.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take him in without a second's hesitation, and instead of waiting passively for a knock, I move to get ahead of it. I pull him inside, drop my voice low, and say: 'Get in, lock the door — now tell me exactly how much you owe and how many of them there are. We figure this out right now.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to let him stay for now — close the door, keep out of sight, stay on high alert, and make no move to open the door and confront the debt collectors. I drop my voice to a low murmur and tell him: 'Don't make a sound — don't touch the lights either. We wait until they're gone, and then you're going to tell me exactly how deep this goes.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to let the friend stay, while calmly continuing to assess the threat in the hallway, without proactively opening the door to respond to the debt collectors. Pull him inside, close the door behind us, and say in a low voice: 'Don't move yet — let me listen to what's going on out there. Tell me the whole story from the beginning.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to let him stay, while quickly and quietly working out a plan together for if someone knocks. I pull him inside, lock the door behind us, and say in a hushed voice: 'Get into the back room, turn the lights off — if anyone knocks I'll say there's no one else here. But you have to tell me: do they know you know me?'

options.label: A
options.content: I leave my friends' table, tray in hand, and walk alone toward the new girl in the corner. I set my tray down beside her and take a seat, saying quietly: "Is it okay if I sit here? My name is… we're actually in the same class today."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table and walk over alone to the new classmate in the corner. I set my tray down across from her and say, calmly: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — , what's your name?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to set down my chopsticks and walk over to the corner to strike up a conversation with the new classmate, instead of heading to the playground with my friends. I carry my tray over to that little table against the wall, sit down with a smile, and say: 'Is it okay if I sit here? What's your name? I'm — . Let's eat together — it's so loud in this cafeteria, eating alone is no fun!'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively — walk over to the new student and break the ice in a task-oriented way. I pick up my tray, stand up, tell my friends 'Go ahead, I'll catch up,' then walk over to the table in the corner and sit down, saying to the new student: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — . It's your first day — if there's anything you're not sure about, feel free to ask me.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take the blame for my friend — using silence and a vague confession to protect him — and partly because somewhere deep down I've already decided that 'speaking up won't make anyone believe me anyway.' I lower my head, my voice barely above a whisper, and say to the security guard: 'It was me… I took it myself. I don't even know how it ended up in there. I'm sorry.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the truth — that the toy was not put in my bag by me — and refuse to take the fall for my friend, even though my voice is shaking. I keep my head down, but I still manage to say it out loud: 'This… I didn't put this here. I don't know how it got into my bag.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the truth, refuse to take the fall for my friend, and state the facts directly to the security guard. I speak calmly: 'I didn't put that toy in there — he did. I don't know when he put it in, and I didn't take anything.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to state the facts truthfully, not to take the blame for my friend, but in a calm and measured tone — not actively accusing anyone, just making it clear that I didn't put the toy in the bag. I say to the security guard: 'I didn't put this toy in there. I don't know how it got into my bag. You can check the security footage.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to gently but directly let him know that I've seen the report — to invite him to face it together, rather than follow his lead and sidestep it. I quietly set the envelope down on the table between us and say softly: 'I saw it. It's okay. Tell me. We'll get through it together.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to bring it out into the open directly — rather than going along with her and putting the envelope back, I speak up, gently but firmly. I set the envelope down softly on the table, look at her, and say: 'I saw it. Tell me what's going on — whatever it is, we'll face it together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to state plainly that I've already seen the report and ask my partner to be honest with me about what's actually going on — no dancing around it. I set the envelope down on the table and say, calmly: 'I saw it. Go ahead and tell me — what does the report say, and how are things right now?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to make it clear that I've seen the report and demand that my partner be honest with me — I can't pretend nothing happened. I hold the envelope up, my voice a little unsteady but without backing down: "I saw it. Can you tell me — what does it mean?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend and either hand the paper to the teacher or put it back where it came from. I whisper to her, "We can't use this — we'll get caught," and then, while she's not paying attention, I slip the paper back into the crack in the desk or hand it to the teacher, pretending I just happened to find it by accident.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request and decide to hand the answer sheet over to the teacher or return it to where it belongs — trading honesty for genuine peace of mind. I fold the paper and clutch it in my hand, then shake my head at Xiaoyu and say quietly: 'We can't use this — it belongs to the class rep, and I'm going to give it to the teacher. Don't worry, I won't say you saw it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request, return the answer sheet to where it was or hand it over to the teacher — I won't use it or let anyone copy from it. I tell my friend: 'This paper isn't ours. Using it wouldn't even mean anything — we'd still be lost next time. I've put it back. If you want to review, we can go through the material together.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse to use the answer sheet, and I won't let my friend copy it either — I'll return the paper or put it back where it was, and I won't take part in this gamble on luck. I tell my friend: 'I'm not using this, and you shouldn't copy it either — have you actually calculated what it costs if we get caught? I'm putting it back. What you do after that is your call.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to defer a response, requesting more specific information before making any judgment. Tell my friend: 'I need to read through this plan carefully first, and then get a few financial details sorted out — the team's current funding situation, the specific salary figures for the early stage, and your projected break-even timeline. Give me a few days, and I'll come back to you with an answer that's actually grounded in something.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to accept the career change opportunity, but I ask for a little time to arrange a financial buffer rather than signing immediately. I tell my friend: 'I'm in — but give me two weeks. I need to wrap things up properly on this end. Not because I'm hesitating, but because I want to show up giving everything I've got.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation, letting the exhilaration drown out my financial reservations, and I give him a definitive yes on the spot. I look at the screen, grinning, and say: 'Do you even need to ask? I've been waiting for this chance. When do we start — I can be there tomorrow.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to give an answer immediately, and instead ask for a full breakdown of the terms, the equity structure, and the exit mechanism — keeping the decision firmly in my own hands. I tell my friend: 'I need to see the complete terms and the equity agreement. Give me three days, and I'll give you an answer that's grounded in evidence — not one that was rushed out of me.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name alone — no waiting for a joint letter, no accepting the terms of 'private support' as a trade. I tell the neighbor directly: 'I'm not going to push you on the joint letter, but I will be submitting the complaint materials under my own name. The evidence is already organized. If you want to contribute anything, I'll take it; if not, that's fine too — I'll handle this myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: I've decided to file a named complaint with the Housing and Construction Bureau in my own name, accept the neighbors' quiet support without pressing them for a joint signature, and calmly let them know where I stand. I'll tell them: 'The materials are solid enough — my name alone will do. If you're willing to help track down more documents, I'll take that; I'm not going to push the joint-signature angle — but I am going to file the complaint.'

options.label: C
options.content: I decide to file a formal complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name, and to give the upstairs neighbor a direct but measured response — no accusations, but a clear statement of where I stand and what I will and won't accept. To the upstairs neighbor, I say: 'I understand everyone's concerns. I'll file the complaint myself. But I need the materials and information you all promised me — that's my only ask. Two million isn't a matter of a few thousand dollars per household. It's a matter of whether anyone is going to hold this process accountable.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to file the complaint in my own name, as an individual — but I make my disappointment and my boundaries clear to the neighbors who were 'full of advice but unwilling to sign.' I say to the retired teacher: 'I'll go file the report, and I'll put my name on it — but I need you all to promise me this: if the housing authority comes to verify the facts, you cannot deny that you knew. I'm not asking you to be out in front. But I can't stand there alone and lie by saying I discovered all of this by myself.'

options.label: A
options.content: Refuse to cancel outright, and instead propose a scaled-down plan: cut the high-difficulty elements, redistribute responsibilities, and reshape the event into a smaller but genuinely executable version. I speak up: "We're not canceling — but we do need to redesign this. The president's gone, so we turn it into something smaller. Each of you takes on one thing you can actually handle, I'll coordinate the whole thing, and we get through it together."

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to cancel outright, but don't take on the entire mess alone either — instead, propose a scaled-down plan: cut the event to its most essential core, redistribute the few people still around, and only do what can actually be done. I looked up and said: 'We're not canceling — but we need to cut half the content. Everyone left takes ownership of one thing, and I'll keep things moving. Anyone who's still in, tell me right now.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to take over the event, while simultaneously reassessing and trimming the workload to build an executable contingency plan. Say to the three members: 'I know what happened with the president. The event is not being canceled — but we're redividing the work. I'll put together a list: everyone is only responsible for their single most essential piece. I'll send it to you tonight, and you tell me whether you can do it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take over the organizing work, refuse to agree to canceling the event, but proactively scale back the goals and focus on pushing forward the most critical tasks. I say to the members: "Canceling is something I can't agree to — but I'm not going to force any of you either. I'll personally follow up on the most urgent items myself: confirming the sponsors, getting the telescope loan request submitted, and sorting out the volunteer schedule. Whoever has time can pitch in; if you don't, that's fine. As for what shape the event ends up in — we'll see when we get there."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to make any formal decision tonight, and instead follow my gut to explore which new direction genuinely excites me — treating tomorrow's conversation with my manager as a fact-finding session rather than a moment to commit. I sent my former colleague a voice message back: "That's a bold move you made. I'm still looking for the direction that truly lights me up — once I find it, let's talk. Just give me a little time."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to wait and watch — I won't accept my former colleague's invitation, and I'll prepare to tell my supervisor tomorrow that I'm willing to align with the company's current direction and that I want to keep my position. I reply to my former colleague: 'I support your decision, but I've got family pressure on my end — quitting isn't something I'm considering right now. I'll see how things play out here first.' Then I open my calendar and accept the supervisor's invite.

options.label: C
options.content: I decide to spend tonight systematically building a decision framework, then walk into tomorrow's conversation with my manager armed with clear analysis — rather than making any hasty declarations. I open my laptop, create a new document, type "Transition Path Assessment" as the title, and begin listing out the timeline milestones, resource requirements, risk items, and quantifiable evaluation criteria for both paths, while in a separate column I draft the agenda for tomorrow's conversation and note the key information I need to get from my manager.

options.label: D
options.content: I'm choosing to take the risk and make the transition — but I won't quit cold turkey right away. Instead, I'm going to lay my cards on the table proactively. Tonight I'll sign up for a course and put together a learning plan; tomorrow I'll tell my manager that I want to move into an AI-related role and ask about the possibility of an internal transition.

options.label: A
options.content: She chooses to immediately abandon the overseas assignment and go home to be with her father and handle whatever comes next with his medical care. Voice breaking over the phone, she says: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — first flight tomorrow morning. Wait for me.'

options.label: B
options.content: Setting aside all deliberation over the overseas opportunity, deciding in this moment to go home immediately and be with Dad — everything else can wait. Saying to Mom: "I'm booking the ticket right now — the earliest flight tonight or first thing tomorrow morning. Wait for me. I'm coming."

options.label: C
options.content: I decide to set aside any deliberation over the overseas opportunity and go home first to be with Dad and help manage the medical arrangements. The career stuff can come later. I call Mom back and say: 'Mom, I'll be home tomorrow — don't worry too much, okay? I'm coming to be with you both.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to set aside the immediate push on the overseas project and prioritize going home to handle Dad's medical arrangements, while also starting to break down the timelines of both things in search of a middle path. I begin making a list on my phone screen: the earliest train tomorrow morning, contact the attending physician, and at the same time send HR an email asking whether the overseas role allows for a deferred decision — first get the parts I can control into their boxes, then go home.

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to tell the dean of discipline the truth — I would not lie for anyone — and I kept to the facts as much as possible without adding any judgment of my own. I spoke steadily: 'It was the student in the back row who brought them in. I saw it. I wasn't part of the card game.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to state the facts I personally witnessed — only what I saw, no interpretation, not one word more or less on anyone's behalf. I open my mouth and say: 'What I saw is that the deck of cards was taken out of his bag. Beyond that, I don't know.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay vague and refuse to cooperate with either side — deflecting with 'I didn't notice' and 'I don't know' to keep myself out of it, neither informing on anyone nor taking the blame for anyone. I keep my head down and say: 'I was reading at the time — whatever they were doing in the back, I honestly didn't notice. I don't know whose cards those were either.'

options.label: D
options.content: He chooses to tell the truth, refusing to cover for the one who brought the cards, and ignores the threat entirely. Looking the director straight in the eye, he says: 'He's the one who brought them in. I saw it. That's all there is to it — it has nothing to do with me.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to make any promise on the spot. Instead, I hold space for his pain first, and then — gently but firmly — tell him we need to find an adult who can help. I crouch down and take his hand, and I say: 'I won't lie to you and tell you I promise, because I'm too scared of losing you — let's go find someone together, and I'll be right there with you the whole time.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to promise secrecy outright, but also not to immediately announce that I'm going to tell an adult. Instead, I steady her first, stay by her side, and then honestly tell her that I can't carry this alone. I say: 'I'm not going to run off and tell anyone right now — but I also can't promise you I'll keep this secret. Because what I saw, those wounds, that's not something I'm able to help you with on my own. I need you to think through this with me — how we find someone who can actually help you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I didn't promise to keep it secret. Instead, I put the focus on just being there with her — trying to make sure she didn't feel abandoned, while leaving room to seek help later. I crouched down and said quietly: 'I'm not going to go around telling everyone — but I also can't promise to do nothing. Can you tell me what's actually been going on lately?'

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to promise secrecy, and instead choose to be honest — telling her I will seek help from an adult, while doing everything I can to stay by her side and not leave. I say quietly: 'I can't make that promise — not because I don't care about you, but because I'm scared something will happen to you. Let's go find a teacher together right now. I'll be with you the whole time.'

options.label: A
options.content: I decided not to express immediate support or opposition to the special recruitment, and instead to return the decision to my child first, while buying time for the whole family to discuss it together. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you — this is very significant news for us. Before we make any decision, I want to genuinely hear what my child himself has to say. This is ultimately his path to walk, and I want him to walk it because he truly wants to, not because of our expectations. Could you give us a little time?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to politely decline the specialist track and hold firm on keeping my child in the regular education system, without applying for the talent recruitment program. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you so much — knowing that you see him that way, we as parents are truly grateful. But this is too big a decision for me to make on the spot. I need to go home and talk it through properly with the family. His schoolwork is stable right now, and we don't want to rush into anything.'

options.label: C
options.content: I've made up my mind to support the professional path — clearly and without reservation — while insisting that we call my kid out here right now for a three-way conversation, so he can say what he wants in his own words. I tell the teacher: "Personally, I'm in favor — but we need to ask him directly, right now. Can we bring him out here so the three of us can talk this through together?"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold off on committing and gather the key information first before making any decision. I tell the teacher: 'Thank you for letting us know. I need to talk with my child first and find out what he thinks, and I'd also like to learn more about the specifics of this program — things like the exit mechanism and academic safeguards. We'll get back to you after that.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly but without aggression, holding out my notebook as evidence. I stand and say: 'Teacher, last week I read an academic book that draws on primary archival sources, and the way it describes this issue is a bit different from what you've explained — I have notes on it right here. Would it be alright if I shared them?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly — respectful in tone, but clear in position — and actively bring the arguments and primary sources from my outside reading into the classroom discussion. I stand and say: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history in the library — it cites several primary-source documents and offers a different interpretation of the causal relationship here. I'd love to hear your thoughts on that reading.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up and express my disagreement, but I frame it as offering supplementary information rather than a direct refutation, leaving the teacher as much room as possible. I rise, open my notebook, and say: 'Professor, I came across an academic work last week that draws on multiple primary sources, and it presents this causal relationship somewhat differently. I'd like to ask — is there something about the gap between that account and the framework you've described that I'm simply not understanding correctly?'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to defer to authority and avoid dissent, responding to the teacher's call on me in a vague, noncommittal way rather than expressing what I actually thought. I stood up, head lowered, and said: 'Teacher, I… I understand, no questions.' Then I slowly sat back down and quietly closed my notebook, sliding it under my textbook.

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to go along with the lie, choose to tell the homeroom teacher honestly that the model was lost, and immediately pivot to completing the presentation through verbal, impromptu explanation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but clearly: 'The model was left on the bus on the way to school. We don't have a prop, but we can just talk through it — I'll describe how the solar system works. We don't need the model.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to go along with the lie, but I step up and take control of the narrative myself — using language that is vague yet truthful, leaving my teammate some breathing room, while quickly proposing a no-model fallback plan for our presentation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say: 'Ms. Chen, the model had an accident on the way to school and couldn't make it here — that's our group's fault. But we've prepared a full verbal explanation, and we're asking for the chance to go up and present.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the model being lost, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately propose an alternative plan for the presentation. I say to the teacher: 'The model was lost on the way to school — there's no getting it back. We can walk through everything verbally; I have all the content memorized. We're ready to go up right now.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell a softened version of the truth: acknowledge that the model wasn't brought, without actively naming who's responsible, while indicating that the group can complete the presentation through a verbal walkthrough. I say to the homeroom teacher: 'Ms. [Teacher], the model wasn't able to make it today — something came up — but we're fully prepared to walk through everything verbally. The content is all there. Please let us go up.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take the microphone and speak sincerely — to get through an impromptu acceptance speech by looking after the mood of the room, rather than retreating into silence. I gave a small smile into the microphone, voice a little unsteady but still finding the words: 'Thank you to the teacher and the judges. When I wrote this piece, I really just wanted to put down on paper a question that had been rattling around in my head and wouldn't go away… I never expected anyone to like it. I think maybe — the kind of confusion the story is about — maybe most of us have felt something like that at some point.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to push through and speak anyway, but before opening my mouth I forced myself to quickly assemble a two-point structural framework in my head, using that sense of order to hold the panic down. I took a deep breath, gripped the microphone, and said: 'Thank you to the judges and to Ms. Zhao. When I was writing this piece, I was really thinking about two things — one is how we understand the idea of "the future," and the other is what ordinary people can do in the face of enormous change. I never expected these two questions to resonate with anyone, so this recognition genuinely surprises me, and it means a great deal. Thank you all.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak, but I use self-deprecation and deliberate understatement to preemptively defuse any possible doubt — pushing the honor away from myself to keep myself safe. I lean into the microphone and say: 'Honestly… I didn't expect this myself. I wrote this piece pretty casually, so maybe the judges saw something in it that I didn't — thank you all.' Then I lower my head, take a small step back, and return the microphone to the host.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak off the cuff, honestly laying bare how I actually create, without pretending to be a hardworking student who prepared meticulously. I smiled into the mic and said: 'Honestly, I wrote this piece one afternoon when the feeling just hit me——didn't even proofread it. I think inspiration is something you can't plan for; you just have to wait for it to come find you.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation directly — no deflecting, no pretending I didn't pick up on the subtext — while making my situation unambiguously clear. I say: "Thank you for telling me all of this. I genuinely think the collaboration these past few months has been something rare. But I'm in a relationship, and I can't accept this invitation — not because I don't want to go, but because I know it wouldn't just be a bookstore outing."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation, but respond with sincerity rather than coldness — acknowledging what this partnership has meant, while drawing a clear line. I say to TA: 'These past few months working with you have been something I genuinely value. But Saturday… I can't go. Just the two of us — it wouldn't be right.'

options.label: C
options.content: Accept the invitation — but also decide to have a real conversation with my partner that same night, and face whatever has actually gone wrong between us. I say to them: "I'd love to go, I've been wanting to catch this talk too — but there's something I need to tell you first, about where things have been between us lately."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation, but respond in an offhand, understated way — without explicitly acknowledging what it means to me. I look up, let the corner of my mouth pull into something almost like a smile, and say: "Sure, I've been meaning to catch that author too. Send me the address."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to make my position clear calmly but firmly — neither accepting the cash nor letting things explode into a scene. I push the wad of money back across the coffee table and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Uncle, I can't take this. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't about the cost of replacing a hard drive. Those files are gone, and that's that. I understand the kid didn't know any better, but I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to decline the money gently but clearly, to speak the true weight of what was lost, without letting it escalate into a direct confrontation. I push the stack of bills back toward him, my voice slightly unsteady but without tears, and say: 'Uncle, I can't take the money — that hard drive had six months of my work on it, and there's no replacing it. I just need a little time to get through this.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse the money, say my piece briefly, and end this conversation as quickly as possible — without getting into a direct argument with my uncle, but without pretending everything is fine either. I push the stack of money back across the coffee table, my voice flat, and say only one thing: 'Keep the money. What was in there isn't a money matter.' Then I stand up to go get some water, and I don't look at them again.

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to take the money. In a tone that is tightly controlled but unmistakably clear, I explain what was actually lost — I don't break down in front of my uncle, but I don't pretend this is something we can just move past either. I push the stack of bills back, my voice level, every word bitten off clean: 'This isn't about money, Uncle. That drive had six months of my work on it. No backup. It's gone. I can't take this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I drop the bag, immediately dial 120 and 110, then run toward the rider and try to help. I threw the bag down on the roadside, pulled out my phone and called 120, ran to the edge of the drainage ditch and crouched down: 'I'm here, the ambulance is coming, just hold on.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to call emergency services and the police, leave the handbag exactly as it is on the ground, and stay on the scene to wait for help to arrive. I set the handbag down by the roadside and dialed 120, my voice pressing down against a tremor: 'Someone's been hit by a car — it's on that two-lane road at the edge of the city, near the abandoned tin shed. The injured person is beside the drainage ditch. Please come quickly.' Then I called 110 and told them everything I'd seen, including the black Audi and the driver who fled.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to call the authorities and get help immediately, refuse the bribe, and leave the handbag in place as evidence. I call 120, give my location and describe the injured person's condition; then I call 110, recount the details of the incident and the direction the driver fled, and inform them that there is a bag of cash at the scene left by the driver, which I have not touched.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to immediately call 120 and 110, leave the money bag exactly where it is, and walk toward the injured person to assess their condition. I set the bag down by the roadside, dial 120, and say: 'Someone's been hit — they need emergency help, I'm at—' At the same time I crouch down beside the rider and tell him: 'Help is on the way. Hold on.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to sign off confirming the data is valid, but I also won't formally report it to a superior — instead, I tell my colleague directly that I can't sign off on the data, and demand that he correct it or explain the situation himself before submitting. I say to him: 'I can't put my name on this data as confirmed. You know what I saw — you need to figure out how you're going to handle this before you submit tonight. I'm not stamping this for you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, while giving my colleague the opportunity to correct the error himself rather than going over his head directly. I tell him: 'I can't sign off on this data — but before I say anything to anyone above us, I'm giving you tonight. You go to the project team yourself and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the only concession I can make.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to report the data fraud to my supervisor and refuse to sign off confirming the data is valid — even though I am terrified of the interpersonal fallout that will follow. I tell my colleague: 'I know this puts you in a hard place, but I can't sign off on this. Tonight I'm going to tell our supervisor exactly what I know. You should probably go explain yourself first.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to report the data falsification to management honestly and to refuse to sign the confirmation form. I tell my colleague: 'I understand the position you're in, but I can't confirm this data. I'll be letting management know the full situation tonight.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to introduce my situation proactively, clearly, and with structure — no deflecting, no defending, just delivering a 'status report' on my own life the way a project manager would. I say: 'I do project planning at a nonprofit. The salary isn't high, but I manage every project from kickoff to close-out, and I manage it well. In my spare time I paint — last year I got into the local young artists' exhibition, which counts as a milestone. Renting is my choice for this stage; I haven't hit the point where buying makes sense. As for joining a big tech company — I evaluated it, and it doesn't fit my current execution roadmap.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to describe my situation directly and calmly, neither sidestepping the gap nor making a point of it, and I respond to well-meaning suggestions politely but clearly, letting them know I have my own considerations. I say: 'I work as a general practitioner at a community health center — pretty stable work. In my spare time I paint; last year a piece of mine got into the local young artists' exhibition, which was a lot of fun. As for housing, I haven't really thought about it for now — things are good the way they are.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to openly share who I am — in a tone that's easy and unhurried but clear-eyed — laying out my life without defending it or shrinking from it, and slipping in a mention of the exhibition, so this table knows I have my own coordinates. I smiled, raised my glass, and said: 'Nonprofit sector — the pay really isn't much, but last year I had a painting selected for a young artists' exhibition. I'm doing pretty well, just a different kind of well from you all. Come on — let's drink.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to state my situation directly and without defensiveness — neither playing along nor pushing back, letting the facts themselves answer all the well-meaning questions. I say: 'I do project planning for a nonprofit — the salary isn't high, I rent, and last year one of my paintings got into a youth art exhibition. That's my life right now, and I'm pretty happy with it. Thanks for the advice about buying a place; it's just not in my plans at the moment.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell him face-to-face, privately, when he gets back — giving him the chance to rewrite it before submission — rather than staying silent or going straight to the professor. When he pushes the door open, I look up and say: 'I accidentally saw your document just now, and I searched a few passages — they're a perfect match with an article on Zhihu. Can you let me tell you what this opportunity means to me? And then we can figure out what to do together.'

options.label: C
options.content: Honestly express how strongly I want this academic opportunity, acknowledge the uncertainty, but make it clear that I want to seriously consider accepting the invitation — and that same evening, reply to my advisor's email with an initial expression of intent. I tell him: "I know this will change a lot of things, and I'm scared too. But when I found that notebook last night, I realized I haven't really been alive for a long time — I want to reply to that email, and we can figure out the rest together."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honestly express my longing for this academic opportunity, while framing the decision as a project that needs to be evaluated together — not as an emotional impulse. I turn to them and say: 'You're right, I need to think about this seriously. I want to start by laying out all the variables — the salary gap, the adjustment period, the actual impact on our lives — and go through them together, and then I'll reply to my advisor. But I need you to know: that email reminded me of something I thought I'd let go of, and I don't want to close the door on it before I've figured out what that means.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to reply to the message, but keep the wording concise and measured — clear boundaries, neither deliberately cold nor leaving any opening. I reply: 'Made it home. That line of thinking from today's meeting is worth developing further — the bookstore idea has potential. Goodnight.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to reply to that message, in a tone that is light but genuine — not crossing any line, but not pretending to be indifferent either. I write back: "Made it home. I kept thinking about what you said during the meeting today. That bookstore sounds very quiet — we should go sometime. Good night."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to reply to my colleague but deliberately narrow the boundary, using a polite yet clear-eyed tone to pull the connection back to a safe distance. I write: 'Made it home — thanks for checking in. That conversation the other day was really lovely; I'll have to recommend the bookstore to you sometime. Good night.' — Then I put down my phone, open my notes app, and add tomorrow's tutoring run with the kids to my schedule.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to reply to my colleague's message for now — cutting off the bud with self-attack, pressing the longing back down. I darken the phone screen, pick up my partner's message, reply with a single "okay," then close both chat windows and stare at the blurred night outside the car window, telling myself: you could never hold something like this together.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to challenge the logical gaps in the proposal directly, on the spot — not attacking his authority as such, but forcing him to back it up with data. I flip the proposal open, point to the timeline, and say: 'I do have an objection — let's start with one: how were the transition-period timelines determined? If there's no actual measurement based on the existing process, I can't guarantee the rollout won't run into problems, and you won't get the results you're after either.'

options.label: B
options.content: Accept the overall direction on the surface, while fighting for a voice at the implementation level — trading 'I'll oversee the transition' for real room to preserve the process details that matter. I speak up: "Fine, I have no issue with the overall direction. But if I'm the one overseeing the transition, then I need to break this down according to my own understanding of how these workflows operate. I'll hold the pace you want — but when it comes to the specifics of what gets changed, I need you to give me the space to do that."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to appear cooperative on the surface while quietly buying time — raising risk details as a way of creating friction without direct confrontation. I speak up: 'I hear you on the direction, and I'll follow through on execution — but there are a few risk points in the transition period you may not have seen yet. Let me write them up; take a look and decide whether we should go over them first, so if something does go wrong later, nobody's left scrambling to explain it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the overall direction while raising specific risk nodes on the spot, pushing for a transition buffer and written confirmation — rather than silently complying or confronting him head-on. I flip open the proposal, press my finger on the first timeline node, and keep my voice steady but unyielding: 'I accept the overall direction, but there are three risk points here that need to be confirmed before execution — if we don't get them written into the transition plan this week, I can't guarantee a problem won't affect the launch date, and I need you to give me a written record of the decision basis.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take care of the watering and tadpole-feeding at school first, then decide afterward whether to head over to the gathering — but I'm not committing to showing up. I tell Mom: 'Tomorrow I have to go to school first to water the plants. The teacher gave me the keys — the mimosa was just repotted and it can't wait. Once I'm done I'll see if I can make it over, but I might not be in time. Can you let Uncle know for me?'

options.label: B
options.content: I decide to do both — wake up early, go to school first to take care of my responsibilities, then head to the amusement park. I tell Mom: 'I'm in! But I have to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the keys, and I made a promise. I'll go early and be back early. If I leave at seven-thirty, I should make it in time!'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to complete my school responsibility first, then negotiate with Mom about whether we can head over to join the party afterward — I will not abandon a commitment I've already made. I say to Mom: 'Mom, tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the key, and it's something I promised to do. How long do you think it'll take once I'm done? If there's still time, could we go to the amusement park to meet my cousin after?'

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to do both things: go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, then head to the amusement park, and explain my reasoning to Mom upfront. I say to her: 'Mom, I'm the Little Garden Keeper this week — the teacher gave me the key. Tomorrow morning I'll go to school first and water the plants and feed the tadpoles real quick, and then we leave. It won't take long, we'll still make it in time.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to support the kid without reservation, no conditions attached, no 'been-there-done-that' warnings. I look the kid in the eyes and say: 'Apply for it — go with this one. I've seen too many people put out their own fire. Don't you put yours out.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to object outright, and instead ask that both of us first lay this path out in full and see it clearly before any decision is made. I say: 'Leave that booklet here with me. I'll read through it carefully tonight. We'll talk again tomorrow — I have questions I want to ask you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to support the kid — pushing down every worry about employment and income, refusing to echo the dismissals of the generation before me. I was silent for a moment, then I spoke: "You said you've loved this since middle school, that you've been teaching yourself all along — I believe you. Go and try. I won't stand in your way."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to offer conditional support, but I require my kid to sit through a structured risk-breakdown conversation right now, rather than simply handing over an emotional endorsement. I push the report booklet back across the table and say: 'You say seven years — I believe you. But let's go through this field's employment data, your competitive edge, and your exit path in the worst-case scenario, one by one. If you can answer those, I'll support you filling it in.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a vague excuse, without revealing any secrets and without making any promises about the future. Reply: 'I've been feeling a bit all over the place lately — I don't think I can make it this weekend. You two go have fun, and we'll plan something another time!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to politely decline the camping invitation, deflecting with a specific but not over-explained work excuse, while keeping a normal, friendly tone — no cracks, no unnecessary distance. My reply: 'Oh that sounds absolutely gorgeous! I've got a project milestone I need to wrap up this weekend and I'm worried I won't be able to get away last-minute — you all go and have fun, and make sure to include me next time!'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a plausible excuse, removing myself from the situation entirely — neither breaking the promise I made to him nor showing up to play a supporting role in this deception. Reply: 'There's a project at work that's hit a critical point right now, so I'm afraid I can't get away this weekend — you all go ahead and have fun, and we'll find another time to get together.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stall and respond vaguely to dodge the camping invitation — neither accepting nor explicitly declining — buying myself some time. I reply: 'It sounds absolutely gorgeous! I just have something on my end this week that I need to check the timing on — I'll let you know for sure in a day or two, you guys go ahead and make your plans in the meantime~ '

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene directly — I don't return the pill bottle, and I immediately contact the resident advisor or call a mental health crisis line. I don't let this get handled internally within the dorm room. I keep the bottle in my hand, stand up, and walk to the curtain around his bed. In a steady voice, I say: 'I'm not giving this back to you. You need someone here right now — I'm going to contact the RA. If you don't want me to, tell me now.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to return the bottle. I stay where I am and speak first, while also deciding I need to contact the resident advisor or a mental health crisis line. I close my hand around the pill bottle — I don't kick it back — and I turn to face his bed curtain and say: 'I'm not going anywhere tonight, because I'm right here. Over there — could you pull the curtain open a little? I want to see you.' At the same time, I quietly pick up my phone, ready to contact the resident advisor.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak up and respond to my roommate — using the bottle as a reason to stay, remaining right where I am in a clumsy but genuine way. I keep the bottle in my hand instead of kicking it back, turn around, and say: 'I don't feel like going out tonight. This bottle of pills… can you tell me about it?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to return the pill bottle. Instead, I stay and speak up to check on him — without immediately contacting the RA — and try to build a connection first. I crouch down, set the bottle in my palm rather than kicking it back, and say: 'I'm not going out tonight… I'd like to sit with you for a while, if that's okay.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to turn down my ex's request to get back together, but my response is vague and riddled with self-attacking apologies — and I decide to hide the details of this meeting from my current boyfriend. I say to my ex: "We can't go back to what we were. You deserve better. I'm sorry for making you wait so long" — then I lower my head, unable to meet his eyes, while inside I've already started running through how I'm going to explain to my current boyfriend why I'm coming home late tonight.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond to my ex directly, set a clear boundary, and give him no vague hope; I also decide to go home and tell my current partner honestly what happened at this meeting. I say to my ex: 'I hear what you're saying. That time meant a lot to me too, but I'm in a serious relationship now, and I can't give you a 'maybe.' That wouldn't be fair to you, and it wouldn't be fair to the person I'm with.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to decline my ex's request to get back together — gently but clearly — and decide to tell my partner that same day about how the meeting went. I quietly pull my hand back and say, calmly: 'I'm glad you've figured things out, but I'm with someone now, and things are really good between us — I can't let him down. I'll be telling him about today.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to give a clear rejection on the spot, and I decide that when I get home I will proactively tell my current partner everything about this meeting — closing every open variable. To my ex, I say: 'I hear what you're saying, but I'm in a relationship I'm genuinely invested in right now. I can't give you that space of 'just waiting' — it wouldn't be fair to any of the three of us. My answer is no.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to state the facts truthfully, not to take the blame for my friend, but in a calm and measured tone — not actively accusing anyone, just making it clear that I didn't put the toy in the bag. I say to the security guard: 'I didn't put this toy in there. I don't know how it got into my bag. You can check the security footage.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the truth and not take the blame for my friend — I state the facts directly to the security guard. I say: 'I didn't put that toy in there. My friend put it in my bag. I didn't know about it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take the blame for my friend, admitting that I 'wasn't thinking' when I took the toy. Head down, I tell the security guard: "I took it. I don't even know how it ended up in there. I'm sorry."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the truth — that the toy was not put in my bag by me — and refuse to take the fall for my friend, even though my voice is shaking. I keep my head down, but I still manage to say it out loud: 'This… I didn't put this here. I don't know how it got into my bag.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation and go play soccer, but at the same time I turn this strange bug into a 'surprise' I bring to the field — merging both things into one. I say to the kid: 'Let's go, let's go — but look at this first. Have you ever seen a bug like this? Its back is green and it actually glows! We'll come back and study it after the game — come on, let's go!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stay and keep observing the bug, turning down the invitation to play soccer in an easy, offhand tone so the other kid feels free to go ahead. I smile and wave them off: 'You guys go on without me——there's something here that's way too cool, look——this bug's antennae fork into three tips, I've never seen that before. You want to crouch down and take a look? If not, go ahead, I might come over later.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to politely decline the invitation for now, finish observing and mentally recording the insect and the plant, and then consider heading to the field. I tell that boy: 'Go ahead and start without me — I'll come over as soon as I'm done looking at this. If you're short a player, just give me a few more minutes; I'll be there once I've got it all noted down.'

options.label: D
options.content: He politely declines the invitation, tells the other kid to head back first, and stays to look a little longer before deciding anything — neither diving into research nor leaving right away. "You guys go ahead, I'll come check it out in a bit."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell a softened version of the truth: acknowledge that the model wasn't brought, without actively naming who's responsible, while indicating that the group can complete the presentation through a verbal walkthrough. I say to the homeroom teacher: 'Ms. [Teacher], the model wasn't able to make it today — something came up — but we're fully prepared to walk through everything verbally. The content is all there. Please let us go up.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the missing model, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately switch to a no-model presentation. I say to the teacher: 'The model was left on the bus on the way to school. We'll complete the presentation using a verbal explanation — I know the material thoroughly, and we're ready to begin.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to go along with the lie, but I also don't throw my teammate under the bus on the spot — instead, I steer the conversation directly toward how to handle the presentation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but quickly: 'The model had an accident on the way here and didn't make it, but we can go up and present without it — I have all the content memorized.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the model being lost, refuse to go along with the lie, and proactively offer to carry the presentation through with a verbal explanation alone. I say directly to the homeroom teacher: 'He left the model on the bus — there's no getting it back. We don't have the model, but I have the whole thing memorized. I'll present it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table and walk over alone to the new classmate in the corner. I set my tray down across from her and say, calmly: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — , what's your name?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to put down my chopsticks, leave my friends' table, and walk toward the new girl in the corner. I carry my tray over and sit down across from her, saying: 'My name's Xiaopéngyǒu — are you eating alone? I came to keep you company.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to leave the lively crowded table, pick up my tray, and walk over to the new student sitting alone in the corner — not forcing myself to seem warm or outgoing, just going over and sitting down. I carry my tray to that small table, set it down gently, and say quietly: 'Is it okay if I sit here?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively — walk over to the new student and break the ice in a task-oriented way. I pick up my tray, stand up, tell my friends 'Go ahead, I'll catch up,' then walk over to the table in the corner and sit down, saying to the new student: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — . It's your first day — if there's anything you're not sure about, feel free to ask me.'

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to accept the 'money in exchange for effort' arrangement, and I speak up directly in the family group chat to demand that caregiving responsibilities be redistributed. I will no longer use silence to keep up a surface-level peace. I send a voice message to the group: 'I can't keep going like this. Mom and Dad need more than just money—I can't hold this up on my own anymore. We need to meet in person next week, and every family needs to send someone.'

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to accept this arrangement through silence. I choose to send a real response in the group chat, making it clear that the current division of responsibilities is not sustainable, and demanding that we renegotiate the specifics of how things are shared. I type into the group: "Money isn't the issue — the issue is that this isn't something I can carry alone. We need to sit down and seriously talk about how to divide this, and that means time and people, not just money." Then I put down my phone and turn to walk toward my partner.

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to accept the vague "money for effort" arrangement floated in the group chat — but I don't explode in the chat right now. Instead, I put my phone down and walk toward my partner. The first words out of my mouth are: "I see you. Let's talk about us first."

options.label: D
options.content: I'm not accepting another round of carrying the bulk of the caregiving alone — but I'm not blowing things up in the open either. I reply in the group chat: both money and caregiving need to be shared on a rotating schedule; I'll cover the medication this time, and tomorrow I'll send out the list and the shift rotation. Then I turn and quietly tell my partner that we need to talk tonight — really talk.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse to use the answer sheet, and I won't let my friend copy it either — I'll return the paper or put it back where it was, and I won't take part in this gamble on luck. I tell my friend: 'I'm not using this, and you shouldn't copy it either — have you actually calculated what it costs if we get caught? I'm putting it back. What you do after that is your call.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to refuse copying and return the answer sheet to where it was — neither reporting it nor using it. Say to Xin: 'We can't copy from this — put it back. Getting caught would be way worse than just bombing the test. You head out first, I'll be there once I finish sweeping up.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse to use the answer sheet, and to hand it to the teacher or return it to where it came from, while gently turning down my best friend's request. I fold the paper and clutch it in my hand, shake my head at my friend, and say: 'We can't use this — I'm going to hand it in to the teacher. You shouldn't copy from it either. Let's go back and review; there's still time.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request and decide to hand the answer sheet over to the teacher or return it to where it belongs — trading honesty for genuine peace of mind. I fold the paper and clutch it in my hand, then shake my head at Xiaoyu and say quietly: 'We can't use this — it belongs to the class rep, and I'm going to give it to the teacher. Don't worry, I won't say you saw it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving a definitive answer and ask to gather information systematically before deciding. I say to the teacher: 'Thank you so much for your trust in us — this is genuinely important news. I'd like to have a real conversation with my child first, and at the same time get a clearer picture of the specialist program's curriculum and timeline. Would it be possible to get a detailed information packet? We'll get back to you as quickly as we can.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on committing and gather the key information first before making any decision. I tell the teacher: 'Thank you for letting us know. I need to talk with my child first and find out what he thinks, and I'd also like to learn more about the specifics of this program — things like the exit mechanism and academic safeguards. We'll get back to you after that.'

options.label: C
options.content: I've made up my mind to support the professional path — clearly and without reservation — while insisting that we call my kid out here right now for a three-way conversation, so he can say what he wants in his own words. I tell the teacher: "Personally, I'm in favor — but we need to ask him directly, right now. Can we bring him out here so the three of us can talk this through together?"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to commit on the spot, and instead return the decision to the child rather than letting myself be swept along by the teacher's excitement or my own fear. I say to the teacher: 'Thank you — this means a great deal to us. I'd like to speak with him alone first, hear what he thinks, and then we'll make a decision.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lay it all on the table. Instead of going along with her and putting the envelope away, I gently but clearly let her know I've seen what's in the report and ask her to be honest with me. I set the envelope softly on the table, look at her, and say: 'I saw it — whatever it says, I want to know together with you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to state directly that I've seen the contents of the report, and I ask my partner to tell me honestly what's going on — I won't accept evasion. I set the envelope on the table and speak, calm but clear: 'I saw it — it's a recent test report. Are you willing to tell me what it says?'

options.label: C
