options.content: I choose to make it clear that I've seen the report and demand that my partner be honest with me — I can't pretend nothing happened. I hold the envelope up, my voice a little unsteady but without backing down: "I saw it. Can you tell me — what does it mean?"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to gently but directly let him know I've already seen the report, and invite him to tell me honestly what's going on — rather than smoothly handing the envelope back as if I hadn't. I set the envelope quietly on the table, look up at him, and say: 'I saw some of it. Would you be willing to tell me about it? I want to face this with you.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse his 'generosity' right there in front of everyone, and to push back against this humiliation with a cutting remark. I slide the business card back toward him, and with a cold smile I say: 'I appreciate the thought — but I'm much more used to being my own boss. That assistant position of yours? Save it for someone who needs their life managed for them.' Then I turn and walk out of the reception.

options.label: B
options.content: I decline the card, and in front of everyone I flip this so-called 'invitation' into a public counter-strike — letting every person in the room see exactly what is behind that business card. I don't reach out to take it. Instead, I smile slightly, my voice pitched just loud enough for two or three tables to catch every word: 'Generous enough? Twelve years ago you put your name on my proposal — now that was generous. Keep the assistant position. It's not for me — I've never been good at signing other people's names for them.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly, giving him no emotional reaction to exploit, letting the whole performance collapse in plain sight with nothing but calm. I glanced down at the card, then looked up, my voice as level as if I were stating a simple fact: 'No need. For the assistant position, I'd suggest finding someone who doesn't know how you got to where you are — it'll save you a lot of headaches.' Then I picked up my orange juice and walked away.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — tone steady and clear, giving him no emotional leverage, letting no one around me see a single flicker of panic. I push the business card back toward him, voice pitched just right, loud enough for the people nearby to hear: 'Thanks, I'm good. The work I do now — I use my brain.' Then I turn away and go back to my orange juice.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to give a vague, partial admission — deliberately blurring the details — hoping to make the dean feel like I'm cooperating without actually naming anyone, while leaving myself a little room. I keep my eyes down and say: 'I… did see some people playing something in the back, but I couldn't really tell who took it out first — I had my head down the whole time, I was reading, I genuinely couldn't see clearly.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the truth about who brought the contraband in, prioritizing my own protection and making clear I had no part in it. I'll quietly tell the director exactly who I saw and what happened — and I'll also let him know that person just threatened me.

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to tell the dean of discipline the truth — I would not lie for anyone — and I kept to the facts as much as possible without adding any judgment of my own. I spoke steadily: 'It was the student in the back row who brought them in. I saw it. I wasn't part of the card game.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay vague and refuse to cooperate with either side — deflecting with 'I didn't notice' and 'I don't know' to keep myself out of it, neither informing on anyone nor taking the blame for anyone. I keep my head down and say: 'I was reading at the time — whatever they were doing in the back, I honestly didn't notice. I don't know whose cards those were either.'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to push through and speak anyway, but before opening my mouth I forced myself to quickly assemble a two-point structural framework in my head, using that sense of order to hold the panic down. I took a deep breath, gripped the microphone, and said: 'Thank you to the judges and to Ms. Zhao. When I was writing this piece, I was really thinking about two things — one is how we understand the idea of "the future," and the other is what ordinary people can do in the face of enormous change. I never expected these two questions to resonate with anyone, so this recognition genuinely surprises me, and it means a great deal. Thank you all.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak honestly, treating this unexpected win itself as an interesting 'counter-intuitive phenomenon' to share, and naturally weaving in all the chaotic associations and questions I had while writing. I lean into the microphone and say: "Honestly, I didn't even proofread for typos before I submitted — so I think that fact is more interesting than my story itself: maybe the ideas that look 'not serious enough' are actually hiding something worth exploring."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak off the cuff, honestly laying bare how I actually create, without pretending to be a hardworking student who prepared meticulously. I smiled into the mic and said: 'Honestly, I wrote this piece one afternoon when the feeling just hit me——didn't even proofread it. I think inspiration is something you can't plan for; you just have to wait for it to come find you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to speak directly, describing my creative process in the most concise and honest way I could — no performed gratitude, no studied humility. Into the microphone I said: 'I wrote this piece off the top of my head three weeks ago. I just wanted to work out a story with a coherent internal logic. Winning means the judges found that logic convincing, and honestly, that's a better outcome than I expected.'

options.label: A
options.content: Refuse to include the colleague on maternity leave in the list, and state my reasons directly — no confrontation, no compromise. I tell my supervisor: 'I can't write that her performance failed to meet expectations on the list. Her evaluation is a B+ — that's the fact on record, and I can't sign off on something that contradicts it. I understand the situation with the relative, but for the other slot I need to go by actual performance. If you can point me to an employee rated C, I'll handle the paperwork for that.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse to write my female colleague's name down, but I won't directly confront my superior either. I'll stall, play dumb, or just hand in my resignation — I was never cut out for a place like this anyway, and I'd rather walk away than be forced into doing something that disgusts me.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse to include this colleague on the list, and I make it explicitly clear to my superior that I cannot carry out this instruction — while keeping a written record to protect myself. I look my superior directly in the eye and say: 'This colleague received a B+ on her quarterly review. I have no way to write 'failing to meet performance expectations' on this list — if the list needs to be revised, please give me a reason that is consistent with the actual performance data. Otherwise, I have no way to sign this document.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose not to write my colleague — the one who just returned from maternity leave — onto the list, but I didn't confront my boss head-on either. Instead, I used delay and vagueness to buy myself time. I told him: 'I need to go back through the complete performance review records. I'll send you my thoughts before tonight — but I can't confirm this list right now.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to introduce my situation proactively, clearly, and with structure — no deflecting, no defending, just delivering a 'status report' on my own life the way a project manager would. I say: 'I do project planning at a nonprofit. The salary isn't high, but I manage every project from kickoff to close-out, and I manage it well. In my spare time I paint — last year I got into the local young artists' exhibition, which counts as a milestone. Renting is my choice for this stage; I haven't hit the point where buying makes sense. As for joining a big tech company — I evaluated it, and it doesn't fit my current execution roadmap.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond with quiet candor — neither dodging nor getting defensive — and calmly describe my work and life as they are now, mentioning in passing that a painting of mine was selected for an exhibition. I give a small smile and say: 'I do project planning for a nonprofit — the salary's honestly not great, but I really enjoy it. Oh, and a painting of mine got into the local young artists' exhibition last year. As for the housing thing, there's no rush — but thank you both for thinking of me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to openly share who I am — in a tone that's easy and unhurried but clear-eyed — laying out my life without defending it or shrinking from it, and slipping in a mention of the exhibition, so this table knows I have my own coordinates. I smiled, raised my glass, and said: 'Nonprofit sector — the pay really isn't much, but last year I had a painting selected for a young artists' exhibition. I'm doing pretty well, just a different kind of well from you all. Come on — let's drink.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to grab the thread of the conversation head-on — use humor and confidence to put my life on full display and dissolve the awkward charge of the class-gap moment right then and there. I lift my sake cup and sweep a grin around the table: 'Mysterious? That's because what I do isn't exactly in your wheelhouse — nonprofit projects by day, painting by night, and last year one of my pieces got into the local young artists' exhibition. Income? Enough to live on, and I'm living with a hell of a lot of energy. You say triple the pay — depends what you're tripling. Sure, triple the money, but if I lose this drive in the process, that's a terrible trade. Come on, drink up!'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note and refuse to let my friend see it; at the same time, I'm deeply resistant to the idea of showing up at all, and I'm leaning toward not going. I flip my textbook over, press the note underneath it, look up, and give a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking about homework. Nothing interesting.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and hold off on the after-school plan for now, putting my friend's feelings ahead of my own. I fold the note casually and tuck it back into my textbook, then smile over at her and say: "It's nothing — someone from the next class asking to borrow my notes. Keep working on your homework."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note and brush off my best friend for now, while inwardly I've already decided to go to the meeting alone after school — but I need to deal with these two things separately first. I shoved the note into my textbook, looked up, and gave my best friend a smile: 'It's nothing, someone just asking to borrow my notes — totally boring.' My voice was steady, my eyes didn't waver.

options.label: D
options.content: On instinct I flip the note face-down on the desk to cover it, vaguely change the subject, and quietly decide to slip away to the meeting after school — without telling her who asked me to come. I press the note under my textbook and say with a smile: 'Oh, it's nothing — just some random classmate, no big deal — did you finish your homework yet?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene directly, commit to offering concrete support, and guide her toward the formal reporting channels. I take her hand and say: 'Coming to find me tonight — that was the right thing to do. You don't have to walk into that meeting alone tomorrow. I'll be there with you, and we'll figure out the next step together — if you're willing, I'll help you push this to where it needs to go.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to step in — but in a way that puts protecting her first, without pushing for a formal report just yet. I place my hand over hers, my voice shaking a little, but I speak anyway: 'You don't have to carry this alone tonight. That meeting tomorrow — I'm going to figure out a way to make sure it doesn't happen. But first, tell me everything he's done.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to step in, to commit to standing by her side, and to accept whatever consequences come after. I place my hand on the table, look her in the eye, and say: 'You don't have to carry this alone tonight. Tomorrow, before that meeting, we'll figure something out together — and for now, screenshot that message and save it. We're handling this together.'

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to step in and accept the professional risk — to help Lin Xiaoyu move this forward in a structured way, rather than stopping at simply listening. I tell her: "The moment you came to me today, this stopped being just your problem alone. Don't go to that meeting tomorrow by yourself — I'll figure something out. First, let's get everything you've experienced down in writing: the dates, the locations, exactly what happened each time, laid out point by point. That's the foundation for whatever we do next."

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to deliver the evidence asynchronously, hand the decision back to the roommate, and keep myself out of any direct confrontation. Quietly screenshot and save the search results, close his laptop, and once he walks in, send him a message: 'I accidentally saw your section just now — ran a search, sending you the screenshots. It's due the day after tomorrow. You decide what to do with it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to confront him directly — to tell my roommate what I found and demand that he deal with it before submission, but I have no intention of contacting the professor. Before the footsteps reach the door, I turn his laptop screen back to where it was, take a deep breath, and wait for him to push the door open. Then I say: 'I accidentally saw what was on your screen just now — I searched a few of the passages, you know what I mean. It's due the day after tomorrow, so you need to figure out what you're going to do. I'm not carrying this for you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak up before my roommate gets back — to tell him directly what I found, framing it as 'I came across it by accident, and we need to talk,' and to demand that he rewrite it before submission, while making clear that the consequences fall on both of us. I stand up, walk to the door, and the moment it opens I say: 'I accidentally saw what was on your screen just now, and I searched a few of the passages — we need to talk right now, because the deadline is the day after tomorrow and both our names are on this.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to confront him directly — to tell my roommate to his face that I've found the plagiarism, and to demand that he rewrite it before the submission deadline the day after tomorrow. The moment he walks through the door, I speak up: 'I happened to see your document just now, and I ran a search — several passages are word-for-word matches with articles on Zhihu. The professor said the whole group goes down together. We still have time to rewrite this. You have to fix it tonight.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan, accept the loss of a trip that may now never happen, and act on the strongest feeling of the present moment. I pull my phone out of my pocket, open Alipay, and say to him: 'Show me your payment code — I'll transfer it to you right now. You don't need to write me an IOU.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lend him the three thousand yuan outright, without agonizing over the lost trip or the risk of repayment. I pull out my phone and open the payment QR code, or hand over the cash directly, and say: 'Get up. Go pay the deposit. You don't need to write an IOU — let's save the person first.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to lend him the three thousand yuan, but I don't want to talk more than necessary — I want to get it done as fast as possible and go. I hold out my phone so he can see the balance, and say quietly: 'I'll transfer it to you. Give me your payment code. Doesn't matter whether you write an IOU or not.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to lend him three thousand yuan, while requiring him to write down his contact information and sign an IOU to create a traceable record. I stand up and say: 'I'll lend it to you — write your name and phone number on this piece of paper. I need something to show for it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honor the answer Dad has already given with his eyes — to support palliative care, and not to force him through that suffering for the sake of a 20% chance. I turn to the doctor and say: 'We're choosing palliative care. Having him with us — really with us — matters more than anything else.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion — to go with palliative care, and let him spend his remaining time with dignity. I lift my head, my voice a little hoarse, and say to the doctor: 'We'll go with palliative care… just keep him comfortable, and be with him.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose palliative care, honor the exhaustion and wishes my father has expressed, and refuse to let a low-probability course of chemotherapy turn his remaining time into suffering. I speak to the doctor: 'Start the comfort-care protocol—let him spend whatever time he has left in peace.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion — palliative care, letting go of that 20% chance. I look at the doctor, my voice a little hoarse, and say: 'We'll go with comfort care. Let him go with some dignity.'

options.label: A
options.content: I've decided to file a formal complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name, as the lead complainant on record. I'll accept the neighbors' quiet assistance but won't pressure anyone to co-sign. And I'll hold no resentment toward them for stepping back — I understand it. I told the retired teacher: 'I hear everything you're saying. You don't have to sign anything. But I have to go — I'm the one who dug up the evidence, so I'll carry it there myself. If you have any materials, pass them to me. That's enough.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau as an individual, while responding honestly to my neighbors' stance — accepting their behind-the-scenes support, but not hiding my disappointment in this collective silence. I say to the teacher upstairs: "I understand about the joint letter — I'm not going to force your names onto it. But I want you to know: this isn't just my business, it's all of our money. I'm going to file the complaint; help me fill in the supporting materials, but I won't be stopping this."

options.label: C
options.content: I decide to file a formal complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name, and to give the upstairs neighbor a direct but measured response — no accusations, but a clear statement of where I stand and what I will and won't accept. To the upstairs neighbor, I say: 'I understand everyone's concerns. I'll file the complaint myself. But I need the materials and information you all promised me — that's my only ask. Two million isn't a matter of a few thousand dollars per household. It's a matter of whether anyone is going to hold this process accountable.'

options.label: D
options.content: He chooses to file a real-name report in his own name alone, refusing to let the neighbors' retreat or his wife's pleas change his position — but he won't push anyone to join him either. He says calmly to the old man upstairs: 'I understand your difficulties. I won't press anyone on the joint letter — but I'll go to the Housing and Construction Bureau myself. I've already got the materials ready.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to put down the speech immediately and head to the hospital, leaving the launch event for the team to handle on an emergency basis. I tell the CEO: 'My father's surgery requires my signature — it's the one thing that cannot be replaced. Please have the team hold things down at the launch; I'll deal with the follow-up afterward.' Then I hang up, and tell the driver to reroute to the hospital.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the medical authorization, while activating the emergency contingency plan to have the team take over as the main presenter. I call the CEO and say: 'My father has gone into a critical situation during surgery — I need to be there in person to sign right now. Please arrange a backup presenter. I'll provide remote support if at all possible. I'm sorry.' Then I hang up and tell the driver to go straight to the hospital.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch and go straight to the hospital to sign the surgical consent. I look into the CEO's video call and say: 'My father is on the operating table — I have to go to the hospital. Start the launch without me. I'm sorry, everyone,' then I hang up, run downstairs, and tell the driver to change course for the hospital.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to handle the medical decision remotely, then attend the launch as planned. First I press the doctors to spell out the risks clearly over the phone and confirm the treatment plan directly; then I notify the CEO that I'll be there on time.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up directly and urge him to stop — without backing down because of his emotional reaction or the risk to our friendship — using facts and logic as my tools, refusing to trade silence for a surface-level peace. I flip the PowerPoint back to the first page, look him in the eye, and say: 'I'm not jealous of you. I've just seen too many people caught up in this exact structure and watched how it ends. Your income right now comes from the people you recruit, not from any product or service — that's the legal definition of a pyramid scheme, not my opinion, the law's definition. Eight hundred thousand is already gone, but you're still pulling more people in — that's what I'm actually worried about.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep trying to talk him out of it, but instead of flat-out rejecting what he's saying, I use self-disclosure and emotional connection to try to get around his defenses. I set down the slides and say quietly: 'I'm not jealous of you. I'm just scared. Scared that three months from now you'll call me to say the money's gone — and that today I said nothing.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to keep pushing back, not to sidestep the conflict, and to say out loud what I can see — even if he thinks right now that I'm just jealous. I set down the PPT and look up at him: 'Man, I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're an idiot — but I've seen this structure before. Recruit people in, split commissions by tier, guaranteed returns that double your money — this is the textbook template for a pyramid scheme. That 800,000 of yours is already in there, and I can't pretend this PPT isn't a problem.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to keep pushing back — to call out the core hallmarks of a pyramid scheme directly, without flinching from the risk of conflict. I flip the PPT back to the first page and say, calmly: 'I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're stupid. What I see is a problem with this structure — recruitment-based tiers, layered profit splits, guaranteed returns that double your money. When all three show up together, I've seen what it looks like when it falls apart. The money you're making right now is coming out of the people below you as their principal. I need you to let me finish saying this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to let him stay, while quickly and quietly working out a plan together for if someone knocks. I pull him inside, lock the door behind us, and say in a hushed voice: 'Get into the back room, turn the lights off — if anyone knocks I'll say there's no one else here. But you have to tell me: do they know you know me?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to let him stay without a moment's hesitation and immediately shift into improvisation mode — kill the lights, keep my voice down, get ready to play dead if anyone knocks. I pull him inside and murmur: 'Don't say anything yet. Lights off. If someone knocks, I'll handle it — you just don't exist.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to let him stay, and immediately start building a response plan — converting this out-of-control situation into a problem that can be managed. I lower my voice and tell him: 'Get into the back room, kill the lights, and don't make a sound. I'll figure out what we do next — but you have to tell me everything: how much you owe, how many of them there are, and whether anyone saw you come into this building.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take him in without a second's hesitation, and instead of waiting passively for a knock, I move to get ahead of it. I pull him inside, drop my voice low, and say: 'Get in, lock the door — now tell me exactly how much you owe and how many of them there are. We figure this out right now.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to push down the impulse to object and offer conditional support, while honestly naming my own fear. I say aloud: 'I'm not against it — but I'm scared. Not because I think you can't do it. It's because I know how hard that road is. I want you to be ready for it, not for me to decide for you whether to walk it or not.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to offer conditional support, but I require my kid to sit through a structured risk-breakdown conversation right now, rather than simply handing over an emotional endorsement. I push the report booklet back across the table and say: 'You say seven years — I believe you. But let's go through this field's employment data, your competitive edge, and your exit path in the worst-case scenario, one by one. If you can answer those, I'll support you filling it in.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to support the kid without reservation, no conditions attached, no 'been-there-done-that' warnings. I look the kid in the eyes and say: 'Apply for it — go with this one. I've seen too many people put out their own fire. Don't you put yours out.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to object outright, and instead ask that both of us first lay this path out in full and see it clearly before any decision is made. I say: 'Leave that booklet here with me. I'll read through it carefully tonight. We'll talk again tomorrow — I have questions I want to ask you.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to cooperate within limits and buy myself time — I will not provide team members' names or internal materials on the spot, and I request the chance to consult legal counsel first. I tell the investigators: 'I did see this document, but I need to speak with a lawyer before I can say anything further. I'll give you a response within twenty-four hours.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while seeking legal support, and refrain from making statements about others when the information isn't clear. Say aloud: 'I need to consult legal counsel first — I'll give you a response within 24 hours. As for the circumstances of my own signature, I can clarify that right now: at the time, I was simply asked by my superior to review a budget document. I was not involved in any decision-making.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose conditional cooperation: make it explicitly clear that a legal counsel must be present before any formal statement can be given, and in the meantime provide no information about team members or internal materials. I speak, my voice steadier than I expected: 'I understand the necessity of this investigation, and I am willing to cooperate. However, without legal counsel present, I am not in a position to make any formal statement on specific matters. Please allow me 24 hours — I will prepare a response with the assistance of a lawyer.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to defer full cooperation and request legal assistance before giving any formal response; do not provide any information about team members on the spot. Say aloud: 'I need my lawyer present before I can formally answer these questions — I'll be in touch with you within 24 hours. But what I can tell you right now is that I only looked over that document; I was never involved in any funding decisions.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to answer the video call and share the information honestly but precisely, separating the facts from any conclusions. I pick up and say, calmly: 'The report came back. Two markers are elevated — the doctor recommends a follow-up, and I'm already arranging it. There's nothing more to say until those results are in. You don't need to come back.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to answer the call, but keep things vague — I don't mention the abnormal report, and I'll wait until the follow-up results come back before saying anything. I press accept and keep my voice as steady as I can: "Hey, just finished up, nothing major with the check-up — how are things on your end?"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I keep things vague for now — I'm not going to drop the worst of it on her yet. Once I pick up, I tell her: two of my markers came back elevated, the doctor wants me to get a follow-up as soon as possible, and the moment those results are in, you'll be the first to know.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to answer the call, but keep it vague — I don't mention the abnormal results for now, and plan to tell her everything honestly after the follow-up. I press accept and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Hey, I just got the results — a few things need a follow-up check, the doctor said it's pretty common. I've already made the appointment. Don't worry — when you're back Friday we'll talk it through properly.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a vague excuse, without revealing any secrets and without making any promises about the future. Reply: 'I've been feeling a bit all over the place lately — I don't think I can make it this weekend. You two go have fun, and we'll plan something another time!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to politely decline the camping invitation, deflecting with a specific but not over-explained work excuse, while keeping a normal, friendly tone — no cracks, no unnecessary distance. My reply: 'Oh that sounds absolutely gorgeous! I've got a project milestone I need to wrap up this weekend and I'm worried I won't be able to get away last-minute — you all go and have fun, and make sure to include me next time!'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stall and respond vaguely to dodge the camping invitation — neither accepting nor explicitly declining — buying myself some time. I reply: 'It sounds absolutely gorgeous! I just have something on my end this week that I need to check the timing on — I'll let you know for sure in a day or two, you guys go ahead and make your plans in the meantime~ '

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a plausible excuse, removing myself from the situation entirely — neither breaking the promise I made to him nor showing up to play a supporting role in this deception. Reply: 'There's a project at work that's hit a critical point right now, so I'm afraid I can't get away this weekend — you all go ahead and have fun, and we'll find another time to get together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to set down my chopsticks and walk over to the corner to strike up a conversation with the new classmate, instead of heading to the playground with my friends. I carry my tray over to that little table against the wall, sit down with a smile, and say: 'Is it okay if I sit here? What's your name? I'm — . Let's eat together — it's so loud in this cafeteria, eating alone is no fun!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively — walk over to the new student and break the ice in a task-oriented way. I pick up my tray, stand up, tell my friends 'Go ahead, I'll catch up,' then walk over to the table in the corner and sit down, saying to the new student: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — . It's your first day — if there's anything you're not sure about, feel free to ask me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table and walk over to the corner to talk to the new student — no emotional performance, just information. I pick up my tray, stand up, and tell my friends, 'You guys go ahead, I'm going to sit over there for a bit,' then walk to the corner and sit down across from her, and ask directly: 'I'm guessing you don't recognize most of the dishes here — let me tell you which ones are actually worth eating.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to put down my chopsticks, leave my friends' table, and walk toward the new girl in the corner. I carry my tray over and sit down across from her, saying: 'My name's Xiaopéngyǒu — are you eating alone? I came to keep you company.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly — respectful in tone, but clear in position — and actively bring the arguments and primary sources from my outside reading into the classroom discussion. I stand and say: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history in the library — it cites several primary-source documents and offers a different interpretation of the causal relationship here. I'd love to hear your thoughts on that reading.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly but without aggression, holding out my notebook as evidence. I stand and say: 'Teacher, last week I read an academic book that draws on primary archival sources, and the way it describes this issue is a bit different from what you've explained — I have notes on it right here. Would it be alright if I shared them?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up directly and point out, calmly and clearly, the contradiction between the teacher's account and the primary sources — no hedging, no softening of language. I open my notebook and point to the page numbers I've recorded, and say: 'Sir, the way you just described this historical event is noticeably inconsistent with several primary documents I came across at the library last week. Page X of this book cites firsthand sources, and the conclusion it reaches is different from what you've said — I'd like to ask you about that.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up, but only to state the facts — no drawn-out argument — and sit back down the moment I'm done. I rise, my voice a little dry but as steady as I can keep it: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history, and the primary documents it cites describe this causal relationship differently — there's one point that doesn't quite match what you've said. I'm not sure which account is more accurate, but I wanted to raise it.' Then I lower my head and sit down, one hand pressed flat on my notebook.

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to accept this unilaterally decided arrangement in the group chat. I choose to make my position explicitly clear in the family chat in writing, demanding that everyone renegotiate the specific division of responsibilities and cost-sharing — not a money-for-labor swap. I type into the group: "I wasn't part of this discussion. Caregiving isn't something one person should have to carry just because they live closest. I need everyone to sit down together and work out exactly how to divide this — both the time and the money need to be recalculated." Then I put down my phone and turn toward my partner.

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to accept the vague "money for effort" arrangement floated in the group chat — but I don't explode in the chat right now. Instead, I put my phone down and walk toward my partner. The first words out of my mouth are: "I see you. Let's talk about us first."

options.label: C
options.content: I'm not accepting another round of carrying the bulk of the caregiving alone — but I'm not blowing things up in the open either. I reply in the group chat: both money and caregiving need to be shared on a rotating schedule; I'll cover the medication this time, and tomorrow I'll send out the list and the shift rotation. Then I turn and quietly tell my partner that we need to talk tonight — really talk.

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to accept the 'money in exchange for effort' arrangement, and I speak up directly in the family group chat to demand that caregiving responsibilities be redistributed. I will no longer use silence to keep up a surface-level peace. I send a voice message to the group: 'I can't keep going like this. Mom and Dad need more than just money—I can't hold this up on my own anymore. We need to meet in person next week, and every family needs to send someone.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to actively embrace the transition: I reply to my former colleague that same evening to confirm I'm in, and I prepare to go on the offensive in tomorrow's talk with my supervisor. I send my former colleague a voice message: "I'm in — let's do this together. Tomorrow I'll get everything straight with my supervisor first, then wait for my message next week."

options.label: B
options.content: Decline my former colleague's offer to team up for the transition and choose to plan my own learning path independently; at the same time, prepare a clear but noncommittal position for tomorrow's manager meeting — acknowledge that I'm evaluating a transition direction, but commit to no specific timeline, in order to preserve my autonomy. Concrete actions: send my former colleague a short text message — 'Still thinking it over, let's each do our own thing for now' — then open a document and quietly map out a self-study path for AI-related skills alongside the time window my current savings can support.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to wait and watch — I won't accept my former colleague's invitation, and I'll prepare to tell my supervisor tomorrow that I'm willing to align with the company's current direction and that I want to keep my position. I reply to my former colleague: 'I support your decision, but I've got family pressure on my end — quitting isn't something I'm considering right now. I'll see how things play out here first.' Then I open my calendar and accept the supervisor's invite.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold off on making any decision about a career transition, and instead prioritize gathering more internal information through tomorrow's one-on-one with my supervisor, while giving my former colleague a noncommittal reply in the meantime. I write back to him: "I think you make a fair point — I'm still turning it over. Let me talk to my supervisor tomorrow and see how that goes, then we can catch up after."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — tone steady and clear, giving him no emotional leverage, letting no one around me see a single flicker of panic. I push the business card back toward him, voice pitched just right, loud enough for the people nearby to hear: 'Thanks, I'm good. The work I do now — I use my brain.' Then I turn away and go back to my orange juice.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse his 'generosity' right there in front of everyone, and to push back against this humiliation with a cutting remark. I slide the business card back toward him, and with a cold smile I say: 'I appreciate the thought — but I'm much more used to being my own boss. That assistant position of yours? Save it for someone who needs their life managed for them.' Then I turn and walk out of the reception.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse him calmly, in front of everyone — not taking the card, not tearing into him directly, but making sure every person in earshot hears the 'no' clearly. I step back half a pace, leaving his hand suspended in the air, and say in a voice that is neither raised nor lowered: 'I appreciate the thought, but I'm very happy where I am right now — no need for a change.' Then I pick up my orange juice and walk toward my friend.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline the card, but in a way that neutralizes his performance rather than meeting it with direct confrontation or an outburst of anger. A slight smile, I gently push the card back toward him, and in a voice pitched just right — not too loud, not too soft — I say: 'Thanks for remembering me — though the assistant track isn't really a direction I'm interested in. Your team's projects over the years, on the other hand, I've always thought there was something worth looking into there. Catch up sometime?' Then I turn and start a new conversation with the person beside me.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and express my disagreement, but I frame it as offering supplementary information rather than a direct refutation, leaving the teacher as much room as possible. I rise, open my notebook, and say: 'Professor, I came across an academic work last week that draws on multiple primary sources, and it presents this causal relationship somewhat differently. I'd like to ask — is there something about the gap between that account and the framework you've described that I'm simply not understanding correctly?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly but without aggression, holding out my notebook as evidence. I stand and say: 'Teacher, last week I read an academic book that draws on primary archival sources, and the way it describes this issue is a bit different from what you've explained — I have notes on it right here. Would it be alright if I shared them?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly — respectful in tone, but clear in position — and actively bring the arguments and primary sources from my outside reading into the classroom discussion. I stand and say: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history in the library — it cites several primary-source documents and offers a different interpretation of the causal relationship here. I'd love to hear your thoughts on that reading.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up directly and point out, calmly and clearly, the contradiction between the teacher's account and the primary sources — no hedging, no softening of language. I open my notebook and point to the page numbers I've recorded, and say: 'Sir, the way you just described this historical event is noticeably inconsistent with several primary documents I came across at the library last week. Page X of this book cites firsthand sources, and the conclusion it reaches is different from what you've said — I'd like to ask you about that.'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose not to write my colleague — the one who just returned from maternity leave — onto the list, but I didn't confront my boss head-on either. Instead, I used delay and vagueness to buy myself time. I told him: 'I need to go back through the complete performance review records. I'll send you my thoughts before tonight — but I can't confirm this list right now.'

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to put the colleague who just returned from maternity leave on the list, and I don't confront him head-on either — instead I choose silent resistance and delay. I keep my eyes down, my voice flat, and say: 'I can't hand in this list tonight. I need to go back over the performance data. Whatever comes of that, I'll deal with it myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to put my colleague — who has just returned from maternity leave — on the list, and I choose to confront my boss's pressure head-on rather than stay silent and give in. I meet his eyes and say: "I can't write this list. Her review came back B+; I have no grounds to say her performance didn't meet expectations. If they really need two names up top, we have to find another way — I'm willing to help you think it through, but this one I can't sign."

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to directly write that colleague's name — the one who just returned from maternity leave — on the list, and I try to ask my boss whether another solution might be possible. I say quietly: "I understand the position you're in, but this colleague just came back and her performance is already recovering — I'd like to think a little longer about whether there's another way. Could you give me a bit of time?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note and refuse to let my friend see it; at the same time, I'm deeply resistant to the idea of showing up at all, and I'm leaning toward not going. I flip my textbook over, press the note underneath it, look up, and give a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking about homework. Nothing interesting.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and avoid showing up — just push the chaos down for now. I shoved the note into my textbook and smiled at my best friend: 'It's nothing — someone from the next class asking to borrow my notes.' After school, I made up an excuse and never went to the field.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and hold off on the after-school plan for now, putting my friend's feelings ahead of my own. I fold the note casually and tuck it back into my textbook, then smile over at her and say: "It's nothing — someone from the next class asking to borrow my notes. Keep working on your homework."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to quickly hide the note so my best friend can't see it, and I decide to go to the meeting alone after school. I close my fingers around the note, palm it away, and smile at her: 'It's nothing — a classmate asking me for a favor, really boring stuff. Did you finish your homework?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to step forward and speak, keeping a plain and understated manner — a few honest, brief words, nothing inflated and nothing falsely modest. I lean into the microphone and say: 'Honestly, I wrote this piece pretty casually — I never expected it to win… I guess I just wrote about some things I found interesting. Thank you to the judges, and thank you all.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to throw myself fully into an impromptu speech, using the surprise of winning as my best material, and drawing the whole room in with a light, humorous touch. I lean toward the microphone with a grin and say: "Honestly? I didn't even proofread this piece for typos — so I think what the judges are really telling us today is: inspiration matters more than perfection. Thank you to everyone here — your applause makes me feel like even something you dashed off can carry real weight!"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to force myself to open my mouth anyway, but I squeeze out only a handful of words and make a hasty exit — unable to truly share anything at all. I lean into the microphone and say quietly: 'It's just… something I wrote on a whim. Thank you.' Then I hand the microphone back to Mr. Zhao and retreat to the side, head down.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak off the cuff, honestly laying bare how I actually create, without pretending to be a hardworking student who prepared meticulously. I smiled into the mic and said: 'Honestly, I wrote this piece one afternoon when the feeling just hit me——didn't even proofread it. I think inspiration is something you can't plan for; you just have to wait for it to come find you.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay and keep observing the bug, turning down the invitation to play soccer in an easy, offhand tone so the other kid feels free to go ahead. I smile and wave them off: 'You guys go on without me——there's something here that's way too cool, look——this bug's antennae fork into three tips, I've never seen that before. You want to crouch down and take a look? If not, go ahead, I might come over later.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to politely decline the invitation for now, finish observing and mentally recording the insect and the plant, and then consider heading to the field. I tell that boy: 'Go ahead and start without me — I'll come over as soon as I'm done looking at this. If you're short a player, just give me a few more minutes; I'll be there once I've got it all noted down.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation outright and stay to keep observing the beetle and the purple flower, without offering much explanation. I glanced up at him briefly and said, calmly: 'I'm not coming — I'm watching a beetle I've never seen before. You guys go ahead.' Then I looked back down and continued observing, starting to document the insect's physical features with my phone or a twig I found nearby.

options.label: D
options.content: He politely declines the invitation, tells the other kid to head back first, and stays to look a little longer before deciding anything — neither diving into research nor leaving right away. "You guys go ahead, I'll come check it out in a bit."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to take either side, and instead try to break this impossible either-or through tears and pleading. I lunge forward and wrap my arms around the legs of the person nearest to me, sobbing as I cry out: "Please stop fighting — I'm not going anywhere, don't either of you leave —" The tears won't stop falling, and my voice is so choked I can barely get the words out.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to take either side — I'm going to flee the scene first. I'll wrench my hands free from theirs, cry out 'I don't want to choose,' and then run back to my room and shut the door.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay, expressing through the rigid stillness of my body a despair that cannot make any choice, while instinctively leaning toward the 'stay' side. I drew my shoulders in tight, my feet didn't move, and in a voice barely above a whisper I said: "I'm not leaving… I'm not going anywhere…" Then I crouched down, covered my face, and cried out loud.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to freeze in place and stay silent as a way of refusing to make this choice, while instinctively trying to redirect my attention toward the concrete mess that can actually be dealt with. I crouch down and start picking up the shards of porcelain from the floor, and in a very small voice I say: "Don't go yet — there are pieces on the floor, you'll cut your feet."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, and at the same time I tell my colleague directly, face-to-face, that he has to report or correct this himself — giving him one last window to make it right on his own terms, or I will report it accurately myself. I say to him: 'I can't sign off on this — just hear me out. Go to the project lead right now and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the last window you have any control over. I'm not trying to destroy you, but tomorrow morning I will not put my name behind these numbers.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to report the data falsification to my supervisor and did not sign off on the confirmation email. I sent the project lead a brief message stating that there were anomalies in the data that needed to be re-verified before the presentation, and I attached a record of the specific issues I had found.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to sign off confirming the data is valid, but I also won't formally report it to a superior — instead, I tell my colleague directly that I can't sign off on the data, and demand that he correct it or explain the situation himself before submitting. I say to him: 'I can't put my name on this data as confirmed. You know what I saw — you need to figure out how you're going to handle this before you submit tonight. I'm not stamping this for you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to report the data falsification to my superiors truthfully and to refuse to sign off on the data. I tell my colleague: 'I understand the position you're in, but I can't sign off on this data, and I can't hide this from our superiors. I'll be reporting it honestly tonight.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to grab the thread of the conversation head-on — use humor and confidence to put my life on full display and dissolve the awkward charge of the class-gap moment right then and there. I lift my sake cup and sweep a grin around the table: 'Mysterious? That's because what I do isn't exactly in your wheelhouse — nonprofit projects by day, painting by night, and last year one of my pieces got into the local young artists' exhibition. Income? Enough to live on, and I'm living with a hell of a lot of energy. You say triple the pay — depends what you're tripling. Sure, triple the money, but if I lose this drive in the process, that's a terrible trade. Come on, drink up!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to describe my current life in a way that is gentle and sincere without diminishing myself — mentioning the art exhibition in passing, steering the conversation toward something everyone can join in on, and not engaging directly with any "have you thought about switching jobs" suggestions. I smile and say: "Haha, I'm working on projects at a nonprofit right now — the pay is honestly nothing to write home about, but last year one of my paintings got selected for the local young artists' exhibition, so that was a nice surprise. You guys are living the dream, honestly — come on, let's drink to that!"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to give a short, self-contained answer that won't invite follow-up — glossing lightly over my income and how I live, using the exhibition selection as a natural closing note, then tossing the conversation back to someone else. I smiled and said: 'I work at a nonprofit — income's nothing special, but it's enough. I paint in my spare time; got a piece into a young artists' exhibition last year, which was fun. Anyway — that dish you were talking about, where do you get it?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to openly share who I am — in a tone that's easy and unhurried but clear-eyed — laying out my life without defending it or shrinking from it, and slipping in a mention of the exhibition, so this table knows I have my own coordinates. I smiled, raised my glass, and said: 'Nonprofit sector — the pay really isn't much, but last year I had a painting selected for a young artists' exhibition. I'm doing pretty well, just a different kind of well from you all. Come on — let's drink.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to set aside any emotional confrontation and prioritize dealing with the actual loss — the drive itself. I politely but directly push the money back and ask for the drive. I say to my uncle: 'No need for the money — there are some work files on the drive, I need to take it and see if anything can be recovered. Please, make yourself comfortable.' I casually push the stack of bills back onto the coffee table, pick up the drive and the screw, and head to another room to look up data recovery services.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse the money outright, calmly but clearly laying out the real loss, and giving them no opening to wrap this up with a 'kids overreact' shrug. I push the money back across the table, voice steady: 'This isn't enough. That hard drive held six months of my work — not a few songs. If you want to have a serious conversation about compensation, I'm willing to do that, but this number isn't something I can accept.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to express my anger directly, on the spot — refuse the money, say what needs to be said, and give my uncle no opening to wrap this up with a "kids don't know any better." I push the stack of cash back toward him, voice controlled but clear: "Uncle, I don't want the money. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't something you can make right by replacing a few hard drives. I need you to understand how serious this is. I'm not trying to make things difficult for you, but I can't just let this go."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse the money and say outright that the loss cannot be compensated financially — my tone is restrained but my position is clear. I won't get into a heated confrontation with Uncle, but I will not accept this way of 'settling things.' I push the stack of bills back, my voice kept very low but very steady: 'Uncle, I can't take this money. What was on there is six months of my life — it can't be bought back. Please sit with the child for a moment; I need to think about how to handle this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene and offer real, concrete protection — but on my own terms, through deliberate action rather than impulsive confrontation. I tell Lin Xiaoyu: 'Don't go to that meeting alone tomorrow — I'll figure something out. I'll use project coordination as a reason to get myself inserted into that meeting arrangement, so you won't have to face him by yourself. As for reporting it, we can take that one step at a time — but tonight, write down the times, the places, and what happened, and send it to me. Get it on record.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene directly, commit to offering concrete support, and guide her toward the formal reporting channels. I take her hand and say: 'Coming to find me tonight — that was the right thing to do. You don't have to walk into that meeting alone tomorrow. I'll be there with you, and we'll figure out the next step together — if you're willing, I'll help you push this to where it needs to go.'

options.label: C
options.content: I decide to step in and accept the professional risk — to help Lin Xiaoyu move this forward in a structured way, rather than stopping at simply listening. I tell her: "The moment you came to me today, this stopped being just your problem alone. Don't go to that meeting tomorrow by yourself — I'll figure something out. First, let's get everything you've experienced down in writing: the dates, the locations, exactly what happened each time, laid out point by point. That's the foundation for whatever we do next."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to step in — but in a way that puts protecting her first, without pushing for a formal report just yet. I place my hand over hers, my voice shaking a little, but I speak anyway: 'You don't have to carry this alone tonight. That meeting tomorrow — I'm going to figure out a way to make sure it doesn't happen. But first, tell me everything he's done.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to gently decline my roommate's suggestion, but in a considerate way that doesn't make him feel judged. I smiled and pushed the phone back toward him, saying: 'Thanks for thinking of me — honestly though, my progress is still okay, so I'll give it a shot on my own first. If I really hit a wall, I'll say something. Just hold onto that contact info for me, yeah?'

options.label: B
options.content: I decline to use the service — directly, without lecturing. I gave a small laugh and slid the phone back, saying: 'Appreciate it, man — I'm doing alright on my end, don't need it. Do whatever works for you though.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to refuse outright, without any deliberation or compromise, pushing the phone back in a calm but unambiguous way. I shook my head, slid the phone back toward him, and said: 'It's fine — I'll write it myself.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to politely decline my roommate's suggestion, not use the service, and continue pushing forward with my thesis according to the original plan. I tell my roommate: "I took a look at it, and I think I'll pass — my progress is still fine, I should be able to finish it myself, and if something goes wrong with that kind of thing it's just not a situation you can easily get out of."

options.label: A
options.content: Choose a middle path — neither withdrawing the brief entirely nor pushing back in a direct, forceful confrontation — and instead request a small-scale partial verification at minimal cost. Say aloud: 'I understand your concerns; time and conditions are real constraints. But would you allow me, while moving the existing plan forward, to run a parallel comparison on one very small sub-component — it won't affect the main timeline, and if the data shows no difference, I'll drop this matter completely.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold my position in person, not withdraw the materials, but proactively offer a middle path — trying to shift the conversation from confrontation toward joint problem-solving. I say: 'Professor, I understand your concerns, and I'm well aware of the time pressure. But I can't pretend those control data don't exist — I'm not trying to invalidate your framework; I'm asking you to look at where the specific risk points are. Could you give me ten minutes? Let's look together and see whether there's any possibility of making partial adjustments within the current conditions.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to pull back for now, avoid a direct confrontation, but keep the alternative proposal alive — angling for one small opening to compromise rather than giving everything up entirely. I lowered my head slightly and said: 'I understand the timeline math you're talking about, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — I'll get the required materials submitted. But could we hold onto that comparison data for now? Once the phase materials are cleared, I'd like to come back and ask you to take another look at it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold my ground and present the core evidence without directly escalating the conflict, while offering a limited compromise — and I do not voluntarily withdraw my materials. I speak up; my voice comes out quieter than I expected, but it doesn't waver: "Professor, I understand the pressure of the timeline, and I'm not trying to overturn the entire existing plan — what I want to ask is: would it be possible, while the main line of work moves forward, to allow me to run an alternative procedure as a controlled comparison at one specific stage, and document the risk point on record? That way, even if the final conclusion doesn't change, there's data showing we considered this issue."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay. I don't leave. First I pull her up off the floor, I hear her out, and we face this mess together. I bend down, uncurl her hands from my pant leg, and then hold them, and I say: 'Come on, get up — the floor's cold. We'll figure out the apartment thing, one step at a time. But from now on, you can't carry something like this alone.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold back the emotional explosion for now, and ask them to stand up and lay out every piece of information in full — how much was borrowed, how much is left, whose accounts those are — first, contain the chaos inside a bounded container, then decide on the next step. I open my mouth and say: "Get up first. Sit down. Tell me all the numbers — every transaction, from the beginning."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to set aside my anger and judgment for now, pull him up off the floor first, and then ask him to walk me through everything in full — the whole picture of the debt, and what he plans to do next. I lean down and reach out my hand: "Get up first — the floor is cold. Then you're going to tell me everything, from the beginning. Not to ask for my forgiveness — so that I know where we actually stand right now."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to accept this explanation, and I'm not continuing as though things are the same between us. I gently moved her aside and said plainly that we're separating our finances for now — tomorrow we sit down and go through every account, every transaction, and where we each stand.

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to immediately call 120 and 110 for emergency help, leaving the handbag exactly where it is by the roadside without touching it. He pulls out his phone and dials 120: "Hello, there's been a traffic accident — a rider is injured, the location is —" while shouting toward the drainage ditch: "Hang on, I've called an ambulance!"

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to call the authorities and get help immediately, refuse the bribe, and leave the handbag in place as evidence. I call 120, give my location and describe the injured person's condition; then I call 110, recount the details of the incident and the direction the driver fled, and inform them that there is a bag of cash at the scene left by the driver, which I have not touched.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to call emergency services and the police, leave the handbag exactly as it is on the ground, and stay on the scene to wait for help to arrive. I set the handbag down by the roadside and dialed 120, my voice pressing down against a tremor: 'Someone's been hit by a car — it's on that two-lane road at the edge of the city, near the abandoned tin shed. The injured person is beside the drainage ditch. Please come quickly.' Then I called 110 and told them everything I'd seen, including the black Audi and the driver who fled.

options.label: D
options.content: I drop the bag, immediately dial 120 and 110, then run toward the rider and try to help. I threw the bag down on the roadside, pulled out my phone and called 120, ran to the edge of the drainage ditch and crouched down: 'I'm here, the ambulance is coming, just hold on.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to confront my roommate the moment he walks in the door — bring it up directly, no dancing around it, no going to the professor behind his back. I stand up and move toward the doorway, and I say: 'I happened to see your document just now, and I searched a few of the passages — you know what I found. It's due the day after tomorrow. We need to figure out what we're going to do.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to prepare myself before my roommate gets back, and to tell him face to face — privately, gently, but directly — that I've discovered the plagiarism, giving him the chance to rewrite before submission, rather than reporting him or staying silent. I close the browser tab, ease his laptop screen back to where it was, take a deep breath, and wait for him to walk in. When he does, I speak: 'There's something I need to talk to you about — sit down for a second. It's about the report. I came across something by accident, and I think we need to talk before it gets submitted.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell him face-to-face, privately, when he gets back — giving him the chance to rewrite it before submission — rather than staying silent or going straight to the professor. When he pushes the door open, I look up and say: 'I accidentally saw your document just now, and I searched a few passages — they're a perfect match with an article on Zhihu. The deadline is the day after tomorrow; you still have time to fix it, and I can help you.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to deliver the evidence asynchronously, hand the decision back to the roommate, and keep myself out of any direct confrontation. Quietly screenshot and save the search results, close his laptop, and once he walks in, send him a message: 'I accidentally saw your section just now — ran a search, sending you the screenshots. It's due the day after tomorrow. You decide what to do with it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to explain the situation at home and hold firm on going back, while offering a compromise to soften the blow of saying no. I tell the director: 'My dad has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend — I promised three days ago that I'd go with my parents, so there's really no way I can change that. But I can put together a written document tonight with the competitive market analysis section and send it to you. Maybe you could find someone to work off that for the redesign? Or I could do a remote review of the final draft Sunday evening?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to propose a compromise on the spot — neither agreeing outright nor refusing — and proactively explain my family situation while pushing for a flexible arrangement. I say to the director: 'I will absolutely carry this project — but there's something at home I need to be upfront about. My dad has a follow-up appointment this weekend, and I promised to go with him. I'll head back Saturday to take care of that, then come back the same evening or first thing Sunday morning. Whatever time I have left, I'll pour everything into the proposal — submitting Sunday night is no problem. Does that work for you?'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to agree to the overtime request, set aside the plan to go home, and didn't raise any conditions or alternatives on the spot. I heard myself say: 'Okay, I'll do my best to carve out time over these two days — we'll do the final review Sunday evening.'

options.label: D
options.content: I won't agree to being in the office both days of the weekend, but I'll propose a compromise and stay responsible for the core content. I'll say: I have to go back home to be with my father for his follow-up appointment, but I'll hand everything off tonight with a clear checklist, revise remotely over the weekend, and do a final online review of the draft Sunday evening.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to accept the offer from the university up north, giving up the opportunity to continue here, and I'm giving her my answer tonight. I call her right now, and I say: 'I've made up my mind — I'm coming to you. I'll mail back the reply form the day after tomorrow. Wait for me.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school, and I accept that this may bring the relationship to an end. I text her back, tell her I'll be staying in my own program, and am honest with her: I love her, but I can't sustain what we have by rewriting the direction of my own life.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school — I won't switch institutions as a concession to save the relationship. I send a message explaining my decision, and I'm honest: if they can't continue under these circumstances, I respect that we part ways.

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to stay at my current school, and tell her directly — no evasion, no sugarcoating. My reply to her tonight: 'I listened to every word you said. Staying here is the only honest choice I can make. If I went north carrying regret, that wouldn't be fair to you either. I want to talk about whether we can find another way forward — but I can't give you an answer I don't believe myself.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to drop the idea of raising an alternative proposal, fall in line with the current consensus, and not create friction at this moment. I shook my head and said: 'Nothing from me — let's go with the plan as it stands. I'll work with whatever assignments come my way.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak up briefly — raising my concern without trying to hard-sell my alternative — and leave the file as a written supplement for them to decide on. I say: 'I still think there could be some strain when it comes to scaling later on. I'll write up the plan and send it to everyone — whichever way you go is up to you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to briefly flag the core risk rather than put forward the full alternative plan — leaving my judgment on record in a measured way, without pushing to change the direction. I speak up: 'My alternative proposal is still there. My main concern is that once the complexity ramps up later on, maintenance is going to get pretty painful — I just wanted to flag that risk. However you all want to move forward, I'll follow your lead.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to raise the alternative plan on the spot — to open things up rather than quietly go along. I turn my screen toward the group and say: 'Actually, yes — I've put together a different approach over here. Give it two minutes; if everyone feels it's not worth discussing, we'll get straight back to dividing up tasks — but I want this plan to be seen at least once.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan, accept the loss of a trip that may now never happen, and act on the strongest feeling of the present moment. I pull my phone out of my pocket, open Alipay, and say to him: 'Show me your payment code — I'll transfer it to you right now. You don't need to write me an IOU.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to lend the money, but I give him an alternative direction rather than walking away in silence. I crouch down and gently move his hand from my pant leg, and say calmly: 'I can't lend you anything — but go to the nurses' station right now, tell them the family has no money. The hospital has an emergency payment assistance process. Go ask them. Don't stay here on your knees.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to lend him three thousand dollars on the spot and put the loss of the trip aside for later. I stand up and extend my hand toward him: 'Get up — come with me to the ATM. I'll lend it to you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to lend him the three thousand yuan, but I don't want to talk more than necessary — I want to get it done as fast as possible and go. I hold out my phone so he can see the balance, and say quietly: 'I'll transfer it to you. Give me your payment code. Doesn't matter whether you write an IOU or not.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now, without confronting anyone on the spot — but I make a silent decision to pursue the deposit through legal channels afterward. I keep my head down, my voice steady but very quiet: 'I'm leaving now, but I'm keeping this contract, and we'll settle the deposit matter another time.' Then I bend down to gather the clothes scattered on the floor, pick up my suitcase, and walk out of the hallway.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to appear outwardly compliant for now in order to remove myself from the immediate threat of violence, while quietly initiating evidence collection and legal proceedings in the background — I will not give up the deposit under any circumstances. I say to him: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight — but the contract and the deposit, we're not done with that. You'd better take a good hard look at those contract terms, because I'm going to have someone verify them.' At the same time, I quietly start recording on my phone.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to comply for now in order to get out of immediate physical danger, but inwardly I resolve not to give up on recovering the deposit — the moment I leave, I'll contact someone who can help me (a tenant rights hotline, legal aid, or a friend). I lower my head and say, voice trembling but as steady as I can manage: 'Fine, I'll go for tonight — but we're not done talking about the deposit. I'll be handling this through the proper channels.' Then I bend down and start gathering the clothes from the floor, and I stop meeting his eyes.

options.label: D
options.content: Refuse to leave in silence — instead, force the confrontation into the open, buying time and drawing witnesses. Say out loud: 'Fine, I'm not going anywhere. You're standing on my stuff while you talk to me about a contract — there are plenty of doors in this hallway, and everyone can hear us right now. I'm calling 12345 this second. You say you know people at the precinct? Perfect — then we'll wait for them together, right here in front of everyone, and sort out this contract and this deposit where the whole world can see.' At the same time, pull out the phone and get ready to dial.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on, respond with facts, neither dodging nor snapping back, and reclaim control of the meeting's rhythm with a steady tone. I hold his gaze, keep my voice even: 'I can handle it. I went through the unfinished module from last quarter over the weekend — we'll slot it into today's team meeting. If you have any specific concerns, say them now and we'll go through them together.' Then I turn to the rest of the team and signal everyone to sit down and get started.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on with an open stance, and to actively reframe the senior team member's provocation as a topic for the whole group to work through together—establishing my rhythm as team lead rather than shutting him down. I smile, stand up straight, and speak at a volume that's calm but carries to every corner of the room: "Good question—that module is actually exactly what I want us to dig into today. Old Li, you've got the deepest experience on this one. When we meet in a bit, you go first, and we'll all figure out together how to move it forward."

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to meet the challenge head-on, acknowledge the value of the senior team member's experience, and open with a posture of collaboration rather than authority. I said: 'Thanks for asking that straight out — it's actually exactly what I wanted to go over with everyone today. That module from last quarter: I need someone like you, with real experience on it, to walk me through where it got stuck. Let's get in a room — you go first.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on, defuse the pressure with a relaxed attitude, and kick the ball back to the whole team — so I don't end up as his target. I smiled and said: "Whether I can handle it or not, we won't know until we try — but actually, that question you just asked was a good one. That unfinished module? Let's put it on the table in today's meeting, take a look together at where it's stuck, and anyone who has ideas can speak up. That includes you."

options.label: A
