options.content: I choose to turn down my ex's request to get back together, but my response is vague and riddled with self-attacking apologies — and I decide to hide the details of this meeting from my current boyfriend. I say to my ex: "We can't go back to what we were. You deserve better. I'm sorry for making you wait so long" — then I lower my head, unable to meet his eyes, while inside I've already started running through how I'm going to explain to my current boyfriend why I'm coming home late tonight.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to give a clear rejection on the spot, and I decide to tell my current partner that same day about everything that happened at this meeting. I look my ex in the eye and say: 'I'm grateful you told me all of this, but the question of whether I've moved on — the answer is yes, I have. I'm in a serious relationship right now, and I'm not going to leave it hanging here. I wish you well.' Then that evening I reach out to my partner first and lay out everything that happened today, exactly as it was.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to turn my ex down — gently but clearly, leaving no room for ambiguity — and at the same time I decide to tell my current partner honestly what this meeting was about. I speak up: "I haven't completely forgotten what we had, but I'm in a new relationship now, and I'm not going back. Thank you for telling me all of this." When I get home, I'll tell my current partner directly what happened today.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to respond to my ex directly, set a clear boundary, and give him no vague hope; I also decide to go home and tell my current partner honestly what happened at this meeting. I say to my ex: 'I hear what you're saying. That time meant a lot to me too, but I'm in a serious relationship now, and I can't give you a 'maybe.' That wouldn't be fair to you, and it wouldn't be fair to the person I'm with.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to strike back loudly and immediately, actively turning this livestreamed scene into my own stage for clearing my name. I hold my phone screen up high, my voice cutting through the noise of the carriage, staring straight into the camera: 'Everyone, look closely — what's on my phone is a work group chat, check the timestamp yourself — I did not photograph anyone. This is a false accusation!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to force myself to stay calm — no running, no fighting back hard — and in the most direct way possible I hold up my phone screen showing the work group chat, so everyone in the carriage and the livestream camera can see the evidence. I say loudly: 'Look carefully — what's on my phone is the work group chat. Screen facing up. Everyone can see it. I didn't photograph anyone.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to break the situation with physical evidence rather than verbal argument, forcing myself to speak but taking the shortest possible path straight to the proof. I raise my phone screen high and turn it toward the people filming around me, and with everything I have I squeeze out one sentence: 'Look at the screen — work group chat, timestamp, I was scrolling the group, I wasn't photographing you. See for yourselves.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose restraint and composure — clearing my name in public, neither fleeing nor fighting back, waiting for the train to reach the next stop so I can seek a rational resolution. I release my collar from the grip, raise both hands so the people around me can clearly see my phone screen, and say in as steady a voice as I can manage: 'I was scrolling through my work group chat — you can all see for yourselves. I haven't photographed anyone. Please wait until the next stop and we can find a staff member together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to let him stay for now — close the door, keep out of sight, stay on high alert, and make no move to open the door and confront the debt collectors. I drop my voice to a low murmur and tell him: 'Don't make a sound — don't touch the lights either. We wait until they're gone, and then you're going to tell me exactly how deep this goes.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to let him stay, and immediately start building a response plan — converting this out-of-control situation into a problem that can be managed. I lower my voice and tell him: 'Get into the back room, kill the lights, and don't make a sound. I'll figure out what we do next — but you have to tell me everything: how much you owe, how many of them there are, and whether anyone saw you come into this building.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take him in without a second's hesitation, and instead of waiting passively for a knock, I move to get ahead of it. I pull him inside, drop my voice low, and say: 'Get in, lock the door — now tell me exactly how much you owe and how many of them there are. We figure this out right now.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to let my friend stay, while keeping quiet and staying low-key in case anyone knocks. I turn off all the lights, signal him not to make a sound, and we sit with our backs against the wall beside the door, waiting for the footsteps to pass. If someone knocks, I'll say in a low voice 'no one's here' — or just not answer at all.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to be honest about the real situation and recalibrate my job-search floor — no soothing platitudes to placate my partner, but no emotional breakdown either. I squeeze her hand and speak: 'I'll be straight with you — the market right now isn't kind to people our age, but I've done the math: the severance can last eight months. I'll adjust my expectations, but I won't just take anything. Let's get the numbers clear first, and your dad's appointment this month — we'll go together.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose a half-open confession — no complete answer, but no more pretending to be calm either. I squeeze her hand and say quietly: "I don't know what comes next, but I don't want to lie to you and say I have a plan — I'm really all over the place right now, but I'm not just going to give up."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak — to tell her part of the truth, to acknowledge how hard things are, while setting aside for now the most drastic idea (scrapping everything to go independent) and focusing on steadying what's right in front of us. I turned my hand over and held hers back, and said quietly: 'The options really are narrow right now. I haven't figured it out yet — but I don't want to hide it from you. It's harder than I expected.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to be honest with my partner about the real situation and my inner fear, while also deciding not to lower my salary floor — and instead to consider a more aggressive path toward starting something of my own or a full pivot. I squeeze her hand and say: "I'm going to be straight with you — I'm scared right now, but I'm not going to go work in a call center. I want to talk to you about a much bigger bet."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up, but in a private, peer-to-peer way rather than as a public critique. First, I reply to my colleague's message: 'I feel the same way — let's think together about how to bring it up.' Then I send the young colleague a private message: 'Seeing your project update, a few details reminded me of something from my own early career. If you'd ever like to talk it through, I'm happy to share — though of course it's just one perspective; you know your situation far better than I do.'

options.label: B
options.content: I've decided to reach out directly via private message, pointing out the specific structural risk points in the process — no emotional preamble, no 'just for your reference' disclaimer tacked on at the end. And I'll reply to my colleague: 'I'll say it directly. You don't need to get involved.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to use my colleague's private message as the entry point — passing along a word of caution from experience indirectly rather than directly, so it doesn't come across as a forceful intervention through a public comment. My reply to my colleague: "I saw it too, and your instinct might be right — if you're open to it, we could talk. I once ran a project with a very similar structure, and there are a few wrong turns that might be worth mentioning."

options.label: D
options.content: Choose a light-touch intervention over silence — leave a door open through open-ended questions rather than pouring experience over him. Send the young colleague a private message: 'Saw your project — there's a detail that brought some interesting questions to mind for me. If you ever feel like talking through certain nodes in the process architecture, I'm around whenever — purely out of curiosity, not advice.' And reply to my colleague: 'I feel the same way, but this is his exploration. We can just be here when he wants to talk.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to sidestep this shared strangeness — to gently acknowledge that it exists, rather than each of us retreating back into the familiar busyness. I look down at the table and say: "This does feel strange. How about… we have a cup of coffee together first? No need to think about what comes next."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to make any active changes for now, and instead return to the familiar busyness to keep the days stable. I tell him: let's just keep things the way they've always been — I'll head to the shop first, and when I get back I'll tidy up around the house.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to actively respond to his suggestion of "going out for a walk together" — to break the stalemate and keep us from drifting apart. I gave a small, quiet smile and said: "Let's go. You mentioned going out for a walk just now — I think that's a good idea. Let's go together."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take the initiative and break the deadlock — pulling my housemate out the door with me, filling this strange morning with movement. I stand up, give the table a light slap, and say with a smile: 'Come on, let's go for a walk — just the two of us. We can figure out what comes next as we go. Beats sitting here staring into space.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to put down the speech immediately and head to the hospital, leaving the launch event for the team to handle on an emergency basis. I tell the CEO: 'My father's surgery requires my signature — it's the one thing that cannot be replaced. Please have the team hold things down at the launch; I'll deal with the follow-up afterward.' Then I hang up, and tell the driver to reroute to the hospital.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to handle the medical decision remotely, then attend the launch as planned. First I press the doctors to spell out the risks clearly over the phone and confirm the treatment plan directly; then I notify the CEO that I'll be there on time.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the surgical consent. I call the CEO and say, 'My father is in surgery — I can't be there, I'm sorry,' then hang up and tell the driver to go straight to the hospital.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to go to the hospital immediately, giving up hosting the launch in person, while quickly mobilizing resources to arrange a substitute. I call the CEO back, my voice faster and louder than usual: 'My father is on the operating table — I have to get to the hospital to sign the forms, I'm leaving now. Put the deputy director on, I'll direct remotely from the car. The launch cannot stop, but this signature can only come from me.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up directly, without sidestepping the conflict — but I position myself as his ally, not his opponent, opening the conversation through emotional connection rather than a frontal rebuttal. I set the PPT aside, lean forward, and look him straight in the eyes: 'Man, I'm not jealous of you. I'm scared for you. You've put 800,000 in — I'm not in any position to regret that for you. But if I don't say something today, then what kind of friend have I even been? Tell me honestly: can you afford to lose this money?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak up directly and not shy away from the conflict, even at the risk of losing this friendship. I set down the PPT and look up at him, my voice low but steady: 'I'm not jealous of you — I'm scared for you. This is a pyramid scheme. I mean that in the most literal sense — recruiting heads, tiered commissions, fabricated returns. The eight hundred thousand you put in is almost certainly gone, and so is the money of every one of those dozen-odd people you brought in. You can be angry with me, but I can't stand here and watch you keep walking deeper into this.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak my honest assessment gently but directly — not sidestepping the pyramid-scheme structure for what it is, but keeping the focus on my friend's state of mind rather than on who's right or wrong, and refusing to let his defensive attacks pull me off course. I set down the PowerPoint, look at him, and say: "I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're stupid — I just saw some things in this material that made me genuinely worried about you, and I want to talk about it seriously, not to tear you down, but because you're someone I care about."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up directly and urge him to stop — without backing down because of his emotional reaction or the risk to our friendship — using facts and logic as my tools, refusing to trade silence for a surface-level peace. I flip the PowerPoint back to the first page, look him in the eye, and say: 'I'm not jealous of you. I've just seen too many people caught up in this exact structure and watched how it ends. Your income right now comes from the people you recruit, not from any product or service — that's the legal definition of a pyramid scheme, not my opinion, the law's definition. Eight hundred thousand is already gone, but you're still pulling more people in — that's what I'm actually worried about.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the missing model, refuse to go along with any lie, and proactively ask to go on stage immediately and deliver the presentation through words alone. I say directly to the homeroom teacher: 'Ms. Li, the model was lost on the bus — that's our mistake — but we have our content ready. Please let us go on. I'll present it. We don't need the model.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the missing model — I won't go along with the lie, but I also won't actively assign blame to anyone; I simply state the facts and ask to continue with a verbal presentation instead. I say quietly to the homeroom teacher: 'The model was lost on the way to school and didn't make it here — would it be alright if we just walked through the content verbally?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to go along with the lie, but I also don't throw my teammate under the bus on the spot — instead, I steer the conversation directly toward how to handle the presentation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but quickly: 'The model had an accident on the way here and didn't make it, but we can go up and present without it — I have all the content memorized.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell a softened version of the truth: acknowledge that the model wasn't brought, without actively naming who's responsible, while indicating that the group can complete the presentation through a verbal walkthrough. I say to the homeroom teacher: 'Ms. [Teacher], the model wasn't able to make it today — something came up — but we're fully prepared to walk through everything verbally. The content is all there. Please let us go up.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to complete my school responsibility first, then negotiate with Mom about whether we can head over to join the party afterward — I will not abandon a commitment I've already made. I say to Mom: 'Mom, tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the key, and it's something I promised to do. How long do you think it'll take once I'm done? If there's still time, could we go to the amusement park to meet my cousin after?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to take care of the watering and tadpole-feeding at school first, then decide afterward whether to head over to the gathering — but I'm not committing to showing up. I tell Mom: 'Tomorrow I have to go to school first to water the plants. The teacher gave me the keys — the mimosa was just repotted and it can't wait. Once I'm done I'll see if I can make it over, but I might not be in time. Can you let Uncle know for me?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to do both, handling them in sequence by planning the timing in advance so I can honor my responsibility and still make it to the outing. I tell Mom: 'I'm excited, I really am! But tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the keys, and I promised her I'd do it. Once I'm done we'll head straight out; we should still make it in time. Can you let Uncle know we'll be there a little bit later?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to do both, planning to go to school first thing Saturday morning to water the mimosa and feed the tadpoles, then head over to the amusement park for my cousin's birthday party. I tell Mom: 'I really want to go! But the teacher gave me the key — the mimosa needs watering and the tadpoles need feeding. I'll go to school first thing tomorrow morning and get it all done. I should be there by nine-thirty at the latest — that's plenty of time. How about you all head out first and I'll catch up?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to object outright, and instead ask that both of us first lay this path out in full and see it clearly before any decision is made. I say: 'Leave that booklet here with me. I'll read through it carefully tonight. We'll talk again tomorrow — I have questions I want to ask you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to support the kid in taking this path — but in the way I do best: turning it into a two-way conversation with stakes, not a one-sided verdict. I stand up, walk over and sit down beside them, pick up the report binder and flip through it, then look up and say: 'Alright. Walk me through this direction of yours. Convince me, and I'll sign off on it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to support the kid — pushing down every worry about employment and income, refusing to echo the dismissals of the generation before me. I was silent for a moment, then I spoke: "You said you've loved this since middle school, that you've been teaching yourself all along — I believe you. Go and try. I won't stand in your way."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to support my child in pursuing this path — I voice my worries, but I don't use them to invalidate their choice. I say: 'I hear you. You've been preparing for so long, and I have no reason to say no. I have my fears, but those are my homework to do — not a chain around your neck. Let's figure out together how to make this path as solid as it can be.'

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to make a choice. Instead I try to break the deadlock with my voice and my body — pulling both of their attention away from "waiting for me to take a side." I lunge forward and wrap my arms around the waist of the one crouching by the door, then spin back and scream at the other one through my tears: "You can't both leave! Nobody is allowed to leave! I won't let you!"

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to take either side, and instead try to break this impossible either-or through tears and pleading. I lunge forward and wrap my arms around the legs of the person nearest to me, sobbing as I cry out: "Please stop fighting — I'm not going anywhere, don't either of you leave —" The tears won't stop falling, and my voice is so choked I can barely get the words out.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to take either side. Instead, I let my tears and my body break the moment — interrupt the pressure to choose — and try to make both adults stop. I crouch down, wrap my arms around my knees, and cry out loud. The only words I manage to say, over and over, are: 'I don't want you to split up. I'm not going anywhere.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose silence and freeze in place, refusing to open my mouth or make any choice at all, letting the stillness of my body stand in for words. I sink down into a crouch, bury my face in my knees, and can't make a single sound — only shaking.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up without a moment's hesitation, lay out the full explanation in front of everyone, and readily agree to enter the speech competition. I rise to my feet, sweep my eyes across the whole class, and say with a smile: "The principle of the seesaw is simple — put the elephant on one end, then keep stacking people onto the other end until the two sides balance. Count how many people there are and roughly how much each one weighs, add it all up, and that's the elephant's weight. I'll go to the speech competition — thank you, Teacher!"

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and share my idea in front of everyone; the suggestion about the speech competition is set aside for now, neither accepted nor explicitly refused. I rise from my chair, my voice a little rushed at first but growing steadier as I go: 'It's just… you put an elephant on one end, then you stack people onto the other end one by one, and you count how many people it takes — because you can measure each person's weight ahead of time —'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to stand up and share my idea in front of everyone, but gave a vague, noncommittal response to the suggestion about the speech competition. I stood up and walked through my idea — the seesaw-and-elephant weighing method — and the words came easier and easier as I went. When the teacher brought up the competition at the end, I just smiled and said: "I'll think about it."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to quietly decline standing up to explain, and respond to the suggestion about the speech competition with a vague, evasive shake of my head. I lower my gaze and say in a very small voice: "I… I can't really explain it well — teacher, you go ahead and tell them."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to give an answer immediately, and instead ask for a full breakdown of the terms, the equity structure, and the exit mechanism — keeping the decision firmly in my own hands. I tell my friend: 'I need to see the complete terms and the equity agreement. Give me three days, and I'll give you an answer that's grounded in evidence — not one that was rushed out of me.'

options.label: B
options.content: Decline the opportunity gracefully, but with a genuine and respectful reason rather than a hollow brush-off. Say to the friend: 'Thank you for thinking of me — I read through the plan carefully, and I understand what you need. But honestly, the way I am right now just isn't suited to joining a team. Working alone is the only way I can actually do things well. Forcing that to change would only hold you back. Find someone who's a better fit for this.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving a definitive answer and ask for a specific window of time to think — rather than refusing or agreeing on the spot. I say to my friend: "You know I've been following this direction seriously for a long time, and your invitation genuinely means a lot to me — I need a few days to really think it through. Can you give me a week? I don't want to give you an answer I haven't earned."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation, letting the exhilaration drown out my financial reservations, and I give him a definitive yes on the spot. I look at the screen, grinning, and say: 'Do you even need to ask? I've been waiting for this chance. When do we start — I can be there tomorrow.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to set down my chopsticks and walk over to the corner to strike up a conversation with the new classmate, instead of heading to the playground with my friends. I carry my tray over to that little table against the wall, sit down with a smile, and say: 'Is it okay if I sit here? What's your name? I'm — . Let's eat together — it's so loud in this cafeteria, eating alone is no fun!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to put down my chopsticks, leave my friends' table, and walk toward the new girl in the corner. I carry my tray over and sit down across from her, saying: 'My name's Xiaopéngyǒu — are you eating alone? I came to keep you company.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table and walk over alone to the new classmate in the corner. I set my tray down across from her and say, calmly: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — , what's your name?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to leave the lively crowded table, pick up my tray, and walk over to the new student sitting alone in the corner — not forcing myself to seem warm or outgoing, just going over and sitting down. I carry my tray to that small table, set it down gently, and say quietly: 'Is it okay if I sit here?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to actively embrace the transition: I reply to my former colleague that same evening to confirm I'm in, and I prepare to go on the offensive in tomorrow's talk with my supervisor. I send my former colleague a voice message: "I'm in — let's do this together. Tomorrow I'll get everything straight with my supervisor first, then wait for my message next week."

options.label: B
options.content: Decline my former colleague's offer to team up for the transition and choose to plan my own learning path independently; at the same time, prepare a clear but noncommittal position for tomorrow's manager meeting — acknowledge that I'm evaluating a transition direction, but commit to no specific timeline, in order to preserve my autonomy. Concrete actions: send my former colleague a short text message — 'Still thinking it over, let's each do our own thing for now' — then open a document and quietly map out a self-study path for AI-related skills alongside the time window my current savings can support.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to wait and watch — I won't accept my former colleague's invitation, and I'll prepare to tell my supervisor tomorrow that I'm willing to align with the company's current direction and that I want to keep my position. I reply to my former colleague: 'I support your decision, but I've got family pressure on my end — quitting isn't something I'm considering right now. I'll see how things play out here first.' Then I open my calendar and accept the supervisor's invite.

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to proactively transition, while treating tomorrow's manager meeting as an intelligence-gathering opportunity rather than a moment to seek validation. Tonight, reply to my former colleague: 'I'm already looking into this — I don't need a bundled bootcamp, but we're pointing in the same direction, so let's keep each other posted on progress'; at the same time, put together my own transition timeline to use during tomorrow's conversation as a way of testing what the company's real expectations are for traditional roles.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to avoid the confrontation and escape the scene for now — no calling her out, no scene. I push every last bit of the anger and hurt down. I pick up my bag, keep my head low, walk out of the classroom, find a corner where no one can find me, shove my earbuds in, and pretend the entire world doesn't exist.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation, not here, not yet. I'll wait until I've calmly worked out what outcome I actually want before deciding on a next step. I turn off my phone screen, look up, give her one expressionless glance, then drop my eyes and open my textbook.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose silence and retreat — no confrontation, no questions, every word swallowed back down, replacing the urge to explode with coldness and distance. I lower my head, shift my gaze away from her, and say quietly, "It's nothing, my eyes are just a little dry," then flip my textbook open and hold it in front of my face, not looking at her again.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to confront her directly — to put the screenshot and the side group chat out in the open, no holding back and no dancing around it. I turn toward her, lower my voice, but speak with complete directness: "I saw that group chat. I saw what you said. Can you tell me — what exactly is going on?"

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to put my colleague — who has just returned from maternity leave — on the list, and I choose to confront my boss's pressure head-on rather than stay silent and give in. I meet his eyes and say: "I can't write this list. Her review came back B+; I have no grounds to say her performance didn't meet expectations. If they really need two names up top, we have to find another way — I'm willing to help you think it through, but this one I can't sign."

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to put the colleague who just returned from maternity leave on the list, and I don't confront him head-on either — instead I choose silent resistance and delay. I keep my eyes down, my voice flat, and say: 'I can't hand in this list tonight. I need to go back over the performance data. Whatever comes of that, I'll deal with it myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse to include the colleague on maternity leave in the list, and state my reasons directly — no confrontation, no compromise. I tell my supervisor: 'I can't write that her performance failed to meet expectations on the list. Her evaluation is a B+ — that's the fact on record, and I can't sign off on something that contradicts it. I understand the situation with the relative, but for the other slot I need to go by actual performance. If you can point me to an employee rated C, I'll handle the paperwork for that.'

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to write the colleague who just returned from maternity leave onto the list, and I choose to voice my dissent directly rather than comply in silence. I meet my superior's eyes and say: 'I can't write this list the way you're asking. If someone has to go, I need a real reason — not this one.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to report the data falsification to my superiors truthfully and to refuse to sign off on the data. I tell my colleague: 'I understand the position you're in, but I can't sign off on this data, and I can't hide this from our superiors. I'll be reporting it honestly tonight.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, while giving my colleague the opportunity to correct the error himself rather than going over his head directly. I tell him: 'I can't sign off on this data — but before I say anything to anyone above us, I'm giving you tonight. You go to the project team yourself and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the only concession I can make.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to report the data falsification to my supervisor and did not sign off on the confirmation email. I sent the project lead a brief message stating that there were anomalies in the data that needed to be re-verified before the presentation, and I attached a record of the specific issues I had found.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, and at the same time I tell my colleague directly, face-to-face, that he has to report or correct this himself — giving him one last window to make it right on his own terms, or I will report it accurately myself. I say to him: 'I can't sign off on this — just hear me out. Go to the project lead right now and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the last window you have any control over. I'm not trying to destroy you, but tomorrow morning I will not put my name behind these numbers.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to confront the conflict head-on, right here and now — not suppressing my emotions, not running from the relationship — staying to face it together, even with anger and tenderness pulling at me at the same time. I crouch down, lift her hands away from my pant leg, and hold them. My voice comes out a little rough: 'Get up. I can't hear you when you're kneeling like that. Then I want you to tell me everything, from the beginning, one thing at a time. I want to know all of it. I'm angry — but I'm not going anywhere.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to accept this explanation, and I'm not continuing as though things are the same between us. I gently moved her aside and said plainly that we're separating our finances for now — tomorrow we sit down and go through every account, every transaction, and where we each stand.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay. I don't leave. First I pull her up off the floor, I hear her out, and we face this mess together. I bend down, uncurl her hands from my pant leg, and then hold them, and I say: 'Come on, get up — the floor's cold. We'll figure out the apartment thing, one step at a time. But from now on, you can't carry something like this alone.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold my emotions in check for now — no immediate decision to leave, no immediate forgiveness — and instead ask him to lay everything out. I crouch down and say, in a voice that's quiet but steady: 'Get up first. Then tell me everything — how much is still owed, who these people are, what the situation looks like right now. All of it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to grab the thread of the conversation head-on — use humor and confidence to put my life on full display and dissolve the awkward charge of the class-gap moment right then and there. I lift my sake cup and sweep a grin around the table: 'Mysterious? That's because what I do isn't exactly in your wheelhouse — nonprofit projects by day, painting by night, and last year one of my pieces got into the local young artists' exhibition. Income? Enough to live on, and I'm living with a hell of a lot of energy. You say triple the pay — depends what you're tripling. Sure, triple the money, but if I lose this drive in the process, that's a terrible trade. Come on, drink up!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond with quiet candor — neither dodging nor getting defensive — and calmly describe my work and life as they are now, mentioning in passing that a painting of mine was selected for an exhibition. I give a small smile and say: 'I do project planning for a nonprofit — the salary's honestly not great, but I really enjoy it. Oh, and a painting of mine got into the local young artists' exhibition last year. As for the housing thing, there's no rush — but thank you both for thinking of me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to give a short, self-contained answer that won't invite follow-up — glossing lightly over my income and how I live, using the exhibition selection as a natural closing note, then tossing the conversation back to someone else. I smiled and said: 'I work at a nonprofit — income's nothing special, but it's enough. I paint in my spare time; got a piece into a young artists' exhibition last year, which was fun. Anyway — that dish you were talking about, where do you get it?'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to go on the offensive — to strike first with a breezy, self-assured tone, packaging my life as a clear-eyed choice rather than a losing position, and dropping a mention of the art show as a line of defense. I smiled, picked up my glass, and said: 'Ha, I'm running projects at a nonprofit right now — the pay's nothing to write home about, honestly — but last year I had a painting selected for the local young artists' exhibition. I feel like the life I'm living is the life I actually want. As for the whole housing thing — I'll get to it eventually.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation, but respond in an offhand, understated way — without explicitly acknowledging what it means to me. I look up, let the corner of my mouth pull into something almost like a smile, and say: "Sure, I've been meaning to catch that author too. Send me the address."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation, but to be honest about why rather than brush it off with an excuse. I glanced down at the table for a moment, then looked up and said: 'I'm really grateful you said all of this — these past few months have meant something to me too — but I can't go. Not because I don't have time. It's because I know what it would mean for both of us, and I can't do that to you, and I can't do that to myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: Accept the invitation — but also decide to have a real conversation with my partner that same night, and face whatever has actually gone wrong between us. I say to them: "I'd love to go, I've been wanting to catch this talk too — but there's something I need to tell you first, about where things have been between us lately."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation directly — no deflecting, no pretending I didn't pick up on the subtext — while making my situation unambiguously clear. I say: "Thank you for telling me all of this. I genuinely think the collaboration these past few months has been something rare. But I'm in a relationship, and I can't accept this invitation — not because I don't want to go, but because I know it wouldn't just be a bookstore outing."

options.label: A
options.content: I immediately dial 120 and then 110, leave the handbag untouched in a visible spot on the side of the road, and move toward the injured person to offer basic reassurance. I set the bag where the streetlight can reach it, then crouch down at the edge of the ditch, waiting for the call to connect, and say to him: 'I'm here. I've called an ambulance. Hold on.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to call emergency services and the police, leave the handbag exactly as it is on the ground, and stay on the scene to wait for help to arrive. I set the handbag down by the roadside and dialed 120, my voice pressing down against a tremor: 'Someone's been hit by a car — it's on that two-lane road at the edge of the city, near the abandoned tin shed. The injured person is beside the drainage ditch. Please come quickly.' Then I called 110 and told them everything I'd seen, including the black Audi and the driver who fled.

options.label: C
options.content: He chooses to immediately call 120 and 110 for emergency help, leaving the handbag exactly where it is by the roadside without touching it. He pulls out his phone and dials 120: "Hello, there's been a traffic accident — a rider is injured, the location is —" while shouting toward the drainage ditch: "Hang on, I've called an ambulance!"

options.label: D
options.content: I call 120 and 110 immediately, set the handbag down on the roadside without touching it further, and walk toward the rider to check his injuries while continuing to call for help. I put the bag on the ground and dialed 120, my voice trembling but clear: 'Someone's been hit — I'm at… I need an ambulance, hurry.'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to refuse outright, without any deliberation or compromise, pushing the phone back in a calm but unambiguous way. I shook my head, slid the phone back toward him, and said: 'It's fine — I'll write it myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: Decline outright — no hedging, no need for a lengthy explanation. I push the phone back and say, calmly: 'It's fine, I can finish it myself. That kind of thing carries way too much risk — it's not worth it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I decline to use the service — directly, without lecturing. I gave a small laugh and slid the phone back, saying: 'Appreciate it, man — I'm doing alright on my end, don't need it. Do whatever works for you though.'

options.label: D
options.content: Refuse to use the service and insist on completing the thesis independently. I shook my head, pushed the phone back, and said: "No thanks — this one I'll do myself. There's still so much about my own topic I haven't worked out yet; if I handed it off to someone else, I'd never be able to sleep."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion — palliative care, letting go of that 20% chance. I look at the doctor, my voice a little hoarse, and say: 'We'll go with comfort care. Let him go with some dignity.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose palliative care, honoring the silent exhaustion in my father's eyes, refusing to force him through one more draining ordeal for the sake of a one-in-twenty hope. I say quietly to the doctor: 'We'll go with palliative care — keep him comfortable, and let me be with him.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose palliative care, honor the exhaustion and wishes my father has expressed, and refuse to let a low-probability course of chemotherapy turn his remaining time into suffering. I speak to the doctor: 'Start the comfort-care protocol—let him spend whatever time he has left in peace.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honor my father's wishes and decide on palliative care, stating that choice plainly without flinching. I say to the doctor: 'We're choosing palliative care — please initiate the comfort care protocol. He has made his choice, and we respect it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I decided not to express immediate support or opposition to the special recruitment, and instead to return the decision to my child first, while buying time for the whole family to discuss it together. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you — this is very significant news for us. Before we make any decision, I want to genuinely hear what my child himself has to say. This is ultimately his path to walk, and I want him to walk it because he truly wants to, not because of our expectations. Could you give us a little time?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on responding to the teacher for now, and instead sit down alone with my child for a real conversation — to give the decision back to the person it belongs to. I tell the teacher: 'Thank you so much. I need to talk it over with my child first, hear what they think, and then we'll make a decision together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I've made up my mind to support the professional path — clearly and without reservation — while insisting that we call my kid out here right now for a three-way conversation, so he can say what he wants in his own words. I tell the teacher: "Personally, I'm in favor — but we need to ask him directly, right now. Can we bring him out here so the three of us can talk this through together?"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to politely decline the specialist track and hold firm on keeping my child in the regular education system, without applying for the talent recruitment program. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you so much — knowing that you see him that way, we as parents are truly grateful. But this is too big a decision for me to make on the spot. I need to go home and talk it through properly with the family. His schoolwork is stable right now, and we don't want to rush into anything.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to put down the speech immediately and head to the hospital, leaving the launch event for the team to handle on an emergency basis. I tell the CEO: 'My father's surgery requires my signature — it's the one thing that cannot be replaced. Please have the team hold things down at the launch; I'll deal with the follow-up afterward.' Then I hang up, and tell the driver to reroute to the hospital.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the surgical consent. I call the CEO and say, 'My father is in surgery — I can't be there, I'm sorry,' then hang up and tell the driver to go straight to the hospital.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to go to the hospital immediately, giving up hosting the launch in person, while quickly mobilizing resources to arrange a substitute. I call the CEO back, my voice faster and louder than usual: 'My father is on the operating table — I have to get to the hospital to sign the forms, I'm leaving now. Put the deputy director on, I'll direct remotely from the car. The launch cannot stop, but this signature can only come from me.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to handle the medical decision remotely, then attend the launch as planned. First I press the doctors to spell out the risks clearly over the phone and confirm the treatment plan directly; then I notify the CEO that I'll be there on time.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to let my friend stay, while keeping quiet and staying low-key in case anyone knocks. I turn off all the lights, signal him not to make a sound, and we sit with our backs against the wall beside the door, waiting for the footsteps to pass. If someone knocks, I'll say in a low voice 'no one's here' — or just not answer at all.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to let him stay, while quickly and quietly working out a plan together for if someone knocks. I pull him inside, lock the door behind us, and say in a hushed voice: 'Get into the back room, turn the lights off — if anyone knocks I'll say there's no one else here. But you have to tell me: do they know you know me?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take him in without a second's hesitation, and instead of waiting passively for a knock, I move to get ahead of it. I pull him inside, drop my voice low, and say: 'Get in, lock the door — now tell me exactly how much you owe and how many of them there are. We figure this out right now.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose without a moment's hesitation to let him stay, while readying myself to handle a knock at the door that could come at any second. I push him into the back room and drop my voice: 'Get in there, keep the lights off, I'll answer the door — I'll tell them there's only me here.'

options.label: A
options.content: Setting aside all deliberation over the overseas opportunity, deciding in this moment to go home immediately and be with Dad — everything else can wait. Saying to Mom: "I'm booking the ticket right now — the earliest flight tonight or first thing tomorrow morning. Wait for me. I'm coming."

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to let go of the overseas opportunity and go home immediately to be by Dad's side and handle the medical arrangements. Say to Mom: 'Mom, I'll be back tomorrow — tell me which hospital Dad's in, and I'll take care of everything from there.'

options.label: C
options.content: She chooses to immediately abandon the overseas assignment and go home to be with her father and handle whatever comes next with his medical care. Voice breaking over the phone, she says: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — first flight tomorrow morning. Wait for me.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to immediately set aside all preparations for the overseas project and return home as soon as possible to be with Dad through his medical care, putting the career decision on hold for now. Call Mom and say: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — I'll be there on the earliest flight tomorrow. Don't worry, I'll handle everything.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to answer the call but brush it off vaguely — "everything's fine, nothing major" — and leave it at that until the follow-up results come in. I picked up, keeping my voice as steady as I could: "Nothing to worry about, just a couple of minor indicators a little off — the doctor said to just keep an eye on it. Don't stress, we'll talk when I'm back Friday."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to answer the call, but gloss over the checkup results, planning to tell the full truth after the follow-up. I press the answer button and say: 'The results came back — there are a few indicators that need to be confirmed again. Let me get through the follow-up next week and then I'll fill you in properly. How are things going on your end in Hangzhou?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I keep things vague for now — I'm not going to drop the worst of it on her yet. Once I pick up, I tell her: two of my markers came back elevated, the doctor wants me to get a follow-up as soon as possible, and the moment those results are in, you'll be the first to know.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to answer the call, but keep things vague — I don't mention the abnormal report, and I'll wait until the follow-up results come back before saying anything. I press accept and keep my voice as steady as I can: "Hey, just finished up, nothing major with the check-up — how are things on your end?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to state the facts truthfully, not to take the blame for my friend, but in a calm and measured tone — not actively accusing anyone, just making it clear that I didn't put the toy in the bag. I say to the security guard: 'I didn't put this toy in there. I don't know how it got into my bag. You can check the security footage.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the security guard honestly that I didn't put the toy in my bag, while not directly pointing the finger at my friend — instead I say I have no idea how it got there and ask the guard to check the security footage. I look up at the guard and say: 'Sir, I really didn't put this toy in my bag. I don't know how it got in there. You have security cameras — could you check them?'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to take the fall for my friend temporarily — but I didn't fully confess. Instead I kept things vague to buy some breathing room. I lowered my head and said to the security guard: 'It was me… I don't know how it ended up in there, I didn't mean to…' My voice was barely above a whisper, my hands were shaking, and I couldn't stop the corners of my eyes from going red.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the truth — that the toy was not put in my bag by me — and refuse to take the fall for my friend, even though my voice is shaking. I keep my head down, but I still manage to say it out loud: 'This… I didn't put this here. I don't know how it got into my bag.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to oppose him outright, but I can't bring myself to offer real support either — I replace a clear answer with the hedged worry of someone who's 'been through it' and a conditional softening. I say: 'You're telling me you've been at this since middle school… It's not that I won't let you try, but you need to think it through — if it doesn't pan out, do you have a fallback?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to support the kid — pushing down every worry about employment and income, refusing to echo the dismissals of the generation before me. I was silent for a moment, then I spoke: "You said you've loved this since middle school, that you've been teaching yourself all along — I believe you. Go and try. I won't stand in your way."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to object outright, and instead ask that both of us first lay this path out in full and see it clearly before any decision is made. I say: 'Leave that booklet here with me. I'll read through it carefully tonight. We'll talk again tomorrow — I have questions I want to ask you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to support the kid in taking this path — but in the way I do best: turning it into a two-way conversation with stakes, not a one-sided verdict. I stand up, walk over and sit down beside them, pick up the report binder and flip through it, then look up and say: 'Alright. Walk me through this direction of yours. Convince me, and I'll sign off on it.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to defer full cooperation and request legal assistance before giving any formal response; do not provide any information about team members on the spot. Say aloud: 'I need my lawyer present before I can formally answer these questions — I'll be in touch with you within 24 hours. But what I can tell you right now is that I only looked over that document; I was never involved in any funding decisions.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while seeking legal support, and refrain from making statements about others when the information isn't clear. Say aloud: 'I need to consult legal counsel first — I'll give you a response within 24 hours. As for the circumstances of my own signature, I can clarify that right now: at the time, I was simply asked by my superior to review a budget document. I was not involved in any decision-making.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to delay full cooperation and seek legal counsel immediately — selectively acknowledging the basic facts while refusing to provide any information about team members. To the investigator: 'I understand what you're asking. I did see that document — that much is true. But without a lawyer present, I won't be providing any further information. That's the more responsible approach for both of us. I need 24 hours. I'll make good use of them.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to cooperate within limits — refusing to provide any additional information on the spot — and make it clear that I need legal assistance before I can cooperate further. I speak calmly and steadily: 'I need to consult a lawyer first. Until then, I'm not in a position to provide any further information — but I am willing to cooperate fully with the investigation within the proper legal framework.'

options.label: A
options.content: Refuse to accept the asymmetrical 'money for labor' arrangement, and raise in the group chat the need to renegotiate a caregiving division of responsibilities that names specific people. Reply in the group chat: 'Let's set the money aside for now — I can't keep going the way things are. I need everyone to be clear about who can come, when they can come, and what they'll be responsible for. Let's get on a video call this week.' Then put the phone down, walk over to my partner, and say: 'I know. Let's talk.'

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to silently accept the arrangement being decided in that group chat — but I choose to put the phone down first and turn to face my partner, to have this conversation between the two of us before anything else, rather than exploding in the chat right now. I press the phone face-down onto the couch, stand up, and walk toward her. 'Let's talk first,' I say. 'Just the two of us.'

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to accept this unilaterally decided arrangement in the group chat. I choose to make my position explicitly clear in the family chat in writing, demanding that everyone renegotiate the specific division of responsibilities and cost-sharing — not a money-for-labor swap. I type into the group: "I wasn't part of this discussion. Caregiving isn't something one person should have to carry just because they live closest. I need everyone to sit down together and work out exactly how to divide this — both the time and the money need to be recalculated." Then I put down my phone and turn toward my partner.

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to accept the "money in exchange for effort" arrangement and call out the fundamental problem with this proposal directly in the group chat, demanding a clear division of responsibilities rather than letting anyone buy their way out. I type in the group chat: "Living closer is a geographical fact, not a reason to take on all the caregiving. Money can be contributed, but so can presence — we'll get on a video call to work out a rotation schedule, and until we've sorted that out properly, I'm not fronting anyone's medical bills."

options.label: A
options.content: Decline the opportunity gracefully, but with a genuine and respectful reason rather than a hollow brush-off. Say to the friend: 'Thank you for thinking of me — I read through the plan carefully, and I understand what you need. But honestly, the way I am right now just isn't suited to joining a team. Working alone is the only way I can actually do things well. Forcing that to change would only hold you back. Find someone who's a better fit for this.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving a definitive answer and ask for a specific window of time to think — rather than refusing or agreeing on the spot. I say to my friend: "You know I've been following this direction seriously for a long time, and your invitation genuinely means a lot to me — I need a few days to really think it through. Can you give me a week? I don't want to give you an answer I haven't earned."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation, letting the exhilaration drown out my financial reservations, and I give him a definitive yes on the spot. I look at the screen, grinning, and say: 'Do you even need to ask? I've been waiting for this chance. When do we start — I can be there tomorrow.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline this opportunity to change careers, turning down my friend's invitation on the grounds of protecting what I've built. I tell my friend: 'I understand your enthusiasm, and I do trust your instincts — but I can't walk away from my shop and my clients. This path isn't the right one for me. Find someone who's a better fit, and I'll be right here cheering you on.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request and return the answer sheet to where it was or hand it to the teacher — I won't keep it for myself. I tell my friend: 'I can't let you copy from this, and I'm not going to use it either. Head back, okay? I'm going to put it back.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend and either hand the paper to the teacher or put it back where it came from. I whisper to her, "We can't use this — we'll get caught," and then, while she's not paying attention, I slip the paper back into the crack in the desk or hand it to the teacher, pretending I just happened to find it by accident.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request and decide to hand the answer sheet over to the teacher or return it to where it belongs — trading honesty for genuine peace of mind. I fold the paper and clutch it in my hand, then shake my head at Xiaoyu and say quietly: 'We can't use this — it belongs to the class rep, and I'm going to give it to the teacher. Don't worry, I won't say you saw it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse to use the answer sheet, and I won't let my friend copy it either — I'll return the paper or put it back where it was, and I won't take part in this gamble on luck. I tell my friend: 'I'm not using this, and you shouldn't copy it either — have you actually calculated what it costs if we get caught? I'm putting it back. What you do after that is your call.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a plausible excuse, removing myself from the situation entirely — neither breaking the promise I made to him nor showing up to play a supporting role in this deception. Reply: 'There's a project at work that's hit a critical point right now, so I'm afraid I can't get away this weekend — you all go ahead and have fun, and we'll find another time to get together.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to dodge the camping invitation with a vague excuse, buying time and space — neither outright refusing nor agreeing. Reply: 'It's been so long! Something just came up on our end this weekend, so we probably won't make it — can we plan something another time? Hope you guys have a blast! ～'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to accept the camping invitation and respond with surface-level enthusiasm, but leave myself a vague out — while quietly working to make sure the four of us never actually go. I write back: "That sounds amazing! The guesthouse must have such a beautiful view — let me just check my schedule on this end, you guys lock in the details first, and I'll get back to you as soon as I can!"

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a personal excuse, without revealing anything about the secret, and use a brief, vague reason to quietly remove myself from the situation. Reply: 'I haven't been feeling great lately — I probably won't be able to make it this weekend. Hope you all have a great time, let's plan something again soon!'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to soften the moment gently — neither flatly refusing the invitation nor immediately giving up my observation — trying to buy myself a little time. I stay crouched where I am, look up, and give him a small smile: "Go ahead and warm up without me — I just need two more minutes. This bug is seriously weird, I've never seen anything like it — I'll be right there!"

options.label: B
options.content: I decline the invitation and stay behind to keep observing the insect and the little purple flower, trying to document every detail with my phone or the notebook I have on me. I tell the kid: "Go on ahead without me — there's something here I haven't finished looking at. I've never seen this bug before in my life, and I need to figure out what it is first."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation and go play soccer, but at the same time I turn this strange bug into a 'surprise' I bring to the field — merging both things into one. I say to the kid: 'Let's go, let's go — but look at this first. Have you ever seen a bug like this? Its back is green and it actually glows! We'll come back and study it after the game — come on, let's go!'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to politely decline the invitation and stay to keep observing, brushing him off in a short, direct way. I keep my eyes on the bug and say offhandedly: 'You guys go ahead — I'm watching this. I'm not coming.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to set down my chopsticks and walk over to the corner to strike up a conversation with the new classmate, instead of heading to the playground with my friends. I carry my tray over to that little table against the wall, sit down with a smile, and say: 'Is it okay if I sit here? What's your name? I'm — . Let's eat together — it's so loud in this cafeteria, eating alone is no fun!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table and walk over to the corner to talk to the new student — no emotional performance, just information. I pick up my tray, stand up, and tell my friends, 'You guys go ahead, I'm going to sit over there for a bit,' then walk to the corner and sit down across from her, and ask directly: 'I'm guessing you don't recognize most of the dishes here — let me tell you which ones are actually worth eating.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively — walk over to the new student and break the ice in a task-oriented way. I pick up my tray, stand up, tell my friends 'Go ahead, I'll catch up,' then walk over to the table in the corner and sit down, saying to the new student: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — . It's your first day — if there's anything you're not sure about, feel free to ask me.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to leave the lively crowded table, pick up my tray, and walk over to the new student sitting alone in the corner — not forcing myself to seem warm or outgoing, just going over and sitting down. I carry my tray to that small table, set it down gently, and say quietly: 'Is it okay if I sit here?'

options.label: A
options.content: I decline the card, and in front of everyone I flip this so-called 'invitation' into a public counter-strike — letting every person in the room see exactly what is behind that business card. I don't reach out to take it. Instead, I smile slightly, my voice pitched just loud enough for two or three tables to catch every word: 'Generous enough? Twelve years ago you put your name on my proposal — now that was generous. Keep the assistant position. It's not for me — I've never been good at signing other people's names for them.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly, giving him no emotional reaction to exploit, letting the whole performance collapse in plain sight with nothing but calm. I glanced down at the card, then looked up, my voice as level as if I were stating a simple fact: 'No need. For the assistant position, I'd suggest finding someone who doesn't know how you got to where you are — it'll save you a lot of headaches.' Then I picked up my orange juice and walked away.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse his 'generosity' right there in front of everyone, and to push back against this humiliation with a cutting remark. I slide the business card back toward him, and with a cold smile I say: 'I appreciate the thought — but I'm much more used to being my own boss. That assistant position of yours? Save it for someone who needs their life managed for them.' Then I turn and walk out of the reception.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — no taking the card, no explanation, no anger — cutting off this performance with silence and by walking away. I glanced down at the card for a moment, didn't reach for it, then raised my eyes and said calmly: 'No, thank you,' set my orange juice glass on the nearest table, and turned and walked toward the exit.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take over and re-break down the tasks, pushing the event forward with minimal manpower, while making each member's baseline responsibilities explicit. I speak up: 'Canceling isn't the optimal solution — we can't just let everything we've already put in go to zero. I'll coordinate what's left, but each of you has to claim at least one concrete task; I can't have it all fall on me alone. Tell me — which one of you has time tonight to check in with the sponsors and confirm their status?'

options.label: B
options.content: I decide not to cancel the event, but I propose a compromise: split the remaining tasks, explicitly assign the parts that require external communication (confirming sponsors, contacting guest speakers) to whichever members are still willing to stay, and take on the parts I can handle independently myself (the venue layout plan, organizing the volunteer schedule). I speak up: 'We're not cancelling. Whoever can make phone calls, go make them — I'll handle the scheduling and the venue plan. We work separately. Ten days is enough.'

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse to cancel outright, and instead propose a scaled-down plan: cut the high-difficulty elements, redistribute responsibilities, and reshape the event into a smaller but genuinely executable version. I speak up: "We're not canceling — but we do need to redesign this. The president's gone, so we turn it into something smaller. Each of you takes on one thing you can actually handle, I'll coordinate the whole thing, and we get through it together."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to cancel the event outright, and instead propose a compromise: scale things down, redistribute responsibilities, ask the remaining members to each take on whatever they can manage, and reach out to the student affairs committee to explain the situation and ask for support. I speak up: 'I think we shouldn't rush to say cancel just yet — can you hear me out for a second? We scale the event down, everyone only handles one or two of the most critical things, and I'll go coordinate the rest with the student affairs committee. That way nobody's finals get wrecked, and the event doesn't just disappear into nothing.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell the truth about who brought the contraband in, prioritizing my own protection and making clear I had no part in it. I'll quietly tell the director exactly who I saw and what happened — and I'll also let him know that person just threatened me.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to cooperate with the vice principal to a limited degree — I'll say who brought the cards in, but I won't volunteer anything extra or embellish. I keep my head down and speak: 'It was Chen in the back row — he brought them in. I saw it, but I wasn't part of it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to give a vague, partial admission — deliberately blurring the details — hoping to make the dean feel like I'm cooperating without actually naming anyone, while leaving myself a little room. I keep my eyes down and say: 'I… did see some people playing something in the back, but I couldn't really tell who took it out first — I had my head down the whole time, I was reading, I genuinely couldn't see clearly.'

options.label: D
options.content: He chooses to tell the truth, refusing to cover for the one who brought the cards, and ignores the threat entirely. Looking the director straight in the eye, he says: 'He's the one who brought them in. I saw it. That's all there is to it — it has nothing to do with me.'

options.label: A
options.content: I've made up my mind to support the professional path — clearly and without reservation — while insisting that we call my kid out here right now for a three-way conversation, so he can say what he wants in his own words. I tell the teacher: "Personally, I'm in favor — but we need to ask him directly, right now. Can we bring him out here so the three of us can talk this through together?"

options.label: B
options.content: I decided not to express immediate support or opposition to the special recruitment, and instead to return the decision to my child first, while buying time for the whole family to discuss it together. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you — this is very significant news for us. Before we make any decision, I want to genuinely hear what my child himself has to say. This is ultimately his path to walk, and I want him to walk it because he truly wants to, not because of our expectations. Could you give us a little time?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold off on committing and gather the key information first before making any decision. I tell the teacher: 'Thank you for letting us know. I need to talk with my child first and find out what he thinks, and I'd also like to learn more about the specifics of this program — things like the exit mechanism and academic safeguards. We'll get back to you after that.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold off on responding to the teacher for now, and instead sit down alone with my child for a real conversation — to give the decision back to the person it belongs to. I tell the teacher: 'Thank you so much. I need to talk it over with my child first, hear what they think, and then we'll make a decision together.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to let go of the overseas opportunity and return home immediately to be with Dad and handle the follow-up medical arrangements. Say to Mom: 'Mom, wait for me — I'll be back first thing tomorrow morning. You don't have to carry this alone.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to go back immediately to handle my father's medical affairs, while simultaneously reaching out to the company to negotiate a delay or explore remote options for the overseas opportunity. This isn't a pure emotional sacrifice — I'm treating it as a project that requires my on-site presence. I tell my mother: 'I'll book a ticket tonight and be there tomorrow. In the meantime, have the doctors pull together all the test results — when I arrive, I'll go straight to the attending physician and talk it through myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: She chooses to immediately abandon the overseas assignment and go home to be with her father and handle whatever comes next with his medical care. Voice breaking over the phone, she says: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — first flight tomorrow morning. Wait for me.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to go home immediately to deal with Dad's illness, setting the overseas project decision aside for now, and will reach out to the company once the situation becomes clearer. I tell my mother: 'I understand — tell me which hospital Dad is in. I'll look up tickets tonight and take the earliest flight out. You stay calm for now. Once I'm there, I'll go talk to the doctors myself.'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose not to write my colleague — the one who just returned from maternity leave — onto the list, but I didn't confront my boss head-on either. Instead, I used delay and vagueness to buy myself time. I told him: 'I need to go back through the complete performance review records. I'll send you my thoughts before tonight — but I can't confirm this list right now.'

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to write the colleague who just returned from maternity leave onto the list, and I choose to voice my dissent directly rather than comply in silence. I meet my superior's eyes and say: 'I can't write this list the way you're asking. If someone has to go, I need a real reason — not this one.'

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to directly write that colleague's name — the one who just returned from maternity leave — on the list, and I try to ask my boss whether another solution might be possible. I say quietly: "I understand the position you're in, but this colleague just came back and her performance is already recovering — I'd like to think a little longer about whether there's another way. Could you give me a bit of time?"

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to include the colleague who just returned from maternity leave on the layoff list, and I name the real nature of what my superior is asking directly, without offering any cooperative cover. I meet his eyes and say: 'The arrangement you're describing isn't something I can carry out — putting a B+ employee on record as failing to meet expectations while keeping someone who's been C two quarters running is a list I won't sign. If there's a second round of names, I'll accept being on it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I decline to use the service — directly, without lecturing. I gave a small laugh and slid the phone back, saying: 'Appreciate it, man — I'm doing alright on my end, don't need it. Do whatever works for you though.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to gently decline my roommate's suggestion, but in a considerate way that doesn't make him feel judged. I smiled and pushed the phone back toward him, saying: 'Thanks for thinking of me — honestly though, my progress is still okay, so I'll give it a shot on my own first. If I really hit a wall, I'll say something. Just hold onto that contact info for me, yeah?'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to refuse the service and commit to finishing the thesis on my own, without any compromise. I shook my head and pushed the phone back, saying: "I don't need this — I'll write it myself. Don't send me the contact info."

options.label: D
options.content: Decline outright — no hedging, no need for a lengthy explanation. I push the phone back and say, calmly: 'It's fine, I can finish it myself. That kind of thing carries way too much risk — it's not worth it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to campaign independently, stand by the original proposal, and let the idea's own appeal win people over — rather than merging it with another plan just to play it safe. I tell my friend: "I want to try on my own first. This weekend I'll go find people, show them the sketches, let them really picture that space — and if I've made my case and still lose, I'll accept it. But I don't want to gut the whole thing before I've even had a chance to show it."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to step away from the competition — I won't go talk to her about merging, and I won't spend the weekend canvassing votes. I'll let the proposal lose on its own terms. I tell my friend: "Forget it — if everyone likes her proposal, they should go with it. I'll keep mine for myself."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to merge and not to campaign blindly—instead, I'm going to do a precise read of where the votes actually are. I'll identify the small number of people in the class who genuinely want the show to be good, not the ones who'd be happy just singing a few songs, and focus all my energy on convincing them. I tell my friend: 'Merging is off the table—if we do that, my concept is gone. Help me think: who in the class actually wants to put on a real show? Not the 'whatever, just sing' crowd—starting with those people, I'll go pitch my concept.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take the initiative and talk to her about collaborating, to try to merge the two proposals, putting the harmony of our relationship ahead of fighting over credit. I tell my friend: "I'll go have a chat with her and see if we can piece the two proposals together — credit can wait, as long as this doesn't blow up into a whole thing in class."

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to stay at my current school, and tell her directly — no evasion, no sugarcoating. My reply to her tonight: 'I listened to every word you said. Staying here is the only honest choice I can make. If I went north carrying regret, that wouldn't be fair to you either. I want to talk about whether we can find another way forward — but I can't give you an answer I don't believe myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to accept the offer from the northern university, and I give a clear answer tonight — no more stalling, no more vague non-responses. I send a voice message, my voice a little hoarse: 'I've made up my mind. I'm coming to you. We do this together. We'll find a new path over there — but you don't carry this alone anymore.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to give up my acceptance here and take the offer from the university up north — and tonight I give my partner a clear answer. I sent back a voice message: 'I'm coming to you. It's already decided. Wait for me.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school — I won't switch institutions as a concession to save the relationship. I send a message explaining my decision, and I'm honest: if they can't continue under these circumstances, I respect that we part ways.

options.label: A
