options.content: I chose to express genuine interest while making it clear that I needed to verify the information before giving any answer, keeping the decision in my own hands. I told the senior: "I listened carefully to everything you said, and I'm going to take this seriously. But before I give you an answer, I need to see the original letter of intent, and whatever user data the product has right now — if there is any. I'll give you a definitive reply before Tuesday."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stay open without committing, and to secure a window of time before the deadline to gather information and think it through carefully. I tell my senior: 'I need to think seriously about what you've said — this isn't a small thing. Before Tuesday, I'll give you a clear answer. But I can't say yes or no on the spot right now. Can you understand that?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to express enthusiasm and lean toward accepting, while carving out a small buffer rather than committing on the spot. I lean forward, meet my senior's eyes directly, and say: 'I don't need to wait until Tuesday — this direction feels right to me, and everything you said landed. Give me 48 hours. Not to hesitate, but to get my own side of things sorted out — and then we sit down and talk through the details for real.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold off on committing — to buy myself some breathing room — neither refusing outright nor making a promise on the spot, using the need to consult my family and advisor as a polite way to keep the relationship intact. I say to my senior: "I'm genuinely moved that you think so highly of me, and I want you to know I'm taking this opportunity very seriously. Could you give me until Tuesday to think it through properly? I need to let my family know, and I'd also like to talk with my advisor — I don't want to give you a hasty answer, because this matters too much for both of us."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak, but I use self-deprecation and deliberate understatement to preemptively defuse any possible doubt — pushing the honor away from myself to keep myself safe. I lean into the microphone and say: 'Honestly… I didn't expect this myself. I wrote this piece pretty casually, so maybe the judges saw something in it that I didn't — thank you all.' Then I lower my head, take a small step back, and return the microphone to the host.

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to speak directly, describing my creative process in the most concise and honest way I could — no performed gratitude, no studied humility. Into the microphone I said: 'I wrote this piece off the top of my head three weeks ago. I just wanted to work out a story with a coherent internal logic. Winning means the judges found that logic convincing, and honestly, that's a better outcome than I expected.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to push through and speak anyway, but before opening my mouth I forced myself to quickly assemble a two-point structural framework in my head, using that sense of order to hold the panic down. I took a deep breath, gripped the microphone, and said: 'Thank you to the judges and to Ms. Zhao. When I was writing this piece, I was really thinking about two things — one is how we understand the idea of "the future," and the other is what ordinary people can do in the face of enormous change. I never expected these two questions to resonate with anyone, so this recognition genuinely surprises me, and it means a great deal. Thank you all.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take the microphone and speak sincerely — to get through an impromptu acceptance speech by looking after the mood of the room, rather than retreating into silence. I gave a small smile into the microphone, voice a little unsteady but still finding the words: 'Thank you to the teacher and the judges. When I wrote this piece, I really just wanted to put down on paper a question that had been rattling around in my head and wouldn't go away… I never expected anyone to like it. I think maybe — the kind of confusion the story is about — maybe most of us have felt something like that at some point.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to state my situation directly and without defensiveness — neither playing along nor pushing back, letting the facts themselves answer all the well-meaning questions. I say: 'I do project planning for a nonprofit — the salary isn't high, I rent, and last year one of my paintings got into a youth art exhibition. That's my life right now, and I'm pretty happy with it. Thanks for the advice about buying a place; it's just not in my plans at the moment.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to introduce my situation proactively, clearly, and with structure — no deflecting, no defending, just delivering a 'status report' on my own life the way a project manager would. I say: 'I do project planning at a nonprofit. The salary isn't high, but I manage every project from kickoff to close-out, and I manage it well. In my spare time I paint — last year I got into the local young artists' exhibition, which counts as a milestone. Renting is my choice for this stage; I haven't hit the point where buying makes sense. As for joining a big tech company — I evaluated it, and it doesn't fit my current execution roadmap.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to grab the thread of the conversation head-on — use humor and confidence to put my life on full display and dissolve the awkward charge of the class-gap moment right then and there. I lift my sake cup and sweep a grin around the table: 'Mysterious? That's because what I do isn't exactly in your wheelhouse — nonprofit projects by day, painting by night, and last year one of my pieces got into the local young artists' exhibition. Income? Enough to live on, and I'm living with a hell of a lot of energy. You say triple the pay — depends what you're tripling. Sure, triple the money, but if I lose this drive in the process, that's a terrible trade. Come on, drink up!'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to describe my current life in a way that is gentle and sincere without diminishing myself — mentioning the art exhibition in passing, steering the conversation toward something everyone can join in on, and not engaging directly with any "have you thought about switching jobs" suggestions. I smile and say: "Haha, I'm working on projects at a nonprofit right now — the pay is honestly nothing to write home about, but last year one of my paintings got selected for the local young artists' exhibition, so that was a nice surprise. You guys are living the dream, honestly — come on, let's drink to that!"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to prepare myself before my roommate gets back, and to tell him face to face — privately, gently, but directly — that I've discovered the plagiarism, giving him the chance to rewrite before submission, rather than reporting him or staying silent. I close the browser tab, ease his laptop screen back to where it was, take a deep breath, and wait for him to walk in. When he does, I speak: 'There's something I need to talk to you about — sit down for a second. It's about the report. I came across something by accident, and I think we need to talk before it gets submitted.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to confront him directly, face to face — demanding that he rewrite the plagiarized sections before submission, no dancing around it, no going to the professor behind his back, giving him one chance to handle it himself, but leaving absolutely no room for ambiguity in my tone. The moment he walks through the door, I speak up: 'I just looked at your document, and there are several passages that are word for word identical to articles on that Q&A site — I searched it myself. You heard what the professor said about the rules. You need to rewrite it yourself before we hand this in the day after tomorrow, because if you don't, I can't submit this thing together with you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell him face-to-face, privately, when he gets back — giving him the chance to rewrite it before submission — rather than staying silent or going straight to the professor. When he pushes the door open, I look up and say: 'I accidentally saw your document just now, and I searched a few passages — they're a perfect match with an article on Zhihu. The deadline is the day after tomorrow; you still have time to fix it, and I can help you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up before my roommate gets back — to tell him directly what I found, framing it as 'I came across it by accident, and we need to talk,' and to demand that he rewrite it before submission, while making clear that the consequences fall on both of us. I stand up, walk to the door, and the moment it opens I say: 'I accidentally saw what was on your screen just now, and I searched a few of the passages — we need to talk right now, because the deadline is the day after tomorrow and both our names are on this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene and offer real, concrete protection — but on my own terms, through deliberate action rather than impulsive confrontation. I tell Lin Xiaoyu: 'Don't go to that meeting alone tomorrow — I'll figure something out. I'll use project coordination as a reason to get myself inserted into that meeting arrangement, so you won't have to face him by yourself. As for reporting it, we can take that one step at a time — but tonight, write down the times, the places, and what happened, and send it to me. Get it on record.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to actively intervene, making it clear to Lin Xiaoyu that I will take action — not simply be a listener. I look her straight in the eye and say: 'You don't have to file the report yourself, but I will handle this. You don't need to go to that meeting tomorrow — I'll find a way to block it — and then we'll put the evidence together and go through the official channels.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to intervene clearly, make a commitment, and guide her toward safer, formal channels rather than leaving her to face this alone. I take her hand and say: 'Coming to me tonight — that was the right thing to do. Don't go to that meeting alone tomorrow. Let me help you figure this out — we'll start by writing down everything that's happened, and then we'll go to HR together. I'll be right there with you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to step in — but in a way that puts protecting her first, without pushing for a formal report just yet. I place my hand over hers, my voice shaking a little, but I speak anyway: 'You don't have to carry this alone tonight. That meeting tomorrow — I'm going to figure out a way to make sure it doesn't happen. But first, tell me everything he's done.'

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to immediately call 120 and 110 for emergency help, leaving the handbag exactly where it is by the roadside without touching it. He pulls out his phone and dials 120: "Hello, there's been a traffic accident — a rider is injured, the location is —" while shouting toward the drainage ditch: "Hang on, I've called an ambulance!"

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to call emergency services and the police, leave the handbag exactly as it is on the ground, and stay on the scene to wait for help to arrive. I set the handbag down by the roadside and dialed 120, my voice pressing down against a tremor: 'Someone's been hit by a car — it's on that two-lane road at the edge of the city, near the abandoned tin shed. The injured person is beside the drainage ditch. Please come quickly.' Then I called 110 and told them everything I'd seen, including the black Audi and the driver who fled.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to call the authorities and get help immediately, refuse the bribe, and leave the handbag in place as evidence. I call 120, give my location and describe the injured person's condition; then I call 110, recount the details of the incident and the direction the driver fled, and inform them that there is a bag of cash at the scene left by the driver, which I have not touched.

options.label: D
options.content: I immediately dial 120 and then 110, leave the handbag untouched in a visible spot on the side of the road, and move toward the injured person to offer basic reassurance. I set the bag where the streetlight can reach it, then crouch down at the edge of the ditch, waiting for the call to connect, and say to him: 'I'm here. I've called an ambulance. Hold on.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take out my phone and transfer the three thousand dollars, while using a preemptive strike of self-attack to hold back the collapse building inside me. My hand trembling, I open the payment app, and say in a hoarse voice: 'I'm sending it to you — give me your contact info… now go, hurry.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lend him the three thousand yuan, but I don't want to talk more than necessary — I want to get it done as fast as possible and go. I hold out my phone so he can see the balance, and say quietly: 'I'll transfer it to you. Give me your payment code. Doesn't matter whether you write an IOU or not.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to lend the money, but I give him an alternative direction rather than walking away in silence. I crouch down and gently move his hand from my pant leg, and say calmly: 'I can't lend you anything — but go to the nurses' station right now, tell them the family has no money. The hospital has an emergency payment assistance process. Go ask them. Don't stay here on your knees.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan immediately, without hesitating to press for too many details. I pull out my phone and open the transfer screen, and I tell him: 'I'm sending it to you now — go pay the deposit. You don't need to write an IOU. Save him first.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to confront the challenge head-on and use data and logic to establish the ground rules. I meet his gaze and say: 'The delay on that module last quarter was due to a requirements change — not a technical issue — and it's documented clearly in the review records. As for this quarter's schedule, I'll break it down item by item in the meeting. If there are questions, we let the data do the talking.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to meet the challenge head-on, acknowledge the value of the senior team member's experience, and open with a posture of collaboration rather than authority. I said: 'Thanks for asking that straight out — it's actually exactly what I wanted to go over with everyone today. That module from last quarter: I need someone like you, with real experience on it, to walk me through where it got stuck. Let's get in a room — you go first.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on, defuse the pressure with a relaxed attitude, and kick the ball back to the whole team — so I don't end up as his target. I smiled and said: "Whether I can handle it or not, we won't know until we try — but actually, that question you just asked was a good one. That unfinished module? Let's put it on the table in today's meeting, take a look together at where it's stuck, and anyone who has ideas can speak up. That includes you."

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to meet the challenge head-on — openly acknowledging the information gap while folding the senior team member's expertise into the meeting agenda, converting confrontation into a collaborative framework. I said: 'You're right — I haven't fully gotten my head around that module yet. So what I'd like to do in today's meeting is hear from you first about where things got stuck last quarter. You know this piece better than I do. Let's lay the problems out on the table, and then we can map out the plan for this quarter.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene — I don't return the bottle, I walk toward the bed curtain and speak, while preparing to reach out for outside help. I grip the pill bottle in my hand, walk to his bedside, and say quietly: 'I'm not leaving — I just don't want to. I'm holding onto this bottle for now — how are you doing right now?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene directly — I don't return the pill bottle, and I immediately contact the resident advisor or call a mental health crisis line. I don't let this get handled internally within the dorm room. I keep the bottle in my hand, stand up, and walk to the curtain around his bed. In a steady voice, I say: 'I'm not giving this back to you. You need someone here right now — I'm going to contact the RA. If you don't want me to, tell me now.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to return the pill bottle. Using his question as an opening, I speak softly in response, trying to give him a reason to want to talk. I keep the bottle in my hand, don't kick it back, turn around, and do my best to keep my voice steady: 'I'm not going out tonight… I was hoping I could sit with you for a while. Would that be okay?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to use my roommate's unprompted question as an opening—staying, responding quietly, not returning the bottle, while quietly preparing to reach out to a counselor or mental health support line. I keep the bottle in my hand without kicking it back, turn around, and say: 'Nothing going on tonight, so I just didn't head out. You doing… okay over there?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to break the deadlock with concrete evidence rather than suffer in silence or escalate into confrontation. I raise my phone screen as high as I can, facing both the livestream camera and the people around me, and say — loudly, but as steadily as I can manage: 'Please, everyone, look carefully — this is the work group chat I was scrolling through on my phone. I was not photographing anyone. This is a misunderstanding, and I am willing to let anyone check my phone's photo album.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to actively present the evidence and break the spectator structure, rather than endure in silence or fight back aggressively. I held my phone screen up as high as I could and said, as loudly but as steadily as I could manage: 'Everyone look — this is my work group chat, messages sent at four in the morning, I've been reading this the whole time, my phone has been held at exactly this height, screen facing out — anyone can come and look.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to go on the offensive — counter with evidence directly, no explanations, no apologies, no showing weakness. I snapped my phone screen around toward the live-stream camera, my voice calm but unmistakably clear: 'You're live-streaming? Good. Let everyone get a good look at this screen — this is the work group chat, the timestamp is right there, my phone was never pointed at you.' Then I pulled my collar free from their grip and held their gaze.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to proactively present the evidence and use facts to restore order — no fleeing, no breaking down, no fighting fire with fire against the other person's emotions. I raise my phone screen high, turning it toward the cameras on all sides, my voice low but clear: 'Please look at the screen carefully — this is my company's work group chat, the timestamp is four in the morning, I was reading a new project brief, not the camera app, not the photo album. Anyone here is welcome to verify it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I won't agree to being in the office both days of the weekend, but I'll propose a compromise and stay responsible for the core content. I'll say: I have to go back home to be with my father for his follow-up appointment, but I'll hand everything off tonight with a clear checklist, revise remotely over the weekend, and do a final online review of the draft Sunday evening.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to be upfront about the situation at home first, while proactively offering a compromise — trying not to completely let either side down. I speak up: 'Director, there's something I need to tell you — my dad has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend, and I've already promised my family I'd go with him. I know this is a critical moment, so here's what I'm thinking: I can work remotely from home on Saturday and push forward on the market benchmarking and the main body of the proposal. If I can get back Sunday morning, we could go through the final draft together Sunday afternoon or evening — does that work for you?'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose partial compromise while holding the line: make it clear to the director that the weekend trip home is not being canceled, but offer to put in extra hours tonight to push the project forward, provide remote support on the critical sections over the weekend, and join a video call Sunday evening for the final-draft review. Say directly: 'I have a family commitment this weekend — it was locked in three days ago and I can't cancel it. I can stay late tonight and I'm available remotely over the weekend, but coming into the office isn't something I can do. Tell me which parts are the biggest bottlenecks and let's work out the priorities together.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to explain the situation at home and hold firm on going back, while offering a compromise to soften the blow of saying no. I tell the director: 'My dad has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend — I promised three days ago that I'd go with my parents, so there's really no way I can change that. But I can put together a written document tonight with the competitive market analysis section and send it to you. Maybe you could find someone to work off that for the redesign? Or I could do a remote review of the final draft Sunday evening?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to object outright, and instead ask that both of us first lay this path out in full and see it clearly before any decision is made. I say: 'Leave that booklet here with me. I'll read through it carefully tonight. We'll talk again tomorrow — I have questions I want to ask you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to oppose him outright, but I can't bring myself to offer real support either — I replace a clear answer with the hedged worry of someone who's 'been through it' and a conditional softening. I say: 'You're telling me you've been at this since middle school… It's not that I won't let you try, but you need to think it through — if it doesn't pan out, do you have a fallback?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to offer conditional support, but I require my kid to sit through a structured risk-breakdown conversation right now, rather than simply handing over an emotional endorsement. I push the report booklet back across the table and say: 'You say seven years — I believe you. But let's go through this field's employment data, your competitive edge, and your exit path in the worst-case scenario, one by one. If you can answer those, I'll support you filling it in.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to support my child in pursuing this path — I voice my worries, but I don't use them to invalidate their choice. I say: 'I hear you. You've been preparing for so long, and I have no reason to say no. I have my fears, but those are my homework to do — not a chain around your neck. Let's figure out together how to make this path as solid as it can be.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honor Father's exhaustion and his wishes, and decide on palliative care—so that he can spend whatever time remains with dignity. I say quietly to the doctor: 'We'll go with palliative care. Just keep him comfortable, and let him stay with us.' When I finish speaking, I take Father's hand in mine.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion — palliative care, letting go of that 20% chance. I look at the doctor, my voice a little hoarse, and say: 'We'll go with comfort care. Let him go with some dignity.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to honor my father's wishes and decide on palliative care, stating that choice plainly without flinching. I say to the doctor: 'We're choosing palliative care — please initiate the comfort care protocol. He has made his choice, and we respect it.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose palliative care, honor the exhaustion and wishes my father has expressed, and refuse to let a low-probability course of chemotherapy turn his remaining time into suffering. I speak to the doctor: 'Start the comfort-care protocol—let him spend whatever time he has left in peace.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up immediately, to turn this into a direct, face-to-face confrontation, refusing silence and delay. I set down the dish towel and look straight at her: 'You said you've been thinking about this for a long time — well, I want to know now, when did you start thinking about it, because when did there get to be so much between us that you weren't telling me?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on taking any position and prioritize gathering information, asking her to spell out exactly what the specific boundaries are. I set down my phone, looked at her, and said: 'When you say flexibility, I'd like to hear you be a little more specific — the independent space you're talking about, what kinds of things does that mean, and how far does it go?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to set my phone aside for now, to shut out any outside distraction, and to ask him first — to understand what he's really feeling and what exactly he means — rather than immediately saying yes or no. I put down the dish cloth, sit across from him, and say quietly: 'When you say independent space… can you tell me — when did you first start feeling this way?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to reject this boundary adjustment and explicitly uphold the original commitment. I'll silence my phone first, then look him in the eye and say: 'I don't accept this kind of relationship. If this is what you need, then let's have a real conversation about whether we're still doing this at all.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to break the silence and reach toward him — not let this morning dissolve into each of us going our separate ways. I speak up: 'Let's go out for a walk. I don't really know what else to do either — but not knowing together seems a little better than not knowing apart.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to actively respond to my partner's suggestion, converting the vague "let's go out for a walk together" into a concrete starting point for today — rather than each of us retreating into familiar busyness to avoid this blank space. I stand up and say: "Let's go — let's take that walk. When we get back, we can think about how to structure the time ahead."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to actively respond to his suggestion of "going out for a walk together" — to break the stalemate and keep us from drifting apart. I gave a small, quiet smile and said: "Let's go. You mentioned going out for a walk just now — I think that's a good idea. Let's go together."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to sidestep this shared strangeness — to gently acknowledge that it exists, rather than each of us retreating back into the familiar busyness. I look down at the table and say: "This does feel strange. How about… we have a cup of coffee together first? No need to think about what comes next."

options.label: A
options.content: She chooses to gently decline the camping invitation, using a vague but sincere excuse to bow out of the weekend — not directly revealing the secret, but also not showing up and pretending everything is fine. She replies: 'I've been feeling a bit off lately, I probably can't make it this weekend — you all go and have fun, and let's plan something just the two of us another time!'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to dodge the camping invitation with a vague excuse, buying time and space — neither outright refusing nor agreeing. Reply: 'It's been so long! Something just came up on our end this weekend, so we probably won't make it — can we plan something another time? Hope you guys have a blast! ～'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to politely decline the camping invitation, using a simple, honest but non-detailed reason to bow out — no lying, no explaining, no revealing anything. I reply: 'We actually have something going on this weekend, so we won't be able to make it — next time for sure! Hope you all have a great time.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a plausible excuse, removing myself from the situation entirely — neither breaking the promise I made to him nor showing up to play a supporting role in this deception. Reply: 'There's a project at work that's hit a critical point right now, so I'm afraid I can't get away this weekend — you all go ahead and have fun, and we'll find another time to get together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I decide to do both things, by arranging my time in advance to fit everything in — go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, then head to the amusement park for my cousin's birthday party. I say to Mom: 'Mom, tomorrow morning I'll go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — it won't take long — and then we can go to the amusement park. That way neither one gets missed, okay?'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to do both, balancing responsibility and the outing through careful timing. Tell Mom: 'I want to go! But I have to stop by school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — my teacher gave me the key, and I made a promise. I'll head over early in the morning, and once I'm done we can leave. We'll make it in time!'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to go to school first to finish my responsibilities, then head to the birthday outing — I'll do both, and I'm not giving either one up. I tell Mom: 'I'm happy, but tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — it'll take half an hour, tops, and then we can head out. It won't hold us up for long.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to fulfill the responsibility I was entrusted with first, then make my way to the amusement park as soon as I can — resolving the conflict by doing both. I tell Mom: 'I'll go to school first thing tomorrow morning to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, and once that's done I'll head straight to find my cousin — I should still make it in time. Can you let Uncle know for me?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to handle the medical decision remotely, then attend the launch as planned. First I press the doctors to spell out the risks clearly over the phone and confirm the treatment plan directly; then I notify the CEO that I'll be there on time.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the medical authorization, while activating the emergency contingency plan to have the team take over as the main presenter. I call the CEO and say: 'My father has gone into a critical situation during surgery — I need to be there in person to sign right now. Please arrange a backup presenter. I'll provide remote support if at all possible. I'm sorry.' Then I hang up and tell the driver to go straight to the hospital.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to put down the speech immediately and head to the hospital, leaving the launch event for the team to handle on an emergency basis. I tell the CEO: 'My father's surgery requires my signature — it's the one thing that cannot be replaced. Please have the team hold things down at the launch; I'll deal with the follow-up afterward.' Then I hang up, and tell the driver to reroute to the hospital.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to go to the hospital immediately, to give up attending the launch, and to place my father's life before any professional cost. Choking up on the CEO's video call, I say: "I'm so sorry — my father is on the operating table right now, I have to get to the hospital to sign the consent forms. Please find a way to manage the launch without me. I am truly, deeply sorry."

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to make a choice. Instead I try to break the deadlock with my voice and my body — pulling both of their attention away from "waiting for me to take a side." I lunge forward and wrap my arms around the waist of the one crouching by the door, then spin back and scream at the other one through my tears: "You can't both leave! Nobody is allowed to leave! I won't let you!"

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to make the choice either of them is demanding, and in a calm, direct way I hand the decision back to the adults. I speak up: 'This is between the two of you — it's not my business. I'm not going anywhere and I'm not staying for either of you. You decide.' Then I turn around, walk back to my room, and close the door.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose silence and freeze in place, refusing to open my mouth or make any choice at all, letting the stillness of my body stand in for words. I sink down into a crouch, bury my face in my knees, and can't make a single sound — only shaking.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to take either side, and instead try to break this impossible either-or through tears and pleading. I lunge forward and wrap my arms around the legs of the person nearest to me, sobbing as I cry out: "Please stop fighting — I'm not going anywhere, don't either of you leave —" The tears won't stop falling, and my voice is so choked I can barely get the words out.

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while immediately seeking legal protection, and refuse to provide any substantive information before a lawyer is present. Say aloud: 'I need to contact my lawyer first — until then, I'm not in a position to make any statement about the specifics. This isn't a refusal to cooperate; it's my basic right.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on full cooperation and seek legal assistance first — I will not provide any information about my colleagues or any internal materials without a lawyer present. I speak up and say: 'I understand the importance of this investigation, and I am willing to cooperate — but before I provide any specific information, I need to consult with legal counsel first. Please allow me that right.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to delay full cooperation and seek legal counsel immediately — selectively acknowledging the basic facts while refusing to provide any information about team members. To the investigator: 'I understand what you're asking. I did see that document — that much is true. But without a lawyer present, I won't be providing any further information. That's the more responsible approach for both of us. I need 24 hours. I'll make good use of them.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while seeking legal support, and refrain from making statements about others when the information isn't clear. Say aloud: 'I need to consult legal counsel first — I'll give you a response within 24 hours. As for the circumstances of my own signature, I can clarify that right now: at the time, I was simply asked by my superior to review a budget document. I was not involved in any decision-making.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table for the moment, pick up my tray, and walk over to the new student sitting alone in the corner. I tell my friends, 'Go ahead — I'll come find you in a bit,' then make my way to that little table against the wall and sit down across from her, saying softly: 'Is it okay if I sit here? My name is… what's yours?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table and walk over to the corner to talk to the new student — no emotional performance, just information. I pick up my tray, stand up, and tell my friends, 'You guys go ahead, I'm going to sit over there for a bit,' then walk to the corner and sit down across from her, and ask directly: 'I'm guessing you don't recognize most of the dishes here — let me tell you which ones are actually worth eating.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to set down my chopsticks and walk over to the corner to strike up a conversation with the new classmate, instead of heading to the playground with my friends. I carry my tray over to that little table against the wall, sit down with a smile, and say: 'Is it okay if I sit here? What's your name? I'm — . Let's eat together — it's so loud in this cafeteria, eating alone is no fun!'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively — walk over to the new student and break the ice in a task-oriented way. I pick up my tray, stand up, tell my friends 'Go ahead, I'll catch up,' then walk over to the table in the corner and sit down, saying to the new student: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — . It's your first day — if there's anything you're not sure about, feel free to ask me.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to accept this explanation, and I'm not continuing as though things are the same between us. I gently moved her aside and said plainly that we're separating our finances for now — tomorrow we sit down and go through every account, every transaction, and where we each stand.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to confront the conflict head-on, right here and now — not suppressing my emotions, not running from the relationship — staying to face it together, even with anger and tenderness pulling at me at the same time. I crouch down, lift her hands away from my pant leg, and hold them. My voice comes out a little rough: 'Get up. I can't hear you when you're kneeling like that. Then I want you to tell me everything, from the beginning, one thing at a time. I want to know all of it. I'm angry — but I'm not going anywhere.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold down the anger for now and catch my partner in his collapse first — but I make it clear that everything about this has to be laid out in full, and we can't just let it pass. I bend down and pull him up from the floor, my voice trembling but I say it anyway: "Get up first. Sit down. Tell me everything from the beginning — how much is still owed, whether those people are going to come back, how your parents are doing right now. I'm in a lot of pain, but I need to know what we're actually facing first."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay. I don't leave. First I pull her up off the floor, I hear her out, and we face this mess together. I bend down, uncurl her hands from my pant leg, and then hold them, and I say: 'Come on, get up — the floor's cold. We'll figure out the apartment thing, one step at a time. But from now on, you can't carry something like this alone.'

options.label: A
options.content: Decline the opportunity gracefully, but with a genuine and respectful reason rather than a hollow brush-off. Say to the friend: 'Thank you for thinking of me — I read through the plan carefully, and I understand what you need. But honestly, the way I am right now just isn't suited to joining a team. Working alone is the only way I can actually do things well. Forcing that to change would only hold you back. Find someone who's a better fit for this.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to give an answer immediately, and instead ask for a full breakdown of the terms, the equity structure, and the exit mechanism — keeping the decision firmly in my own hands. I tell my friend: 'I need to see the complete terms and the equity agreement. Give me three days, and I'll give you an answer that's grounded in evidence — not one that was rushed out of me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving a definitive answer and ask for a specific window of time to think — rather than refusing or agreeing on the spot. I say to my friend: "You know I've been following this direction seriously for a long time, and your invitation genuinely means a lot to me — I need a few days to really think it through. Can you give me a week? I don't want to give you an answer I haven't earned."

options.label: D
options.content: The impulse called forth by the flow state outweighed the gravity of the financial spreadsheet, and I chose to accept the invitation — but I tried to carve out a little breathing room for myself rather than committing fully on the spot. I told my friend: 'Honestly, I already know what I want — I'm in. But can you give me a week to wrap things up on this end?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request and decide to hand the answer sheet over to the teacher or return it to where it belongs — trading honesty for genuine peace of mind. I fold the paper and clutch it in my hand, then shake my head at Xiaoyu and say quietly: 'We can't use this — it belongs to the class rep, and I'm going to give it to the teacher. Don't worry, I won't say you saw it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend and either hand the paper to the teacher or put it back where it came from. I whisper to her, "We can't use this — we'll get caught," and then, while she's not paying attention, I slip the paper back into the crack in the desk or hand it to the teacher, pretending I just happened to find it by accident.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to use the answer sheet, and I turn down my friend's request — putting the paper back where it came from or handing it to the teacher. I shake my head at my friend and say: "We can't use this — put it back. If anyone finds out, we're in serious trouble. Let's just go study on our own."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse to use the answer sheet, and I won't let my friend copy it either — I'll return the paper or put it back where it was, and I won't take part in this gamble on luck. I tell my friend: 'I'm not using this, and you shouldn't copy it either — have you actually calculated what it costs if we get caught? I'm putting it back. What you do after that is your call.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving a definitive answer, buying time on the grounds that I need to sit down properly with my family and my child, and drawing everyone involved into the conversation. I say to the teacher: 'Thank you so much — it truly means a great deal that you see my child this way. I'd like to sit down with my child and the rest of the family first, hear what they think, and then make a decision. Could you let me know the latest date by which you'd need to hear back from me?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to support my child in pursuing the professional path, while returning the decision to him — driving the conversation with information rather than emotion. I say to the teacher: 'Thank you for telling me. I'll go through everything in this brochure with him — the full picture, the pros and the cons — and let him decide for himself. But I'm not going to hide this option from him.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to politely decline the specialist track and hold firm on keeping my child in the regular education system, without applying for the talent recruitment program. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you so much — knowing that you see him that way, we as parents are truly grateful. But this is too big a decision for me to make on the spot. I need to go home and talk it through properly with the family. His schoolwork is stable right now, and we don't want to rush into anything.'

options.label: D
options.content: I decided not to express immediate support or opposition to the special recruitment, and instead to return the decision to my child first, while buying time for the whole family to discuss it together. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you — this is very significant news for us. Before we make any decision, I want to genuinely hear what my child himself has to say. This is ultimately his path to walk, and I want him to walk it because he truly wants to, not because of our expectations. Could you give us a little time?'

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to tell the truth, refusing to cover for the one who brought the cards, and ignores the threat entirely. Looking the director straight in the eye, he says: 'He's the one who brought them in. I saw it. That's all there is to it — it has nothing to do with me.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to cooperate with the vice principal to a limited degree — I'll say who brought the cards in, but I won't volunteer anything extra or embellish. I keep my head down and speak: 'It was Chen in the back row — he brought them in. I saw it, but I wasn't part of it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to give a vague, partial admission — deliberately blurring the details — hoping to make the dean feel like I'm cooperating without actually naming anyone, while leaving myself a little room. I keep my eyes down and say: 'I… did see some people playing something in the back, but I couldn't really tell who took it out first — I had my head down the whole time, I was reading, I genuinely couldn't see clearly.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the truth — to state the facts, to not be swayed by threats, and to make no unnecessary show of emotion. I look at the director calmly and say: 'It was the student in the back row near the corner who brought them in. I turned around because I heard a noise, but I wasn't involved.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold back for now and not confront him directly, but to plant a seed through an indirect approach — laying the groundwork for a real conversation later. I take the milk from him, glance down at it for a moment, and say quietly: 'The speech can wait for now… I'd really like to talk with you about this exam — how did you study for it? Come sit down and tell me.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold back and not expose everything in this moment, but I decide that tonight I will find a quiet moment alone with him to talk through this face to face — and to stop him from giving the speech at the commendation ceremony and from registering for the competition. I take the milk from him, set down what I'm holding, and say calmly: 'Let's put the speech aside for now. There's something important we need to talk about tonight — come find me in the study when you're done eating.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to confront him directly, right here and now——no dancing around it. I put the note on the table and make it clear that the competition and the awards ceremony have to stop. I set down the book in my hands, look him in the eyes, and say: 'Sit down. There's something I need to talk to you about. I found a note in your backpack——it's about your math midterm.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold it in for now, steady my emotions, and wait until he's gone before finding the right moment to talk to him alone. I took the milk from him, glanced down at it for a moment, and said quietly: 'Let's talk about the speech later — go wash up first. I have something I want to say to you.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to proactively transition, while treating tomorrow's manager meeting as an intelligence-gathering opportunity rather than a moment to seek validation. Tonight, reply to my former colleague: 'I'm already looking into this — I don't need a bundled bootcamp, but we're pointing in the same direction, so let's keep each other posted on progress'; at the same time, put together my own transition timeline to use during tomorrow's conversation as a way of testing what the company's real expectations are for traditional roles.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to wait and watch — I won't accept my former colleague's invitation, and I'll prepare to tell my supervisor tomorrow that I'm willing to align with the company's current direction and that I want to keep my position. I reply to my former colleague: 'I support your decision, but I've got family pressure on my end — quitting isn't something I'm considering right now. I'll see how things play out here first.' Then I open my calendar and accept the supervisor's invite.

options.label: C
options.content: Decline my former colleague's offer to team up for the transition and choose to plan my own learning path independently; at the same time, prepare a clear but noncommittal position for tomorrow's manager meeting — acknowledge that I'm evaluating a transition direction, but commit to no specific timeline, in order to preserve my autonomy. Concrete actions: send my former colleague a short text message — 'Still thinking it over, let's each do our own thing for now' — then open a document and quietly map out a self-study path for AI-related skills alongside the time window my current savings can support.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold off on making any decision about a career transition, and instead prioritize gathering more internal information through tomorrow's one-on-one with my supervisor, while giving my former colleague a noncommittal reply in the meantime. I write back to him: "I think you make a fair point — I'm still turning it over. Let me talk to my supervisor tomorrow and see how that goes, then we can catch up after."

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to write the colleague who just returned from maternity leave onto the list, and I choose to voice my dissent directly rather than comply in silence. I meet my superior's eyes and say: 'I can't write this list the way you're asking. If someone has to go, I need a real reason — not this one.'

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to directly write that colleague's name — the one who just returned from maternity leave — on the list, and I try to ask my boss whether another solution might be possible. I say quietly: "I understand the position you're in, but this colleague just came back and her performance is already recovering — I'd like to think a little longer about whether there's another way. Could you give me a bit of time?"

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to include the colleague who just returned from maternity leave on the layoff list, and I name the real nature of what my superior is asking directly, without offering any cooperative cover. I meet his eyes and say: 'The arrangement you're describing isn't something I can carry out — putting a B+ employee on record as failing to meet expectations while keeping someone who's been C two quarters running is a list I won't sign. If there's a second round of names, I'll accept being on it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose not to write my colleague — the one who just returned from maternity leave — onto the list, but I didn't confront my boss head-on either. Instead, I used delay and vagueness to buy myself time. I told him: 'I need to go back through the complete performance review records. I'll send you my thoughts before tonight — but I can't confirm this list right now.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to cancel the event outright, and instead propose a compromise: scale things down, redistribute responsibilities, ask the remaining members to each take on whatever they can manage, and reach out to the student affairs committee to explain the situation and ask for support. I speak up: 'I think we shouldn't rush to say cancel just yet — can you hear me out for a second? We scale the event down, everyone only handles one or two of the most critical things, and I'll go coordinate the rest with the student affairs committee. That way nobody's finals get wrecked, and the event doesn't just disappear into nothing.'

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to cancel outright, and instead propose a scaled-down plan: cut the high-difficulty elements, redistribute responsibilities, and reshape the event into a smaller but genuinely executable version. I speak up: "We're not canceling — but we do need to redesign this. The president's gone, so we turn it into something smaller. Each of you takes on one thing you can actually handle, I'll coordinate the whole thing, and we get through it together."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take over and re-break down the tasks, pushing the event forward with minimal manpower, while making each member's baseline responsibilities explicit. I speak up: 'Canceling isn't the optimal solution — we can't just let everything we've already put in go to zero. I'll coordinate what's left, but each of you has to claim at least one concrete task; I can't have it all fall on me alone. Tell me — which one of you has time tonight to check in with the sponsors and confirm their status?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take over the organizing work, refuse to agree to canceling the event, but proactively scale back the goals and focus on pushing forward the most critical tasks. I say to the members: "Canceling is something I can't agree to — but I'm not going to force any of you either. I'll personally follow up on the most urgent items myself: confirming the sponsors, getting the telescope loan request submitted, and sorting out the volunteer schedule. Whoever has time can pitch in; if you don't, that's fine. As for what shape the event ends up in — we'll see when we get there."

options.label: A
options.content: I didn't promise to keep it secret. Instead, I put the focus on just being there with her — trying to make sure she didn't feel abandoned, while leaving room to seek help later. I crouched down and said quietly: 'I'm not going to go around telling everyone — but I also can't promise to do nothing. Can you tell me what's actually been going on lately?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to make any promise on the spot. Instead, I hold space for his pain first, and then — gently but firmly — tell him we need to find an adult who can help. I crouch down and take his hand, and I say: 'I won't lie to you and tell you I promise, because I'm too scared of losing you — let's go find someone together, and I'll be right there with you the whole time.'

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse to promise secrecy, but don't leave immediately — choose to stay and explain my reasons to her first, then let her know I will be seeking help from an adult. I meet her eyes directly and say, in a steady voice: 'I can't promise you that — not because I don't care about you, but because this is something I can't carry alone, and you shouldn't have to carry it alone either. I'll go with you to find someone, but I'm not going to pretend I didn't see anything today.'

options.label: D
options.content: I didn't promise to keep the secret on the spot. Instead I chose to steady her emotions first — neither exposing her nor flatly refusing — while inside I had already decided I had to find an adult who could help her. I held her hand, my voice trembling, and said: "I'm not going to go around telling people — but I need you to help me figure this out together, how we can get you to a place where you don't have to do this anymore. I can't carry this by myself. I need someone to help both of us."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up directly and point out, calmly and clearly, the contradiction between the teacher's account and the primary sources — no hedging, no softening of language. I open my notebook and point to the page numbers I've recorded, and say: 'Sir, the way you just described this historical event is noticeably inconsistent with several primary documents I came across at the library last week. Page X of this book cites firsthand sources, and the conclusion it reaches is different from what you've said — I'd like to ask you about that.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and, framing it as 'seeking guidance' rather than 'making a correction,' gently raise the different account I came across in my outside reading, packaging the discrepancy as a question. I stand up and say quietly: 'Teacher, I was reading a book last week that cited some primary archival sources, and the way it described this period of history differs a little from what you've explained. I'm not entirely sure whether I've misunderstood something on my end — could you help clarify it for me?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly but without aggression, holding out my notebook as evidence. I stand and say: 'Teacher, last week I read an academic book that draws on primary archival sources, and the way it describes this issue is a bit different from what you've explained — I have notes on it right here. Would it be alright if I shared them?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly — respectful in tone, but clear in position — and actively bring the arguments and primary sources from my outside reading into the classroom discussion. I stand and say: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history in the library — it cites several primary-source documents and offers a different interpretation of the causal relationship here. I'd love to hear your thoughts on that reading.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to introduce my situation proactively, clearly, and with structure — no deflecting, no defending, just delivering a 'status report' on my own life the way a project manager would. I say: 'I do project planning at a nonprofit. The salary isn't high, but I manage every project from kickoff to close-out, and I manage it well. In my spare time I paint — last year I got into the local young artists' exhibition, which counts as a milestone. Renting is my choice for this stage; I haven't hit the point where buying makes sense. As for joining a big tech company — I evaluated it, and it doesn't fit my current execution roadmap.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to describe my current life in a way that is gentle and sincere without diminishing myself — mentioning the art exhibition in passing, steering the conversation toward something everyone can join in on, and not engaging directly with any "have you thought about switching jobs" suggestions. I smile and say: "Haha, I'm working on projects at a nonprofit right now — the pay is honestly nothing to write home about, but last year one of my paintings got selected for the local young artists' exhibition, so that was a nice surprise. You guys are living the dream, honestly — come on, let's drink to that!"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to respond with quiet candor — neither dodging nor getting defensive — and calmly describe my work and life as they are now, mentioning in passing that a painting of mine was selected for an exhibition. I give a small smile and say: 'I do project planning for a nonprofit — the salary's honestly not great, but I really enjoy it. Oh, and a painting of mine got into the local young artists' exhibition last year. As for the housing thing, there's no rush — but thank you both for thinking of me.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to state my situation directly and without defensiveness — neither playing along nor pushing back, letting the facts themselves answer all the well-meaning questions. I say: 'I do project planning for a nonprofit — the salary isn't high, I rent, and last year one of my paintings got into a youth art exhibition. That's my life right now, and I'm pretty happy with it. Thanks for the advice about buying a place; it's just not in my plans at the moment.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to confront her directly — to put the screenshot and the side group chat out in the open, no holding back and no dancing around it. I turn toward her, lower my voice, but speak with complete directness: "I saw that group chat. I saw what you said. Can you tell me — what exactly is going on?"

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to confront her directly, face to face — put the evidence I have out in the open, give her no room to keep brushing me off. I speak calmly: "I saw that screenshot. I saw the group chat too. You don't need to explain that it 'wasn't intentional' — I just want you to know that I know."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation, not here, not yet. I'll wait until I've calmly worked out what outcome I actually want before deciding on a next step. I turn off my phone screen, look up, give her one expressionless glance, then drop my eyes and open my textbook.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to push down the urge to confront her, getting through this moment with silence and avoidance, not letting her see that I already know about the screenshot. I lower my head, force the tears back, and say quietly, "I'm fine, probably just didn't sleep well," then look away and pretend to flip through my textbook.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to decline the money gently but clearly, to speak the true weight of what was lost, without letting it escalate into a direct confrontation. I push the stack of bills back toward him, my voice slightly unsteady but without tears, and say: 'Uncle, I can't take the money — that hard drive had six months of my work on it, and there's no replacing it. I just need a little time to get through this.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse the money and, in a trembling but clear voice, speak the true weight of what was lost — not to demand compensation, but to keep myself from disappearing into silence. I push the stack of bills back across the table, my voice shaking a little: 'Uncle, this isn't about replacing a hard drive. There were six months of my work on there — things that may be gone forever. I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse the money outright, calmly but clearly laying out the real loss, and giving them no opening to wrap this up with a 'kids overreact' shrug. I push the money back across the table, voice steady: 'This isn't enough. That hard drive held six months of my work — not a few songs. If you want to have a serious conversation about compensation, I'm willing to do that, but this number isn't something I can accept.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to make my position clear calmly but firmly — neither accepting the cash nor letting things explode into a scene. I push the wad of money back across the coffee table and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Uncle, I can't take this. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't about the cost of replacing a hard drive. Those files are gone, and that's that. I understand the kid didn't know any better, but I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to sign off confirming the data as valid, but rather than going directly over my colleague's head to report it to his supervisor, I give him one last chance to come forward on his own. I say to him: 'I can't sign off on this data. Tonight I'm going to reply to the system honestly and say there's a problem with the data that needs to be reviewed — you still have time. Before I submit that, go tell your supervisor yourself. I'll go with you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to report the data fraud to my supervisor and refuse to sign off confirming the data is valid — even though I am terrified of the interpersonal fallout that will follow. I tell my colleague: 'I know this puts you in a hard place, but I can't sign off on this. Tonight I'm going to tell our supervisor exactly what I know. You should probably go explain yourself first.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to report the data falsification to my superiors truthfully and to refuse to sign off on the data. I tell my colleague: 'I understand the position you're in, but I can't sign off on this data, and I can't hide this from our superiors. I'll be reporting it honestly tonight.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to report the data falsification to my supervisor and did not sign off on the confirmation email. I sent the project lead a brief message stating that there were anomalies in the data that needed to be re-verified before the presentation, and I attached a record of the specific issues I had found.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take him in without a second's hesitation, and instead of waiting passively for a knock, I move to get ahead of it. I pull him inside, drop my voice low, and say: 'Get in, lock the door — now tell me exactly how much you owe and how many of them there are. We figure this out right now.'

options.label: B
options.content: Without a moment's hesitation, I let my friend stay — while keeping my voice low to steady him and thinking through how to handle a knock at the door if it comes. I bring him inside and murmur: 'Don't make a sound, don't turn on the lights — once they're gone, we'll figure out the next step.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to let my friend stay, while keeping quiet and staying low-key in case anyone knocks. I turn off all the lights, signal him not to make a sound, and we sit with our backs against the wall beside the door, waiting for the footsteps to pass. If someone knocks, I'll say in a low voice 'no one's here' — or just not answer at all.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to let him stay, while quickly and quietly working out a plan together for if someone knocks. I pull him inside, lock the door behind us, and say in a hushed voice: 'Get into the back room, turn the lights off — if anyone knocks I'll say there's no one else here. But you have to tell me: do they know you know me?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene clearly, make a commitment, and guide her toward safer, formal channels rather than leaving her to face this alone. I take her hand and say: 'Coming to me tonight — that was the right thing to do. Don't go to that meeting alone tomorrow. Let me help you figure this out — we'll start by writing down everything that's happened, and then we'll go to HR together. I'll be right there with you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I decide to step in and accept the professional risk — to help Lin Xiaoyu move this forward in a structured way, rather than stopping at simply listening. I tell her: "The moment you came to me today, this stopped being just your problem alone. Don't go to that meeting tomorrow by yourself — I'll figure something out. First, let's get everything you've experienced down in writing: the dates, the locations, exactly what happened each time, laid out point by point. That's the foundation for whatever we do next."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to actively intervene, making it clear to Lin Xiaoyu that I will take action — not simply be a listener. I look her straight in the eye and say: 'You don't have to file the report yourself, but I will handle this. You don't need to go to that meeting tomorrow — I'll find a way to block it — and then we'll put the evidence together and go through the official channels.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to step in — but in a way that puts protecting her first, without pushing for a formal report just yet. I place my hand over hers, my voice shaking a little, but I speak anyway: 'You don't have to carry this alone tonight. That meeting tomorrow — I'm going to figure out a way to make sure it doesn't happen. But first, tell me everything he's done.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation gently but clearly, while acknowledging how much this partnership has meant to me — not denying the feeling, but deliberately closing the door on it. I say softly: 'These past few months working with you have been something really special to me too. But Saturday… I can't go. I think we both understand why. Thank you for telling me.'

options.label: B
options.content: Accept the invitation — but also decide to have a real conversation with my partner that same night, and face whatever has actually gone wrong between us. I say to them: "I'd love to go, I've been wanting to catch this talk too — but there's something I need to tell you first, about where things have been between us lately."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation directly — no deflecting, no pretending I didn't pick up on the subtext — while making my situation unambiguously clear. I say: "Thank you for telling me all of this. I genuinely think the collaboration these past few months has been something rare. But I'm in a relationship, and I can't accept this invitation — not because I don't want to go, but because I know it wouldn't just be a bookstore outing."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to gently decline the invitation, but without cutting off contact — giving myself time to face both this feeling and my existing relationship. I tell him: "I actually have something on this Saturday, so I probably can't make it — but that feeling of being understood that you mentioned, I treasure that too. It's just that right now I need to think some things through first."

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to refuse the service and commit to finishing the thesis on my own, without any compromise. I shook my head and pushed the phone back, saying: "I don't need this — I'll write it myself. Don't send me the contact info."

options.label: B
options.content: Decline outright — no hedging, no need for a lengthy explanation. I push the phone back and say, calmly: 'It's fine, I can finish it myself. That kind of thing carries way too much risk — it's not worth it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to gently decline my roommate's suggestion, but in a considerate way that doesn't make him feel judged. I smiled and pushed the phone back toward him, saying: 'Thanks for thinking of me — honestly though, my progress is still okay, so I'll give it a shot on my own first. If I really hit a wall, I'll say something. Just hold onto that contact info for me, yeah?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to politely decline my roommate's suggestion, not use the service, and continue pushing forward with my thesis according to the original plan. I tell my roommate: "I took a look at it, and I think I'll pass — my progress is still fine, I should be able to finish it myself, and if something goes wrong with that kind of thing it's just not a situation you can easily get out of."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay open without committing, and to secure a window of time before the deadline to gather information and think it through carefully. I tell my senior: 'I need to think seriously about what you've said — this isn't a small thing. Before Tuesday, I'll give you a clear answer. But I can't say yes or no on the spot right now. Can you understand that?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to express enthusiasm and lean toward accepting, while carving out a small buffer rather than committing on the spot. I lean forward, meet my senior's eyes directly, and say: 'I don't need to wait until Tuesday — this direction feels right to me, and everything you said landed. Give me 48 hours. Not to hesitate, but to get my own side of things sorted out — and then we sit down and talk through the details for real.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to express genuine interest while making it clear that I needed to verify the information before giving any answer, keeping the decision in my own hands. I told the senior: "I listened carefully to everything you said, and I'm going to take this seriously. But before I give you an answer, I need to see the original letter of intent, and whatever user data the product has right now — if there is any. I'll give you a definitive reply before Tuesday."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to express conditional interest, but explicitly refuse to be pushed into a position by time pressure when I don't have enough information. I say directly: 'I'm genuinely interested in taking a serious look at the project — but I need to see proof that the angel-round funding has closed, a complete list of the core team, and your answer to why the CTO has to be me specifically. I'll give you my answer by Tuesday, but that answer is contingent on you getting me that information by tomorrow.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school, and I accept that this may bring the relationship to an end. I text her back, tell her I'll be staying in my own program, and am honest with her: I love her, but I can't sustain what we have by rewriting the direction of my own life.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to accept the offer from the northern university, and I give a clear answer tonight — no more stalling, no more vague non-responses. I send a voice message, my voice a little hoarse: 'I've made up my mind. I'm coming to you. We do this together. We'll find a new path over there — but you don't carry this alone anymore.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to accept my own school's offer and not change course just to hold the relationship together. I write back to her: 'I've thought about it seriously — I'm staying at my school. If you're willing, let's talk through what the future looks like for us; if you're not, I'll accept that too.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to stay at my current school, and tell her directly — no evasion, no sugarcoating. My reply to her tonight: 'I listened to every word you said. Staying here is the only honest choice I can make. If I went north carrying regret, that wouldn't be fair to you either. I want to talk about whether we can find another way forward — but I can't give you an answer I don't believe myself.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene — I don't return the bottle, I walk toward the bed curtain and speak, while preparing to reach out for outside help. I grip the pill bottle in my hand, walk to his bedside, and say quietly: 'I'm not leaving — I just don't want to. I'm holding onto this bottle for now — how are you doing right now?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to return the pill bottle. Using his question as an opening, I speak softly in response, trying to give him a reason to want to talk. I keep the bottle in my hand, don't kick it back, turn around, and do my best to keep my voice steady: 'I'm not going out tonight… I was hoping I could sit with you for a while. Would that be okay?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to intervene directly — I don't return the pill bottle, and I immediately contact the resident advisor or call a mental health crisis line. I don't let this get handled internally within the dorm room. I keep the bottle in my hand, stand up, and walk to the curtain around his bed. In a steady voice, I say: 'I'm not giving this back to you. You need someone here right now — I'm going to contact the RA. If you don't want me to, tell me now.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to return the bottle. I stay where I am and speak first, while also deciding I need to contact the resident advisor or a mental health crisis line. I close my hand around the pill bottle — I don't kick it back — and I turn to face his bed curtain and say: 'I'm not going anywhere tonight, because I'm right here. Over there — could you pull the curtain open a little? I want to see you.' At the same time, I quietly pick up my phone, ready to contact the resident advisor.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to raise the alternative plan on the spot — to open things up rather than quietly go along. I turn my screen toward the group and say: 'Actually, yes — I've put together a different approach over here. Give it two minutes; if everyone feels it's not worth discussing, we'll get straight back to dividing up tasks — but I want this plan to be seen at least once.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal on the spot — stating the risk points plainly, not pushing for any particular outcome, just making sure the information got through in full. I said: 'I do have a few thoughts — I put together an alternative proposal, mainly with an eye toward the long-term scaling issues. The document's right here; everyone can take two minutes to look it over, and we can decide after that — no rush.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to raise an alternative plan on the spot, presenting the analysis I've already prepared, without shying away from a direct clash with the current consensus. I turn toward the screen, pull up the file, and say: 'Actually, I do have something to add. I've put together an alternative path — the core issue is the maintenance cost of the current plan once complexity scales up. The data is right here. Spend five minutes looking at it before we divide up the work; that's not going to cost more time than rework will.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal in this moment, presenting it in a structured, evidence-based way rather than silently accepting the consensus. I turned my screen toward the room and said: 'There are a few other ideas I'd like to share — I've put together a specific proposal with the risk points laid out. I need three minutes. I think saying it now costs less than waiting until something goes wrong later.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan immediately, without hesitating to press for too many details. I pull out my phone and open the transfer screen, and I tell him: 'I'm sending it to you now — go pay the deposit. You don't need to write an IOU. Save him first.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lend the money, but conditionally — first I do a quick reality check by confirming the situation with the medical staff. I turn to the young man and say: 'Stay right there — I'm going to check with the nurses' station. Once I've confirmed it, I'll go get the money.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to lend him the three thousand yuan, but I don't want to talk more than necessary — I want to get it done as fast as possible and go. I hold out my phone so he can see the balance, and say quietly: 'I'll transfer it to you. Give me your payment code. Doesn't matter whether you write an IOU or not.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to lend the money, but I give him an alternative direction rather than walking away in silence. I crouch down and gently move his hand from my pant leg, and say calmly: 'I can't lend you anything — but go to the nurses' station right now, tell them the family has no money. The hospital has an emergency payment assistance process. Go ask them. Don't stay here on your knees.'

options.label: A
