options.content: I choose to be upfront about the situation at home first, while proactively offering a compromise — trying not to completely let either side down. I speak up: 'Director, there's something I need to tell you — my dad has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend, and I've already promised my family I'd go with him. I know this is a critical moment, so here's what I'm thinking: I can work remotely from home on Saturday and push forward on the market benchmarking and the main body of the proposal. If I can get back Sunday morning, we could go through the final draft together Sunday afternoon or evening — does that work for you?'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose partial compromise while holding the line: make it clear to the director that the weekend trip home is not being canceled, but offer to put in extra hours tonight to push the project forward, provide remote support on the critical sections over the weekend, and join a video call Sunday evening for the final-draft review. Say directly: 'I have a family commitment this weekend — it was locked in three days ago and I can't cancel it. I can stay late tonight and I'm available remotely over the weekend, but coming into the office isn't something I can do. Tell me which parts are the biggest bottlenecks and let's work out the priorities together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to agree immediately, but also not to refuse outright — instead I try to propose a middle-ground arrangement, asking whether I could handle part of the work remotely or negotiate with the director to have certain modules shared with a colleague. I say: 'Director, I do have something I need to take care of at home this weekend — my father has a follow-up appointment, and I promised I'd go with him. I wanted to ask: would it be possible for me to pull together the market benchmarking section tonight, work remotely on Saturday, and then come into the office Sunday to go over the final draft? Or is there any chance a colleague could take on the slide redesign portion?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honestly explain the situation at home while proposing a middle-ground arrangement — such as handling part of the work remotely — rather than simply agreeing or outright refusing. I keep my eyes slightly down, voice low but as steady as I can manage: 'Director, my father has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend — I've already promised my family I'd go back to be with him. Could we do it this way: I push as far as I can tonight and before I leave tomorrow morning, I stay reachable remotely on the way there, and for the final draft Sunday night I'll do my best to either get back in time or go through it with you online. Would that work?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to directly turn down my ex's request to get back together, and I decide to tell my current partner honestly about how this meeting went. I say to my ex: 'I hear what you're saying. That time was real for me too — but the problems we couldn't solve back then are still there now. I have no way to verify that you've changed, and I have my own life. I'm not going to give you a reason to keep waiting — that's not fair to you, and it's not fair to the relationship I'm in.' When I get home, I'll bring it up with my current partner myself and tell him everything that happened today.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to turn down my ex's request to get back together, but my response is vague and riddled with self-attacking apologies — and I decide to hide the details of this meeting from my current boyfriend. I say to my ex: "We can't go back to what we were. You deserve better. I'm sorry for making you wait so long" — then I lower my head, unable to meet his eyes, while inside I've already started running through how I'm going to explain to my current boyfriend why I'm coming home late tonight.

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to turn my ex down — gently but without ambiguity — and decide to tell my current partner about the encounter that same day. To my ex, I say: 'I never pretended during those two years, but I'm in a new relationship now, and I can't give you a reason to keep waiting — that wouldn't be fair to you, and it wouldn't be fair to him. I genuinely hope you find happiness, but there's no going back for us.' Then, when I get home, I bring it up myself and tell my current partner everything that happened today.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to respond to my ex honestly about my uncertainty, and at the same time I decide to tell my current partner, that same day, everything about this meeting. I say to my ex: 'I can't tell you I've completely moved on — but I'm with someone now, and I'm not going to let the fact that I still feel something destroy what we have. Tonight I'm going to tell him that we met.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to call the authorities and get help immediately, refuse the bribe, and leave the handbag in place as evidence. I call 120, give my location and describe the injured person's condition; then I call 110, recount the details of the incident and the direction the driver fled, and inform them that there is a bag of cash at the scene left by the driver, which I have not touched.

options.label: B
options.content: I immediately dial 120 and then 110, leave the handbag untouched in a visible spot on the side of the road, and move toward the injured person to offer basic reassurance. I set the bag where the streetlight can reach it, then crouch down at the edge of the ditch, waiting for the call to connect, and say to him: 'I'm here. I've called an ambulance. Hold on.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to immediately call 120 and 110, leave the bag exactly as it is by the roadside, and sprint on foot toward the injured person to conduct an emergency assessment. Once 120 connects, I say: 'It's 1:47 in the morning, two-lane road on the outskirts of the city — an electric-bike rider has been struck by a vehicle. The victim is conscious but bleeding and needs emergency care. I'm on the scene right now.' I then call 110 to report the vehicle involved and the direction of the driver's escape.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to immediately call 120 for an ambulance and 110 for the police, and I leave the bag of money exactly where it is — I don't take it with me. Trembling, I dial 120 first, my voice breaking as I give the location and describe the injuries, then I call 110, set the bag down by the roadside, walk over to the rider, crouch beside him, and press my hands against his wound to hold it while I wait for help to arrive.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to prepare myself before my roommate gets back, and to tell him face to face — privately, gently, but directly — that I've discovered the plagiarism, giving him the chance to rewrite before submission, rather than reporting him or staying silent. I close the browser tab, ease his laptop screen back to where it was, take a deep breath, and wait for him to walk in. When he does, I speak: 'There's something I need to talk to you about — sit down for a second. It's about the report. I came across something by accident, and I think we need to talk before it gets submitted.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to confront him directly, face to face — demanding that he rewrite the plagiarized sections before submission, no dancing around it, no going to the professor behind his back, giving him one chance to handle it himself, but leaving absolutely no room for ambiguity in my tone. The moment he walks through the door, I speak up: 'I just looked at your document, and there are several passages that are word for word identical to articles on that Q&A site — I searched it myself. You heard what the professor said about the rules. You need to rewrite it yourself before we hand this in the day after tomorrow, because if you don't, I can't submit this thing together with you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to confront him directly — to tell my roommate what I found and demand that he deal with it before submission, but I have no intention of contacting the professor. Before the footsteps reach the door, I turn his laptop screen back to where it was, take a deep breath, and wait for him to push the door open. Then I say: 'I accidentally saw what was on your screen just now — I searched a few of the passages, you know what I mean. It's due the day after tomorrow, so you need to figure out what you're going to do. I'm not carrying this for you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell him face-to-face, privately, when he gets back — giving him the chance to rewrite it before submission — rather than staying silent or going straight to the professor. When he pushes the door open, I look up and say: 'I accidentally saw your document just now, and I searched a few passages — they're a perfect match with an article on Zhihu. The deadline is the day after tomorrow; you still have time to fix it, and I can help you.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to break the deadlock with concrete evidence rather than suffer in silence or escalate into confrontation. I raise my phone screen as high as I can, facing both the livestream camera and the people around me, and say — loudly, but as steadily as I can manage: 'Please, everyone, look carefully — this is the work group chat I was scrolling through on my phone. I was not photographing anyone. This is a misunderstanding, and I am willing to let anyone check my phone's photo album.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to force myself to stay calm — no running, no fighting back hard — and in the most direct way possible I hold up my phone screen showing the work group chat, so everyone in the carriage and the livestream camera can see the evidence. I say loudly: 'Look carefully — what's on my phone is the work group chat. Screen facing up. Everyone can see it. I didn't photograph anyone.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose restraint and composure — clearing my name in public, neither fleeing nor fighting back, waiting for the train to reach the next stop so I can seek a rational resolution. I release my collar from the grip, raise both hands so the people around me can clearly see my phone screen, and say in as steady a voice as I can manage: 'I was scrolling through my work group chat — you can all see for yourselves. I haven't photographed anyone. Please wait until the next stop and we can find a staff member together.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to go on the offensive — counter with evidence directly, no explanations, no apologies, no showing weakness. I snapped my phone screen around toward the live-stream camera, my voice calm but unmistakably clear: 'You're live-streaming? Good. Let everyone get a good look at this screen — this is the work group chat, the timestamp is right there, my phone was never pointed at you.' Then I pulled my collar free from their grip and held their gaze.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up proactively, with a gentle and non-judgmental tone, while also responding to my colleague's private message without overstepping. First, I reply to my colleague: "I have a few thoughts too — want to talk it over together?" Then I send the younger colleague a private message: "Your project really resonated with me — I did something similar once, and there's one small detail I'd love to share with you. It might not even apply, just take it as a reference — would you be up for a quick chat when you have a moment?"

options.label: B
options.content: I've decided to reach out directly via private message, pointing out the specific structural risk points in the process — no emotional preamble, no 'just for your reference' disclaimer tacked on at the end. And I'll reply to my colleague: 'I'll say it directly. You don't need to get involved.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to use my colleague's private message as the entry point — passing along a word of caution from experience indirectly rather than directly, so it doesn't come across as a forceful intervention through a public comment. My reply to my colleague: "I saw it too, and your instinct might be right — if you're open to it, we could talk. I once ran a project with a very similar structure, and there are a few wrong turns that might be worth mentioning."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to use my colleague's private message as the entry point for passing along the information, rather than speaking up directly in the comments. I reply to my colleague: 'I took a look — there are a few specific issues with the process. Do you want me to write them out? You can decide what to do with them.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion — palliative care, letting go of that 20% chance. I look at the doctor, my voice a little hoarse, and say: 'We'll go with comfort care. Let him go with some dignity.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honor the answer Dad has already given with his eyes — to support palliative care, and not to force him through that suffering for the sake of a 20% chance. I turn to the doctor and say: 'We're choosing palliative care. Having him with us — really with us — matters more than anything else.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose palliative care, honor the exhaustion and wishes my father has expressed, and refuse to let a low-probability course of chemotherapy turn his remaining time into suffering. I speak to the doctor: 'Start the comfort-care protocol—let him spend whatever time he has left in peace.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honor Father's exhaustion and his wishes, and decide on palliative care—so that he can spend whatever time remains with dignity. I say quietly to the doctor: 'We'll go with palliative care. Just keep him comfortable, and let him stay with us.' When I finish speaking, I take Father's hand in mine.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to say what I actually think — gently but clearly, without dodging and without accusation — say it all the way through, and leave the ball in his court. I set down the PPT, looked up, and met his eyes: 'I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're stupid. It's precisely because you're my closest friend that I have to tell you — this structure, I've seen it before. It's called a pyramid scheme. I don't want your money. I just don't want to be in a position, three months from now when you call me, where I never said this.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep trying to dissuade him — I'll lay out the factual hallmarks of a pyramid scheme structure directly, decline the investment invitation, and not dignify the 'jealousy' accusation with a response. I say: 'I'm not putting money in, and it's not because I'm jealous. This model of yours — recruiting downlines, tiered profit-sharing, returns that depend on the people below you rather than on any actual product — that's the textbook structure of a pyramid scheme. There's nothing I can do about the eight hundred thousand that's already gone, but don't ask me to hand over this hundred and fifty thousand.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to confront him directly, without flinching from the conflict, even at the risk of blowing up the friendship entirely. I slide the PPT back across the table and speak — calm, but without any give in my voice: 'You want to call it jealousy, fine, think whatever you like. But I have to say this clearly today — this is a pyramid scheme, not a business. The eight hundred thousand you've already put in, I don't know if you'll ever see it again. But you're asking me to get in with you, and that's something I can't do. Won't do.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up directly and urge him to stop — without backing down because of his emotional reaction or the risk to our friendship — using facts and logic as my tools, refusing to trade silence for a surface-level peace. I flip the PowerPoint back to the first page, look him in the eye, and say: 'I'm not jealous of you. I've just seen too many people caught up in this exact structure and watched how it ends. Your income right now comes from the people you recruit, not from any product or service — that's the legal definition of a pyramid scheme, not my opinion, the law's definition. Eight hundred thousand is already gone, but you're still pulling more people in — that's what I'm actually worried about.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to confront the challenge head-on and use data and logic to establish the ground rules. I meet his gaze and say: 'The delay on that module last quarter was due to a requirements change — not a technical issue — and it's documented clearly in the review records. As for this quarter's schedule, I'll break it down item by item in the meeting. If there are questions, we let the data do the talking.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on with an open stance, and to actively reframe the senior team member's provocation as a topic for the whole group to work through together—establishing my rhythm as team lead rather than shutting him down. I smile, stand up straight, and speak at a volume that's calm but carries to every corner of the room: "Good question—that module is actually exactly what I want us to dig into today. Old Li, you've got the deepest experience on this one. When we meet in a bit, you go first, and we'll all figure out together how to move it forward."

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to meet the challenge head-on, acknowledge the value of the senior team member's experience, and open with a posture of collaboration rather than authority. I said: 'Thanks for asking that straight out — it's actually exactly what I wanted to go over with everyone today. That module from last quarter: I need someone like you, with real experience on it, to walk me through where it got stuck. Let's get in a room — you go first.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on and establish authority through data and framework, rather than deflecting or firing back emotionally. I hold his gaze, voice steady: 'Good question. I have the specifics on the unfinished module and the full quarterly schedule right here — let's go through them now. Everyone please turn to item one on the agenda.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold off on full cooperation and seek legal assistance first — I will not provide any information about my colleagues or any internal materials without a lawyer present. I speak up and say: 'I understand the importance of this investigation, and I am willing to cooperate — but before I provide any specific information, I need to consult with legal counsel first. Please allow me that right.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while seeking legal support, and refrain from making statements about others when the information isn't clear. Say aloud: 'I need to consult legal counsel first — I'll give you a response within 24 hours. As for the circumstances of my own signature, I can clarify that right now: at the time, I was simply asked by my superior to review a budget document. I was not involved in any decision-making.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while immediately seeking legal protection, and refuse to provide any substantive information before a lawyer is present. Say aloud: 'I need to contact my lawyer first — until then, I'm not in a position to make any statement about the specifics. This isn't a refusal to cooperate; it's my basic right.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to defer full cooperation and request legal assistance before giving any formal response; do not provide any information about team members on the spot. Say aloud: 'I need my lawyer present before I can formally answer these questions — I'll be in touch with you within 24 hours. But what I can tell you right now is that I only looked over that document; I was never involved in any funding decisions.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to offer conditional support, but I require my kid to sit through a structured risk-breakdown conversation right now, rather than simply handing over an emotional endorsement. I push the report booklet back across the table and say: 'You say seven years — I believe you. But let's go through this field's employment data, your competitive edge, and your exit path in the worst-case scenario, one by one. If you can answer those, I'll support you filling it in.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to support my child in pursuing this path — I voice my worries, but I don't use them to invalidate their choice. I say: 'I hear you. You've been preparing for so long, and I have no reason to say no. I have my fears, but those are my homework to do — not a chain around your neck. Let's figure out together how to make this path as solid as it can be.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold off on any objection — to listen and learn more first, without immediately declaring support or disapproval. I say: 'Show me that portfolio, and walk me through it from the beginning — tell me everything you've been working on all these years.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to object outright, and instead ask that both of us first lay this path out in full and see it clearly before any decision is made. I say: 'Leave that booklet here with me. I'll read through it carefully tonight. We'll talk again tomorrow — I have questions I want to ask you.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to go along with the lie, but I step up and take control of the narrative myself — using language that is vague yet truthful, leaving my teammate some breathing room, while quickly proposing a no-model fallback plan for our presentation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say: 'Ms. Chen, the model had an accident on the way to school and couldn't make it here — that's our group's fault. options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the missing model — I won't go along with the lie, but I also won't actively assign blame to anyone; I simply state the facts and ask to continue with a verbal presentation instead. I say quietly to the homeroom teacher: 'The model was lost on the way to school and didn't make it here — would it be alright if we just walked through the content verbally?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the model being lost, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately pivot to figuring out how to complete the presentation without it. Looking the homeroom teacher in the eye: 'The model was lost on the way to school — he left it on the bus. Without the model, we'll complete the presentation using a verbal explanation and the board. I'll take the lead.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell a softened version of the truth: acknowledge that the model wasn't brought, without actively naming who's responsible, while indicating that the group can complete the presentation through a verbal walkthrough. I say to the homeroom teacher: 'Ms. [Teacher], the model wasn't able to make it today — something came up — but we're fully prepared to walk through everything verbally. The content is all there. Please let us go up.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to soften the moment gently — neither flatly refusing the invitation nor immediately giving up my observation — trying to buy myself a little time. I stay crouched where I am, look up, and give him a small smile: "Go ahead and warm up without me — I just need two more minutes. This bug is seriously weird, I've never seen anything like it — I'll be right there!"

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation outright and stay to keep observing the beetle and the purple flower, without offering much explanation. I glanced up at him briefly and said, calmly: 'I'm not coming — I'm watching a beetle I've never seen before. You guys go ahead.' Then I looked back down and continued observing, starting to document the insect's physical features with my phone or a twig I found nearby.

options.label: C
options.content: He politely declines the invitation, tells the other kid to head back first, and stays to look a little longer before deciding anything — neither diving into research nor leaving right away. "You guys go ahead, I'll come check it out in a bit."

options.label: D
options.content: I decline the invitation and stay behind to keep observing the insect and the little purple flower, trying to document every detail with my phone or the notebook I have on me. I tell the kid: "Go on ahead without me — there's something here I haven't finished looking at. I've never seen this bug before in my life, and I need to figure out what it is first."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lead with pragmatic cooperation while carving out room to embed buffer space in the execution details, rather than directly challenging the overall direction. I open the proposal and say, calmly: 'I'm on board with moving forward — I'll keep a close eye on the new process. There are a few specific risk points in the transition period I want to walk you through right now. It's not about stalling; I just don't want problems cropping up later and throwing off your timeline — can I run you through them?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to engage by asking questions — approaching this with curiosity rather than direct confrontation or silent compliance — in order to open up space for real dialogue within the execution framework. I say: 'My objection isn't that we can't change things — but I want to ask you first: what specific observations about the existing process is this proposal based on? Because some of those steps were designed the way they were to solve particular problems. If you know which problems, we can figure out together whether the new process actually covers them.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to challenge the logical gaps in the proposal directly, on the spot — not attacking his authority as such, but forcing him to back it up with data. I flip the proposal open, point to the timeline, and say: 'I do have an objection — let's start with one: how were the transition-period timelines determined? If there's no actual measurement based on the existing process, I can't guarantee the rollout won't run into problems, and you won't get the results you're after either.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to step up and engage directly, raising specific objections on the spot and working to embed my own judgment and buffer space within the execution framework — rather than offering surface compliance. I flip the proposal open, press my index finger against one of the timeline milestones, and say: 'I have an issue to raise here — not a question of whether it can be changed, but if this milestone goes the way you're describing, I can tell you right now exactly what gaps will open up in the transition period. Let's hear how you want to handle that.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to accept the camping invitation and respond with surface-level enthusiasm, but leave myself a vague out — while quietly working to make sure the four of us never actually go. I write back: "That sounds amazing! The guesthouse must have such a beautiful view — let me just check my schedule on this end, you guys lock in the details first, and I'll get back to you as soon as I can!"

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a plausible excuse, removing myself from the situation entirely — neither breaking the promise I made to him nor showing up to play a supporting role in this deception. Reply: 'There's a project at work that's hit a critical point right now, so I'm afraid I can't get away this weekend — you all go ahead and have fun, and we'll find another time to get together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to politely decline the camping invitation, deflecting with a specific but not over-explained work excuse, while keeping a normal, friendly tone — no cracks, no unnecessary distance. My reply: 'Oh that sounds absolutely gorgeous! I've got a project milestone I need to wrap up this weekend and I'm worried I won't be able to get away last-minute — you all go and have fun, and make sure to include me next time!'

options.label: D
options.content: She chooses to gently decline the camping invitation, using a vague but sincere excuse to bow out of the weekend — not directly revealing the secret, but also not showing up and pretending everything is fine. She replies: 'I've been feeling a bit off lately, I probably can't make it this weekend — you all go and have fun, and let's plan something just the two of us another time!'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to take the fall for my friend temporarily — but I didn't fully confess. Instead I kept things vague to buy some breathing room. I lowered my head and said to the security guard: 'It was me… I don't know how it ended up in there, I didn't mean to…' My voice was barely above a whisper, my hands were shaking, and I couldn't stop the corners of my eyes from going red.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to state the facts truthfully, not to take the blame for my friend, but in a calm and measured tone — not actively accusing anyone, just making it clear that I didn't put the toy in the bag. I say to the security guard: 'I didn't put this toy in there. I don't know how it got into my bag. You can check the security footage.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell a partial truth — neither taking the blame nor directly giving my friend up — using a vague but honest statement to buy time. I say to the security guard: 'This toy wasn't put in there by me. I genuinely don't know how it ended up in my bag. We just came down from the second floor…'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the truth, refuse to take the fall for my friend, and state the facts directly to the security guard. I speak calmly: 'I didn't put that toy in there — he did. I don't know when he put it in, and I didn't take anything.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea concisely, but politely decline the invitation to join the speech competition. I'll say: 'Teacher, I can walk through this method, but I'd rather not join the speech competition — I don't think it's necessary.' Then I'll lay out the logic of the seesaw weighing method in the most straightforward way I can, without dragging it out, and without caring about the skeptical looks.

options.label: B
options.content: She chooses to stand up and share her idea in front of the class, giving a vague, positive response to the suggestion about the speech competition rather than refusing outright. She rises slowly, her voice a little soft but pushing herself to speak clearly: "It's just… using the principle of a seesaw — you put the elephant on one side, then add people one by one to the other side, and by counting the people you can figure out how much the elephant weighs…" When she finishes, she gives the teacher a small nod: "The competition… I — I'll think about it."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to quietly decline standing up to explain, and respond to the suggestion about the speech competition with a vague, evasive shake of my head. I lower my gaze and say in a very small voice: "I… I can't really explain it well — teacher, you go ahead and tell them."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to respond in a vague, retreating way — neither outright refusing the teacher nor actually standing up to perform — trying to shrink myself back into the crowd. I keep my head down and say in a very small voice: "Teacher… what I said just now wasn't very clear, it probably wasn't that good…" and give a small shake of my head, without standing up.

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to refuse copying and return the answer sheet to where it was — neither reporting it nor using it. Say to Xin: 'We can't copy from this — put it back. Getting caught would be way worse than just bombing the test. You head out first, I'll be there once I finish sweeping up.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request and decide to hand the answer sheet over to the teacher or return it to where it belongs — trading honesty for genuine peace of mind. I fold the paper and clutch it in my hand, then shake my head at Xiaoyu and say quietly: 'We can't use this — it belongs to the class rep, and I'm going to give it to the teacher. Don't worry, I won't say you saw it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse to use the answer sheet, and I won't let my friend copy it either — I'll return the paper or put it back where it was, and I won't take part in this gamble on luck. I tell my friend: 'I'm not using this, and you shouldn't copy it either — have you actually calculated what it costs if we get caught? I'm putting it back. What you do after that is your call.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend and either hand the paper to the teacher or put it back where it came from. I whisper to her, "We can't use this — we'll get caught," and then, while she's not paying attention, I slip the paper back into the crack in the desk or hand it to the teacher, pretending I just happened to find it by accident.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lay it all out in the open — to ask my partner to honestly explain what the report says, and to refuse to play along with his attempts to change the subject. I set the envelope flat on the table, look at him, and say: 'I saw it. It's the results of a medical check-up. You can tell me now — what does it say.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to gently but directly let him know that I've seen the report — to invite him to face it together, rather than follow his lead and sidestep it. I quietly set the envelope down on the table between us and say softly: 'I saw it. It's okay. Tell me. We'll get through it together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to make it clear that I've seen the report and demand that my partner be honest with me — I can't pretend nothing happened. I hold the envelope up, my voice a little unsteady but without backing down: "I saw it. Can you tell me — what does it mean?"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to bring it out into the open directly — rather than going along with her and putting the envelope back, I speak up, gently but firmly. I set the envelope down softly on the table, look at her, and say: 'I saw it. Tell me what's going on — whatever it is, we'll face it together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to make the choice either of them is demanding, and in a calm, direct way I hand the decision back to the adults. I speak up: 'This is between the two of you — it's not my business. I'm not going anywhere and I'm not staying for either of you. You decide.' Then I turn around, walk back to my room, and close the door.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to take either side, and instead try to break this impossible either-or through tears and pleading. I lunge forward and wrap my arms around the legs of the person nearest to me, sobbing as I cry out: "Please stop fighting — I'm not going anywhere, don't either of you leave —" The tears won't stop falling, and my voice is so choked I can barely get the words out.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay, expressing through the rigid stillness of my body a despair that cannot make any choice, while instinctively leaning toward the 'stay' side. I drew my shoulders in tight, my feet didn't move, and in a voice barely above a whisper I said: "I'm not leaving… I'm not going anywhere…" Then I crouched down, covered my face, and cried out loud.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse the either-or, to stay where I am without going with anyone, and without declaring support for either side. I look down at the shards of porcelain on the floor and say: "I'm not going anywhere. Whatever this is between you two, you can talk it out — but I'm not choosing."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold back and not expose everything in this moment, but I decide that tonight I will find a quiet moment alone with him to talk through this face to face — and to stop him from giving the speech at the commendation ceremony and from registering for the competition. I take the milk from him, set down what I'm holding, and say calmly: 'Let's put the speech aside for now. There's something important we need to talk about tonight — come find me in the study when you're done eating.'

options.label: B
options.content: He chooses to hold his emotions in check for the moment—letting the child set down the milk and leave first—then calls him back alone afterward and, calmly but directly, lays that note out on the table. He doesn't pick up the thread of the boy's cheerful talk; he waits until the glass is set down, then says: 'Sit down. There's something I need to talk to you about.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to confront him directly, right here and now——no dancing around it. I put the note on the table and make it clear that the competition and the awards ceremony have to stop. I set down the book in my hands, look him in the eyes, and say: 'Sit down. There's something I need to talk to you about. I found a note in your backpack——it's about your math midterm.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to go along with what he's saying for now — no exposing him on the spot — but I use one sentence to keep him here, laying the groundwork for a private conversation later tonight. I take the milk from him, lower my head and drink a sip, then look up at him and say: 'The speech can wait — sit down for a second. Dad has something he wants to talk to you about, just the two of us.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and go alone — I fold it shut quickly and press it under my textbook, smile at my friend and say 'It's nothing, just some random scribbling,' and after school I head to the big banyan tree on the field by myself.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and avoid showing up — just push the chaos down for now. I shoved the note into my textbook and smiled at my best friend: 'It's nothing — someone from the next class asking to borrow my notes.' After school, I made up an excuse and never went to the field.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note and refuse to let my friend see it; at the same time, I'm deeply resistant to the idea of showing up at all, and I'm leaning toward not going. I flip my textbook over, press the note underneath it, look up, and give a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking about homework. Nothing interesting.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note — I shield it with my body, fold it quickly, and stuff it back into my textbook, then brush off my best friend with a lie. As for whether to show up after school, I haven't decided yet, but I'm leaning toward not going, or putting off the decision until the very last second. I press my textbook down over the note and look up at her with a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking to borrow my notes.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to accept the career change opportunity, but I ask for a little time to arrange a financial buffer rather than signing immediately. I tell my friend: 'I'm in — but give me two weeks. I need to wrap things up properly on this end. Not because I'm hesitating, but because I want to show up giving everything I've got.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to decline this opportunity to change careers, turning down my friend's invitation on the grounds of protecting what I've built. I tell my friend: 'I understand your enthusiasm, and I do trust your instincts — but I can't walk away from my shop and my clients. This path isn't the right one for me. Find someone who's a better fit, and I'll be right here cheering you on.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to give an answer immediately, and instead ask for a full breakdown of the terms, the equity structure, and the exit mechanism — keeping the decision firmly in my own hands. I tell my friend: 'I need to see the complete terms and the equity agreement. Give me three days, and I'll give you an answer that's grounded in evidence — not one that was rushed out of me.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation, letting the exhilaration drown out my financial reservations, and I give him a definitive yes on the spot. I look at the screen, grinning, and say: 'Do you even need to ask? I've been waiting for this chance. When do we start — I can be there tomorrow.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — tone steady and clear, giving him no emotional leverage, letting no one around me see a single flicker of panic. I push the business card back toward him, voice pitched just right, loud enough for the people nearby to hear: 'Thanks, I'm good. The work I do now — I use my brain.' Then I turn away and go back to my orange juice.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly, giving him no emotional reaction to exploit, letting the whole performance collapse in plain sight with nothing but calm. I glanced down at the card, then looked up, my voice as level as if I were stating a simple fact: 'No need. For the assistant position, I'd suggest finding someone who doesn't know how you got to where you are — it'll save you a lot of headaches.' Then I picked up my orange juice and walked away.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — no taking the card, no explanation, no anger — cutting off this performance with silence and by walking away. I glanced down at the card for a moment, didn't reach for it, then raised my eyes and said calmly: 'No, thank you,' set my orange juice glass on the nearest table, and turned and walked toward the exit.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse him calmly, in front of everyone — not taking the card, not tearing into him directly, but making sure every person in earshot hears the 'no' clearly. I step back half a pace, leaving his hand suspended in the air, and say in a voice that is neither raised nor lowered: 'I appreciate the thought, but I'm very happy where I am right now — no need for a change.' Then I pick up my orange juice and walk toward my friend.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly — respectful in tone, but clear in position — and actively bring the arguments and primary sources from my outside reading into the classroom discussion. I stand and say: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history in the library — it cites several primary-source documents and offers a different interpretation of the causal relationship here. I'd love to hear your thoughts on that reading.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and directly raise my objection using the documentary evidence in my notebook, keeping my tone as measured as possible while not sidestepping the core contradiction. I say: 'Professor, last week I read an academic work that draws on multiple primary-source archives, and it presents a different account of the causal relationships here — there are a few key historical facts that differ quite significantly from what you've described. Would it be all right if I went over them?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up and express my disagreement, but I frame it as offering supplementary information rather than a direct refutation, leaving the teacher as much room as possible. I rise, open my notebook, and say: 'Professor, I came across an academic work last week that draws on multiple primary sources, and it presents this causal relationship somewhat differently. I'd like to ask — is there something about the gap between that account and the framework you've described that I'm simply not understanding correctly?'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to defer to authority and avoid dissent, responding to the teacher's call on me in a vague, noncommittal way rather than expressing what I actually thought. I stood up, head lowered, and said: 'Teacher, I… I understand, no questions.' Then I slowly sat back down and quietly closed my notebook, sliding it under my textbook.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name alone — no waiting for a joint letter, no accepting the terms of 'private support' as a trade. I tell the neighbor directly: 'I'm not going to push you on the joint letter, but I will be submitting the complaint materials under my own name. The evidence is already organized. If you want to contribute anything, I'll take it; if not, that's fine too — I'll handle this myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: I've decided to file a formal complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name, as the lead complainant on record. I'll accept the neighbors' quiet assistance but won't pressure anyone to co-sign. And I'll hold no resentment toward them for stepping back — I understand it. I told the retired teacher: 'I hear everything you're saying. You don't have to sign anything. But I have to go — I'm the one who dug up the evidence, so I'll carry it there myself. If you have any materials, pass them to me. That's enough.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to file the complaint in my own name, as an individual — but I make my disappointment and my boundaries clear to the neighbors who were 'full of advice but unwilling to sign.' I say to the retired teacher: 'I'll go file the report, and I'll put my name on it — but I need you all to promise me this: if the housing authority comes to verify the facts, you cannot deny that you knew. I'm not asking you to be out in front. But I can't stand there alone and lie by saying I discovered all of this by myself.'

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to file a formal complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name, and to give the upstairs neighbor a direct but measured response — no accusations, but a clear statement of where I stand and what I will and won't accept. To the upstairs neighbor, I say: 'I understand everyone's concerns. I'll file the complaint myself. But I need the materials and information you all promised me — that's my only ask. Two million isn't a matter of a few thousand dollars per household. It's a matter of whether anyone is going to hold this process accountable.'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to speak directly, describing my creative process in the most concise and honest way I could — no performed gratitude, no studied humility. Into the microphone I said: 'I wrote this piece off the top of my head three weeks ago. I just wanted to work out a story with a coherent internal logic. Winning means the judges found that logic convincing, and honestly, that's a better outcome than I expected.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to muddle through and get out as fast as possible. I gripped the microphone, kept my head down, and mumbled a few words — 'I just wrote it casually, didn't think too much about it' — then tucked the trophy and certificate under my arm and practically fled off the stage, sinking back into my seat and folding myself into the crowd.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak honestly, treating this unexpected win itself as an interesting 'counter-intuitive phenomenon' to share, and naturally weaving in all the chaotic associations and questions I had while writing. I lean into the microphone and say: "Honestly, I didn't even proofread for typos before I submitted — so I think that fact is more interesting than my story itself: maybe the ideas that look 'not serious enough' are actually hiding something worth exploring."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to throw myself fully into an impromptu speech, using the surprise of winning as my best material, and drawing the whole room in with a light, humorous touch. I lean toward the microphone with a grin and say: "Honestly? I didn't even proofread this piece for typos — so I think what the judges are really telling us today is: inspiration matters more than perfection. Thank you to everyone here — your applause makes me feel like even something you dashed off can carry real weight!"

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to take full ownership of the situation — no cancellation, no hesitation — and pulled all three club members out of their slump on the spot to redistribute the work. I set my phone down on the table, swept my eyes across all three of them, and said: 'Cancel? Not happening. The president bailed, but the event is still on. Come on — pass me that task breakdown sheet. We're redividing everything right now. Everyone picks up two items, and we start tonight.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to take over the organizing work, refuse to agree to canceling the event, but proactively scale back the goals and focus on pushing forward the most critical tasks. I say to the members: "Canceling is something I can't agree to — but I'm not going to force any of you either. I'll personally follow up on the most urgent items myself: confirming the sponsors, getting the telescope loan request submitted, and sorting out the volunteer schedule. Whoever has time can pitch in; if you don't, that's fine. As for what shape the event ends up in — we'll see when we get there."

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse to cancel outright, and instead propose a scaled-down plan: cut the high-difficulty elements, redistribute responsibilities, and reshape the event into a smaller but genuinely executable version. I speak up: "We're not canceling — but we do need to redesign this. The president's gone, so we turn it into something smaller. Each of you takes on one thing you can actually handle, I'll coordinate the whole thing, and we get through it together."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to cancel the event outright, and instead propose a compromise: scale things down, redistribute responsibilities, ask the remaining members to each take on whatever they can manage, and reach out to the student affairs committee to explain the situation and ask for support. I speak up: 'I think we shouldn't rush to say cancel just yet — can you hear me out for a second? We scale the event down, everyone only handles one or two of the most critical things, and I'll go coordinate the rest with the student affairs committee. That way nobody's finals get wrecked, and the event doesn't just disappear into nothing.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation directly — no deflecting, no pretending I didn't pick up on the subtext — while making my situation unambiguously clear. I say: "Thank you for telling me all of this. I genuinely think the collaboration these past few months has been something rare. But I'm in a relationship, and I can't accept this invitation — not because I don't want to go, but because I know it wouldn't just be a bookstore outing."

options.label: B
options.content: Accept the invitation — but also decide to have a real conversation with my partner that same night, and face whatever has actually gone wrong between us. I say to them: "I'd love to go, I've been wanting to catch this talk too — but there's something I need to tell you first, about where things have been between us lately."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation gently but clearly, while acknowledging how much this partnership has meant to me — not denying the feeling, but deliberately closing the door on it. I say softly: 'These past few months working with you have been something really special to me too. But Saturday… I can't go. I think we both understand why. Thank you for telling me.'

options.label: D
options.content: She chooses to decline the invitation, but does so through self-deprecation rather than stating a clear boundary. I lower my head, pull out a strained smile, and say quietly: "Thank you for saying all that… I've felt like these past few months have been something special too. The bookstore thing — I probably can't make it. I've been all over the place lately, and I'm afraid I wouldn't be very good company." I didn't dare look him in the eyes after that.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to respond with composure — neither dodging nor defending — and describe my current life with concrete facts, politely but clearly declining the well-meaning suggestions. I pick up my glass, smile, and say: 'I run projects at a nonprofit. The pay isn't high, but one of my paintings got selected for the local young artists' exhibition last year — feels pretty solid to me. I appreciate the offer about tripling my salary, but the path I'm on is working out well right now, so I'm not looking to switch.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to openly lay out my life, to present it as a fascinating cultural specimen rather than retreating into defensiveness or evasion. I pick up my glass, smile, and say: 'I work on projects at a nonprofit — the salary's honestly not much, I live in an urban village, but last year one of my paintings got into a youth art exhibition. I can't really keep up with everything you all are talking about, but honestly? I think the people at this table have turned into completely different species from one another — and I find that kind of captivating.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to introduce my situation proactively, clearly, and with structure — no deflecting, no defending, just delivering a 'status report' on my own life the way a project manager would. I say: 'I do project planning at a nonprofit. The salary isn't high, but I manage every project from kickoff to close-out, and I manage it well. In my spare time I paint — last year I got into the local young artists' exhibition, which counts as a milestone. Renting is my choice for this stage; I haven't hit the point where buying makes sense. As for joining a big tech company — I evaluated it, and it doesn't fit my current execution roadmap.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to grab the thread of the conversation head-on — use humor and confidence to put my life on full display and dissolve the awkward charge of the class-gap moment right then and there. I lift my sake cup and sweep a grin around the table: 'Mysterious? That's because what I do isn't exactly in your wheelhouse — nonprofit projects by day, painting by night, and last year one of my pieces got into the local young artists' exhibition. Income? Enough to live on, and I'm living with a hell of a lot of energy. You say triple the pay — depends what you're tripling. Sure, triple the money, but if I lose this drive in the process, that's a terrible trade. Come on, drink up!'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to actively embrace the transition — no more waiting on the sidelines — and to face tomorrow's conversation with my supervisor with an open mind. Tonight I reply to my former colleague: 'I've made up my mind too — let's do this together.' Then I open that AI product course page I've had bookmarked forever, and I click enroll.

options.label: B
options.content: Decline my former colleague's offer to team up for the transition and choose to plan my own learning path independently; at the same time, prepare a clear but noncommittal position for tomorrow's manager meeting — acknowledge that I'm evaluating a transition direction, but commit to no specific timeline, in order to preserve my autonomy. Concrete actions: send my former colleague a short text message — 'Still thinking it over, let's each do our own thing for now' — then open a document and quietly map out a self-study path for AI-related skills alongside the time window my current savings can support.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold off on making any decision about a career transition, and instead prioritize gathering more internal information through tomorrow's one-on-one with my supervisor, while giving my former colleague a noncommittal reply in the meantime. I write back to him: "I think you make a fair point — I'm still turning it over. Let me talk to my supervisor tomorrow and see how that goes, then we can catch up after."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to wait and watch — I won't accept my former colleague's invitation, and I'll prepare to tell my supervisor tomorrow that I'm willing to align with the company's current direction and that I want to keep my position. I reply to my former colleague: 'I support your decision, but I've got family pressure on my end — quitting isn't something I'm considering right now. I'll see how things play out here first.' Then I open my calendar and accept the supervisor's invite.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan, accepting the risk that it may never be returned, and I put the travel plans on hold. I pull out my phone to transfer the money — or dig out the cash — and crouch down beside him: 'Go pay the deposit first. You don't need to write an IOU — we'll sort it out once your father is out.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan immediately, without hesitating to press for too many details. I pull out my phone and open the transfer screen, and I tell him: 'I'm sending it to you now — go pay the deposit. You don't need to write an IOU. Save him first.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to lend the money, but with conditions attached — some way to keep a sense of control. I pulled out my phone, my voice coming out harder than I intended, and said: 'Write an IOU in your phone's notes app, screenshot it and send it to me, then give me your phone number. I'll lend you the three thousand, but I need your real information.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to lend the money, but I give him an alternative direction rather than walking away in silence. I crouch down and gently move his hand from my pant leg, and say calmly: 'I can't lend you anything — but go to the nurses' station right now, tell them the family has no money. The hospital has an emergency payment assistance process. Go ask them. Don't stay here on your knees.'

options.label: A
options.content: I decide to step in and accept the professional risk — to help Lin Xiaoyu move this forward in a structured way, rather than stopping at simply listening. I tell her: "The moment you came to me today, this stopped being just your problem alone. Don't go to that meeting tomorrow by yourself — I'll figure something out. First, let's get everything you've experienced down in writing: the dates, the locations, exactly what happened each time, laid out point by point. That's the foundation for whatever we do next."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to step in — but in a way that puts protecting her first, without pushing for a formal report just yet. I place my hand over hers, my voice shaking a little, but I speak anyway: 'You don't have to carry this alone tonight. That meeting tomorrow — I'm going to figure out a way to make sure it doesn't happen. But first, tell me everything he's done.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take a stand and commit to getting involved — but to move carefully rather than charge straight into a confrontation. I set down my teacup and looked at her: 'The fact that you came to me today means you trust me. That meeting tomorrow — you're not going alone. I'll figure something out. We'll document this first, and then we take it one step at a time.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to intervene directly, to take on whatever professional risk that entails, and not to push the burden back onto Lin Xiaoyu to carry alone. I take her hand and say: 'You're not in this by yourself. You don't have to walk into that meeting tomorrow — I'll figure something out. We'll find a safe way to handle this together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I call 120 and 110 immediately, set the handbag down on the roadside without touching it further, and walk toward the rider to check his injuries while continuing to call for help. I put the bag on the ground and dialed 120, my voice trembling but clear: 'Someone's been hit — I'm at… I need an ambulance, hurry.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to call the authorities and get help immediately, refuse the bribe, and leave the handbag in place as evidence. I call 120, give my location and describe the injured person's condition; then I call 110, recount the details of the incident and the direction the driver fled, and inform them that there is a bag of cash at the scene left by the driver, which I have not touched.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to immediately call 120 and 110, leave the bag exactly as it is by the roadside, and sprint on foot toward the injured person to conduct an emergency assessment. Once 120 connects, I say: 'It's 1:47 in the morning, two-lane road on the outskirts of the city — an electric-bike rider has been struck by a vehicle. The victim is conscious but bleeding and needs emergency care. I'm on the scene right now.' I then call 110 to report the vehicle involved and the direction of the driver's escape.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to call emergency services and the police, leave the handbag exactly as it is on the ground, and stay on the scene to wait for help to arrive. I set the handbag down by the roadside and dialed 120, my voice pressing down against a tremor: 'Someone's been hit by a car — it's on that two-lane road at the edge of the city, near the abandoned tin shed. The injured person is beside the drainage ditch. Please come quickly.' Then I called 110 and told them everything I'd seen, including the black Audi and the driver who fled.

options.label: A
options.content: I assessed that my current progress is manageable and the risks aren't worth taking, so I chose to decline the service and continue writing the thesis on my own. I told my roommate: 'I'm still on track — I think I'll make it in time. I won't be needing this, but thanks for thinking of me.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse the service — I'm not going to look into it further or reach out. I waved my roommate off and said: "Forget it, I'll figure it out myself — this kind of thing isn't for me."

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to refuse the service and commit to finishing the thesis on my own, without any compromise. I shook my head and pushed the phone back, saying: "I don't need this — I'll write it myself. Don't send me the contact info."

options.label: D
options.content: Refuse to use the service and insist on completing the thesis independently. I shook my head, pushed the phone back, and said: "No thanks — this one I'll do myself. There's still so much about my own topic I haven't worked out yet; if I handed it off to someone else, I'd never be able to sleep."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to go home immediately to deal with Dad's illness, setting the overseas project decision aside for now, and will reach out to the company once the situation becomes clearer. I tell my mother: 'I understand — tell me which hospital Dad is in. I'll look up tickets tonight and take the earliest flight out. You stay calm for now. Once I'm there, I'll go talk to the doctors myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to let go of the opportunity abroad and go home immediately to be with my father and handle the medical arrangements; the career opportunity is set aside for now. I tell my mother: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — the earliest flight tonight or first thing tomorrow morning. Wait for me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to go back immediately to handle my father's medical affairs, while simultaneously reaching out to the company to negotiate a delay or explore remote options for the overseas opportunity. This isn't a pure emotional sacrifice — I'm treating it as a project that requires my on-site presence. I tell my mother: 'I'll book a ticket tonight and be there tomorrow. In the meantime, have the doctors pull together all the test results — when I arrive, I'll go straight to the attending physician and talk it through myself.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to set aside the immediate push on the overseas project and prioritize going home to handle Dad's medical arrangements, while also starting to break down the timelines of both things in search of a middle path. I begin making a list on my phone screen: the earliest train tomorrow morning, contact the attending physician, and at the same time send HR an email asking whether the overseas role allows for a deferred decision — first get the parts I can control into their boxes, then go home.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to use the question he asked — the first thing he's said on his own — as my way in. I stay where I am, respond quietly, and don't kick the bottle back. I let my simple presence tell him that someone is listening. I set the bottle on my own desk, turn my chair to face his side of the room, and say calmly: "Didn't feel like going out tonight. You doing… okay over there?"

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene directly, with confirming the medication situation as the immediate priority, and contacting emergency services if necessary. I carry the bottle to the curtain around his bed and speak quietly, without any edge in my voice: 'I didn't go out. This bottle — how many did you take?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to intervene directly — I don't return the pill bottle, and I immediately contact the resident advisor or call a mental health crisis line. I don't let this get handled internally within the dorm room. I keep the bottle in my hand, stand up, and walk to the curtain around his bed. In a steady voice, I say: 'I'm not giving this back to you. You need someone here right now — I'm going to contact the RA. If you don't want me to, tell me now.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up and respond to my roommate — using the bottle as a reason to stay, remaining right where I am in a clumsy but genuine way. I keep the bottle in my hand instead of kicking it back, turn around, and say: 'I don't feel like going out tonight. This bottle of pills… can you tell me about it?'

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to write the colleague who just returned from maternity leave onto the list, and I choose to voice my dissent directly rather than comply in silence. I meet my superior's eyes and say: 'I can't write this list the way you're asking. If someone has to go, I need a real reason — not this one.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse to put the colleague who just returned from maternity leave on the list — I won't go along with my superior's arrangement, and I'll bear whatever consequences come. I kept my head down and stayed silent for a moment, then spoke: 'I can't write this list — her performance review is a B+, I have no grounds to put her on it, and I can't sign off on this.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose not to write my colleague — the one who just returned from maternity leave — onto the list, but I didn't confront my boss head-on either. Instead, I used delay and vagueness to buy myself time. I told him: 'I need to go back through the complete performance review records. I'll send you my thoughts before tonight — but I can't confirm this list right now.'

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to directly write that colleague's name — the one who just returned from maternity leave — on the list, and I try to ask my boss whether another solution might be possible. I say quietly: "I understand the position you're in, but this colleague just came back and her performance is already recovering — I'd like to think a little longer about whether there's another way. Could you give me a bit of time?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell him face-to-face, privately, when he gets back — giving him the chance to rewrite it before submission — rather than staying silent or going straight to the professor. When he pushes the door open, I look up and say: 'I accidentally saw your document just now, and I searched a few passages — they're a perfect match with an article on Zhihu. The deadline is the day after tomorrow; you still have time to fix it, and I can help you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to confront my roommate directly the moment he walks back in — tell him what I found, demand he rewrite it tonight, no dancing around it, and no going to the professor. I wait for him to come through the door and say: 'I just happened to see what was on your screen, looked it up — it's straight from Zhihu, word for word. You need to rewrite it tonight. There's still time before the day after tomorrow. I'm not going to the professor about this, but you have to fix it yourself.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to prepare myself before my roommate gets back, and to tell him face to face — privately, gently, but directly — that I've discovered the plagiarism, giving him the chance to rewrite before submission, rather than reporting him or staying silent. I close the browser tab, ease his laptop screen back to where it was, take a deep breath, and wait for him to walk in. When he does, I speak: 'There's something I need to talk to you about — sit down for a second. It's about the report. I came across something by accident, and I think we need to talk before it gets submitted.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up before my roommate gets back — to tell him directly what I found, framing it as 'I came across it by accident, and we need to talk,' and to demand that he rewrite it before submission, while making clear that the consequences fall on both of us. I stand up, walk to the door, and the moment it opens I say: 'I accidentally saw what was on your screen just now, and I searched a few of the passages — we need to talk right now, because the deadline is the day after tomorrow and both our names are on this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to express my anger directly and refuse the money — but without breaking down or losing control, drawing a boundary in a clear, steady voice. I push the stack of bills back, my voice calmer than I expected, but firm: 'This isn't about money, Uncle. Six months of my work was in there. That can't be bought back.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse the money, say my piece briefly, and end this conversation as quickly as possible — without getting into a direct argument with my uncle, but without pretending everything is fine either. I push the stack of money back across the coffee table, my voice flat, and say only one thing: 'Keep the money. What was in there isn't a money matter.' Then I stand up to go get some water, and I don't look at them again.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to make my position clear calmly but firmly — neither accepting the cash nor letting things explode into a scene. I push the wad of money back across the coffee table and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Uncle, I can't take this. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't about the cost of replacing a hard drive. Those files are gone, and that's that. I understand the kid didn't know any better, but I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse the money and say outright that the loss cannot be compensated financially — my tone is restrained but my position is clear. I won't get into a heated confrontation with Uncle, but I will not accept this way of 'settling things.' I push the stack of bills back, my voice kept very low but very steady: 'Uncle, I can't take this money. What was on there is six months of my life — it can't be bought back. Please sit with the child for a moment; I need to think about how to handle this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to decline my ex's request to get back together — gently but clearly — and decide to tell my partner that same day about how the meeting went. I quietly pull my hand back and say, calmly: 'I'm glad you've figured things out, but I'm with someone now, and things are really good between us — I can't let him down. I'll be telling him about today.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to turn down my ex's request to get back together, but my response is vague and riddled with self-attacking apologies — and I decide to hide the details of this meeting from my current boyfriend. I say to my ex: "We can't go back to what we were. You deserve better. I'm sorry for making you wait so long" — then I lower my head, unable to meet his eyes, while inside I've already started running through how I'm going to explain to my current boyfriend why I'm coming home late tonight.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to respond to my ex honestly about my uncertainty, and at the same time I decide to tell my current partner, that same day, everything about this meeting. I say to my ex: 'I can't tell you I've completely moved on — but I'm with someone now, and I'm not going to let the fact that I still feel something destroy what we have. Tonight I'm going to tell him that we met.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to give a clear rejection on the spot, and I decide that when I get home I will proactively tell my current partner everything about this meeting — closing every open variable. To my ex, I say: 'I hear what you're saying, but I'm in a relationship I'm genuinely invested in right now. I can't give you that space of 'just waiting' — it wouldn't be fair to any of the three of us. My answer is no.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to comply for now in order to get out of immediate physical danger, but inwardly I resolve not to give up on recovering the deposit — the moment I leave, I'll contact someone who can help me (a tenant rights hotline, legal aid, or a friend). I lower my head and say, voice trembling but as steady as I can manage: 'Fine, I'll go for tonight — but we're not done talking about the deposit. I'll be handling this through the proper channels.' Then I bend down and start gathering the clothes from the floor, and I stop meeting his eyes.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to comply for now and leave, preserving the option to pursue the deposit afterward — I will not confront violence head-on. I bend down and gather my clothes from the floor, keeping my voice as steady as I can: 'I'll go tonight — but we're not done when it comes to the deposit.' Then I pick up my suitcase and walk out.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to appear outwardly compliant for now in order to remove myself from the immediate threat of violence, while quietly initiating evidence collection and legal proceedings in the background — I will not give up the deposit under any circumstances. I say to him: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight — but the contract and the deposit, we're not done with that. You'd better take a good hard look at those contract terms, because I'm going to have someone verify them.' At the same time, I quietly start recording on my phone.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to comply for now and leave, protecting my physical safety rather than confronting them on the spot — but I quietly take note of everything about them, with the intention of pursuing the deposit afterward through legal channels or other means. I bend down and pick the clothes up off the floor, drag my suitcase upright, and say: 'Fine, I'll go — but I'll find somewhere to take this deposit issue up properly.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to drop the idea of raising an alternative proposal, fall in line with the current consensus, and not create friction at this moment. I shook my head and said: 'Nothing from me — let's go with the plan as it stands. I'll work with whatever assignments come my way.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to put forward the alternative proposal at this moment — presenting the core judgment in a clear, direct way, no beating around the bush, letting the file speak for itself. I say: "My proposal is still here. I spent a few days putting together an alternative path — including the implementation approach and the risk points. I can walk through it in five minutes. I think raising it now saves more time than reworking things later. Can you give me those five minutes?"

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal in this moment, presenting it in a structured, evidence-based way rather than silently accepting the consensus. I turned my screen toward the room and said: 'There are a few other ideas I'd like to share — I've put together a specific proposal with the risk points laid out. I need three minutes. I think saying it now costs less than waiting until something goes wrong later.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to raise the alternative plan on the spot — to open things up rather than quietly go along. I turn my screen toward the group and say: 'Actually, yes — I've put together a different approach over here. Give it two minutes; if everyone feels it's not worth discussing, we'll get straight back to dividing up tasks — but I want this plan to be seen at least once.'

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to accept this unilaterally decided arrangement in the group chat. I choose to make my position explicitly clear in the family chat in writing, demanding that everyone renegotiate the specific division of responsibilities and cost-sharing — not a money-for-labor swap. I type into the group: "I wasn't part of this discussion. Caregiving isn't something one person should have to carry just because they live closest. I need everyone to sit down together and work out exactly how to divide this — both the time and the money need to be recalculated." Then I put down my phone and turn toward my partner.

options.label: B
options.content: I'm not accepting another round of carrying the bulk of the caregiving alone — but I'm not blowing things up in the open either. I reply in the group chat: both money and caregiving need to be shared on a rotating schedule; I'll cover the medication this time, and tomorrow I'll send out the list and the shift rotation. Then I turn and quietly tell my partner that we need to talk tonight — really talk.

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to accept the "money in exchange for effort" arrangement and call out the fundamental problem with this proposal directly in the group chat, demanding a clear division of responsibilities rather than letting anyone buy their way out.
