I type in the group chat: "Living closer is a geographical fact, not a reason to take on all the caregiving. Money can be contributed, but so can presence — we'll get on a video call to work out a rotation schedule, and until we've sorted that out properly, I'm not fronting anyone's medical bills."

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to accept the vague "money for effort" arrangement floated in the group chat — but I don't explode in the chat right now. Instead, I put my phone down and walk toward my partner. The first words out of my mouth are: "I see you. Let's talk about us first."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose restraint and composure — clearing my name in public, neither fleeing nor fighting back, waiting for the train to reach the next stop so I can seek a rational resolution. I release my collar from the grip, raise both hands so the people around me can clearly see my phone screen, and say in as steady a voice as I can manage: 'I was scrolling through my work group chat — you can all see for yourselves. I haven't photographed anyone. Please wait until the next stop and we can find a staff member together.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to force myself to stay calm — no running, no fighting back hard — and in the most direct way possible I hold up my phone screen showing the work group chat, so everyone in the carriage and the livestream camera can see the evidence. I say loudly: 'Look carefully — what's on my phone is the work group chat. Screen facing up. Everyone can see it. I didn't photograph anyone.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to prove my innocence through physical evidence rather than arguing out loud — I force my phone screen outward and thrust it up high toward the onlookers and the live-stream cameras. Holding the screen facing out, I raise it as high as I can and shout at every lens in front of me: 'Look carefully — this is a work group chat, I was scrolling through messages, I was not taking photos. Can anyone here actually read what's on this screen?'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to actively present the evidence and break the spectator structure, rather than endure in silence or fight back aggressively. I held my phone screen up as high as I could and said, as loudly but as steadily as I could manage: 'Everyone look — this is my work group chat, messages sent at four in the morning, I've been reading this the whole time, my phone has been held at exactly this height, screen facing out — anyone can come and look.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to set my phone aside for now and stay present in this moment, replacing an immediate stance with genuine listening — neither accepting nor rejecting my partner's proposal, but returning the question to the conversation itself. I turn my phone face-down on the sofa, look at my partner, and say: 'I need to understand what you're actually saying first — when you say flexibility, what does that mean to you?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on taking any position and prioritize gathering information, asking her to spell out exactly what the specific boundaries are. I set down my phone, looked at her, and said: 'When you say flexibility, I'd like to hear you be a little more specific — the independent space you're talking about, what kinds of things does that mean, and how far does it go?'

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse the proposed redefinition of the relationship's boundaries; hold to the original commitment. I press the phone face-down first, then tell him plainly: 'I can't accept this kind of redefinition — we need to talk this through properly.'

options.label: D
options.content: He chooses to reject his partner's proposal, with preserving the existing relationship framework as his immediate priority, and sets his phone aside for the moment. I put down my phone, looked at her, and said, my voice a little tight: 'I don't understand what you mean by that kind of flexibility, but I know I don't want to change the way things are between us right now — if you feel like something isn't enough, just tell me directly, and we can talk about it, but not like this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay at the intersection — refusing to drive straight home and pretend everything is normal, but also refusing to completely fall apart — and instead try to treat these twenty years as a text that needs to be reread rather than a mistake that needs to be negated. I didn't start the engine. I turned my phone face-down on the passenger seat and said quietly to myself: 'This isn't a conclusion. This is just a chapter I haven't finished reading yet.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stay put for now — not go home right away, but not let myself completely fall apart either. Instead I try to convert this feeling of losing control into something that can be processed. I flip my phone face-down on the passenger seat, close my eyes, and tell myself: just list out tonight's tasks one by one. Everything else can wait until tomorrow.

options.label: C
options.content: He chooses to suppress the breakdown and force himself back into the rhythm of the everyday, smothering that crack under the weight of family obligation and routine. He flips the phone face-down on the passenger seat, puts the car in gear, and drives home — repeating quietly to himself: there's dinner tonight, there are things to do tomorrow. That's enough.

options.label: D
options.content: I allow myself a brief breakdown at the intersection, but in the end I use self-attack to seal off any genuine questioning, substituting "pretending to accept" for truly facing it. I flip my phone face-down, take a deep breath, and murmur to myself: "You didn't have a choice back then — this is just who you are" — then I put the car in gear and drive home.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on with an open stance, and to actively reframe the senior team member's provocation as a topic for the whole group to work through together—establishing my rhythm as team lead rather than shutting him down. I smile, stand up straight, and speak at a volume that's calm but carries to every corner of the room: "Good question—that module is actually exactly what I want us to dig into today. Old Li, you've got the deepest experience on this one. When we meet in a bit, you go first, and we'll all figure out together how to move it forward."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on—no deflecting, no escalating—and set the tone with a steady, grounded composure. I speak up, voice even, neither rushed nor slow: 'Good question. I've already looked at the unfinished module—this team meeting today is exactly where we sort out the schedule. Let's all sit down and walk through it together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on and transform it into the opening topic for the team meeting — neither avoiding it nor confronting it combatively, but making the question itself the starting point for discussion. I say: 'Good question — that module is actually one of the core things we're tackling today. Let's all sit down and take it apart together. I want to hear everyone's read on it, including yours.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on, defuse the pressure with a relaxed attitude, and kick the ball back to the whole team — so I don't end up as his target. I smiled and said: "Whether I can handle it or not, we won't know until we try — but actually, that question you just asked was a good one. That unfinished module? Let's put it on the table in today's meeting, take a look together at where it's stuck, and anyone who has ideas can speak up. That includes you."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to put down the speech immediately and head to the hospital, leaving the launch event for the team to handle on an emergency basis. I tell the CEO: 'My father's surgery requires my signature — it's the one thing that cannot be replaced. Please have the team hold things down at the launch; I'll deal with the follow-up afterward.' Then I hang up, and tell the driver to reroute to the hospital.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the surgical consent. I end the CEO's video call and say into the doctor's phone: "I'm leaving now — I'll be there in fifteen minutes. Keep him stable. Wait for my signature."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to handle the medical decision remotely, then attend the launch as planned. First I press the doctors to spell out the risks clearly over the phone and confirm the treatment plan directly; then I notify the CEO that I'll be there on time.

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the medical authorization. Call the hospital back to confirm the surgical plan, and at the same time send a text to the CEO: 'My father is in emergency surgery. I have to go to the hospital. I'm sorry.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to let him stay, and immediately start building a response plan — converting this out-of-control situation into a problem that can be managed. I lower my voice and tell him: 'Get into the back room, kill the lights, and don't make a sound. I'll figure out what we do next — but you have to tell me everything: how much you owe, how many of them there are, and whether anyone saw you come into this building.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to let my friend stay, while keeping quiet and staying low-key in case anyone knocks. I turn off all the lights, signal him not to make a sound, and we sit with our backs against the wall beside the door, waiting for the footsteps to pass. If someone knocks, I'll say in a low voice 'no one's here' — or just not answer at all.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose without a moment's hesitation to let him stay, while readying myself to handle a knock at the door that could come at any second. I push him into the back room and drop my voice: 'Get in there, keep the lights off, I'll answer the door — I'll tell them there's only me here.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to let him stay for now — close the door, keep out of sight, stay on high alert, and make no move to open the door and confront the debt collectors. I drop my voice to a low murmur and tell him: 'Don't make a sound — don't touch the lights either. We wait until they're gone, and then you're going to tell me exactly how deep this goes.'

options.label: A
options.content: I decline the invitation and stay behind to keep observing the insect and the little purple flower, trying to document every detail with my phone or the notebook I have on me. I tell the kid: "Go on ahead without me — there's something here I haven't finished looking at. I've never seen this bug before in my life, and I need to figure out what it is first."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to soften the moment gently — neither flatly refusing the invitation nor immediately giving up my observation — trying to buy myself a little time. I stay crouched where I am, look up, and give him a small smile: "Go ahead and warm up without me — I just need two more minutes. This bug is seriously weird, I've never seen anything like it — I'll be right there!"

options.label: C
options.content: He politely declines the invitation, tells the other kid to head back first, and stays to look a little longer before deciding anything — neither diving into research nor leaving right away. "You guys go ahead, I'll come check it out in a bit."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation and go play soccer, but at the same time I turn this strange bug into a 'surprise' I bring to the field — merging both things into one. I say to the kid: 'Let's go, let's go — but look at this first. Have you ever seen a bug like this? Its back is green and it actually glows! We'll come back and study it after the game — come on, let's go!'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to submit only the legitimate business travel expenses and left those two personal receipts in my wallet without turning them in. I tucked the Japanese restaurant receipt and the scarf receipt back into my pocket, and only attached the high-speed rail ticket, the hotel invoice, and the work-meal receipt to the reimbursement form before submitting it.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to claim only the legitimate business travel expenses and leave out those two personal receipts. The Japanese restaurant and scarf receipts go back into my wallet; only the high-speed rail tickets, hotel, and working-meal receipts get attached to the form and submitted — coming in under the cap is perfectly fine.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to submit only the legitimate business travel expenses, leaving those two personal receipts folded in my wallet without turning them in. I fold the Japanese restaurant receipt and the scarf receipt and tuck them back into my wallet, then neatly attach the high-speed rail ticket, the hotel bill, and the work-meal receipt to the reimbursement form, fill in the total — a number more than four hundred yuan short of the ceiling — and push the form to the side, taking a slow, deep breath.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to claim only the legitimate travel expenses, putting the two personal receipts back in my wallet and leaving them off the reimbursement form. I fold the Japanese restaurant receipt and the scarf receipt and tuck them back into my billfold, then neatly attach the high-speed rail ticket, hotel invoice, and business meal receipt, submitting the claim for the actual amount.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to object outright, and instead ask that both of us first lay this path out in full and see it clearly before any decision is made. I say: 'Leave that booklet here with me. I'll read through it carefully tonight. We'll talk again tomorrow — I have questions I want to ask you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to oppose him outright, but I can't bring myself to offer real support either — I replace a clear answer with the hedged worry of someone who's 'been through it' and a conditional softening. I say: 'You're telling me you've been at this since middle school… It's not that I won't let you try, but you need to think it through — if it doesn't pan out, do you have a fallback?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to support the child wholeheartedly in taking this unconventional route, without using my own experience as a reason to put up obstacles. I slide the report card back across the table toward them and say: 'Let me take a look. The fact that you've been teaching yourself since middle school — that already tells me everything I need to know. Go ahead and apply. I'm behind you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to offer conditional support, but I require my kid to sit through a structured risk-breakdown conversation right now, rather than simply handing over an emotional endorsement. I push the report booklet back across the table and say: 'You say seven years — I believe you. But let's go through this field's employment data, your competitive edge, and your exit path in the worst-case scenario, one by one. If you can answer those, I'll support you filling it in.'

options.label: A
options.content: Honestly express how strongly I want this academic opportunity, acknowledge the uncertainty, but make it clear that I want to seriously consider accepting the invitation — and that same evening, reply to my advisor's email with an initial expression of intent. I tell him: "I know this will change a lot of things, and I'm scared too. But when I found that notebook last night, I realized I haven't really been alive for a long time — I want to reply to that email, and we can figure out the rest together."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honestly share my genuine assessment of the academic opportunity, while bringing the question of impact on our family into the decision-making framework, and proposing that we map out the specific variables together before drawing any conclusions. I say to him: 'I really am thinking seriously about this. The salary, the pace, the effect on our life — those are all real questions. We can lay them out one by one and look at them together. There's no rush to decide right now, but I want to get the full picture first before I write back to my advisor.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to avoid the academic opportunity, declining the possibility on the grounds of family stability and practical concerns, and not replying to the advisor's email. Say to her: "You're right — I've thought it over too, and this just isn't the right time. The shop still needs me, and I'm not planning to reply to that email."

options.label: D
options.content: She chooses to honestly express her genuine longing for this academic opportunity, while sincerely receiving his concerns and inviting him into a shared conversation rather than making a unilateral decision; she holds off on replying to her advisor's email for now, and first opens up to the person beside her. She gently takes his hand and says: "I've been staring at this email because I really do want to go — I know it would change a lot of things, and I know you have reservations, and I want to listen to all of them seriously. Can we talk tonight — not me trying to convince you, but the two of us figuring it out together?"

options.label: A
options.content: Choose palliative care, honoring the genuine wish my father expressed through his exhaustion, rather than forcing him to endure suffering for the sake of a 20% chance. I turn to the doctor, my voice slightly unsteady but clear: 'We're choosing palliative care — so he can spend the time he has with us, without going through all of that pain.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose palliative care, honor the exhaustion and wishes my father has expressed, and refuse to let a low-probability course of chemotherapy turn his remaining time into suffering. I speak to the doctor: 'Start the comfort-care protocol—let him spend whatever time he has left in peace.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion — palliative care, letting go of that 20% chance. I look at the doctor, my voice a little hoarse, and say: 'We'll go with comfort care. Let him go with some dignity.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honor my father's wishes and decide on palliative care, stating that choice plainly without flinching. I say to the doctor: 'We're choosing palliative care — please initiate the comfort care protocol. He has made his choice, and we respect it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea in front of the class, while remaining open to the speech competition proposal without rushing to commit. I stand up and say evenly: "Put the elephant on one side of the seesaw, then keep adding people to the other side until it balances — remember how many people that is, then weigh one person, and multiply. That tells you how much the elephant weighs." When I finish, I sit back down and tell the teacher: "The competition — I'll think it over and let you know."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and share my idea in front of everyone; the suggestion about the speech competition is set aside for now, neither accepted nor explicitly refused. I rise from my chair, my voice a little rushed at first but growing steadier as I go: 'It's just… you put an elephant on one end, then you stack people onto the other end one by one, and you count how many people it takes — because you can measure each person's weight ahead of time —'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up without a moment's hesitation, lay out the full explanation in front of everyone, and readily agree to enter the speech competition. I rise to my feet, sweep my eyes across the whole class, and say with a smile: "The principle of the seesaw is simple — put the elephant on one end, then keep stacking people onto the other end until the two sides balance. Count how many people there are and roughly how much each one weighs, add it all up, and that's the elephant's weight. I'll go to the speech competition — thank you, Teacher!"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to look down and avoid it — deflecting with something vague, neither standing up to speak nor agreeing to enter the competition. I lower my head and say quietly: "Teacher… what I said just now wasn't very clear, I wasn't really thinking that much about it — the competition thing… I'm really not up for it."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to take either side. Instead, I let my tears and my body break the moment — interrupt the pressure to choose — and try to make both adults stop. I crouch down, wrap my arms around my knees, and cry out loud. The only words I manage to say, over and over, are: 'I don't want you to split up. I'm not going anywhere.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stay, expressing through the rigid stillness of my body a despair that cannot make any choice, while instinctively leaning toward the 'stay' side. I drew my shoulders in tight, my feet didn't move, and in a voice barely above a whisper I said: "I'm not leaving… I'm not going anywhere…" Then I crouched down, covered my face, and cried out loud.

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to choose between them, trying to break through this forced ultimatum with tears and silence. I crouch down, wrap my arms around my knees, lower my head, and let myself cry out loud — without saying a single word, just shaking my head over and over.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to take either side — I'm going to flee the scene first. I'll wrench my hands free from theirs, cry out 'I don't want to choose,' and then run back to my room and shut the door.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the missing model — I won't go along with the lie, but I also won't actively assign blame to anyone; I simply state the facts and ask to continue with a verbal presentation instead. I say quietly to the homeroom teacher: 'The model was lost on the way to school and didn't make it here — would it be alright if we just walked through the content verbally?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell a softened version of the truth: acknowledge that the model wasn't brought, without actively naming who's responsible, while indicating that the group can complete the presentation through a verbal walkthrough. I say to the homeroom teacher: 'Ms. [Teacher], the model wasn't able to make it today — something came up — but we're fully prepared to walk through everything verbally. The content is all there. Please let us go up.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the model being lost, refuse to go along with the lie, and proactively offer to carry the presentation through with a verbal explanation alone. I say directly to the homeroom teacher: 'He left the model on the bus — there's no getting it back. We don't have the model, but I have the whole thing memorized. I'll present it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to go along with the lie, choose to tell the homeroom teacher honestly that the model was lost, and immediately pivot to completing the presentation through verbal, impromptu explanation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but clearly: 'The model was left on the bus on the way to school. We don't have a prop, but we can just talk through it — I'll describe how the solar system works. We don't need the model.'

options.label: A
options.content: I'm not accepting another round of carrying the bulk of the caregiving alone — but I'm not blowing things up in the open either. I reply in the group chat: both money and caregiving need to be shared on a rotating schedule; I'll cover the medication this time, and tomorrow I'll send out the list and the shift rotation. Then I turn and quietly tell my partner that we need to talk tonight — really talk.

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to accept this arrangement through silence. I choose to send a real response in the group chat, making it clear that the current division of responsibilities is not sustainable, and demanding that we renegotiate the specifics of how things are shared. I type into the group: "Money isn't the issue — the issue is that this isn't something I can carry alone. We need to sit down and seriously talk about how to divide this, and that means time and people, not just money." Then I put down my phone and turn to walk toward my partner.

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to accept this unequal arrangement of trading money for labor, and I make it clear in the group chat that we need to renegotiate how caregiving duties are divided — rather than unilaterally making me carry more. I type in the group: "Let's set the money aside for now. Caregiving isn't one person's job. Next week we sit down face to face and work out exactly how much time and help each household can actually contribute — because the way things are going, none of us can keep this up."

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to accept the vague "money for effort" arrangement floated in the group chat — but I don't explode in the chat right now. Instead, I put my phone down and walk toward my partner. The first words out of my mouth are: "I see you. Let's talk about us first."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to confront him directly, without flinching from the conflict, even at the risk of blowing up the friendship entirely. I slide the PPT back across the table and speak — calm, but without any give in my voice: 'You want to call it jealousy, fine, think whatever you like. But I have to say this clearly today — this is a pyramid scheme, not a business. The eight hundred thousand you've already put in, I don't know if you'll ever see it again. But you're asking me to get in with you, and that's something I can't do. Won't do.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep trying to dissuade him — I'll lay out the factual hallmarks of a pyramid scheme structure directly, decline the investment invitation, and not dignify the 'jealousy' accusation with a response. I say: 'I'm not putting money in, and it's not because I'm jealous. This model of yours — recruiting downlines, tiered profit-sharing, returns that depend on the people below you rather than on any actual product — that's the textbook structure of a pyramid scheme. There's nothing I can do about the eight hundred thousand that's already gone, but don't ask me to hand over this hundred and fifty thousand.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak my honest assessment gently but directly — not sidestepping the pyramid-scheme structure for what it is, but keeping the focus on my friend's state of mind rather than on who's right or wrong, and refusing to let his defensive attacks pull me off course. I set down the PowerPoint, look at him, and say: "I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're stupid — I just saw some things in this material that made me genuinely worried about you, and I want to talk about it seriously, not to tear you down, but because you're someone I care about."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up directly, without sidestepping the conflict — but I position myself as his ally, not his opponent, opening the conversation through emotional connection rather than a frontal rebuttal. I set the PPT aside, lean forward, and look him straight in the eyes: 'Man, I'm not jealous of you. I'm scared for you. You've put 800,000 in — I'm not in any position to regret that for you. But if I don't say something today, then what kind of friend have I even been? Tell me honestly: can you afford to lose this money?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold back the emotional explosion for now, and ask them to stand up and lay out every piece of information in full — how much was borrowed, how much is left, whose accounts those are — first, contain the chaos inside a bounded container, then decide on the next step. I open my mouth and say: "Get up first. Sit down. Tell me all the numbers — every transaction, from the beginning."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to confront the conflict head-on, right here and now — not suppressing my emotions, not running from the relationship — staying to face it together, even with anger and tenderness pulling at me at the same time. I crouch down, lift her hands away from my pant leg, and hold them. My voice comes out a little rough: 'Get up. I can't hear you when you're kneeling like that. Then I want you to tell me everything, from the beginning, one thing at a time. I want to know all of it. I'm angry — but I'm not going anywhere.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to set aside my anger and judgment for now, pull him up off the floor first, and then ask him to walk me through everything in full — the whole picture of the debt, and what he plans to do next. I lean down and reach out my hand: "Get up first — the floor is cold. Then you're going to tell me everything, from the beginning. Not to ask for my forgiveness — so that I know where we actually stand right now."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to accept this explanation, and I'm not continuing as though things are the same between us. I gently moved her aside and said plainly that we're separating our finances for now — tomorrow we sit down and go through every account, every transaction, and where we each stand.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse his 'generosity' right there in front of everyone, and to push back against this humiliation with a cutting remark. I slide the business card back toward him, and with a cold smile I say: 'I appreciate the thought — but I'm much more used to being my own boss. That assistant position of yours? Save it for someone who needs their life managed for them.' Then I turn and walk out of the reception.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — I don't take the card. My manner is calm but clear; I give him nothing to grab onto emotionally. I set the orange juice glass down steadily, look at him, and say in an even voice: 'No need — I'm doing just fine.' Then I shift my gaze away and don't look at him again.

options.label: C
options.content: I decline the card, and in front of everyone I flip this so-called 'invitation' into a public counter-strike — letting every person in the room see exactly what is behind that business card. I don't reach out to take it. Instead, I smile slightly, my voice pitched just loud enough for two or three tables to catch every word: 'Generous enough? Twelve years ago you put your name on my proposal — now that was generous. Keep the assistant position. It's not for me — I've never been good at signing other people's names for them.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline the card, but in a way that neutralizes his performance rather than meeting it with direct confrontation or an outburst of anger. A slight smile, I gently push the card back toward him, and in a voice pitched just right — not too loud, not too soft — I say: 'Thanks for remembering me — though the assistant track isn't really a direction I'm interested in. Your team's projects over the years, on the other hand, I've always thought there was something worth looking into there. Catch up sometime?' Then I turn and start a new conversation with the person beside me.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to bring it out into the open directly — rather than going along with her and putting the envelope back, I speak up, gently but firmly. I set the envelope down softly on the table, look at her, and say: 'I saw it. Tell me what's going on — whatever it is, we'll face it together.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lay it all on the table. Instead of going along with her and putting the envelope away, I gently but clearly let her know I've seen what's in the report and ask her to be honest with me. I set the envelope softly on the table, look at her, and say: 'I saw it — whatever it says, I want to know together with you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to gently but directly let him know I've already seen the report, and invite him to tell me honestly what's going on — rather than smoothly handing the envelope back as if I hadn't. I set the envelope quietly on the table, look up at him, and say: 'I saw some of it. Would you be willing to tell me about it? I want to face this with you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to make it clear that I've already seen the report, and ask my partner to be honest with me — I won't follow the 'it's nothing serious' lead. I set the envelope gently on the table, look at my partner, and say: "I saw it. I don't want to pretend I didn't — will you tell me what's going on?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to promise secrecy—but not by turning away and betraying her. Instead, I stay by her side and tell her honestly that I can't make that promise, while trying to think through with her how to find help. I crouch down, hold her hands, voice trembling but not running: 'I can't promise you that—not because I don't care about you, but because I care too much. Let's find someone who can actually help you, together—just the two of us figuring out how to do it first, okay?'

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to promise secrecy, and instead choose to be honest — telling her I will seek help from an adult, while doing everything I can to stay by her side and not leave. I say quietly: 'I can't make that promise — not because I don't care about you, but because I'm scared something will happen to you. Let's go find a teacher together right now. I'll be with you the whole time.'

options.label: C
options.content: I didn't promise to keep it secret. Instead, I put the focus on just being there with her — trying to make sure she didn't feel abandoned, while leaving room to seek help later. I crouched down and said quietly: 'I'm not going to go around telling everyone — but I also can't promise to do nothing. Can you tell me what's actually been going on lately?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to promise secrecy right away. Instead, I steady her first — with my body and my words — so she feels caught, held. And inside, I've already decided I need to find a way to tell an adult. I take her hand, my voice shaking a little: 'I'm not going anywhere, I'm not going anywhere at all — but I can't promise to keep this secret, because I'm scared something will happen to you, and I'm more afraid of losing you than I am of you being angry at me. Can we just talk first? Tell me what's actually been going on with you lately.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak directly, briefly, and honestly — no performed emotion, no excessive modesty, no deliberate exaggeration of the creative process. I take the microphone and say, calmly: 'I wrote this piece off the top of my head three weeks ago. There was no particular inspiration — I just wrote whatever came to mind. I'm genuinely surprised to have won first place. Thank you to the judges.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to take the microphone and speak sincerely — to get through an impromptu acceptance speech by looking after the mood of the room, rather than retreating into silence. I gave a small smile into the microphone, voice a little unsteady but still finding the words: 'Thank you to the teacher and the judges. When I wrote this piece, I really just wanted to put down on paper a question that had been rattling around in my head and wouldn't go away… I never expected anyone to like it. I think maybe — the kind of confusion the story is about — maybe most of us have felt something like that at some point.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak honestly, treating this unexpected win itself as an interesting 'counter-intuitive phenomenon' to share, and naturally weaving in all the chaotic associations and questions I had while writing. I lean into the microphone and say: "Honestly, I didn't even proofread for typos before I submitted — so I think that fact is more interesting than my story itself: maybe the ideas that look 'not serious enough' are actually hiding something worth exploring."

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to muddle through and get out as fast as possible. I gripped the microphone, kept my head down, and mumbled a few words — 'I just wrote it casually, didn't think too much about it' — then tucked the trophy and certificate under my arm and practically fled off the stage, sinking back into my seat and folding myself into the crowd.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand by my own proposal, campaign independently over the weekend, and not seek a merger with her. I tell my friend: "Forget the merger — I'm not doing anything where the credit can't be sorted out. I'll go talk to people myself this weekend, bring the sketches along, and let them see for themselves."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to step away from the competition — I won't go talk to her about merging, and I won't spend the weekend canvassing votes. I'll let the proposal lose on its own terms. I tell my friend: "Forget it — if everyone likes her proposal, they should go with it. I'll keep mine for myself."

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to proactively approach the other person about collaboration, merging the two proposals together, and abandon the route of campaigning solo for votes. Say to my friend: 'Let's go talk to her — her plan solves the rehearsal problem, my plan has the content, and the two things can be pieced together. The credit doesn't matter; what matters is that the plan actually gets made.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to merge and not to campaign blindly—instead, I'm going to do a precise read of where the votes actually are. I'll identify the small number of people in the class who genuinely want the show to be good, not the ones who'd be happy just singing a few songs, and focus all my energy on convincing them. I tell my friend: 'Merging is off the table—if we do that, my concept is gone. Help me think: who in the class actually wants to put on a real show? Not the 'whatever, just sing' crowd—starting with those people, I'll go pitch my concept.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to leave the lively crowded table, pick up my tray, and walk over to the new student sitting alone in the corner — not forcing myself to seem warm or outgoing, just going over and sitting down. I carry my tray to that small table, set it down gently, and say quietly: 'Is it okay if I sit here?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively — walk over to the new student and break the ice in a task-oriented way. I pick up my tray, stand up, tell my friends 'Go ahead, I'll catch up,' then walk over to the table in the corner and sit down, saying to the new student: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — . It's your first day — if there's anything you're not sure about, feel free to ask me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to leave my original table, carry my tray over to the new student sitting alone in the corner, and skip going to the playground with my friends. I walk over with my tray and sit down across from her, saying quietly: 'Is it okay if I sit here? My name is — what's yours?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table and walk over to the new classmate sitting alone in the corner — curiosity and empathy driving the decision in equal measure. I pick up my tray, stand, and tell my friends, "Go ahead, I'll catch up," then walk to that corner table, sit down beside her, and ask: "Where are you from? What's fun to do over there?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau as an individual, while responding honestly to my neighbors' stance — accepting their behind-the-scenes support, but not hiding my disappointment in this collective silence. I say to the teacher upstairs: "I understand about the joint letter — I'm not going to force your names onto it. But I want you to know: this isn't just my business, it's all of our money. I'm going to file the complaint; help me fill in the supporting materials, but I won't be stopping this."

options.label: B
options.content: I've decided to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name — but I'm making it clear to the neighbors: quiet support is fine, but I'm not out here taking all the risk for everyone for nothing. I'll keep everyone posted as things develop. To the retired teacher, I said: "I understand why you won't sign your names — but I've already done the digging, and I'm going to file this complaint. However you want to help, that's up to you. Just don't expect me to carry the whole thing by myself and call it done."

options.label: C
options.content: I decide to file a formal complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name, and to give the upstairs neighbor a direct but measured response — no accusations, but a clear statement of where I stand and what I will and won't accept. To the upstairs neighbor, I say: 'I understand everyone's concerns. I'll file the complaint myself. But I need the materials and information you all promised me — that's my only ask. Two million isn't a matter of a few thousand dollars per household. It's a matter of whether anyone is going to hold this process accountable.'

options.label: D
options.content: He chooses to file a real-name report in his own name alone, refusing to let the neighbors' retreat or his wife's pleas change his position — but he won't push anyone to join him either. He says calmly to the old man upstairs: 'I understand your difficulties. I won't press anyone on the joint letter — but I'll go to the Housing and Construction Bureau myself. I've already got the materials ready.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to politely decline the specialist track and hold firm on keeping my child in the regular education system, without applying for the talent recruitment program. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you so much — knowing that you see him that way, we as parents are truly grateful. But this is too big a decision for me to make on the spot. I need to go home and talk it through properly with the family. His schoolwork is stable right now, and we don't want to rush into anything.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on committing and gather the key information first before making any decision. I tell the teacher: 'Thank you for letting us know. I need to talk with my child first and find out what he thinks, and I'd also like to learn more about the specifics of this program — things like the exit mechanism and academic safeguards. We'll get back to you after that.'

options.label: C
options.content: I've made up my mind to support the professional path — clearly and without reservation — while insisting that we call my kid out here right now for a three-way conversation, so he can say what he wants in his own words. I tell the teacher: "Personally, I'm in favor — but we need to ask him directly, right now. Can we bring him out here so the three of us can talk this through together?"

options.label: D
options.content: I decided not to express immediate support or opposition to the special recruitment, and instead to return the decision to my child first, while buying time for the whole family to discuss it together. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you — this is very significant news for us. Before we make any decision, I want to genuinely hear what my child himself has to say. This is ultimately his path to walk, and I want him to walk it because he truly wants to, not because of our expectations. Could you give us a little time?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to let go of the overseas opportunity and go home immediately to take care of Dad and handle the medical arrangements. I say into the phone: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — I'll be there first thing tomorrow morning. Don't worry, I'll take care of everything.'

options.label: B
options.content: She chooses to immediately abandon the overseas assignment and go home to be with her father and handle whatever comes next with his medical care. Voice breaking over the phone, she says: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — first flight tomorrow morning. Wait for me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to go home immediately to deal with Dad's illness, setting the overseas project decision aside for now, and will reach out to the company once the situation becomes clearer. I tell my mother: 'I understand — tell me which hospital Dad is in. I'll look up tickets tonight and take the earliest flight out. You stay calm for now. Once I'm there, I'll go talk to the doctors myself.'

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to set aside any deliberation over the overseas opportunity and go home first to be with Dad and help manage the medical arrangements. The career stuff can come later. I call Mom back and say: 'Mom, I'll be home tomorrow — don't worry too much, okay? I'm coming to be with you both.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to grab the thread of the conversation head-on — use humor and confidence to put my life on full display and dissolve the awkward charge of the class-gap moment right then and there. I lift my sake cup and sweep a grin around the table: 'Mysterious? That's because what I do isn't exactly in your wheelhouse — nonprofit projects by day, painting by night, and last year one of my pieces got into the local young artists' exhibition. Income? Enough to live on, and I'm living with a hell of a lot of energy. You say triple the pay — depends what you're tripling. Sure, triple the money, but if I lose this drive in the process, that's a terrible trade. Come on, drink up!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond with composure — neither dodging nor defending — and describe my current life with concrete facts, politely but clearly declining the well-meaning suggestions. I pick up my glass, smile, and say: 'I run projects at a nonprofit. The pay isn't high, but one of my paintings got selected for the local young artists' exhibition last year — feels pretty solid to me. I appreciate the offer about tripling my salary, but the path I'm on is working out well right now, so I'm not looking to switch.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to introduce my situation proactively, clearly, and with structure — no deflecting, no defending, just delivering a 'status report' on my own life the way a project manager would. I say: 'I do project planning at a nonprofit. The salary isn't high, but I manage every project from kickoff to close-out, and I manage it well. In my spare time I paint — last year I got into the local young artists' exhibition, which counts as a milestone. Renting is my choice for this stage; I haven't hit the point where buying makes sense. As for joining a big tech company — I evaluated it, and it doesn't fit my current execution roadmap.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to openly lay out my life, to present it as a fascinating cultural specimen rather than retreating into defensiveness or evasion. I pick up my glass, smile, and say: 'I work on projects at a nonprofit — the salary's honestly not much, I live in an urban village, but last year one of my paintings got into a youth art exhibition. I can't really keep up with everything you all are talking about, but honestly? I think the people at this table have turned into completely different species from one another — and I find that kind of captivating.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to actively embrace the transition — no more waiting on the sidelines — and to face tomorrow's conversation with my supervisor with an open mind. Tonight I reply to my former colleague: 'I've made up my mind too — let's do this together.' Then I open that AI product course page I've had bookmarked forever, and I click enroll.

options.label: B
options.content: Decline my former colleague's offer to team up for the transition and choose to plan my own learning path independently; at the same time, prepare a clear but noncommittal position for tomorrow's manager meeting — acknowledge that I'm evaluating a transition direction, but commit to no specific timeline, in order to preserve my autonomy. Concrete actions: send my former colleague a short text message — 'Still thinking it over, let's each do our own thing for now' — then open a document and quietly map out a self-study path for AI-related skills alongside the time window my current savings can support.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to wait and watch — I won't accept my former colleague's invitation, and I'll prepare to tell my supervisor tomorrow that I'm willing to align with the company's current direction and that I want to keep my position. I reply to my former colleague: 'I support your decision, but I've got family pressure on my end — quitting isn't something I'm considering right now. I'll see how things play out here first.' Then I open my calendar and accept the supervisor's invite.

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to spend tonight systematically building a decision framework, then walk into tomorrow's conversation with my manager armed with clear analysis — rather than making any hasty declarations. I open my laptop, create a new document, type "Transition Path Assessment" as the title, and begin listing out the timeline milestones, resource requirements, risk items, and quantifiable evaluation criteria for both paths, while in a separate column I draft the agenda for tomorrow's conversation and note the key information I need to get from my manager.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation, but to be honest about why rather than brush it off with an excuse. I glanced down at the table for a moment, then looked up and said: 'I'm really grateful you said all of this — these past few months have meant something to me too — but I can't go. Not because I don't have time. It's because I know what it would mean for both of us, and I can't do that to you, and I can't do that to myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: Accept the invitation — but also decide to have a real conversation with my partner that same night, and face whatever has actually gone wrong between us. I say to them: "I'd love to go, I've been wanting to catch this talk too — but there's something I need to tell you first, about where things have been between us lately."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to gently decline the invitation and hold this feeling exactly where it is, not letting it go any further. I smiled and said: "I might have something on that day — but these past few months working together have been a real pleasure. Hope the project wraps up smoothly."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation directly but calmly, while offering a genuine acknowledgment of the partnership — no avoidance, no stalling. I say to them: 'Thank you for telling me this. These past few months really have felt like smooth collaboration — that's true. But I can't accept the Saturday invitation. I think we both know it's not just an ordinary colleague outing.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to actively respond to his suggestion of "going out for a walk together" — to break the stalemate and keep us from drifting apart. I gave a small, quiet smile and said: "Let's go. You mentioned going out for a walk just now — I think that's a good idea. Let's go together."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to make any active changes for now, and instead return to the familiar busyness to keep the days stable. I tell him: let's just keep things the way they've always been — I'll head to the shop first, and when I get back I'll tidy up around the house.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to sidestep this shared strangeness — to gently acknowledge that it exists, rather than each of us retreating back into the familiar busyness. I look down at the table and say: "This does feel strange. How about… we have a cup of coffee together first? No need to think about what comes next."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to break the silence and take the initiative to suggest we go out for a walk together, rather than each retreating back into our familiar routines. I look up at him and say: 'Let's go — just go for a walk, no need to decide where, just walk.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to express my anger directly, on the spot — refuse the money, say what needs to be said, and give my uncle no opening to wrap this up with a "kids don't know any better." I push the stack of cash back toward him, voice controlled but clear: "Uncle, I don't want the money. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't something you can make right by replacing a few hard drives. I need you to understand how serious this is. I'm not trying to make things difficult for you, but I can't just let this go."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse the money, say my piece briefly, and end this conversation as quickly as possible — without getting into a direct argument with my uncle, but without pretending everything is fine either. I push the stack of money back across the coffee table, my voice flat, and say only one thing: 'Keep the money. What was in there isn't a money matter.' Then I stand up to go get some water, and I don't look at them again.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to express my anger directly and refuse the money — but without breaking down or losing control, drawing a boundary in a clear, steady voice. I push the stack of bills back, my voice calmer than I expected, but firm: 'This isn't about money, Uncle. Six months of my work was in there. That can't be bought back.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse the money and say outright that the loss cannot be compensated financially — my tone is restrained but my position is clear. I won't get into a heated confrontation with Uncle, but I will not accept this way of 'settling things.' I push the stack of bills back, my voice kept very low but very steady: 'Uncle, I can't take this money. What was on there is six months of my life — it can't be bought back. Please sit with the child for a moment; I need to think about how to handle this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on with an open stance, and to actively reframe the senior team member's provocation as a topic for the whole group to work through together—establishing my rhythm as team lead rather than shutting him down. I smile, stand up straight, and speak at a volume that's calm but carries to every corner of the room: "Good question—that module is actually exactly what I want us to dig into today. Old Li, you've got the deepest experience on this one. When we meet in a bit, you go first, and we'll all figure out together how to move it forward."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on—no deflecting, no escalating—and set the tone with a steady, grounded composure. I speak up, voice even, neither rushed nor slow: 'Good question. I've already looked at the unfinished module—this team meeting today is exactly where we sort out the schedule. Let's all sit down and walk through it together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on and transform it into the opening topic for the team meeting — neither avoiding it nor confronting it combatively, but making the question itself the starting point for discussion. I say: 'Good question — that module is actually one of the core things we're tackling today. Let's all sit down and take it apart together. I want to hear everyone's read on it, including yours.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on and establish authority through data and framework, rather than deflecting or firing back emotionally. I hold his gaze, voice steady: 'Good question. I have the specifics on the unfinished module and the full quarterly schedule right here — let's go through them now. Everyone please turn to item one on the agenda.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to decline my ex's request to get back together — gently but clearly — and decide to tell my partner that same day about how the meeting went. I quietly pull my hand back and say, calmly: 'I'm glad you've figured things out, but I'm with someone now, and things are really good between us — I can't let him down. I'll be telling him about today.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to give a clear rejection on the spot, and I decide that when I get home I will proactively tell my current partner everything about this meeting — closing every open variable. To my ex, I say: 'I hear what you're saying, but I'm in a relationship I'm genuinely invested in right now. I can't give you that space of 'just waiting' — it wouldn't be fair to any of the three of us. My answer is no.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to turn down my ex's request to get back together, but my response is vague and riddled with self-attacking apologies — and I decide to hide the details of this meeting from my current boyfriend. I say to my ex: "We can't go back to what we were. You deserve better. I'm sorry for making you wait so long" — then I lower my head, unable to meet his eyes, while inside I've already started running through how I'm going to explain to my current boyfriend why I'm coming home late tonight.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to respond to my ex honestly about my uncertainty, and at the same time I decide to tell my current partner, that same day, everything about this meeting. I say to my ex: 'I can't tell you I've completely moved on — but I'm with someone now, and I'm not going to let the fact that I still feel something destroy what we have. Tonight I'm going to tell him that we met.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to confront him directly, face to face — demanding that he rewrite the plagiarized sections before submission, no dancing around it, no going to the professor behind his back, giving him one chance to handle it himself, but leaving absolutely no room for ambiguity in my tone. The moment he walks through the door, I speak up: 'I just looked at your document, and there are several passages that are word for word identical to articles on that Q&A site — I searched it myself. You heard what the professor said about the rules. You need to rewrite it yourself before we hand this in the day after tomorrow, because if you don't, I can't submit this thing together with you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell him face-to-face, privately, when he gets back — giving him the chance to rewrite it before submission — rather than staying silent or going straight to the professor. When he pushes the door open, I look up and say: 'I accidentally saw your document just now, and I searched a few passages — they're a perfect match with an article on Zhihu. The deadline is the day after tomorrow; you still have time to fix it, and I can help you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to confront my roommate directly the moment he walks back in — tell him what I found, demand he rewrite it tonight, no dancing around it, and no going to the professor. I wait for him to come through the door and say: 'I just happened to see what was on your screen, looked it up — it's straight from Zhihu, word for word. You need to rewrite it tonight. There's still time before the day after tomorrow. I'm not going to the professor about this, but you have to fix it yourself.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up before my roommate gets back — to tell him directly what I found, framing it as 'I came across it by accident, and we need to talk,' and to demand that he rewrite it before submission, while making clear that the consequences fall on both of us. I stand up, walk to the door, and the moment it opens I say: 'I accidentally saw what was on your screen just now, and I searched a few of the passages — we need to talk right now, because the deadline is the day after tomorrow and both our names are on this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up briefly — raising my concern without trying to hard-sell my alternative — and leave the file as a written supplement for them to decide on. I say: 'I still think there could be some strain when it comes to scaling later on. I'll write up the plan and send it to everyone — whichever way you go is up to you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to put forward the alternative proposal at this moment — presenting the core judgment in a clear, direct way, no beating around the bush, letting the file speak for itself. I say: "My proposal is still here. I spent a few days putting together an alternative path — including the implementation approach and the risk points. I can walk through it in five minutes. I think raising it now saves more time than reworking things later. Can you give me those five minutes?"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to raise the alternative plan on the spot — to open things up rather than quietly go along. I turn my screen toward the group and say: 'Actually, yes — I've put together a different approach over here. Give it two minutes; if everyone feels it's not worth discussing, we'll get straight back to dividing up tasks — but I want this plan to be seen at least once.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to drop the idea of raising an alternative proposal, fall in line with the current consensus, and not create friction at this moment. I shook my head and said: 'Nothing from me — let's go with the plan as it stands. I'll work with whatever assignments come my way.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to report the data fraud to my supervisor and refuse to sign off confirming the data is valid — even though I am terrified of the interpersonal fallout that will follow. I tell my colleague: 'I know this puts you in a hard place, but I can't sign off on this. Tonight I'm going to tell our supervisor exactly what I know. You should probably go explain yourself first.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to report the data falsification truthfully to my supervisor, and refuse to sign off on the fabricated data. Reply directly in the project management system, flag the data as anomalous and unverifiable, and inform my supervisor of the situation.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to report the data falsification to my superiors truthfully and to refuse to sign off on the data. I tell my colleague: 'I understand the position you're in, but I can't sign off on this data, and I can't hide this from our superiors. I'll be reporting it honestly tonight.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, while giving my colleague the opportunity to correct the error himself rather than going over his head directly. I tell him: 'I can't sign off on this data — but before I say anything to anyone above us, I'm giving you tonight. You go to the project team yourself and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the only concession I can make.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honestly explain the situation at home while also trying to propose a middle-ground arrangement — working remotely or staggering the tasks to get part of the work done — rather than simply agreeing or outright refusing. I say to the director: 'I understand how important this client situation is, but I have a family emergency this weekend — my father has a follow-up medical appointment at the hospital and I have to be there with him; that's something I already promised them. I wanted to ask: would it be possible for me to work on the core competitive benchmarking section tonight and tomorrow morning remotely, send it to you Saturday evening for a first look, and then handle the remaining formatting and layout adjustments when I get back Sunday? If that timeline doesn't work for you, just let me know and we'll figure something else out.'

options.label: B
options.content: I don't agree outright to being on-site the entire weekend. Instead, I propose a division of labor and a remote-coverage plan. I explain that I need to go back home to accompany my parents for Dad's follow-up appointment, but that I'll start tonight by drawing up a list of gaps, coordinate with colleagues on the market side to share the load, and complete my portion of the work remotely over the weekend.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to explain the situation at home and hold firm on going back, while offering a compromise to soften the blow of saying no. I tell the director: 'My dad has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend — I promised three days ago that I'd go with my parents, so there's really no way I can change that. But I can put together a written document tonight with the competitive market analysis section and send it to you. Maybe you could find someone to work off that for the redesign? Or I could do a remote review of the final draft Sunday evening?'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose partial compromise while holding the line: make it clear to the director that the weekend trip home is not being canceled, but offer to put in extra hours tonight to push the project forward, provide remote support on the critical sections over the weekend, and join a video call Sunday evening for the final-draft review. Say directly: 'I have a family commitment this weekend — it was locked in three days ago and I can't cancel it. I can stay late tonight and I'm available remotely over the weekend, but coming into the office isn't something I can do. Tell me which parts are the biggest bottlenecks and let's work out the priorities together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to go on the offensive — counter with evidence directly, no explanations, no apologies, no showing weakness. I snapped my phone screen around toward the live-stream camera, my voice calm but unmistakably clear: 'You're live-streaming? Good. Let everyone get a good look at this screen — this is the work group chat, the timestamp is right there, my phone was never pointed at you.' Then I pulled my collar free from their grip and held their gaze.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to prove my innocence through physical evidence rather than arguing out loud — I force my phone screen outward and thrust it up high toward the onlookers and the live-stream cameras. Holding the screen facing out, I raise it as high as I can and shout at every lens in front of me: 'Look carefully — this is a work group chat, I was scrolling through messages, I was not taking photos. Can anyone here actually read what's on this screen?'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to actively present the evidence and break the spectator structure, rather than endure in silence or fight back aggressively. I held my phone screen up as high as I could and said, as loudly but as steadily as I could manage: 'Everyone look — this is my work group chat, messages sent at four in the morning, I've been reading this the whole time, my phone has been held at exactly this height, screen facing out — anyone can come and look.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to proactively present the evidence and use facts to restore order — no fleeing, no breaking down, no fighting fire with fire against the other person's emotions. I raise my phone screen high, turning it toward the cameras on all sides, my voice low but clear: 'Please look at the screen carefully — this is my company's work group chat, the timestamp is four in the morning, I was reading a new project brief, not the camera app, not the photo album. Anyone here is welcome to verify it.'

options.label: A
options.content: Decline outright — no hedging, no need for a lengthy explanation. I push the phone back and say, calmly: 'It's fine, I can finish it myself. That kind of thing carries way too much risk — it's not worth it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to refuse the service and commit to finishing the thesis on my own, without any compromise. I shook my head and pushed the phone back, saying: "I don't need this — I'll write it myself. Don't send me the contact info."

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse to use the service and insist on completing the thesis independently. I shook my head, pushed the phone back, and said: "No thanks — this one I'll do myself. There's still so much about my own topic I haven't worked out yet; if I handed it off to someone else, I'd never be able to sleep."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse the service — I'm not going to look into it further or reach out. I waved my roommate off and said: "Forget it, I'll figure it out myself — this kind of thing isn't for me."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to give an answer immediately, and instead ask for a full breakdown of the terms, the equity structure, and the exit mechanism — keeping the decision firmly in my own hands. I tell my friend: 'I need to see the complete terms and the equity agreement. Give me three days, and I'll give you an answer that's grounded in evidence — not one that was rushed out of me.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving a definitive answer and ask for a specific window of time to think — rather than refusing or agreeing on the spot. I say to my friend: "You know I've been following this direction seriously for a long time, and your invitation genuinely means a lot to me — I need a few days to really think it through. Can you give me a week? I don't want to give you an answer I haven't earned."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to decline this opportunity to change careers, turning down my friend's invitation on the grounds of protecting what I've built. I tell my friend: 'I understand your enthusiasm, and I do trust your instincts — but I can't walk away from my shop and my clients. This path isn't the right one for me. Find someone who's a better fit, and I'll be right here cheering you on.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the career change opportunity, but I ask for a little time to arrange a financial buffer rather than signing immediately. I tell my friend: 'I'm in — but give me two weeks. I need to wrap things up properly on this end. Not because I'm hesitating, but because I want to show up giving everything I've got.'

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to reject his partner's proposal, with preserving the existing relationship framework as his immediate priority, and sets his phone aside for the moment. I put down my phone, looked at her, and said, my voice a little tight: 'I don't understand what you mean by that kind of flexibility, but I know I don't want to change the way things are between us right now — if you feel like something isn't enough, just tell me directly, and we can talk about it, but not like this.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to set my phone aside for now and stay present in this moment, replacing an immediate stance with genuine listening — neither accepting nor rejecting my partner's proposal, but returning the question to the conversation itself. I turn my phone face-down on the sofa, look at my partner, and say: 'I need to understand what you're actually saying first — when you say flexibility, what does that mean to you?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to reject this boundary adjustment and explicitly uphold the original commitment. I'll silence my phone first, then look him in the eye and say: 'I don't accept this kind of relationship. If this is what you need, then let's have a real conversation about whether we're still doing this at all.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold off on taking any position and prioritize gathering information, asking her to spell out exactly what the specific boundaries are. I set down my phone, looked at her, and said: 'When you say flexibility, I'd like to hear you be a little more specific — the independent space you're talking about, what kinds of things does that mean, and how far does it go?'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose palliative care — honoring the exhaustion and resignation in his father's eyes, refusing to trade his last shred of dignity for a 20% chance. I lifted my head, my voice a little hoarse, and said to the doctor: 'Palliative care. Let him be comfortable. We'll be with him.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion — palliative care, letting go of that 20% chance. I look at the doctor, my voice a little hoarse, and say: 'We'll go with comfort care. Let him go with some dignity.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to honor my father's wishes and decide on palliative care, stating that choice plainly without flinching. I say to the doctor: 'We're choosing palliative care — please initiate the comfort care protocol. He has made his choice, and we respect it.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose palliative care, honor the exhaustion and wishes my father has expressed, and refuse to let a low-probability course of chemotherapy turn his remaining time into suffering. I speak to the doctor: 'Start the comfort-care protocol—let him spend whatever time he has left in peace.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to avoid the academic opportunity, declining the possibility on the grounds of family stability and practical concerns, and not replying to the advisor's email. Say to her: "You're right — I've thought it over too, and this just isn't the right time. The shop still needs me, and I'm not planning to reply to that email."

options.label: B
options.content: She chooses to honestly express her genuine longing for this academic opportunity, while sincerely receiving his concerns and inviting him into a shared conversation rather than making a unilateral decision; she holds off on replying to her advisor's email for now, and first opens up to the person beside her. She gently takes his hand and says: "I've been staring at this email because I really do want to go — I know it would change a lot of things, and I know you have reservations, and I want to listen to all of them seriously. Can we talk tonight — not me trying to convince you, but the two of us figuring it out together?"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to honestly express my assessment of the academic opportunity and to formally reply to my advisor accepting the invitation. I'll say: 'I've thought it through. This opportunity is the right call logically, and I'm going to take it. As for the life logistics — I'll handle them. But they're not a reason to walk away from this.'

options.label: D
options.content: Honestly express how strongly I want this academic opportunity, acknowledge the uncertainty, but make it clear that I want to seriously consider accepting the invitation — and that same evening, reply to my advisor's email with an initial expression of intent. I tell him: "I know this will change a lot of things, and I'm scared too. But when I found that notebook last night, I realized I haven't really been alive for a long time — I want to reply to that email, and we can figure out the rest together."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to reply, but keep the emotional register within a friendly, restrained boundary — not actively deepening things, but not coldly cutting them off either. I write back: "Made it home. The meeting today was actually pretty worthwhile. That bookstore sounds worth checking out sometime — it seems really quiet. Good night."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to reply to my colleague's message for now — cutting off the bud with self-attack, pressing the longing back down. I darken the phone screen, pick up my partner's message, reply with a single "okay," then close both chat windows and stare at the blurred night outside the car window, telling myself: you could never hold something like this together.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to reply to my colleague but deliberately narrow the boundary, using a polite yet clear-eyed tone to pull the connection back to a safe distance. I write: 'Made it home — thanks for checking in. That conversation the other day was really lovely; I'll have to recommend the bookstore to you sometime. Good night.' — Then I put down my phone, open my notes app, and add tomorrow's tutoring run with the kids to my schedule.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to reply to my colleague's message. I flip the phone face-down on the table and let that message just sit there. My partner's "tutoring tomorrow" message I answer with a quick "okay," and then I close my eyes and don't think about any of it anymore.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to fulfill the responsibility I was entrusted with first, then make my way to the amusement park as soon as I can — resolving the conflict by doing both. I tell Mom: 'I'll go to school first thing tomorrow morning to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, and once that's done I'll head straight to find my cousin — I should still make it in time. Can you let Uncle know for me?'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to do both, balancing responsibility and the outing through careful timing. Tell Mom: 'I want to go! But I have to stop by school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — my teacher gave me the key, and I made a promise. I'll head over early in the morning, and once I'm done we can leave. We'll make it in time!'

options.label: C
options.content: I decide to do both things: go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, then head to the amusement park, and explain my reasoning to Mom upfront. I say to her: 'Mom, I'm the Little Garden Keeper this week — the teacher gave me the key. Tomorrow morning I'll go to school first and water the plants and feed the tadpoles real quick, and then we leave. It won't take long, we'll still make it in time.'

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to do both things, by arranging my time in advance to fit everything in — go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, then head to the amusement park for my cousin's birthday party. I say to Mom: 'Mom, tomorrow morning I'll go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — it won't take long — and then we can go to the amusement park. That way neither one gets missed, okay?'

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to make the choice either of them is demanding, and in a calm, direct way I hand the decision back to the adults.
