I speak up: 'This is between the two of you — it's not my business. I'm not going anywhere and I'm not staying for either of you. You decide.' Then I turn around, walk back to my room, and close the door.

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to choose between them, trying to break through this forced ultimatum with tears and silence. I crouch down, wrap my arms around my knees, lower my head, and let myself cry out loud — without saying a single word, just shaking my head over and over.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay, expressing through the rigid stillness of my body a despair that cannot make any choice, while instinctively leaning toward the 'stay' side. I drew my shoulders in tight, my feet didn't move, and in a voice barely above a whisper I said: "I'm not leaving… I'm not going anywhere…" Then I crouched down, covered my face, and cried out loud.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to freeze in place and stay silent as a way of refusing to make this choice, while instinctively trying to redirect my attention toward the concrete mess that can actually be dealt with. I crouch down and start picking up the shards of porcelain from the floor, and in a very small voice I say: "Don't go yet — there are pieces on the floor, you'll cut your feet."

options.label: A
options.content: Accept the overall direction on the surface, while fighting for a voice at the implementation level — trading 'I'll oversee the transition' for real room to preserve the process details that matter. I speak up: "Fine, I have no issue with the overall direction. But if I'm the one overseeing the transition, then I need to break this down according to my own understanding of how these workflows operate. I'll hold the pace you want — but when it comes to the specifics of what gets changed, I need you to give me the space to do that."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to accept the overall direction while raising specific risk nodes on the spot, pushing for a transition buffer and written confirmation — rather than silently complying or confronting him head-on. I flip open the proposal, press my finger on the first timeline node, and keep my voice steady but unyielding: 'I accept the overall direction, but there are three risk points here that need to be confirmed before execution — if we don't get them written into the transition plan this week, I can't guarantee a problem won't affect the launch date, and I need you to give me a written record of the decision basis.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to appear cooperative on the surface while quietly buying time — raising risk details as a way of creating friction without direct confrontation. I speak up: 'I hear you on the direction, and I'll follow through on execution — but there are a few risk points in the transition period you may not have seen yet. Let me write them up; take a look and decide whether we should go over them first, so if something does go wrong later, nobody's left scrambling to explain it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to engage by asking questions — approaching this with curiosity rather than direct confrontation or silent compliance — in order to open up space for real dialogue within the execution framework. I say: 'My objection isn't that we can't change things — but I want to ask you first: what specific observations about the existing process is this proposal based on? Because some of those steps were designed the way they were to solve particular problems. If you know which problems, we can figure out together whether the new process actually covers them.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the model being lost, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately pivot to figuring out how to complete the presentation without it. Looking the homeroom teacher in the eye: 'The model was lost on the way to school — he left it on the bus. Without the model, we'll complete the presentation using a verbal explanation and the board. I'll take the lead.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the model being lost, refuse to go along with the lie, and proactively offer to carry the presentation through with a verbal explanation alone. I say directly to the homeroom teacher: 'He left the model on the bus — there's no getting it back. We don't have the model, but I have the whole thing memorized. I'll present it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to go along with the lie, choose to tell the homeroom teacher honestly that the model was lost, and immediately pivot to completing the presentation through verbal, impromptu explanation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but clearly: 'The model was left on the bus on the way to school. We don't have a prop, but we can just talk through it — I'll describe how the solar system works. We don't need the model.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to go along with the lie, but I also don't throw my teammate under the bus on the spot — instead, I steer the conversation directly toward how to handle the presentation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but quickly: 'The model had an accident on the way here and didn't make it, but we can go up and present without it — I have all the content memorized.'

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to accept this arrangement through silence. I choose to send a real response in the group chat, making it clear that the current division of responsibilities is not sustainable, and demanding that we renegotiate the specifics of how things are shared. I type into the group: "Money isn't the issue — the issue is that this isn't something I can carry alone. We need to sit down and seriously talk about how to divide this, and that means time and people, not just money." Then I put down my phone and turn to walk toward my partner.

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to accept this unilaterally decided arrangement in the group chat. I choose to make my position explicitly clear in the family chat in writing, demanding that everyone renegotiate the specific division of responsibilities and cost-sharing — not a money-for-labor swap. I type into the group: "I wasn't part of this discussion. Caregiving isn't something one person should have to carry just because they live closest. I need everyone to sit down together and work out exactly how to divide this — both the time and the money need to be recalculated." Then I put down my phone and turn toward my partner.

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to accept this unequal arrangement of trading money for labor, and I make it clear in the group chat that we need to renegotiate how caregiving duties are divided — rather than unilaterally making me carry more. I type in the group: "Let's set the money aside for now. Caregiving isn't one person's job. Next week we sit down face to face and work out exactly how much time and help each household can actually contribute — because the way things are going, none of us can keep this up."

options.label: D
options.content: I'm not accepting another round of carrying the bulk of the caregiving alone — but I'm not blowing things up in the open either. I reply in the group chat: both money and caregiving need to be shared on a rotating schedule; I'll cover the medication this time, and tomorrow I'll send out the list and the shift rotation. Then I turn and quietly tell my partner that we need to talk tonight — really talk.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the surgical consent. I end the CEO's video call and say into the doctor's phone: "I'm leaving now — I'll be there in fifteen minutes. Keep him stable. Wait for my signature."

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the medical authorization. Call the hospital back to confirm the surgical plan, and at the same time send a text to the CEO: 'My father is in emergency surgery. I have to go to the hospital. I'm sorry.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the medical authorization, while activating the emergency contingency plan to have the team take over as the main presenter. I call the CEO and say: 'My father has gone into a critical situation during surgery — I need to be there in person to sign right now. Please arrange a backup presenter. I'll provide remote support if at all possible. I'm sorry.' Then I hang up and tell the driver to go straight to the hospital.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to put down the speech immediately and head to the hospital, leaving the launch event for the team to handle on an emergency basis. I tell the CEO: 'My father's surgery requires my signature — it's the one thing that cannot be replaced. Please have the team hold things down at the launch; I'll deal with the follow-up afterward.' Then I hang up, and tell the driver to reroute to the hospital.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to use the answer sheet, and I turn down my friend's request as well, deciding to return the paper to the class rep or put it back where I found it. I tell my friend: 'This isn't ours — I'm going to give it back. If you want to study together, we could go to the library after school. Honestly, there's still a lot about this test I haven't figured out, and working through it might actually turn out to be more interesting than we'd think.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request and decide to hand the answer sheet over to the teacher or put it back where it belongs — I'm not keeping this risk around. I tell my friend: 'I can't copy from this, and you shouldn't either. This belongs to the teacher — I'm turning it in. You should go ahead.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend and either hand the paper to the teacher or put it back where it came from. I whisper to her, "We can't use this — we'll get caught," and then, while she's not paying attention, I slip the paper back into the crack in the desk or hand it to the teacher, pretending I just happened to find it by accident.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to use the answer sheet, and I turn down my friend's request — putting the paper back where it came from or handing it to the teacher. I shake my head at my friend and say: "We can't use this — put it back. If anyone finds out, we're in serious trouble. Let's just go study on our own."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation, letting the exhilaration drown out my financial reservations, and I give him a definitive yes on the spot. I look at the screen, grinning, and say: 'Do you even need to ask? I've been waiting for this chance. When do we start — I can be there tomorrow.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to decline this opportunity to change careers, turning down my friend's invitation on the grounds of protecting what I've built. I tell my friend: 'I understand your enthusiasm, and I do trust your instincts — but I can't walk away from my shop and my clients. This path isn't the right one for me. Find someone who's a better fit, and I'll be right here cheering you on.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to accept the career change opportunity, but I ask for a little time to arrange a financial buffer rather than signing immediately. I tell my friend: 'I'm in — but give me two weeks. I need to wrap things up properly on this end. Not because I'm hesitating, but because I want to show up giving everything I've got.'

options.label: D
options.content: Decline the opportunity gracefully, but with a genuine and respectful reason rather than a hollow brush-off. Say to the friend: 'Thank you for thinking of me — I read through the plan carefully, and I understand what you need. But honestly, the way I am right now just isn't suited to joining a team. Working alone is the only way I can actually do things well. Forcing that to change would only hold you back. Find someone who's a better fit for this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak my honest assessment gently but directly — not sidestepping the pyramid-scheme structure for what it is, but keeping the focus on my friend's state of mind rather than on who's right or wrong, and refusing to let his defensive attacks pull me off course. I set down the PowerPoint, look at him, and say: "I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're stupid — I just saw some things in this material that made me genuinely worried about you, and I want to talk about it seriously, not to tear you down, but because you're someone I care about."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to confront him directly, without flinching from the conflict, even at the risk of blowing up the friendship entirely. I slide the PPT back across the table and speak — calm, but without any give in my voice: 'You want to call it jealousy, fine, think whatever you like. But I have to say this clearly today — this is a pyramid scheme, not a business. The eight hundred thousand you've already put in, I don't know if you'll ever see it again. But you're asking me to get in with you, and that's something I can't do. Won't do.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to keep trying to dissuade him — I'll lay out the factual hallmarks of a pyramid scheme structure directly, decline the investment invitation, and not dignify the 'jealousy' accusation with a response. I say: 'I'm not putting money in, and it's not because I'm jealous. This model of yours — recruiting downlines, tiered profit-sharing, returns that depend on the people below you rather than on any actual product — that's the textbook structure of a pyramid scheme. There's nothing I can do about the eight hundred thousand that's already gone, but don't ask me to hand over this hundred and fifty thousand.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up directly and urge him to stop — without backing down because of his emotional reaction or the risk to our friendship — using facts and logic as my tools, refusing to trade silence for a surface-level peace. I flip the PowerPoint back to the first page, look him in the eye, and say: 'I'm not jealous of you. I've just seen too many people caught up in this exact structure and watched how it ends. Your income right now comes from the people you recruit, not from any product or service — that's the legal definition of a pyramid scheme, not my opinion, the law's definition. Eight hundred thousand is already gone, but you're still pulling more people in — that's what I'm actually worried about.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to make clear that I've seen the report and ask my partner to be honest with me about what's going on — I won't follow his lead into avoidance. I set the envelope down gently on the table, didn't hand it back to him, and said quietly: 'I saw it. Can you tell me what's going on?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lay it all out in the open — to ask my partner to honestly explain what the report says, and to refuse to play along with his attempts to change the subject. I set the envelope flat on the table, look at him, and say: 'I saw it. It's the results of a medical check-up. You can tell me now — what does it say.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to make it clear that I've already seen the report, and ask my partner to be honest with me — I won't follow the 'it's nothing serious' lead. I set the envelope gently on the table, look at my partner, and say: "I saw it. I don't want to pretend I didn't — will you tell me what's going on?"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to bring it out into the open directly — rather than going along with her and putting the envelope back, I speak up, gently but firmly. I set the envelope down softly on the table, look at her, and say: 'I saw it. Tell me what's going on — whatever it is, we'll face it together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I decided not to express immediate support or opposition to the special recruitment, and instead to return the decision to my child first, while buying time for the whole family to discuss it together. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you — this is very significant news for us. Before we make any decision, I want to genuinely hear what my child himself has to say. This is ultimately his path to walk, and I want him to walk it because he truly wants to, not because of our expectations. Could you give us a little time?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on committing and gather the key information first before making any decision. I tell the teacher: 'Thank you for letting us know. I need to talk with my child first and find out what he thinks, and I'd also like to learn more about the specifics of this program — things like the exit mechanism and academic safeguards. We'll get back to you after that.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to politely decline the specialist track and hold firm on keeping my child in the regular education system, without applying for the talent recruitment program. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you so much — knowing that you see him that way, we as parents are truly grateful. But this is too big a decision for me to make on the spot. I need to go home and talk it through properly with the family. His schoolwork is stable right now, and we don't want to rush into anything.'

options.label: D
options.content: I've made up my mind to support the professional path — clearly and without reservation — while insisting that we call my kid out here right now for a three-way conversation, so he can say what he wants in his own words. I tell the teacher: "Personally, I'm in favor — but we need to ask him directly, right now. Can we bring him out here so the three of us can talk this through together?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — I don't take the card. My manner is calm but clear; I give him nothing to grab onto emotionally. I set the orange juice glass down steadily, look at him, and say in an even voice: 'No need — I'm doing just fine.' Then I shift my gaze away and don't look at him again.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — tone steady and clear, giving him no emotional leverage, letting no one around me see a single flicker of panic. I push the business card back toward him, voice pitched just right, loud enough for the people nearby to hear: 'Thanks, I'm good. The work I do now — I use my brain.' Then I turn away and go back to my orange juice.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly, giving him no emotional reaction to exploit, letting the whole performance collapse in plain sight with nothing but calm. I glanced down at the card, then looked up, my voice as level as if I were stating a simple fact: 'No need. For the assistant position, I'd suggest finding someone who doesn't know how you got to where you are — it'll save you a lot of headaches.' Then I picked up my orange juice and walked away.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline the card, but in a way that neutralizes his performance rather than meeting it with direct confrontation or an outburst of anger. A slight smile, I gently push the card back toward him, and in a voice pitched just right — not too loud, not too soft — I say: 'Thanks for remembering me — though the assistant track isn't really a direction I'm interested in. Your team's projects over the years, on the other hand, I've always thought there was something worth looking into there. Catch up sometime?' Then I turn and start a new conversation with the person beside me.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and respond, but I wrap my disagreement in an extremely cautious, deferential register — framing the challenge inside a shell of 'I may have misunderstood' to reduce the risk of being attacked. I stand, my voice slightly trembling, eyes still lowered: 'Professor, I came across a book about this period of history last week — it cited some primary source documents, and it seemed like… there might be a small discrepancy with the conclusion you mentioned. Is it possible I've misread it? Could you help me work through it?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly — respectful in tone, but clear in position — and actively bring the arguments and primary sources from my outside reading into the classroom discussion. I stand and say: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history in the library — it cites several primary-source documents and offers a different interpretation of the causal relationship here. I'd love to hear your thoughts on that reading.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak up from a posture of seeking guidance rather than issuing a challenge, using a question to bring the differing account from my outside reading into the classroom rather than directly pointing out that the teacher is wrong. I stand up, steady my voice slightly, and say: 'Sir, I was reading an academic book on this period of history last week, and it cited some archival sources whose interpretation of this issue seems to differ a little from what you've described — I'm not sure if I've misunderstood something, but would you be willing to take a look with me?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly but without aggression, holding out my notebook as evidence. I stand and say: 'Teacher, last week I read an academic book that draws on primary archival sources, and the way it describes this issue is a bit different from what you've explained — I have notes on it right here. Would it be alright if I shared them?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to temporarily conceal the contents of the note, while deciding to go to the after-school meetup — to understand the situation first before making any plans. I quickly fold the note and tuck it into the deepest part of my textbook, then look up and smile at my best friend, saying: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking to borrow my notes. Nothing interesting.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and hold off on the after-school plan for now, putting my friend's feelings ahead of my own. I fold the note casually and tuck it back into my textbook, then smile over at her and say: "It's nothing — someone from the next class asking to borrow my notes. Keep working on your homework."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note and refuse to let my friend see it; at the same time, I'm deeply resistant to the idea of showing up at all, and I'm leaning toward not going. I flip my textbook over, press the note underneath it, look up, and give a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking about homework. Nothing interesting.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note — I shield it with my body, fold it quickly, and stuff it back into my textbook, then brush off my best friend with a lie. As for whether to show up after school, I haven't decided yet, but I'm leaning toward not going, or putting off the decision until the very last second. I press my textbook down over the note and look up at her with a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking to borrow my notes.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to campaign independently, stand by the original proposal, and let the idea's own appeal win people over — rather than merging it with another plan just to play it safe. I tell my friend: "I want to try on my own first. This weekend I'll go find people, show them the sketches, let them really picture that space — and if I've made my case and still lose, I'll accept it. But I don't want to gut the whole thing before I've even had a chance to show it."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to step away from the competition — I won't go talk to her about merging, and I won't spend the weekend canvassing votes. I'll let the proposal lose on its own terms. I tell my friend: "Forget it — if everyone likes her proposal, they should go with it. I'll keep mine for myself."

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to proactively approach the other person about collaboration, merging the two proposals together, and abandon the route of campaigning solo for votes. Say to my friend: 'Let's go talk to her — her plan solves the rehearsal problem, my plan has the content, and the two things can be pieced together. The credit doesn't matter; what matters is that the plan actually gets made.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to proactively approach her about merging our plans, with preserving the creative core as the priority, and letting go of my fixation on who gets the credit. I walk back into the classroom, find her, and say with a smile: 'Your concept is really fun — I wanted to chat and see if we could combine the two, make something that has a strong theme but still feels light and easy.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to immediately announce a cancellation. Instead, I do a quick feasibility check first — break down the remaining tasks and see what can be compressed or reassigned. I tell the three members: 'Don't rush into any decisions yet. Give me ten minutes — let's list out what's still left and how long each thing would take, and then we'll see if it's doable. If it's not, we go to the student affairs office. But at least let's get the full picture first.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to take over the organizing work, refuse to agree to canceling the event, but proactively scale back the goals and focus on pushing forward the most critical tasks. I say to the members: "Canceling is something I can't agree to — but I'm not going to force any of you either. I'll personally follow up on the most urgent items myself: confirming the sponsors, getting the telescope loan request submitted, and sorting out the volunteer schedule. Whoever has time can pitch in; if you don't, that's fine. As for what shape the event ends up in — we'll see when we get there."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to immediately declare the event cancelled. Instead, I propose a compromise: scale down the scope, redistribute the tasks, and first get a clear picture of what can still be pushed forward. I speak up: 'Let's not rush to say cancel — let's sit down and go through what absolutely has to get done, see if we can cut some of it, split the rest among ourselves, and if we really can't manage, then we go talk to the student union.'

options.label: D
options.content: Refuse to cancel outright, but don't take on the entire mess alone either — instead, propose a scaled-down plan: cut the event to its most essential core, redistribute the few people still around, and only do what can actually be done. I looked up and said: 'We're not canceling — but we need to cut half the content. Everyone left takes ownership of one thing, and I'll keep things moving. Anyone who's still in, tell me right now.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to give a short, self-contained answer that won't invite follow-up — glossing lightly over my income and how I live, using the exhibition selection as a natural closing note, then tossing the conversation back to someone else. I smiled and said: 'I work at a nonprofit — income's nothing special, but it's enough. I paint in my spare time; got a piece into a young artists' exhibition last year, which was fun. Anyway — that dish you were talking about, where do you get it?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to openly share who I am — in a tone that's easy and unhurried but clear-eyed — laying out my life without defending it or shrinking from it, and slipping in a mention of the exhibition, so this table knows I have my own coordinates. I smiled, raised my glass, and said: 'Nonprofit sector — the pay really isn't much, but last year I had a painting selected for a young artists' exhibition. I'm doing pretty well, just a different kind of well from you all. Come on — let's drink.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to respond with quiet candor — neither dodging nor getting defensive — and calmly describe my work and life as they are now, mentioning in passing that a painting of mine was selected for an exhibition. I give a small smile and say: 'I do project planning for a nonprofit — the salary's honestly not great, but I really enjoy it. Oh, and a painting of mine got into the local young artists' exhibition last year. As for the housing thing, there's no rush — but thank you both for thinking of me.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to describe my situation directly and calmly, neither sidestepping the gap nor making a point of it, and I respond to well-meaning suggestions politely but clearly, letting them know I have my own considerations. I say: 'I work as a general practitioner at a community health center — pretty stable work. In my spare time I paint; last year a piece of mine got into the local young artists' exhibition, which was a lot of fun. As for housing, I haven't really thought about it for now — things are good the way they are.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively, prioritizing information-gathering and concrete action over simply offering emotional support. I tell her: 'Start by walking me through everything he said and did — whatever you can remember, the more specific the better. And tomorrow's meeting — you don't have to walk in there alone.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to step in — but in a way that puts protecting her first, without pushing for a formal report just yet. I place my hand over hers, my voice shaking a little, but I speak anyway: 'You don't have to carry this alone tonight. That meeting tomorrow — I'm going to figure out a way to make sure it doesn't happen. But first, tell me everything he's done.'

options.label: C
options.content: I decide to step in and accept the professional risk — to help Lin Xiaoyu move this forward in a structured way, rather than stopping at simply listening. I tell her: "The moment you came to me today, this stopped being just your problem alone. Don't go to that meeting tomorrow by yourself — I'll figure something out. First, let's get everything you've experienced down in writing: the dates, the locations, exactly what happened each time, laid out point by point. That's the foundation for whatever we do next."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take a side and get involved, but in a careful, gradual way — offering emotional support first, then working out the next steps together. I rest my hand on the table and tell her: 'Coming to find me tonight — that was the right call. That meeting tomorrow, you're not going in alone. Let's figure out how to handle this first. I'm not going to let you carry this by yourself.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to step forward and speak, keeping a plain and understated manner — a few honest, brief words, nothing inflated and nothing falsely modest. I lean into the microphone and say: 'Honestly, I wrote this piece pretty casually — I never expected it to win… I guess I just wrote about some things I found interesting. Thank you to the judges, and thank you all.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak off the cuff, honestly laying bare how I actually create, without pretending to be a hardworking student who prepared meticulously. I smiled into the mic and said: 'Honestly, I wrote this piece one afternoon when the feeling just hit me——didn't even proofread it. I think inspiration is something you can't plan for; you just have to wait for it to come find you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take the microphone and speak sincerely — to get through an impromptu acceptance speech by looking after the mood of the room, rather than retreating into silence. I gave a small smile into the microphone, voice a little unsteady but still finding the words: 'Thank you to the teacher and the judges. When I wrote this piece, I really just wanted to put down on paper a question that had been rattling around in my head and wouldn't go away… I never expected anyone to like it. I think maybe — the kind of confusion the story is about — maybe most of us have felt something like that at some point.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to speak directly, describing my creative process in the most concise and honest way I could — no performed gratitude, no studied humility. Into the microphone I said: 'I wrote this piece off the top of my head three weeks ago. I just wanted to work out a story with a coherent internal logic. Winning means the judges found that logic convincing, and honestly, that's a better outcome than I expected.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to immediately call 120 and then 110, leave the bag exactly as it is on the ground by the roadside without touching the cash, and at the same time move toward the injured rider to check on him and do what I can to keep him calm. I pull out my phone and dial, voice shaking a little: "Hello, is this 120? There's been a traffic accident — on that two-lane road at the edge of the city, the rider is badly hurt, he needs an ambulance, please hurry —" Then I crouch down and move toward the rider by the drainage ditch: "I'm here. Help is on the way. Hold on."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to immediately call 120 for an ambulance and 110 for the police, and I leave the bag of money exactly where it is — I don't take it with me. Trembling, I dial 120 first, my voice breaking as I give the location and describe the injuries, then I call 110, set the bag down by the roadside, walk over to the rider, crouch beside him, and press my hands against his wound to hold it while I wait for help to arrive.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to call the authorities and get help immediately, refuse the bribe, and leave the handbag in place as evidence. I call 120, give my location and describe the injured person's condition; then I call 110, recount the details of the incident and the direction the driver fled, and inform them that there is a bag of cash at the scene left by the driver, which I have not touched.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to immediately call 120 and 110, leave the bag exactly as it is by the roadside, and sprint on foot toward the injured person to conduct an emergency assessment. Once 120 connects, I say: 'It's 1:47 in the morning, two-lane road on the outskirts of the city — an electric-bike rider has been struck by a vehicle. The victim is conscious but bleeding and needs emergency care. I'm on the scene right now.' I then call 110 to report the vehicle involved and the direction of the driver's escape.

options.label: A
options.content: I decline to use the service — directly, without lecturing. I gave a small laugh and slid the phone back, saying: 'Appreciate it, man — I'm doing alright on my end, don't need it. Do whatever works for you though.'

options.label: B
options.content: Decline outright — no hedging, no need for a lengthy explanation. I push the phone back and say, calmly: 'It's fine, I can finish it myself. That kind of thing carries way too much risk — it's not worth it.'

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse to use the service, and stick to the original plan to complete the thesis independently. Push the phone back, and say calmly: 'My progress is fine right now — I have enough time following the plan. I'm not going to consider this.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to politely decline my roommate's suggestion, not use the service, and continue pushing forward with my thesis according to the original plan. I tell my roommate: "I took a look at it, and I think I'll pass — my progress is still fine, I should be able to finish it myself, and if something goes wrong with that kind of thing it's just not a situation you can easily get out of."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on with an open stance, and to actively reframe the senior team member's provocation as a topic for the whole group to work through together—establishing my rhythm as team lead rather than shutting him down. I smile, stand up straight, and speak at a volume that's calm but carries to every corner of the room: "Good question—that module is actually exactly what I want us to dig into today. Old Li, you've got the deepest experience on this one. When we meet in a bit, you go first, and we'll all figure out together how to move it forward."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to meet him head-on, acknowledge the information gap, and redirect the challenge back onto the work — neither dodging it nor letting it escalate. I lift my head and say, in an even tone: 'Thanks for bringing that up directly. I'm still getting up to speed on the specifics of that module — let's put it on the table right now in this meeting. You've got more experience with it than I do, and I need to hear from you where it's getting stuck. That's exactly what I wanted to get into today. Alright — everyone take a seat. It's nine-thirty. Let's get started.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on and transform it into the opening topic for the team meeting — neither avoiding it nor confronting it combatively, but making the question itself the starting point for discussion. I say: 'Good question — that module is actually one of the core things we're tackling today. Let's all sit down and take it apart together. I want to hear everyone's read on it, including yours.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to respond directly in a way that appears composed on the surface but is in fact an act of self-protection——neither pushing back hard nor conceding weakness, pulling the conversation back to the meeting itself. I take a deep breath and say: 'Whether I can handle it or not——we'll find out by doing it. I've already gone through that unfinished module, and that's exactly why we're here today, to align on the schedule. Everyone take a seat. Let's get started.'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to go on the offensive — counter with evidence directly, no explanations, no apologies, no showing weakness. I snapped my phone screen around toward the live-stream camera, my voice calm but unmistakably clear: 'You're live-streaming? Good. Let everyone get a good look at this screen — this is the work group chat, the timestamp is right there, my phone was never pointed at you.' Then I pulled my collar free from their grip and held their gaze.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to strike back loudly and immediately, actively turning this livestreamed scene into my own stage for clearing my name. I hold my phone screen up high, my voice cutting through the noise of the carriage, staring straight into the camera: 'Everyone, look closely — what's on my phone is a work group chat, check the timestamp yourself — I did not photograph anyone. This is a false accusation!'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to actively present the evidence and break the spectator structure, rather than endure in silence or fight back aggressively. I held my phone screen up as high as I could and said, as loudly but as steadily as I could manage: 'Everyone look — this is my work group chat, messages sent at four in the morning, I've been reading this the whole time, my phone has been held at exactly this height, screen facing out — anyone can come and look.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose restraint and composure — clearing my name in public, neither fleeing nor fighting back, waiting for the train to reach the next stop so I can seek a rational resolution. I release my collar from the grip, raise both hands so the people around me can clearly see my phone screen, and say in as steady a voice as I can manage: 'I was scrolling through my work group chat — you can all see for yourselves. I haven't photographed anyone. Please wait until the next stop and we can find a staff member together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to report the data issue honestly to my supervisor and did not sign off on the confirmation email. I replied to the project management system's notification, flagging the data as anomalous and requiring review, and that evening I reached out to the project lead to explain the situation — without naming anyone directly, but making clear that the core metrics needed to be re-verified before the briefing.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, while giving my colleague the opportunity to correct the error himself rather than going over his head directly. I tell him: 'I can't sign off on this data — but before I say anything to anyone above us, I'm giving you tonight. You go to the project team yourself and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the only concession I can make.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, and at the same time I tell my colleague directly, face-to-face, that he has to report or correct this himself — giving him one last window to make it right on his own terms, or I will report it accurately myself. I say to him: 'I can't sign off on this — just hear me out. Go to the project lead right now and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the last window you have any control over. I'm not trying to destroy you, but tomorrow morning I will not put my name behind these numbers.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to report the data fraud to my supervisor and refuse to sign off confirming the data is valid — even though I am terrified of the interpersonal fallout that will follow. I tell my colleague: 'I know this puts you in a hard place, but I can't sign off on this. Tonight I'm going to tell our supervisor exactly what I know. You should probably go explain yourself first.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to let him stay, while quickly and quietly working out a plan together for if someone knocks. I pull him inside, lock the door behind us, and say in a hushed voice: 'Get into the back room, turn the lights off — if anyone knocks I'll say there's no one else here. But you have to tell me: do they know you know me?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to let him stay, and immediately start building a response plan — converting this out-of-control situation into a problem that can be managed. I lower my voice and tell him: 'Get into the back room, kill the lights, and don't make a sound. I'll figure out what we do next — but you have to tell me everything: how much you owe, how many of them there are, and whether anyone saw you come into this building.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to let my friend stay, while keeping quiet and staying low-key in case anyone knocks. I turn off all the lights, signal him not to make a sound, and we sit with our backs against the wall beside the door, waiting for the footsteps to pass. If someone knocks, I'll say in a low voice 'no one's here' — or just not answer at all.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to let my friend stay for now, while keeping a low profile and not drawing any attention, waiting for the people in the hallway to leave before figuring out the next steps. I pull him inside, ease the door shut as quietly as I can, and say in a hushed voice: 'Don't make a sound for now — once they're gone, we'll figure out what to do next.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose palliative care — honoring the exhaustion and resignation in his father's eyes, refusing to trade his last shred of dignity for a 20% chance. I lifted my head, my voice a little hoarse, and said to the doctor: 'Palliative care. Let him be comfortable. We'll be with him.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose palliative care, honor the exhaustion and wishes my father has expressed, and refuse to let a low-probability course of chemotherapy turn his remaining time into suffering. I speak to the doctor: 'Start the comfort-care protocol—let him spend whatever time he has left in peace.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to honor the answer Dad has already given with his eyes — to support palliative care, and not to force him through that suffering for the sake of a 20% chance. I turn to the doctor and say: 'We're choosing palliative care. Having him with us — really with us — matters more than anything else.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion and decide on palliative care, so that he can spend his final days with dignity. I take a deep breath and say quietly to the doctor: 'We'll go with palliative care — let him be comfortable.'

options.label: A
options.content: I won't agree to being in the office both days of the weekend, but I'll propose a compromise and stay responsible for the core content. I'll say: I have to go back home to be with my father for his follow-up appointment, but I'll hand everything off tonight with a clear checklist, revise remotely over the weekend, and do a final online review of the draft Sunday evening.

options.label: B
options.content: Choose partial compromise while holding the line: make it clear to the director that the weekend trip home is not being canceled, but offer to put in extra hours tonight to push the project forward, provide remote support on the critical sections over the weekend, and join a video call Sunday evening for the final-draft review. Say directly: 'I have a family commitment this weekend — it was locked in three days ago and I can't cancel it. I can stay late tonight and I'm available remotely over the weekend, but coming into the office isn't something I can do. Tell me which parts are the biggest bottlenecks and let's work out the priorities together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to honestly explain the situation at home while also trying to propose a middle-ground arrangement — working remotely or staggering the tasks to get part of the work done — rather than simply agreeing or outright refusing. I say to the director: 'I understand how important this client situation is, but I have a family emergency this weekend — my father has a follow-up medical appointment at the hospital and I have to be there with him; that's something I already promised them. I wanted to ask: would it be possible for me to work on the core competitive benchmarking section tonight and tomorrow morning remotely, send it to you Saturday evening for a first look, and then handle the remaining formatting and layout adjustments when I get back Sunday? If that timeline doesn't work for you, just let me know and we'll figure something else out.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honestly explain my family situation while proactively offering a compromise — neither a flat refusal nor a full yes. I speak up: 'Director, my father has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend and I've already promised to go with my parents — I can't be in the office both days. But I can get the competitive market analysis section pulled together tonight, I'm available to work remotely Saturday morning, and I was thinking — would it be possible for us to do a focused run-through of the final draft together after I'm back on Sunday?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay open without committing, and to secure a window of time before the deadline to gather information and think it through carefully. I tell my senior: 'I need to think seriously about what you've said — this isn't a small thing. Before Tuesday, I'll give you a clear answer. But I can't say yes or no on the spot right now. Can you understand that?'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to express genuine interest while making it clear that I needed to verify the information before giving any answer, keeping the decision in my own hands. I told the senior: "I listened carefully to everything you said, and I'm going to take this seriously. But before I give you an answer, I need to see the original letter of intent, and whatever user data the product has right now — if there is any. I'll give you a definitive reply before Tuesday."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to express enthusiasm and lean toward accepting, while carving out a small buffer rather than committing on the spot. I lean forward, meet my senior's eyes directly, and say: 'I don't need to wait until Tuesday — this direction feels right to me, and everything you said landed. Give me 48 hours. Not to hesitate, but to get my own side of things sorted out — and then we sit down and talk through the details for real.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay open and actively seek more information, rather than committing or declining on the spot. I say: 'Everything you're describing genuinely excites me — but there are a few things I need to get clear on first. What's your core thesis about the education space, and what are the potholes you've already hit behind this product logic? Give me a few days. I want to actually understand this, not just get swept along by an equity number.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell the security guard the truth — that I didn't put the toy in my backpack — and refuse to take the blame. I say to the security guard: 'I didn't put this toy in there. I don't know how it ended up in my bag. I did not take it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to state the facts truthfully, not to take the blame for my friend, but in a calm and measured tone — not actively accusing anyone, just making it clear that I didn't put the toy in the bag. I say to the security guard: 'I didn't put this toy in there. I don't know how it got into my bag. You can check the security footage.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take the blame for my friend — using silence and a vague confession to protect him — and partly because somewhere deep down I've already decided that 'speaking up won't make anyone believe me anyway.' I lower my head, my voice barely above a whisper, and say to the security guard: 'It was me… I took it myself. I don't even know how it ended up in there. I'm sorry.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to take the fall for my friend temporarily — but I didn't fully confess. Instead I kept things vague to buy some breathing room. I lowered my head and said to the security guard: 'It was me… I don't know how it ended up in there, I didn't mean to…' My voice was barely above a whisper, my hands were shaking, and I couldn't stop the corners of my eyes from going red.

options.label: A
options.content: Refuse the proposed redefinition of the relationship's boundaries; hold to the original commitment. I press the phone face-down first, then tell him plainly: 'I can't accept this kind of redefinition — we need to talk this through properly.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving any answer and instead speak up first — asking my partner to explain, in a calm but serious tone, exactly what they mean, rather than simply accepting or rejecting what they've said. I set down what I'm holding, look at my partner, and say: 'When you say "flexibility," I need you to tell me exactly what you mean by that. I'm not in a position to give you an answer right now.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to set aside any direct response for now, letting confusion and silence stand in for any real position, while getting distracted by my phone. I looked up at her, stayed quiet for a few seconds, my voice coming out a little hoarse: 'All of this you're saying… I need to think about it.' Then, almost without realizing it, I glanced down at my phone screen as it lit up.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold off on taking any position and prioritize gathering information, asking her to spell out exactly what the specific boundaries are. I set down my phone, looked at her, and said: 'When you say flexibility, I'd like to hear you be a little more specific — the independent space you're talking about, what kinds of things does that mean, and how far does it go?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to respond proactively — to go out for a walk together with the person I live with, without forcing any particular conversation, just breaking through this stiff, unfamiliar feeling first. I speak up: "Let's go for a walk — neither of us knows how the days are going to go from here anyway, so we might as well get out and see."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to sidestep this shared strangeness — to gently acknowledge that it exists, rather than each of us retreating back into the familiar busyness. I look down at the table and say: "This does feel strange. How about… we have a cup of coffee together first? No need to think about what comes next."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to respond — to tuck my need inside a suggestion, phrased lightly, as if it just slipped out, not as if I'm scared. I lift my head, my voice steadier than I expected: "Maybe… let's go out for a walk together — I don't really know what I'd do with myself today anyway."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take the initiative and break the deadlock — pulling my housemate out the door with me, filling this strange morning with movement. I stand up, give the table a light slap, and say with a smile: 'Come on, let's go for a walk — just the two of us. We can figure out what comes next as we go. Beats sitting here staring into space.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation directly but calmly, while offering a genuine acknowledgment of the partnership — no avoidance, no stalling. I say to them: 'Thank you for telling me this. These past few months really have felt like smooth collaboration — that's true. But I can't accept the Saturday invitation. I think we both know it's not just an ordinary colleague outing.'

options.label: B
options.content: Accept the invitation — but also decide to have a real conversation with my partner that same night, and face whatever has actually gone wrong between us. I say to them: "I'd love to go, I've been wanting to catch this talk too — but there's something I need to tell you first, about where things have been between us lately."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to gently decline the invitation, but without cutting off contact — giving myself time to face both this feeling and my existing relationship. I tell him: "I actually have something on this Saturday, so I probably can't make it — but that feeling of being understood that you mentioned, I treasure that too. It's just that right now I need to think some things through first."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation, but to be honest about why rather than brush it off with an excuse. I glanced down at the table for a moment, then looked up and said: 'I'm really grateful you said all of this — these past few months have meant something to me too — but I can't go. Not because I don't have time. It's because I know what it would mean for both of us, and I can't do that to you, and I can't do that to myself.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the medical consent forms. I say into the phone, 'I'll be right there,' then I call the CEO: 'My father has had an emergency complication from surgery — I have to go to the hospital. Please find a way to handle the launch without me. I'm so sorry to everyone.' Then I head downstairs and tell the driver to change course for the hospital.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to handle the medical decision remotely, then attend the launch as planned. First I press the doctors to spell out the risks clearly over the phone and confirm the treatment plan directly; then I notify the CEO that I'll be there on time.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the medical authorization, while activating the emergency contingency plan to have the team take over as the main presenter. I call the CEO and say: 'My father has gone into a critical situation during surgery — I need to be there in person to sign right now. Please arrange a backup presenter. I'll provide remote support if at all possible. I'm sorry.' Then I hang up and tell the driver to go straight to the hospital.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to put down the speech immediately and head to the hospital, leaving the launch event for the team to handle on an emergency basis. I tell the CEO: 'My father's surgery requires my signature — it's the one thing that cannot be replaced. Please have the team hold things down at the launch; I'll deal with the follow-up afterward.' Then I hang up, and tell the driver to reroute to the hospital.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to confront him directly, right here and now——no dancing around it. I put the note on the table and make it clear that the competition and the awards ceremony have to stop. I set down the book in my hands, look him in the eyes, and say: 'Sit down. There's something I need to talk to you about. I found a note in your backpack——it's about your math midterm.'

options.label: B
options.content: He chooses to hold his emotions in check for the moment—letting the child set down the milk and leave first—then calls him back alone afterward and, calmly but directly, lays that note out on the table. He doesn't pick up the thread of the boy's cheerful talk; he waits until the glass is set down, then says: 'Sit down. There's something I need to talk to you about.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to go along with what he's saying for now — no exposing him on the spot — but I use one sentence to keep him here, laying the groundwork for a private conversation later tonight. I take the milk from him, lower my head and drink a sip, then look up at him and say: 'The speech can wait — sit down for a second. Dad has something he wants to talk to you about, just the two of us.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — not to expose him on the spot — but to use a single question to plant a crack inside him, leaving room for a deeper conversation later. I take the milk from him, look down at it for a moment, then lift my head and say calmly: 'Let's set the speech aside for now. I have a question I want to ask you — this exam, do you feel like you were truly ready for it? Can you walk me through how you studied for the math?'

options.label: A
options.content: I didn't promise to keep it secret. Instead, I put the focus on just being there with her — trying to make sure she didn't feel abandoned, while leaving room to seek help later. I crouched down and said quietly: 'I'm not going to go around telling everyone — but I also can't promise to do nothing. Can you tell me what's actually been going on lately?'

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to promise secrecy, but don't leave immediately — choose to stay and explain my reasons to her first, then let her know I will be seeking help from an adult. I meet her eyes directly and say, in a steady voice: 'I can't promise you that — not because I don't care about you, but because this is something I can't carry alone, and you shouldn't have to carry it alone either. I'll go with you to find someone, but I'm not going to pretend I didn't see anything today.'

options.label: C
options.content: I won't agree to unconditional secrecy — but I won't flatly refuse her either, and I won't turn around and go straight to a teacher. Instead, I choose to be honest with her first about where I stand, and try to bring her along with me. I say to her: 'I can't promise you complete secrecy — not because I don't care about you, but because I do care, and that's exactly why I can't. But I won't go behind your back. Let's figure out together how to handle this, okay?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to promise secrecy right away. Instead, I steady her first — with my body and my words — so she feels caught, held. And inside, I've already decided I need to find a way to tell an adult. I take her hand, my voice shaking a little: 'I'm not going anywhere, I'm not going anywhere at all — but I can't promise to keep this secret, because I'm scared something will happen to you, and I'm more afraid of losing you than I am of you being angry at me. Can we just talk first? Tell me what's actually been going on with you lately.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to support the kid without reservation, no conditions attached, no 'been-there-done-that' warnings. I look the kid in the eyes and say: 'Apply for it — go with this one. I've seen too many people put out their own fire. Don't you put yours out.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to object outright, and instead ask that both of us first lay this path out in full and see it clearly before any decision is made. I say: 'Leave that booklet here with me. I'll read through it carefully tonight. We'll talk again tomorrow — I have questions I want to ask you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to offer conditional support, but I require my kid to sit through a structured risk-breakdown conversation right now, rather than simply handing over an emotional endorsement. I push the report booklet back across the table and say: 'You say seven years — I believe you. But let's go through this field's employment data, your competitive edge, and your exit path in the worst-case scenario, one by one. If you can answer those, I'll support you filling it in.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold off on any objection — to listen and learn more first, without immediately declaring support or disapproval. I say: 'Show me that portfolio, and walk me through it from the beginning — tell me everything you've been working on all these years.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to set aside the immediate push on the overseas project and prioritize going home to handle Dad's medical arrangements, while also starting to break down the timelines of both things in search of a middle path. I begin making a list on my phone screen: the earliest train tomorrow morning, contact the attending physician, and at the same time send HR an email asking whether the overseas role allows for a deferred decision — first get the parts I can control into their boxes, then go home.

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to immediately set aside all preparations for the overseas project and return home as soon as possible to be with Dad through his medical care, putting the career decision on hold for now. Call Mom and say: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — I'll be there on the earliest flight tomorrow. Don't worry, I'll handle everything.'

options.label: C
options.content: I decide to set aside any deliberation over the overseas opportunity and go home first to be with Dad and help manage the medical arrangements. The career stuff can come later. I call Mom back and say: 'Mom, I'll be home tomorrow — don't worry too much, okay? I'm coming to be with you both.'

options.label: D
options.content: She chooses to immediately abandon the overseas assignment and go home to be with her father and handle whatever comes next with his medical care. Voice breaking over the phone, she says: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — first flight tomorrow morning. Wait for me.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to politely decline the camping invitation, deflecting with a specific but not over-explained work excuse, while keeping a normal, friendly tone — no cracks, no unnecessary distance. My reply: 'Oh that sounds absolutely gorgeous! I've got a project milestone I need to wrap up this weekend and I'm worried I won't be able to get away last-minute — you all go and have fun, and make sure to include me next time!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to accept the camping invitation and respond with surface-level enthusiasm, but leave myself a vague out — while quietly working to make sure the four of us never actually go. I write back: "That sounds amazing! The guesthouse must have such a beautiful view — let me just check my schedule on this end, you guys lock in the details first, and I'll get back to you as soon as I can!"

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a personal excuse, without revealing anything about the secret, and use a brief, vague reason to quietly remove myself from the situation. Reply: 'I haven't been feeling great lately — I probably won't be able to make it this weekend. Hope you all have a great time, let's plan something again soon!'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to politely decline the camping invitation, brushing it off with a vague but not entirely untrue excuse — not giving away the secret, but not pretending everything is fine and agreeing to go either. I reply: "Something came up on our end this week out of nowhere, so we probably won't be able to make it this time — next time for sure! Hope you guys have a blast~ "

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to be honest with my partner about the real situation and my inner fear, while also deciding not to lower my salary floor — and instead to consider a more aggressive path toward starting something of my own or a full pivot. I squeeze her hand and say: "I'm going to be straight with you — I'm scared right now, but I'm not going to go work in a call center. I want to talk to you about a much bigger bet."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak — to tell her part of the truth, to acknowledge how hard things are, while setting aside for now the most drastic idea (scrapping everything to go independent) and focusing on steadying what's right in front of us. I turned my hand over and held hers back, and said quietly: 'The options really are narrow right now. I haven't figured it out yet — but I don't want to hide it from you. It's harder than I expected.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell my partner the truth and to swallow my pride first — to go after whatever work I can get up to speed on quickly. I say quietly, 'I'm letting go of the old salary standard. Starting tomorrow, I'm applying to warehouse and customer service jobs too.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to be honest with my partner about the real situation and the confusion I feel inside — to let go, for now, of my insistence on a salary floor, and to open up to discussing more practical options. I squeeze her hand and say quietly: "I genuinely don't know what the next step looks like right now — but I don't want to carry this alone. Everything you said, let's figure it out together."

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while seeking legal support, and refrain from making statements about others when the information isn't clear. Say aloud: 'I need to consult legal counsel first — I'll give you a response within 24 hours. As for the circumstances of my own signature, I can clarify that right now: at the time, I was simply asked by my superior to review a budget document. I was not involved in any decision-making.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while immediately seeking legal protection, and refuse to provide any substantive information before a lawyer is present. Say aloud: 'I need to contact my lawyer first — until then, I'm not in a position to make any statement about the specifics. This isn't a refusal to cooperate; it's my basic right.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to cooperate within limits while seeking legal counsel, and I will not hastily provide any information involving other people within the 24-hour window. I say to the investigators: 'I need to contact a lawyer first. Once I've confirmed my rights, I will cooperate fully and explain everything I know.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to delay full cooperation and seek legal counsel immediately — selectively acknowledging the basic facts while refusing to provide any information about team members. To the investigator: 'I understand what you're asking. I did see that document — that much is true. But without a lawyer present, I won't be providing any further information. That's the more responsible approach for both of us. I need 24 hours. I'll make good use of them.'

options.label: A
options.content: Accept the overall direction on the surface, while fighting for a voice at the implementation level — trading 'I'll oversee the transition' for real room to preserve the process details that matter. I speak up: "Fine, I have no issue with the overall direction. But if I'm the one overseeing the transition, then I need to break this down according to my own understanding of how these workflows operate. I'll hold the pace you want — but when it comes to the specifics of what gets changed, I need you to give me the space to do that."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to accept the overall direction while raising specific risk nodes on the spot, pushing for a transition buffer and written confirmation — rather than silently complying or confronting him head-on. I flip open the proposal, press my finger on the first timeline node, and keep my voice steady but unyielding: 'I accept the overall direction, but there are three risk points here that need to be confirmed before execution — if we don't get them written into the transition plan this week, I can't guarantee a problem won't affect the launch date, and I need you to give me a written record of the decision basis.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to engage by asking questions — approaching this with curiosity rather than direct confrontation or silent compliance — in order to open up space for real dialogue within the execution framework. I say: 'My objection isn't that we can't change things — but I want to ask you first: what specific observations about the existing process is this proposal based on? Because some of those steps were designed the way they were to solve particular problems. If you know which problems, we can figure out together whether the new process actually covers them.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose conditional cooperation — working within the new direction while using the execution details to carve out some breathing room. I won't challenge the big-picture direction head-on, but I'll lay out every key risk point, one by one. I flip open the proposal and speak calmly: 'I understand the overall direction, and I'll personally oversee the transition. But there are a few specific points I want to walk through — it's not a question of whether we can make the changes, it's about which parts of the rollout are going to break first if we push forward this way. We need to think through how we're going to catch those before they happen.'

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to go along with the lie, choose to tell the homeroom teacher honestly that the model was lost, and immediately pivot to completing the presentation through verbal, impromptu explanation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but clearly: 'The model was left on the bus on the way to school. We don't have a prop, but we can just talk through it — I'll describe how the solar system works. We don't need the model.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the model being lost, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately pivot to figuring out how to complete the presentation without it. Looking the homeroom teacher in the eye: 'The model was lost on the way to school — he left it on the bus. Without the model, we'll complete the presentation using a verbal explanation and the board. I'll take the lead.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell a softened version of the truth: acknowledge that the model wasn't brought, without actively naming who's responsible, while indicating that the group can complete the presentation through a verbal walkthrough. I say to the homeroom teacher: 'Ms. [Teacher], the model wasn't able to make it today — something came up — but we're fully prepared to walk through everything verbally. The content is all there. Please let us go up.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the missing model, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately switch to a no-model presentation. I say to the teacher: 'The model was left on the bus on the way to school. We'll complete the presentation using a verbal explanation — I know the material thoroughly, and we're ready to begin.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea concisely, but politely decline the invitation to join the speech competition. I'll say: 'Teacher, I can walk through this method, but I'd rather not join the speech competition — I don't think it's necessary.' Then I'll lay out the logic of the seesaw weighing method in the most straightforward way I can, without dragging it out, and without caring about the skeptical looks.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and share my seesaw idea in front of the class, but give a vague rather than definite answer about the speech competition proposal. I slowly rise to my feet, my voice a little dry, but I manage to speak: 'It's just… using a seesaw — put the elephant on one side, then keep adding people to the other side, and by counting the people you can figure out the elephant's weight. As for the competition… I… I'll think about it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to respond in a vague, retreating way — neither outright refusing the teacher nor actually standing up to perform — trying to shrink myself back into the crowd. I keep my head down and say in a very small voice: "Teacher… what I said just now wasn't very clear, it probably wasn't that good…" and give a small shake of my head, without standing up.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up and share my idea in front of everyone; the suggestion about the speech competition is set aside for now, neither accepted nor explicitly refused. I rise from my chair, my voice a little rushed at first but growing steadier as I go: 'It's just… you put an elephant on one end, then you stack people onto the other end one by one, and you count how many people it takes — because you can measure each person's weight ahead of time —'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to reply, but with a tone that is warm yet gently distancing — drawing this connection softly to a close without rejecting him outright or letting it go any deeper. I write: "Made it home. I found myself thinking about some of what we talked about that evening during the meeting today. That bookstore really is wonderfully quiet — you should go sometime when you get the chance. Good night."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to reply to the message, but keep the wording concise and measured — clear boundaries, neither deliberately cold nor leaving any opening. I reply: 'Made it home. That line of thinking from today's meeting is worth developing further — the bookstore idea has potential. Goodnight.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to reply, but keep the emotional register within a friendly, restrained boundary — not actively deepening things, but not coldly cutting them off either. I write back: "Made it home. The meeting today was actually pretty worthwhile. That bookstore sounds worth checking out sometime — it seems really quiet. Good night."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to reply to my colleague's message for now — cutting off the bud with self-attack, pressing the longing back down. I darken the phone screen, pick up my partner's message, reply with a single "okay," then close both chat windows and stare at the blurred night outside the car window, telling myself: you could never hold something like this together.

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to make a choice. Instead I try to break the deadlock with my voice and my body — pulling both of their attention away from "waiting for me to take a side." I lunge forward and wrap my arms around the waist of the one crouching by the door, then spin back and scream at the other one through my tears: "You can't both leave! Nobody is allowed to leave! I won't let you!"

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to make the choice either of them is demanding, and in a calm, direct way I hand the decision back to the adults. I speak up: 'This is between the two of you — it's not my business. I'm not going anywhere and I'm not staying for either of you. You decide.' Then I turn around, walk back to my room, and close the door.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to take either side. Instead, I let my tears and my body break the moment — interrupt the pressure to choose — and try to make both adults stop. I crouch down, wrap my arms around my knees, and cry out loud. The only words I manage to say, over and over, are: 'I don't want you to split up. I'm not going anywhere.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay, expressing through the rigid stillness of my body a despair that cannot make any choice, while instinctively leaning toward the 'stay' side. I drew my shoulders in tight, my feet didn't move, and in a voice barely above a whisper I said: "I'm not leaving… I'm not going anywhere…" Then I crouched down, covered my face, and cried out loud.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene directly — I don't return the pill bottle, and I immediately contact the resident advisor or call a mental health crisis line. I don't let this get handled internally within the dorm room. I keep the bottle in my hand, stand up, and walk to the curtain around his bed. In a steady voice, I say: 'I'm not giving this back to you. You need someone here right now — I'm going to contact the RA. If you don't want me to, tell me now.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak up and respond to my roommate — using the bottle as a reason to stay, remaining right where I am in a clumsy but genuine way. I keep the bottle in my hand instead of kicking it back, turn around, and say: 'I don't feel like going out tonight. This bottle of pills… can you tell me about it?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to intervene — I don't return the bottle, I walk toward the bed curtain and speak, while preparing to reach out for outside help. I grip the pill bottle in my hand, walk to his bedside, and say quietly: 'I'm not leaving — I just don't want to. I'm holding onto this bottle for now — how are you doing right now?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to use my roommate's unprompted question as an opening—staying, responding quietly, not returning the bottle, while quietly preparing to reach out to a counselor or mental health support line. I keep the bottle in my hand without kicking it back, turn around, and say: 'Nothing going on tonight, so I just didn't head out. You doing… okay over there?'

options.label: A
