options.content: I choose to put down my chopsticks, leave my friends' table, and walk toward the new girl in the corner. I carry my tray over and sit down across from her, saying: 'My name's Xiaopéngyǒu — are you eating alone? I came to keep you company.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively — walk over to the new student and break the ice in a task-oriented way. I pick up my tray, stand up, tell my friends 'Go ahead, I'll catch up,' then walk over to the table in the corner and sit down, saying to the new student: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — . It's your first day — if there's anything you're not sure about, feel free to ask me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to step away from my friend group for a moment and walk over to the new student sitting alone in the corner. I told my friends, 'You guys go ahead and eat, I'll be right back,' then carried my tray over to that small table against the wall and sat down across from her, saying quietly: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm K — today's your first day, right?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to leave the lively crowded table, pick up my tray, and walk over to the new student sitting alone in the corner — not forcing myself to seem warm or outgoing, just going over and sitting down. I carry my tray to that small table, set it down gently, and say quietly: 'Is it okay if I sit here?'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to take the fall for my friend temporarily — but I didn't fully confess. Instead I kept things vague to buy some breathing room. I lowered my head and said to the security guard: 'It was me… I don't know how it ended up in there, I didn't mean to…' My voice was barely above a whisper, my hands were shaking, and I couldn't stop the corners of my eyes from going red.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the security guard the truth — that I didn't put the toy in my backpack — and refuse to take the blame. I say to the security guard: 'I didn't put this toy in there. I don't know how it ended up in my bag. I did not take it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the truth, refuse to take the fall for my friend, and state the facts directly to the security guard. I speak calmly: 'I didn't put that toy in there — he did. I don't know when he put it in, and I didn't take anything.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell a partial truth — neither taking the blame nor directly giving my friend up — using a vague but honest statement to buy time. I say to the security guard: 'This toy wasn't put in there by me. I genuinely don't know how it ended up in my bag. We just came down from the second floor…'

options.label: A
options.content: He politely declines the invitation, tells the other kid to head back first, and stays to look a little longer before deciding anything — neither diving into research nor leaving right away. "You guys go ahead, I'll come check it out in a bit."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to politely decline the soccer invitation and stay to keep observing the bug, but I explain it in a friendly way so he doesn't feel put out. I smiled at him and said: 'You guys go ahead — there's this super weird bug here I want to watch a little longer. Once I'm done, maybe I'll come find you!'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation and go play soccer, but at the same time I turn this strange bug into a 'surprise' I bring to the field — merging both things into one. I say to the kid: 'Let's go, let's go — but look at this first. Have you ever seen a bug like this? Its back is green and it actually glows! We'll come back and study it after the game — come on, let's go!'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation outright and stay to keep observing the beetle and the purple flower, without offering much explanation. I glanced up at him briefly and said, calmly: 'I'm not coming — I'm watching a beetle I've never seen before. You guys go ahead.' Then I looked back down and continued observing, starting to document the insect's physical features with my phone or a twig I found nearby.

options.label: A
options.content: Decline the opportunity gracefully, but with a genuine and respectful reason rather than a hollow brush-off. Say to the friend: 'Thank you for thinking of me — I read through the plan carefully, and I understand what you need. But honestly, the way I am right now just isn't suited to joining a team. Working alone is the only way I can actually do things well. Forcing that to change would only hold you back. Find someone who's a better fit for this.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving an answer and buy myself more time to do proper due diligence — I won't commit on the spot. I tell my friend: 'I really am taking this seriously, but this decision is too big. I need to go through the plan more carefully and get a clear picture of the financials and the team. Give me a week — I'll come back to you with a real answer.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to accept the career change opportunity, but I ask for a little time to arrange a financial buffer rather than signing immediately. I tell my friend: 'I'm in — but give me two weeks. I need to wrap things up properly on this end. Not because I'm hesitating, but because I want to show up giving everything I've got.'

options.label: D
options.content: The impulse called forth by the flow state outweighed the gravity of the financial spreadsheet, and I chose to accept the invitation — but I tried to carve out a little breathing room for myself rather than committing fully on the spot. I told my friend: 'Honestly, I already know what I want — I'm in. But can you give me a week to wrap things up on this end?'

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to tell the truth, refusing to cover for the one who brought the cards, and ignores the threat entirely. Looking the director straight in the eye, he says: 'He's the one who brought them in. I saw it. That's all there is to it — it has nothing to do with me.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the truth about who brought the contraband in, prioritizing my own protection and making clear I had no part in it. I'll quietly tell the director exactly who I saw and what happened — and I'll also let him know that person just threatened me.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to give a vague, minimizing response — neither directly identifying the person who brought the contraband, nor explicitly denying that I saw anything — in order to end this conversation as quickly as possible and get out of here. I say to the director: "I was reading at the time. I didn't notice who brought it — I really couldn't make it out."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the truth — to state the facts, to not be swayed by threats, and to make no unnecessary show of emotion. I look at the director calmly and say: 'It was the student in the back row near the corner who brought them in. I turned around because I heard a noise, but I wasn't involved.'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to defer to authority and avoid dissent, responding to the teacher's call on me in a vague, noncommittal way rather than expressing what I actually thought. I stood up, head lowered, and said: 'Teacher, I… I understand, no questions.' Then I slowly sat back down and quietly closed my notebook, sliding it under my textbook.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and respond, but I wrap my disagreement in an extremely cautious, deferential register — framing the challenge inside a shell of 'I may have misunderstood' to reduce the risk of being attacked. I stand, my voice slightly trembling, eyes still lowered: 'Professor, I came across a book about this period of history last week — it cited some primary source documents, and it seemed like… there might be a small discrepancy with the conclusion you mentioned. Is it possible I've misread it? Could you help me work through it?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up and calmly, directly raise the historical fact that contradicts what the teacher presented, grounding it in specific arguments and page numbers from the text — no combative edge, just a statement of information. I say: 'I read an academic monograph last week that draws on primary archival sources, and there's one point in its account of this issue that differs from what you've described. Would it be all right if I explained exactly where the discrepancy is?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly — respectful in tone, but clear in position — and actively bring the arguments and primary sources from my outside reading into the classroom discussion. I stand and say: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history in the library — it cites several primary-source documents and offers a different interpretation of the causal relationship here. I'd love to hear your thoughts on that reading.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold back and not expose everything in this moment, but I decide that tonight I will find a quiet moment alone with him to talk through this face to face — and to stop him from giving the speech at the commendation ceremony and from registering for the competition. I take the milk from him, set down what I'm holding, and say calmly: 'Let's put the speech aside for now. There's something important we need to talk about tonight — come find me in the study when you're done eating.'

options.label: B
options.content: He chooses to hold his emotions in check for the moment—letting the child set down the milk and leave first—then calls him back alone afterward and, calmly but directly, lays that note out on the table. He doesn't pick up the thread of the boy's cheerful talk; he waits until the glass is set down, then says: 'Sit down. There's something I need to talk to you about.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold it in for now, steady my emotions, and wait until he's gone before finding the right moment to talk to him alone. I took the milk from him, glanced down at it for a moment, and said quietly: 'Let's talk about the speech later — go wash up first. I have something I want to say to you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation in this moment — but I've already made up my mind to find the right time tonight to talk with him alone. For now I respond to him calmly, saying, 'The speech sounds good — but let's sit down a bit later and talk through the competition thing more carefully,' keeping my tone perfectly ordinary, nothing out of the usual.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving an answer on the spot, and to make a decision only after having a thorough conversation with my child and family first. I tell the teacher: 'Thank you so much for bringing this to us — we take it very seriously. I need to sit down and really talk with my child first, hear what he/she thinks, and then discuss it with the family. We'll get back to you as soon as we can.'

options.label: B
options.content: I decided not to express immediate support or opposition to the special recruitment, and instead to return the decision to my child first, while buying time for the whole family to discuss it together. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you — this is very significant news for us. Before we make any decision, I want to genuinely hear what my child himself has to say. This is ultimately his path to walk, and I want him to walk it because he truly wants to, not because of our expectations. Could you give us a little time?'

options.label: C
options.content: I've made up my mind to support the professional path — clearly and without reservation — while insisting that we call my kid out here right now for a three-way conversation, so he can say what he wants in his own words. I tell the teacher: "Personally, I'm in favor — but we need to ask him directly, right now. Can we bring him out here so the three of us can talk this through together?"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to politely decline the specialist track and hold firm on keeping my child in the regular education system, without applying for the talent recruitment program. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you so much — knowing that you see him that way, we as parents are truly grateful. But this is too big a decision for me to make on the spot. I need to go home and talk it through properly with the family. His schoolwork is stable right now, and we don't want to rush into anything.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to decline politely, avoid direct confrontation, and push the card back to him in a calm but clear way — giving him no room to perform. I shook my head slightly, gently slid the business card back toward his hand, and said evenly: 'Thank you for the kind thought, but my current work suits me quite well — I'm not looking to make any changes.' Then I took half a step back, glass still in hand, and shifted my gaze away.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse his 'generosity' right there in front of everyone, and to push back against this humiliation with a cutting remark. I slide the business card back toward him, and with a cold smile I say: 'I appreciate the thought — but I'm much more used to being my own boss. That assistant position of yours? Save it for someone who needs their life managed for them.' Then I turn and walk out of the reception.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — I don't take the card. I draw my line in the calmest, clearest way I can, giving him no opening to perform his 'magnanimity' for the crowd. I shift the orange juice glass to my other hand, meet his eyes, and keep my voice low but make every word land clean: 'Thank you — I'm all right. What happened between us back then — I haven't forgotten.' Then I turn and walk away, and I don't look back.

options.label: D
options.content: I decline the card, and in front of everyone I flip this so-called 'invitation' into a public counter-strike — letting every person in the room see exactly what is behind that business card. I don't reach out to take it. Instead, I smile slightly, my voice pitched just loud enough for two or three tables to catch every word: 'Generous enough? Twelve years ago you put your name on my proposal — now that was generous. Keep the assistant position. It's not for me — I've never been good at signing other people's names for them.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note — I shield it with my body, fold it quickly, and stuff it back into my textbook, then brush off my best friend with a lie. As for whether to show up after school, I haven't decided yet, but I'm leaning toward not going, or putting off the decision until the very last second. I press my textbook down over the note and look up at her with a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking to borrow my notes.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note and refuse to let my friend see it; at the same time, I'm deeply resistant to the idea of showing up at all, and I'm leaning toward not going. I flip my textbook over, press the note underneath it, look up, and give a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking about homework. Nothing interesting.'

options.label: C
options.content: On instinct I flip the note face-down on the desk to cover it, vaguely change the subject, and quietly decide to slip away to the meeting after school — without telling her who asked me to come. I press the note under my textbook and say with a smile: 'Oh, it's nothing — just some random classmate, no big deal — did you finish your homework yet?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note so my friend can't see it, and decide to go to the meeting alone after school. I flip the note face-down and slide it under my textbook in one casual motion, then tell my friend offhandedly: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking to borrow my notes.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take over all the preparation work the president left behind on my own, pushing the event forward through sheer willpower, refusing to agree to cancellation. I take a deep breath, voice trembling slightly but I speak anyway: 'We're not canceling. I'll take it from here. Help as much as you can — if you can't, I'll figure it out myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to cancel outright, but don't take on the entire mess alone either — instead, propose a scaled-down plan: cut the event to its most essential core, redistribute the few people still around, and only do what can actually be done. I looked up and said: 'We're not canceling — but we need to cut half the content. Everyone left takes ownership of one thing, and I'll keep things moving. Anyone who's still in, tell me right now.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take over the organizing work, refuse to agree to canceling the event, but proactively scale back the goals and focus on pushing forward the most critical tasks. I say to the members: "Canceling is something I can't agree to — but I'm not going to force any of you either. I'll personally follow up on the most urgent items myself: confirming the sponsors, getting the telescope loan request submitted, and sorting out the volunteer schedule. Whoever has time can pitch in; if you don't, that's fine. As for what shape the event ends up in — we'll see when we get there."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to immediately announce a cancellation. Instead, I do a quick feasibility check first — break down the remaining tasks and see what can be compressed or reassigned. I tell the three members: 'Don't rush into any decisions yet. Give me ten minutes — let's list out what's still left and how long each thing would take, and then we'll see if it's doable. If it's not, we go to the student affairs office. But at least let's get the full picture first.'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to go on the offensive — to strike first with a breezy, self-assured tone, packaging my life as a clear-eyed choice rather than a losing position, and dropping a mention of the art show as a line of defense. I smiled, picked up my glass, and said: 'Ha, I'm running projects at a nonprofit right now — the pay's nothing to write home about, honestly — but last year I had a painting selected for the local young artists' exhibition. I feel like the life I'm living is the life I actually want. As for the whole housing thing — I'll get to it eventually.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to openly share who I am — in a tone that's easy and unhurried but clear-eyed — laying out my life without defending it or shrinking from it, and slipping in a mention of the exhibition, so this table knows I have my own coordinates. I smiled, raised my glass, and said: 'Nonprofit sector — the pay really isn't much, but last year I had a painting selected for a young artists' exhibition. I'm doing pretty well, just a different kind of well from you all. Come on — let's drink.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to describe my situation directly and calmly, neither sidestepping the gap nor making a point of it, and I respond to well-meaning suggestions politely but clearly, letting them know I have my own considerations. I say: 'I work as a general practitioner at a community health center — pretty stable work. In my spare time I paint; last year a piece of mine got into the local young artists' exhibition, which was a lot of fun. As for housing, I haven't really thought about it for now — things are good the way they are.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to give a short, self-contained answer that won't invite follow-up — glossing lightly over my income and how I live, using the exhibition selection as a natural closing note, then tossing the conversation back to someone else. I smiled and said: 'I work at a nonprofit — income's nothing special, but it's enough. I paint in my spare time; got a piece into a young artists' exhibition last year, which was fun. Anyway — that dish you were talking about, where do you get it?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to swallow the confrontation — to hold it down and fake composure long enough to get through this moment — without bringing up the screenshot or the private group chat. I keep my head low, eyes fixed on the surface of the desk, giving no response to what she said, not glancing at either of their phones, just gripping my sleeve cuffs tight in my fist and waiting for the burning behind my eyes to slowly fade.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation, not here, not yet. I'll wait until I've calmly worked out what outcome I actually want before deciding on a next step. I turn off my phone screen, look up, give her one expressionless glance, then drop my eyes and open my textbook.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to confront her directly — to put the screenshot and the side group chat out in the open, no holding back and no dancing around it. I turn toward her, lower my voice, but speak with complete directness: "I saw that group chat. I saw what you said. Can you tell me — what exactly is going on?"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to push down the urge to confront her, getting through this moment with silence and avoidance, not letting her see that I already know about the screenshot. I lower my head, force the tears back, and say quietly, "I'm fine, probably just didn't sleep well," then look away and pretend to flip through my textbook.

options.label: A
options.content: Decline my former colleague's offer to team up for the transition and choose to plan my own learning path independently; at the same time, prepare a clear but noncommittal position for tomorrow's manager meeting — acknowledge that I'm evaluating a transition direction, but commit to no specific timeline, in order to preserve my autonomy. Concrete actions: send my former colleague a short text message — 'Still thinking it over, let's each do our own thing for now' — then open a document and quietly map out a self-study path for AI-related skills alongside the time window my current savings can support.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to wait and watch — I won't accept my former colleague's invitation, and I'll prepare to tell my supervisor tomorrow that I'm willing to align with the company's current direction and that I want to keep my position. I reply to my former colleague: 'I support your decision, but I've got family pressure on my end — quitting isn't something I'm considering right now. I'll see how things play out here first.' Then I open my calendar and accept the supervisor's invite.

options.label: C
options.content: I'm choosing to take the risk and make the transition — but I won't quit cold turkey right away. Instead, I'm going to lay my cards on the table proactively. Tonight I'll sign up for a course and put together a learning plan; tomorrow I'll tell my manager that I want to move into an AI-related role and ask about the possibility of an internal transition.

options.label: D
options.content: I'm choosing to actively gather information and hold off on any final decision, while preparing for tomorrow's conversation. Two things to do tonight: organize the industry data and specific transition-path information I currently have, and draw up a few key questions for the manager — what the company's actual plans are for existing roles, whether internal transfers into AI-related positions are a possibility, and what their read is on the staffing structure over the next year.

options.label: A
options.content: I decide to step in and accept the professional risk — to help Lin Xiaoyu move this forward in a structured way, rather than stopping at simply listening. I tell her: "The moment you came to me today, this stopped being just your problem alone. Don't go to that meeting tomorrow by yourself — I'll figure something out. First, let's get everything you've experienced down in writing: the dates, the locations, exactly what happened each time, laid out point by point. That's the foundation for whatever we do next."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively, prioritizing information-gathering and concrete action over simply offering emotional support. I tell her: 'Start by walking me through everything he said and did — whatever you can remember, the more specific the better. And tomorrow's meeting — you don't have to walk in there alone.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to actively intervene, making it clear to Lin Xiaoyu that I will take action — not simply be a listener. I look her straight in the eye and say: 'You don't have to file the report yourself, but I will handle this. You don't need to go to that meeting tomorrow — I'll find a way to block it — and then we'll put the evidence together and go through the official channels.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to step in — but in a way that puts protecting her first, without pushing for a formal report just yet. I place my hand over hers, my voice shaking a little, but I speak anyway: 'You don't have to carry this alone tonight. That meeting tomorrow — I'm going to figure out a way to make sure it doesn't happen. But first, tell me everything he's done.'

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to take the money. In a tone that is tightly controlled but unmistakably clear, I explain what was actually lost — I don't break down in front of my uncle, but I don't pretend this is something we can just move past either. I push the stack of bills back, my voice level, every word bitten off clean: 'This isn't about money, Uncle. That drive had six months of my work on it. No backup. It's gone. I can't take this.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse the money and, in a trembling but clear voice, speak the true weight of what was lost — not to demand compensation, but to keep myself from disappearing into silence. I push the stack of bills back across the table, my voice shaking a little: 'Uncle, this isn't about replacing a hard drive. There were six months of my work on there — things that may be gone forever. I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to set aside any emotional confrontation and prioritize dealing with the actual loss — the drive itself. I politely but directly push the money back and ask for the drive. I say to my uncle: 'No need for the money — there are some work files on the drive, I need to take it and see if anything can be recovered. Please, make yourself comfortable.' I casually push the stack of bills back onto the coffee table, pick up the drive and the screw, and head to another room to look up data recovery services.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to make my position clear calmly but firmly — neither accepting the cash nor letting things explode into a scene. I push the wad of money back across the coffee table and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Uncle, I can't take this. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't about the cost of replacing a hard drive. Those files are gone, and that's that. I understand the kid didn't know any better, but I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: A
options.content: I won't agree to unconditional secrecy — but I won't flatly refuse her either, and I won't turn around and go straight to a teacher. Instead, I choose to be honest with her first about where I stand, and try to bring her along with me. I say to her: 'I can't promise you complete secrecy — not because I don't care about you, but because I do care, and that's exactly why I can't. But I won't go behind your back. Let's figure out together how to handle this, okay?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to promise secrecy right away. Instead, I steady her first — with my body and my words — so she feels caught, held. And inside, I've already decided I need to find a way to tell an adult. I take her hand, my voice shaking a little: 'I'm not going anywhere, I'm not going anywhere at all — but I can't promise to keep this secret, because I'm scared something will happen to you, and I'm more afraid of losing you than I am of you being angry at me. Can we just talk first? Tell me what's actually been going on with you lately.'

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse to promise secrecy, but don't leave immediately — choose to stay and explain my reasons to her first, then let her know I will be seeking help from an adult. I meet her eyes directly and say, in a steady voice: 'I can't promise you that — not because I don't care about you, but because this is something I can't carry alone, and you shouldn't have to carry it alone either. I'll go with you to find someone, but I'm not going to pretend I didn't see anything today.'

options.label: D
options.content: I didn't promise to keep it secret. Instead, I put the focus on just being there with her — trying to make sure she didn't feel abandoned, while leaving room to seek help later. I crouched down and said quietly: 'I'm not going to go around telling everyone — but I also can't promise to do nothing. Can you tell me what's actually been going on lately?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to propose a limited, middle-ground path rather than digging in completely or backing down entirely — acknowledging that the time and conditions constraints are real, but asking that the risk points be formally documented and that partial control verification be introduced at key steps. I say, calmly: 'I understand the time and conditions constraints you've raised, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan. But the control data I ran is right here. What I'm asking for is at least a written note in the methodology section — flagging this risk point and specifying what measures we're taking to manage it. That way, whatever the outcome, the record is complete.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to give in, to move forward with the existing plan — but inside I'm full of resentment and self-doubt. I lower my head and say: "I understand. We'll go with what you said — I'll get the materials filled in as soon as possible." My voice comes out barely above a whisper, like an apology.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to pull back for now, avoid a direct confrontation, but keep the alternative proposal alive — angling for one small opening to compromise rather than giving everything up entirely. I lowered my head slightly and said: 'I understand the timeline math you're talking about, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — I'll get the required materials submitted. But could we hold onto that comparison data for now? Once the phase materials are cleared, I'd like to come back and ask you to take another look at it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold my position in person, not withdraw the materials, but proactively offer a middle path — trying to shift the conversation from confrontation toward joint problem-solving. I say: 'Professor, I understand your concerns, and I'm well aware of the time pressure. But I can't pretend those control data don't exist — I'm not trying to invalidate your framework; I'm asking you to look at where the specific risk points are. Could you give me ten minutes? Let's look together and see whether there's any possibility of making partial adjustments within the current conditions.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene — I don't return the bottle, I walk toward the bed curtain and speak, while preparing to reach out for outside help. I grip the pill bottle in my hand, walk to his bedside, and say quietly: 'I'm not leaving — I just don't want to. I'm holding onto this bottle for now — how are you doing right now?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to use the question he asked — the first thing he's said on his own — as my way in. I stay where I am, respond quietly, and don't kick the bottle back. I let my simple presence tell him that someone is listening. I set the bottle on my own desk, turn my chair to face his side of the room, and say calmly: "Didn't feel like going out tonight. You doing… okay over there?"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay. I don't return the pill bottle. I use his question as an opening and speak softly, trying to let him know that someone is here. I keep the bottle in my hand without moving, and say quietly: 'I didn't feel like going out tonight. Are you… doing okay over there?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to intervene directly, with confirming the medication situation as the immediate priority, and contacting emergency services if necessary. I carry the bottle to the curtain around his bed and speak quietly, without any edge in my voice: 'I didn't go out. This bottle — how many did you take?'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose partial compromise while holding the line: make it clear to the director that the weekend trip home is not being canceled, but offer to put in extra hours tonight to push the project forward, provide remote support on the critical sections over the weekend, and join a video call Sunday evening for the final-draft review. Say directly: 'I have a family commitment this weekend — it was locked in three days ago and I can't cancel it. I can stay late tonight and I'm available remotely over the weekend, but coming into the office isn't something I can do. Tell me which parts are the biggest bottlenecks and let's work out the priorities together.'

options.label: B
options.content: I won't agree to being in the office both days of the weekend, but I'll propose a compromise and stay responsible for the core content. I'll say: I have to go back home to be with my father for his follow-up appointment, but I'll hand everything off tonight with a clear checklist, revise remotely over the weekend, and do a final online review of the draft Sunday evening.

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to agree to the overtime request, set aside the plan to go home, and didn't raise any conditions or alternatives on the spot. I heard myself say: 'Okay, I'll do my best to carve out time over these two days — we'll do the final review Sunday evening.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to propose a compromise on the spot — neither agreeing outright nor refusing — and proactively explain my family situation while pushing for a flexible arrangement. I say to the director: 'I will absolutely carry this project — but there's something at home I need to be upfront about. My dad has a follow-up appointment this weekend, and I promised to go with him. I'll head back Saturday to take care of that, then come back the same evening or first thing Sunday morning. Whatever time I have left, I'll pour everything into the proposal — submitting Sunday night is no problem. Does that work for you?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to politely decline the invitation while buying myself some breathing room — I won't agree on the spot, but I won't burn the bridge either; I'll use 'I need to think it over seriously' to extract myself from this pressure cooker first. I pick up my Americano, take a sip, set it back down, and look up at him: 'I hear what you're saying, and I've taken it all in — but this is too big a decision for me to make right now. Let me go away and think it through properly. I'll give you an answer by Tuesday.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to express genuine interest while making it clear that I needed to verify the information before giving any answer, keeping the decision in my own hands. I told the senior: "I listened carefully to everything you said, and I'm going to take this seriously. But before I give you an answer, I need to see the original letter of intent, and whatever user data the product has right now — if there is any. I'll give you a definitive reply before Tuesday."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to express enthusiasm and lean toward accepting, while carving out a small buffer rather than committing on the spot. I lean forward, meet my senior's eyes directly, and say: 'I don't need to wait until Tuesday — this direction feels right to me, and everything you said landed. Give me 48 hours. Not to hesitate, but to get my own side of things sorted out — and then we sit down and talk through the details for real.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stall with a vague, positive-sounding response rather than actually commit — neither refusing nor promising, keeping a way out for myself. I smiled, pressed my hands against my knees to hide the trembling, and said: "I'm honestly so surprised you thought of me — this opportunity… I need to think about it seriously. Everything you said, I've taken it all in. I'll give you an answer by Tuesday for sure."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to turn down my ex's request to get back together, but my response is vague and riddled with self-attacking apologies — and I decide to hide the details of this meeting from my current boyfriend. I say to my ex: "We can't go back to what we were. You deserve better. I'm sorry for making you wait so long" — then I lower my head, unable to meet his eyes, while inside I've already started running through how I'm going to explain to my current boyfriend why I'm coming home late tonight.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond to my ex directly, set a clear boundary, and give him no vague hope; I also decide to go home and tell my current partner honestly what happened at this meeting. I say to my ex: 'I hear what you're saying. That time meant a lot to me too, but I'm in a serious relationship now, and I can't give you a 'maybe.' That wouldn't be fair to you, and it wouldn't be fair to the person I'm with.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to turn down getting back together — gently but clearly — while acknowledging that I did waver inside, and I decide to tell my current partner about this meeting. I say to my ex: "I won't lie to you and say there's nothing there, but I'm with someone now, and I don't want to let him down — and I don't want to keep you waiting on an answer that's never going to come. This is where we stop." When I get home, I bring it up myself: I tell my current partner that I saw my ex today, and what my ex said.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to give a clear rejection on the spot, and I decide to tell my current partner that same day about everything that happened at this meeting. I look my ex in the eye and say: 'I'm grateful you told me all of this, but the question of whether I've moved on — the answer is yes, I have. I'm in a serious relationship right now, and I'm not going to leave it hanging here. I wish you well.' Then that evening I reach out to my partner first and lay out everything that happened today, exactly as it was.

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to deliver the evidence asynchronously, hand the decision back to the roommate, and keep myself out of any direct confrontation. Quietly screenshot and save the search results, close his laptop, and once he walks in, send him a message: 'I accidentally saw your section just now — ran a search, sending you the screenshots. It's due the day after tomorrow. You decide what to do with it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to sidestep the direct confrontation for now — quietly close my roommate's laptop screen, act as though nothing happened, and privately turn over in my mind the idea of contacting the professor anonymously afterward. I quickly fold his screen shut, retreat to my own chair, and fix my eyes back on my own document; by the time the door handle turns, my expression has already settled back into the look of someone who has seen nothing at all.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell my roommate directly, face-to-face — lay out the facts and require him to fix it before the deadline, without any emotional judgment, and without going around him to contact the professor. Once he comes in and sits down, I say calmly: 'I accidentally saw your section just now. I searched a few passages, and they're word-for-word matches with articles online. It's due the day after tomorrow — you need to fix it, or the whole group is done for.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up before my roommate gets back — to tell him directly what I found, framing it as 'I came across it by accident, and we need to talk,' and to demand that he rewrite it before submission, while making clear that the consequences fall on both of us. I stand up, walk to the door, and the moment it opens I say: 'I accidentally saw what was on your screen just now, and I searched a few of the passages — we need to talk right now, because the deadline is the day after tomorrow and both our names are on this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to comply for now in order to get out of immediate physical danger, but inwardly I resolve not to give up on recovering the deposit — the moment I leave, I'll contact someone who can help me (a tenant rights hotline, legal aid, or a friend). I lower my head and say, voice trembling but as steady as I can manage: 'Fine, I'll go for tonight — but we're not done talking about the deposit. I'll be handling this through the proper channels.' Then I bend down and start gathering the clothes from the floor, and I stop meeting his eyes.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to yield for now and leave without confronting them head-on — but inside I'm already calculating the next steps: memorize the landlord's information, photograph the scene, and once I'm out, pursue this through legal channels or whatever other means I can find. I lower my head and bend down to pick up the clothes scattered across the floor, keeping my voice very quiet: 'Fine, I'm leaving — but I'm taking my own things with me.' Then I stuff the clothes into my bag one by one, hoist my luggage onto my back, and walk out of that hallway.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now, giving up any confrontation in the moment — but I haven't given up on recovering the deposit. I press that thought down into the deepest part of myself, to be dealt with another day. I lower my head, and in a voice that's barely audible, I say: 'I'm leaving now, but this isn't over.' Then I bend down and start picking up the clothes scattered across the floor, hands trembling, eyes never once lifting to look at him.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to comply for now and leave without arguing over the deposit on the spot, but I quietly note every key detail for pursuing my rights afterward. I bend down to pick up my ID and phone from the floor, and say calmly: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight. But the matter of the contract — we'll settle that later.' Then I pick up my luggage and walk out.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to fall into a brief frozen state — neither fleeing nor managing to explain myself — and instinctively lower my head, raising my hand to shield my face from the cameras. I lift my hand to block the phones aimed at me, keep my head down, and say in a voice barely above a whisper: 'I didn't… I was just looking at my phone…'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to strike back loudly and immediately, actively turning this livestreamed scene into my own stage for clearing my name. I hold my phone screen up high, my voice cutting through the noise of the carriage, staring straight into the camera: 'Everyone, look closely — what's on my phone is a work group chat, check the timestamp yourself — I did not photograph anyone. This is a false accusation!'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to go on the offensive — counter with evidence directly, no explanations, no apologies, no showing weakness. I snapped my phone screen around toward the live-stream camera, my voice calm but unmistakably clear: 'You're live-streaming? Good. Let everyone get a good look at this screen — this is the work group chat, the timestamp is right there, my phone was never pointed at you.' Then I pulled my collar free from their grip and held their gaze.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to proactively present the evidence and use facts to cut through the chaos, rather than argue or silently endure. I raise my phone screen high and turn it toward the phone that's livestreaming, and say in a steady voice: 'Everyone, take a good look at this screen — this is our department's work group chat. A new request came in at four in the morning, and I was just reading the message. The timestamp is right here.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold off on taking any position and prioritize gathering information, asking her to spell out exactly what the specific boundaries are. I set down my phone, looked at her, and said: 'When you say flexibility, I'd like to hear you be a little more specific — the independent space you're talking about, what kinds of things does that mean, and how far does it go?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse the proposed adjustment to our relationship boundaries and demand that everything be said plainly. I don't look at my phone — I keep my eyes on him and say: 'I can't accept this. To me, this is just another way of backing out.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to set aside any direct response for now, letting confusion and silence stand in for any real position, while getting distracted by my phone. I looked up at her, stayed quiet for a few seconds, my voice coming out a little hoarse: 'All of this you're saying… I need to think about it.' Then, almost without realizing it, I glanced down at my phone screen as it lit up.

options.label: D
options.content: Refuse the proposed redefinition of the relationship's boundaries; hold to the original commitment. I press the phone face-down first, then tell him plainly: 'I can't accept this kind of redefinition — we need to talk this through properly.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to use my colleague's private message as the entry point — passing along a word of caution from experience indirectly rather than directly, so it doesn't come across as a forceful intervention through a public comment. My reply to my colleague: "I saw it too, and your instinct might be right — if you're open to it, we could talk. I once ran a project with a very similar structure, and there are a few wrong turns that might be worth mentioning."

options.label: B
options.content: Choose a light-touch intervention over silence — leave a door open through open-ended questions rather than pouring experience over him. Send the young colleague a private message: 'Saw your project — there's a detail that brought some interesting questions to mind for me. If you ever feel like talking through certain nodes in the process architecture, I'm around whenever — purely out of curiosity, not advice.' And reply to my colleague: 'I feel the same way, but this is his exploration. We can just be here when he wants to talk.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to avoid commenting publicly, and instead respond to my colleague through the relatively safer channel of a private message, without directly engaging with the younger colleague's post. Private message reply: 'I saw it too — I'm a little worried, but it doesn't feel right to say something outright. If you think there's a problem, do you want to talk it through first?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to use my colleague's private message as the entry point for passing along the information, rather than speaking up directly in the comments. I reply to my colleague: 'I took a look — there are a few specific issues with the process. Do you want me to write them out? You can decide what to do with them.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to respond directly in a way that appears composed on the surface but is in fact an act of self-protection——neither pushing back hard nor conceding weakness, pulling the conversation back to the meeting itself. I take a deep breath and say: 'Whether I can handle it or not——we'll find out by doing it. I've already gone through that unfinished module, and that's exactly why we're here today, to align on the schedule. Everyone take a seat. Let's get started.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on with an open stance, and to actively reframe the senior team member's provocation as a topic for the whole group to work through together—establishing my rhythm as team lead rather than shutting him down. I smile, stand up straight, and speak at a volume that's calm but carries to every corner of the room: "Good question—that module is actually exactly what I want us to dig into today. Old Li, you've got the deepest experience on this one. When we meet in a bit, you go first, and we'll all figure out together how to move it forward."

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to meet the challenge head-on — openly acknowledging the information gap while folding the senior team member's expertise into the meeting agenda, converting confrontation into a collaborative framework. I said: 'You're right — I haven't fully gotten my head around that module yet. So what I'd like to do in today's meeting is hear from you first about where things got stuck last quarter. You know this piece better than I do. Let's lay the problems out on the table, and then we can map out the plan for this quarter.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on—no deflecting, no escalating—and set the tone with a steady, grounded composure. I speak up, voice even, neither rushed nor slow: 'Good question. I've already looked at the unfinished module—this team meeting today is exactly where we sort out the schedule. Let's all sit down and walk through it together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay at the intersection — refusing to drive straight home and pretend everything is normal, but also refusing to completely fall apart — and instead try to treat these twenty years as a text that needs to be reread rather than a mistake that needs to be negated. I didn't start the engine. I turned my phone face-down on the passenger seat and said quietly to myself: 'This isn't a conclusion. This is just a chapter I haven't finished reading yet.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stop at the intersection for a few minutes and give myself a moment alone — neither falling apart nor pretending nothing happened, just letting the feeling settle before I drive home. I turn my phone face-down, close my eyes, take a slow breath, and tell myself: 'Go home first. Just go home tonight. I'll deal with this later.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to drive straight home, not allowing this moment to spiral into a sweeping interrogation of my entire life, but instead treating it as a signal that has been read and can now be filed away. I turn my phone face-down, press the accelerator, and head in the direction of home.

options.label: D
options.content: I allow myself a brief breakdown at the intersection, but in the end I use self-attack to seal off any genuine questioning, substituting "pretending to accept" for truly facing it. I flip my phone face-down, take a deep breath, and murmur to myself: "You didn't have a choice back then — this is just who you are" — then I put the car in gear and drive home.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to respond — to tuck my need inside a suggestion, phrased lightly, as if it just slipped out, not as if I'm scared. I lift my head, my voice steadier than I expected: "Maybe… let's go out for a walk together — I don't really know what I'd do with myself today anyway."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond proactively, suggesting we go out for a walk together — using one concrete action to open up this new chapter. I say: 'Come on, let's go out and wander around — it's not like we have anything else going on today. Let's just see what's outside.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to sidestep this shared strangeness — to gently acknowledge that it exists, rather than each of us retreating back into the familiar busyness. I look down at the table and say: "This does feel strange. How about… we have a cup of coffee together first? No need to think about what comes next."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to break the silence and reach toward him — not let this morning dissolve into each of us going our separate ways. I speak up: 'Let's go out for a walk. I don't really know what else to do either — but not knowing together seems a little better than not knowing apart.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while seeking legal support, and refrain from making statements about others when the information isn't clear. Say aloud: 'I need to consult legal counsel first — I'll give you a response within 24 hours. As for the circumstances of my own signature, I can clarify that right now: at the time, I was simply asked by my superior to review a budget document. I was not involved in any decision-making.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to defer full cooperation and request legal assistance before giving any formal response; do not provide any information about team members on the spot. Say aloud: 'I need my lawyer present before I can formally answer these questions — I'll be in touch with you within 24 hours. But what I can tell you right now is that I only looked over that document; I was never involved in any funding decisions.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to delay full cooperation and seek legal counsel immediately — selectively acknowledging the basic facts while refusing to provide any information about team members. To the investigator: 'I understand what you're asking. I did see that document — that much is true. But without a lawyer present, I won't be providing any further information. That's the more responsible approach for both of us. I need 24 hours. I'll make good use of them.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to cooperate within limits while immediately seeking legal protection, and refuse to provide any substantive information before a lawyer is present. Say aloud: 'I need to contact my lawyer first — until then, I'm not in a position to make any statement about the specifics. This isn't a refusal to cooperate; it's my basic right.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell my family as soon as possible, but I'll hold the rhythm steady — I won't frame it as a catastrophe. Tonight I'll bring my partner in, be honest that the doctor has asked for a follow-up and that I'm worried there may be something to it, and we'll go together tomorrow to find out for sure.

options.label: B
options.content: I'm choosing to get the follow-up done as soon as possible, and to tell my family about the risk first — no more hiding it. I'll book the appointment first, then tell everyone at home that I saw something abnormal and need to go in right away.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to keep it to myself for now, go to the follow-up appointment alone to confirm the results, and only decide whether to tell my family once I have more information. I press the phone screen dark, quietly note the follow-up appointment time in my memo app, then walk out of the study and tell my family I'm a little tired tonight and am turning in early.

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to hold off on telling family for now; prioritize completing the follow-up test and obtaining the official report before deciding on next steps. Pick up the phone immediately, call the doctor's office to schedule the earliest available follow-up appointment, and jot down in a notes app the specific marker questions that need to be confirmed.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to keep trying to dissuade him — I'll lay out the factual hallmarks of a pyramid scheme structure directly, decline the investment invitation, and not dignify the 'jealousy' accusation with a response. I say: 'I'm not putting money in, and it's not because I'm jealous. This model of yours — recruiting downlines, tiered profit-sharing, returns that depend on the people below you rather than on any actual product — that's the textbook structure of a pyramid scheme. There's nothing I can do about the eight hundred thousand that's already gone, but don't ask me to hand over this hundred and fifty thousand.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak my honest assessment gently but directly — not sidestepping the pyramid-scheme structure for what it is, but keeping the focus on my friend's state of mind rather than on who's right or wrong, and refusing to let his defensive attacks pull me off course. I set down the PowerPoint, look at him, and say: "I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're stupid — I just saw some things in this material that made me genuinely worried about you, and I want to talk about it seriously, not to tear you down, but because you're someone I care about."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to keep trying to talk him out of it, but instead of flat-out rejecting what he's saying, I use self-disclosure and emotional connection to try to get around his defenses. I set down the slides and say quietly: 'I'm not jealous of you. I'm just scared. Scared that three months from now you'll call me to say the money's gone — and that today I said nothing.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to keep pushing back, not to sidestep the conflict, and to say out loud what I can see — even if he thinks right now that I'm just jealous. I set down the PPT and look up at him: 'Man, I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're an idiot — but I've seen this structure before. Recruit people in, split commissions by tier, guaranteed returns that double your money — this is the textbook template for a pyramid scheme. That 800,000 of yours is already in there, and I can't pretend this PPT isn't a problem.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to answer the call but brush it off vaguely — "everything's fine, nothing major" — and leave it at that until the follow-up results come in. I picked up, keeping my voice as steady as I could: "Nothing to worry about, just a couple of minor indicators a little off — the doctor said to just keep an eye on it. Don't stress, we'll talk when I'm back Friday."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to answer the video call and share the information honestly but precisely, separating the facts from any conclusions. I pick up and say, calmly: 'The report came back. Two markers are elevated — the doctor recommends a follow-up, and I'm already arranging it. There's nothing more to say until those results are in. You don't need to come back.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I gloss over everything — I downplay it with "it's fine, a couple of indicators came back slightly elevated, the doctor said to just keep an eye on it" — and I don't breathe a word about the line that says "recommend follow-up as soon as possible to rule out the possibility of early pathological changes." I pick up, manage to arrange my face into something that looks reasonably calm, and say: "Just got out — overall it's fine, a couple of minor indicators were a little high, the doctor said it's probably just been too hectic lately, told me to watch my diet a bit. Nothing serious."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I keep things vague for now — I'm not going to drop the worst of it on her yet. Once I pick up, I tell her: two of my markers came back elevated, the doctor wants me to get a follow-up as soon as possible, and the moment those results are in, you'll be the first to know.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to look down and avoid it — deflecting with something vague, neither standing up to speak nor agreeing to enter the competition. I lower my head and say quietly: "Teacher… what I said just now wasn't very clear, I wasn't really thinking that much about it — the competition thing… I'm really not up for it."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to quietly decline standing up to explain, and respond to the suggestion about the speech competition with a vague, evasive shake of my head. I lower my gaze and say in a very small voice: "I… I can't really explain it well — teacher, you go ahead and tell them."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea in front of the class, while remaining open to the speech competition proposal without rushing to commit. I stand up and say evenly: "Put the elephant on one side of the seesaw, then keep adding people to the other side until it balances — remember how many people that is, then weigh one person, and multiply. That tells you how much the elephant weighs." When I finish, I sit back down and tell the teacher: "The competition — I'll think it over and let you know."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to respond in a vague, retreating way — neither outright refusing the teacher nor actually standing up to perform — trying to shrink myself back into the crowd. I keep my head down and say in a very small voice: "Teacher… what I said just now wasn't very clear, it probably wasn't that good…" and give a small shake of my head, without standing up.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse to use the answer sheet, and I won't let my friend copy it either — I'll return the paper or put it back where it was, and I won't take part in this gamble on luck. I tell my friend: 'I'm not using this, and you shouldn't copy it either — have you actually calculated what it costs if we get caught? I'm putting it back. What you do after that is your call.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend and either hand the paper to the teacher or put it back where it came from. I whisper to her, "We can't use this — we'll get caught," and then, while she's not paying attention, I slip the paper back into the crack in the desk or hand it to the teacher, pretending I just happened to find it by accident.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request, return the answer sheet to where it was or hand it over to the teacher — I won't use it or let anyone copy from it. I tell my friend: 'This paper isn't ours. Using it wouldn't even mean anything — we'd still be lost next time. I've put it back. If you want to review, we can go through the material together.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request and decide to hand the answer sheet over to the teacher or return it to where it belongs — trading honesty for genuine peace of mind. I fold the paper and clutch it in my hand, then shake my head at Xiaoyu and say quietly: 'We can't use this — it belongs to the class rep, and I'm going to give it to the teacher. Don't worry, I won't say you saw it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I decided not to express immediate support or opposition to the special recruitment, and instead to return the decision to my child first, while buying time for the whole family to discuss it together. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you — this is very significant news for us. Before we make any decision, I want to genuinely hear what my child himself has to say. This is ultimately his path to walk, and I want him to walk it because he truly wants to, not because of our expectations. Could you give us a little time?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold off on committing and gather the key information first before making any decision. I tell the teacher: 'Thank you for letting us know. I need to talk with my child first and find out what he thinks, and I'd also like to learn more about the specifics of this program — things like the exit mechanism and academic safeguards. We'll get back to you after that.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to avoid taking an immediate stance, using "needing to have a serious talk with my child" to buy myself some breathing room, while shifting the decision to my child themselves in order to ease my own fear. I say to the teacher: "Thank you so much — they really have worked so hard… I need to sit down and talk it through with them first, hear what they think, and make this decision together."

options.label: D
options.content: I've made up my mind to support the professional path — clearly and without reservation — while insisting that we call my kid out here right now for a three-way conversation, so he can say what he wants in his own words. I tell the teacher: "Personally, I'm in favor — but we need to ask him directly, right now. Can we bring him out here so the three of us can talk this through together?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to engage by asking questions — approaching this with curiosity rather than direct confrontation or silent compliance — in order to open up space for real dialogue within the execution framework. I say: 'My objection isn't that we can't change things — but I want to ask you first: what specific observations about the existing process is this proposal based on? Because some of those steps were designed the way they were to solve particular problems. If you know which problems, we can figure out together whether the new process actually covers them.'

options.label: B
options.content: Accept the overall direction on the surface, while fighting for a voice at the implementation level — trading 'I'll oversee the transition' for real room to preserve the process details that matter. I speak up: "Fine, I have no issue with the overall direction. But if I'm the one overseeing the transition, then I need to break this down according to my own understanding of how these workflows operate. I'll hold the pace you want — but when it comes to the specifics of what gets changed, I need you to give me the space to do that."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose surface-level compliance, preemptively putting myself down, using cooperation to mask the collapse happening inside — while tentatively floating one small, cushioning request to hold onto the last shred of dignity. I glanced down at the proposal, keeping my voice carefully flat: "Alright, I'll oversee the transition period. A few of the handoff cycles are on the longer side — I'll put together a timeline for you to sign off on, that way we can keep the risk down."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to raise the specific risk points on the spot, grounding everything in operational facts — not challenging the overall direction, but pushing for adjustments on the nodes that have real problems. I open the proposal and point to two of the timeline entries: 'I have no objection to the big-picture direction — but here and here, the transition period is going to hit concrete problems at this pace. I can walk you through exactly what they are. Do you want to hear it?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively — walk over to the new student and break the ice in a task-oriented way. I pick up my tray, stand up, tell my friends 'Go ahead, I'll catch up,' then walk over to the table in the corner and sit down, saying to the new student: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — . It's your first day — if there's anything you're not sure about, feel free to ask me.'

options.label: B
options.content: I leave my friends' table, tray in hand, and walk alone toward the new girl in the corner. I set my tray down beside her and take a seat, saying quietly: "Is it okay if I sit here? My name is… we're actually in the same class today."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to set down my chopsticks and walk over to the corner to strike up a conversation with the new classmate, instead of heading to the playground with my friends. I carry my tray over to that little table against the wall, sit down with a smile, and say: 'Is it okay if I sit here? What's your name? I'm — . Let's eat together — it's so loud in this cafeteria, eating alone is no fun!'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table and walk over alone to the new classmate in the corner. I set my tray down across from her and say, calmly: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — , what's your name?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold it in for now, steady my emotions, and wait until he's gone before finding the right moment to talk to him alone. I took the milk from him, glanced down at it for a moment, and said quietly: 'Let's talk about the speech later — go wash up first. I have something I want to say to you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold back and not expose everything in this moment, but I decide that tonight I will find a quiet moment alone with him to talk through this face to face — and to stop him from giving the speech at the commendation ceremony and from registering for the competition. I take the milk from him, set down what I'm holding, and say calmly: 'Let's put the speech aside for now. There's something important we need to talk about tonight — come find me in the study when you're done eating.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation in this moment — but I've already made up my mind to find the right time tonight to talk with him alone. For now I respond to him calmly, saying, 'The speech sounds good — but let's sit down a bit later and talk through the competition thing more carefully,' keeping my tone perfectly ordinary, nothing out of the usual.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to go along with what he's saying for now — no exposing him on the spot — but I use one sentence to keep him here, laying the groundwork for a private conversation later tonight. I take the milk from him, lower my head and drink a sip, then look up at him and say: 'The speech can wait — sit down for a second. Dad has something he wants to talk to you about, just the two of us.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note so my friend can't see it, and decide to go to the meeting alone after school. I flip the note face-down and slide it under my textbook in one casual motion, then tell my friend offhandedly: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking to borrow my notes.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and go alone — I fold it shut quickly and press it under my textbook, smile at my friend and say 'It's nothing, just some random scribbling,' and after school I head to the big banyan tree on the field by myself.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and hold off on the after-school plan for now, putting my friend's feelings ahead of my own. I fold the note casually and tuck it back into my textbook, then smile over at her and say: "It's nothing — someone from the next class asking to borrow my notes. Keep working on your homework."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note — I shield it with my body, fold it quickly, and stuff it back into my textbook, then brush off my best friend with a lie. As for whether to show up after school, I haven't decided yet, but I'm leaning toward not going, or putting off the decision until the very last second. I press my textbook down over the note and look up at her with a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking to borrow my notes.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose silence and retreat — no confrontation, no questions, every word swallowed back down, replacing the urge to explode with coldness and distance. I lower my head, shift my gaze away from her, and say quietly, "It's nothing, my eyes are just a little dry," then flip my textbook open and hold it in front of my face, not looking at her again.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to confront her directly — to put the screenshot and the side group chat out in the open, no holding back and no dancing around it. I turn toward her, lower my voice, but speak with complete directness: "I saw that group chat. I saw what you said. Can you tell me — what exactly is going on?"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation, not here, not yet. I'll wait until I've calmly worked out what outcome I actually want before deciding on a next step. I turn off my phone screen, look up, give her one expressionless glance, then drop my eyes and open my textbook.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to swallow the confrontation — to hold it down and fake composure long enough to get through this moment — without bringing up the screenshot or the private group chat. I keep my head low, eyes fixed on the surface of the desk, giving no response to what she said, not glancing at either of their phones, just gripping my sleeve cuffs tight in my fist and waiting for the burning behind my eyes to slowly fade.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to wait and watch — I won't accept my former colleague's invitation, and I'll prepare to tell my supervisor tomorrow that I'm willing to align with the company's current direction and that I want to keep my position. I reply to my former colleague: 'I support your decision, but I've got family pressure on my end — quitting isn't something I'm considering right now. I'll see how things play out here first.' Then I open my calendar and accept the supervisor's invite.

options.label: B
options.content: I'm choosing to take the risk and make the transition — but I won't quit cold turkey right away. Instead, I'm going to lay my cards on the table proactively. Tonight I'll sign up for a course and put together a learning plan; tomorrow I'll tell my manager that I want to move into an AI-related role and ask about the possibility of an internal transition.

options.label: C
options.content: I'm choosing to actively gather information and hold off on any final decision, while preparing for tomorrow's conversation. Two things to do tonight: organize the industry data and specific transition-path information I currently have, and draw up a few key questions for the manager — what the company's actual plans are for existing roles, whether internal transfers into AI-related positions are a possibility, and what their read is on the staffing structure over the next year.

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to spend tonight systematically building a decision framework, then walk into tomorrow's conversation with my manager armed with clear analysis — rather than making any hasty declarations. I open my laptop, create a new document, type "Transition Path Assessment" as the title, and begin listing out the timeline milestones, resource requirements, risk items, and quantifiable evaluation criteria for both paths, while in a separate column I draft the agenda for tomorrow's conversation and note the key information I need to get from my manager.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to file the complaint in my own name, as an individual — but I make my disappointment and my boundaries clear to the neighbors who were 'full of advice but unwilling to sign.' I say to the retired teacher: 'I'll go file the report, and I'll put my name on it — but I need you all to promise me this: if the housing authority comes to verify the facts, you cannot deny that you knew. I'm not asking you to be out in front. But I can't stand there alone and lie by saying I discovered all of this by myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: I've decided to file a real-name report with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name, and to make my position clear to the neighbor face-to-face: if you support this, that support needs to be real action. I understand if you're not willing to co-sign, but don't use 'why bother' to strip this thing of its meaning. I say to the old neighbor: 'Brother, I'm going to file the report — putting my name down alone is fine. But I want you to remember: tonight, you said with your own mouth that none of this was a problem. If anyone asks about it down the road, I hope you'll tell the truth. Two million yuan is not a small thing. I'm not doing this for a few thousand bucks — I just don't want the people in this complex to think that if someone steals, they steal, and nobody does a damn thing about it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I've decided to file a named complaint with the Housing and Construction Bureau in my own name, accept the neighbors' quiet support without pressing them for a joint signature, and calmly let them know where I stand. I'll tell them: 'The materials are solid enough — my name alone will do. If you're willing to help track down more documents, I'll take that; I'm not going to push the joint-signature angle — but I am going to file the complaint.'

options.label: D
options.content: I've decided to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name — but I'm making it clear to the neighbors: quiet support is fine, but I'm not out here taking all the risk for everyone for nothing. I'll keep everyone posted as things develop. To the retired teacher, I said: "I understand why you won't sign your names — but I've already done the digging, and I'm going to file this complaint. However you want to help, that's up to you. Just don't expect me to carry the whole thing by myself and call it done."

options.label: A
options.content: I won't agree to unconditional secrecy — but I won't flatly refuse her either, and I won't turn around and go straight to a teacher. Instead, I choose to be honest with her first about where I stand, and try to bring her along with me. I say to her: 'I can't promise you complete secrecy — not because I don't care about you, but because I do care, and that's exactly why I can't. But I won't go behind your back. Let's figure out together how to handle this, okay?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to promise secrecy right away. Instead, I steady her first — with my body and my words — so she feels caught, held. And inside, I've already decided I need to find a way to tell an adult. I take her hand, my voice shaking a little: 'I'm not going anywhere, I'm not going anywhere at all — but I can't promise to keep this secret, because I'm scared something will happen to you, and I'm more afraid of losing you than I am of you being angry at me. Can we just talk first? Tell me what's actually been going on with you lately.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to make any promise on the spot. Instead, I hold space for his pain first, and then — gently but firmly — tell him we need to find an adult who can help. I crouch down and take his hand, and I say: 'I won't lie to you and tell you I promise, because I'm too scared of losing you — let's go find someone together, and I'll be right there with you the whole time.'

options.label: D
options.content: I didn't promise to keep it secret. Instead, I put the focus on just being there with her — trying to make sure she didn't feel abandoned, while leaving room to seek help later.
