I crouched down and said quietly: 'I'm not going to go around telling everyone — but I also can't promise to do nothing. Can you tell me what's actually been going on lately?'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose partial compromise while holding the line: make it clear to the director that the weekend trip home is not being canceled, but offer to put in extra hours tonight to push the project forward, provide remote support on the critical sections over the weekend, and join a video call Sunday evening for the final-draft review. Say directly: 'I have a family commitment this weekend — it was locked in three days ago and I can't cancel it. I can stay late tonight and I'm available remotely over the weekend, but coming into the office isn't something I can do. Tell me which parts are the biggest bottlenecks and let's work out the priorities together.'

options.label: B
options.content: I won't agree to being in the office both days of the weekend, but I'll propose a compromise and stay responsible for the core content. I'll say: I have to go back home to be with my father for his follow-up appointment, but I'll hand everything off tonight with a clear checklist, revise remotely over the weekend, and do a final online review of the draft Sunday evening.

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to agree to the overtime request, set aside the plan to go home, and didn't raise any conditions or alternatives on the spot. I heard myself say: 'Okay, I'll do my best to carve out time over these two days — we'll do the final review Sunday evening.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honestly explain the situation at home while proposing a middle-ground arrangement — such as handling part of the work remotely — rather than simply agreeing or outright refusing. I keep my eyes slightly down, voice low but as steady as I can manage: 'Director, my father has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend — I've already promised my family I'd go back to be with him. Could we do it this way: I push as far as I can tonight and before I leave tomorrow morning, I stay reachable remotely on the way there, and for the final draft Sunday night I'll do my best to either get back in time or go through it with you online. Would that work?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse the money and, in a trembling but clear voice, speak the true weight of what was lost — not to demand compensation, but to keep myself from disappearing into silence. I push the stack of bills back across the table, my voice shaking a little: 'Uncle, this isn't about replacing a hard drive. There were six months of my work on there — things that may be gone forever. I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to set aside any emotional confrontation and prioritize dealing with the actual loss — the drive itself. I politely but directly push the money back and ask for the drive. I say to my uncle: 'No need for the money — there are some work files on the drive, I need to take it and see if anything can be recovered. Please, make yourself comfortable.' I casually push the stack of bills back onto the coffee table, pick up the drive and the screw, and head to another room to look up data recovery services.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to decline the money gently but clearly, to speak the true weight of what was lost, without letting it escalate into a direct confrontation. I push the stack of bills back toward him, my voice slightly unsteady but without tears, and say: 'Uncle, I can't take the money — that hard drive had six months of my work on it, and there's no replacing it. I just need a little time to get through this.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to make my position clear calmly but firmly — neither accepting the cash nor letting things explode into a scene. I push the wad of money back across the coffee table and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Uncle, I can't take this. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't about the cost of replacing a hard drive. Those files are gone, and that's that. I understand the kid didn't know any better, but I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school — I won't switch institutions as a concession to save the relationship. I send a message explaining my decision, and I'm honest: if they can't continue under these circumstances, I respect that we part ways.

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to stay at my current school, and tell her directly — no evasion, no sugarcoating. My reply to her tonight: 'I listened to every word you said. Staying here is the only honest choice I can make. If I went north carrying regret, that wouldn't be fair to you either. I want to talk about whether we can find another way forward — but I can't give you an answer I don't believe myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to accept my own school's offer and not change course just to hold the relationship together. I write back to her: 'I've thought about it seriously — I'm staying at my school. If you're willing, let's talk through what the future looks like for us; if you're not, I'll accept that too.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school, and I accept that this may bring the relationship to an end. I text her back, tell her I'll be staying in my own program, and am honest with her: I love her, but I can't sustain what we have by rewriting the direction of my own life.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now, giving up any confrontation in the moment — but I haven't given up on recovering the deposit. I press that thought down into the deepest part of myself, to be dealt with another day. I lower my head, and in a voice that's barely audible, I say: 'I'm leaving now, but this isn't over.' Then I bend down and start picking up the clothes scattered across the floor, hands trembling, eyes never once lifting to look at him.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to comply for now in order to get out of immediate physical danger, but inwardly I resolve not to give up on recovering the deposit — the moment I leave, I'll contact someone who can help me (a tenant rights hotline, legal aid, or a friend). I lower my head and say, voice trembling but as steady as I can manage: 'Fine, I'll go for tonight — but we're not done talking about the deposit. I'll be handling this through the proper channels.' Then I bend down and start gathering the clothes from the floor, and I stop meeting his eyes.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to comply for now and leave without arguing over the deposit on the spot, but I quietly note every key detail for pursuing my rights afterward. I bend down to pick up my ID and phone from the floor, and say calmly: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight. But the matter of the contract — we'll settle that later.' Then I pick up my luggage and walk out.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to appear outwardly compliant for now in order to remove myself from the immediate threat of violence, while quietly initiating evidence collection and legal proceedings in the background — I will not give up the deposit under any circumstances. I say to him: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight — but the contract and the deposit, we're not done with that. You'd better take a good hard look at those contract terms, because I'm going to have someone verify them.' At the same time, I quietly start recording on my phone.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to drive straight home, not allowing this moment to spiral into a sweeping interrogation of my entire life, but instead treating it as a signal that has been read and can now be filed away. I turn my phone face-down, press the accelerator, and head in the direction of home.

options.label: B
options.content: He chooses to break the silence and reach out rather than suppress it alone or drive home and pretend everything is normal. He dials an old friend's number, his voice a little rough: 'I'm stuck at a red light right now — there's something I've been sitting on for a long time. Do you have a few minutes to talk?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay at the intersection — refusing to drive straight home and pretend everything is normal, but also refusing to completely fall apart — and instead try to treat these twenty years as a text that needs to be reread rather than a mistake that needs to be negated. I didn't start the engine. I turned my phone face-down on the passenger seat and said quietly to myself: 'This isn't a conclusion. This is just a chapter I haven't finished reading yet.'

options.label: D
options.content: I allow myself a brief breakdown at the intersection, but in the end I use self-attack to seal off any genuine questioning, substituting "pretending to accept" for truly facing it. I flip my phone face-down, take a deep breath, and murmur to myself: "You didn't have a choice back then — this is just who you are" — then I put the car in gear and drive home.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to keep trying to talk him out of it, but instead of flat-out rejecting what he's saying, I use self-disclosure and emotional connection to try to get around his defenses. I set down the slides and say quietly: 'I'm not jealous of you. I'm just scared. Scared that three months from now you'll call me to say the money's gone — and that today I said nothing.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep trying to dissuade him — I'll lay out the factual hallmarks of a pyramid scheme structure directly, decline the investment invitation, and not dignify the 'jealousy' accusation with a response. I say: 'I'm not putting money in, and it's not because I'm jealous. This model of yours — recruiting downlines, tiered profit-sharing, returns that depend on the people below you rather than on any actual product — that's the textbook structure of a pyramid scheme. There's nothing I can do about the eight hundred thousand that's already gone, but don't ask me to hand over this hundred and fifty thousand.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak my honest assessment gently but directly — not sidestepping the pyramid-scheme structure for what it is, but keeping the focus on my friend's state of mind rather than on who's right or wrong, and refusing to let his defensive attacks pull me off course. I set down the PowerPoint, look at him, and say: "I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're stupid — I just saw some things in this material that made me genuinely worried about you, and I want to talk about it seriously, not to tear you down, but because you're someone I care about."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up directly and urge him to stop — without backing down because of his emotional reaction or the risk to our friendship — using facts and logic as my tools, refusing to trade silence for a surface-level peace. I flip the PowerPoint back to the first page, look him in the eye, and say: 'I'm not jealous of you. I've just seen too many people caught up in this exact structure and watched how it ends. Your income right now comes from the people you recruit, not from any product or service — that's the legal definition of a pyramid scheme, not my opinion, the law's definition. Eight hundred thousand is already gone, but you're still pulling more people in — that's what I'm actually worried about.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to reply to my colleague but deliberately narrow the boundary, using a polite yet clear-eyed tone to pull the connection back to a safe distance. I write: 'Made it home — thanks for checking in. That conversation the other day was really lovely; I'll have to recommend the bookstore to you sometime. Good night.' — Then I put down my phone, open my notes app, and add tomorrow's tutoring run with the kids to my schedule.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to reply, but keep the emotional register within a friendly, restrained boundary — not actively deepening things, but not coldly cutting them off either. I write back: "Made it home. The meeting today was actually pretty worthwhile. That bookstore sounds worth checking out sometime — it seems really quiet. Good night."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to reply to the message, but keep the wording concise and measured — clear boundaries, neither deliberately cold nor leaving any opening. I reply: 'Made it home. That line of thinking from today's meeting is worth developing further — the bookstore idea has potential. Goodnight.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to reply to my colleague's message for now — cutting off the bud with self-attack, pressing the longing back down. I darken the phone screen, pick up my partner's message, reply with a single "okay," then close both chat windows and stare at the blurred night outside the car window, telling myself: you could never hold something like this together.

options.label: A
options.content: Refuse to accept the asymmetrical 'money for labor' arrangement, and raise in the group chat the need to renegotiate a caregiving division of responsibilities that names specific people. Reply in the group chat: 'Let's set the money aside for now — I can't keep going the way things are. I need everyone to be clear about who can come, when they can come, and what they'll be responsible for. Let's get on a video call this week.' Then put the phone down, walk over to my partner, and say: 'I know. Let's talk.'

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to silently accept the arrangement being decided in that group chat — but I choose to put the phone down first and turn to face my partner, to have this conversation between the two of us before anything else, rather than exploding in the chat right now. I press the phone face-down onto the couch, stand up, and walk toward her. 'Let's talk first,' I say. 'Just the two of us.'

options.label: C
options.content: I'm not accepting another round of carrying the bulk of the caregiving alone — but I'm not blowing things up in the open either. I reply in the group chat: both money and caregiving need to be shared on a rotating schedule; I'll cover the medication this time, and tomorrow I'll send out the list and the shift rotation. Then I turn and quietly tell my partner that we need to talk tonight — really talk.

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to accept this unilaterally decided arrangement in the group chat. I choose to make my position explicitly clear in the family chat in writing, demanding that everyone renegotiate the specific division of responsibilities and cost-sharing — not a money-for-labor swap. I type into the group: "I wasn't part of this discussion. Caregiving isn't something one person should have to carry just because they live closest. I need everyone to sit down together and work out exactly how to divide this — both the time and the money need to be recalculated." Then I put down my phone and turn toward my partner.

options.label: A
options.content: On instinct I flip the note face-down on the desk to cover it, vaguely change the subject, and quietly decide to slip away to the meeting after school — without telling her who asked me to come. I press the note under my textbook and say with a smile: 'Oh, it's nothing — just some random classmate, no big deal — did you finish your homework yet?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note and brush off my best friend for now, while inwardly I've already decided to go to the meeting alone after school — but I need to deal with these two things separately first. I shoved the note into my textbook, looked up, and gave my best friend a smile: 'It's nothing, someone just asking to borrow my notes — totally boring.' My voice was steady, my eyes didn't waver.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note and refuse to let my friend see it; at the same time, I'm deeply resistant to the idea of showing up at all, and I'm leaning toward not going. I flip my textbook over, press the note underneath it, look up, and give a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking about homework. Nothing interesting.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and hold off on the after-school plan for now, putting my friend's feelings ahead of my own. I fold the note casually and tuck it back into my textbook, then smile over at her and say: "It's nothing — someone from the next class asking to borrow my notes. Keep working on your homework."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay. I don't leave. First I pull her up off the floor, I hear her out, and we face this mess together. I bend down, uncurl her hands from my pant leg, and then hold them, and I say: 'Come on, get up — the floor's cold. We'll figure out the apartment thing, one step at a time. But from now on, you can't carry something like this alone.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold my emotions in check for now — no immediate decision to leave, no immediate forgiveness — and instead ask him to lay everything out. I crouch down and say, in a voice that's quiet but steady: 'Get up first. Then tell me everything — how much is still owed, who these people are, what the situation looks like right now. All of it.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to set aside blame for now and shift into information-gathering and damage-assessment mode. Say aloud: 'Get up first. Walk me through the details — how much is still owed, who these people are, and whether there are any more threats coming.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to accept this explanation, and I'm not continuing as though things are the same between us. I gently moved her aside and said plainly that we're separating our finances for now — tomorrow we sit down and go through every account, every transaction, and where we each stand.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold back for now, to bide my time — no confrontation in the moment, but inside I've already started building a framework for "how to handle this." I press my hand flat on the desk, eyes fixed straight ahead, and wait for them to finish laughing. I don't say a single word — but in my mind, I've logged every detail of what I saw today.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to confront her directly — to put the screenshot and the side group chat out in the open, no holding back and no dancing around it. I turn toward her, lower my voice, but speak with complete directness: "I saw that group chat. I saw what you said. Can you tell me — what exactly is going on?"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to avoid the confrontation and escape the scene for now — no calling her out, no scene. I push every last bit of the anger and hurt down. I pick up my bag, keep my head low, walk out of the classroom, find a corner where no one can find me, shove my earbuds in, and pretend the entire world doesn't exist.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation, not here, not yet. I'll wait until I've calmly worked out what outcome I actually want before deciding on a next step. I turn off my phone screen, look up, give her one expressionless glance, then drop my eyes and open my textbook.

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to proactively approach the other person about collaboration, merging the two proposals together, and abandon the route of campaigning solo for votes. Say to my friend: 'Let's go talk to her — her plan solves the rehearsal problem, my plan has the content, and the two things can be pieced together. The credit doesn't matter; what matters is that the plan actually gets made.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to proactively approach her and propose merging the two plans into a single executable version, rather than fighting it out at the ballot. I tell my friend: "I'll go talk to her — keep the time-tunnel framework, fold her free-party elements in. That way the plan can actually run, and it's more solid than trying to pull votes."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to campaign hard for votes and stand by my original plan — no merger. I tell my friend: 'No merging — my plan is my plan. Come on, this weekend we go talk to everyone one by one. I'm bringing the sketch. Let them see it for themselves.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to step away from the competition — I won't go talk to her about merging, and I won't spend the weekend canvassing votes. I'll let the proposal lose on its own terms. I tell my friend: "Forget it — if everyone likes her proposal, they should go with it. I'll keep mine for myself."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lend him three thousand yuan, while requiring him to write down his contact information and sign an IOU to create a traceable record. I stand up and say: 'I'll lend it to you — write your name and phone number on this piece of paper. I need something to show for it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lend him the three thousand yuan, but I don't want to talk more than necessary — I want to get it done as fast as possible and go. I hold out my phone so he can see the balance, and say quietly: 'I'll transfer it to you. Give me your payment code. Doesn't matter whether you write an IOU or not.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan, accept the loss of a trip that may now never happen, and act on the strongest feeling of the present moment. I pull my phone out of my pocket, open Alipay, and say to him: 'Show me your payment code — I'll transfer it to you right now. You don't need to write me an IOU.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to lend the money, but I give him an alternative direction rather than walking away in silence. I crouch down and gently move his hand from my pant leg, and say calmly: 'I can't lend you anything — but go to the nurses' station right now, tell them the family has no money. The hospital has an emergency payment assistance process. Go ask them. Don't stay here on your knees.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to agree immediately, but also not to refuse outright — instead I try to propose a middle-ground arrangement, asking whether I could handle part of the work remotely or negotiate with the director to have certain modules shared with a colleague. I say: 'Director, I do have something I need to take care of at home this weekend — my father has a follow-up appointment, and I promised I'd go with him. I wanted to ask: would it be possible for me to pull together the market benchmarking section tonight, work remotely on Saturday, and then come into the office Sunday to go over the final draft? Or is there any chance a colleague could take on the slide redesign portion?'

options.label: B
options.content: I don't agree outright to being on-site the entire weekend. Instead, I propose a division of labor and a remote-coverage plan. I explain that I need to go back home to accompany my parents for Dad's follow-up appointment, but that I'll start tonight by drawing up a list of gaps, coordinate with colleagues on the market side to share the load, and complete my portion of the work remotely over the weekend.

options.label: C
options.content: I won't agree to being in the office both days of the weekend, but I'll propose a compromise and stay responsible for the core content. I'll say: I have to go back home to be with my father for his follow-up appointment, but I'll hand everything off tonight with a clear checklist, revise remotely over the weekend, and do a final online review of the draft Sunday evening.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to explain the situation at home and hold firm on going back, while offering a compromise to soften the blow of saying no. I tell the director: 'My dad has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend — I promised three days ago that I'd go with my parents, so there's really no way I can change that. But I can put together a written document tonight with the competitive market analysis section and send it to you. Maybe you could find someone to work off that for the redesign? Or I could do a remote review of the final draft Sunday evening?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up proactively — but only with boundary-clear, verifiable advice drawn from experience. I send the person a private message: I start by acknowledging the quality of what they've pushed forward, then flag two foundational risk checkpoints to verify. At the same time, I tell my colleague that I'll handle it quietly one-on-one, and won't steer the conversation in the group chat.

options.label: B
options.content: Choose a light-touch intervention over silence — leave a door open through open-ended questions rather than pouring experience over him. Send the young colleague a private message: 'Saw your project — there's a detail that brought some interesting questions to mind for me. If you ever feel like talking through certain nodes in the process architecture, I'm around whenever — purely out of curiosity, not advice.' And reply to my colleague: 'I feel the same way, but this is his exploration. We can just be here when he wants to talk.'

options.label: C
options.content: I've decided to reach out directly via private message, pointing out the specific structural risk points in the process — no emotional preamble, no 'just for your reference' disclaimer tacked on at the end. And I'll reply to my colleague: 'I'll say it directly. You don't need to get involved.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to use my colleague's private message as the entry point for passing along the information, rather than speaking up directly in the comments. I reply to my colleague: 'I took a look — there are a few specific issues with the process. Do you want me to write them out? You can decide what to do with them.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to accept my own school's offer and not change course just to hold the relationship together. I write back to her: 'I've thought about it seriously — I'm staying at my school. If you're willing, let's talk through what the future looks like for us; if you're not, I'll accept that too.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to give up my acceptance here and take the offer from the university up north — and tonight I give my partner a clear answer. I sent back a voice message: 'I'm coming to you. It's already decided. Wait for me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school, and I accept that this may bring the relationship to an end. I text her back, tell her I'll be staying in my own program, and am honest with her: I love her, but I can't sustain what we have by rewriting the direction of my own life.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school — I won't switch institutions as a concession to save the relationship. I send a message explaining my decision, and I'm honest: if they can't continue under these circumstances, I respect that we part ways.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to respond — to tuck my need inside a suggestion, phrased lightly, as if it just slipped out, not as if I'm scared. I lift my head, my voice steadier than I expected: "Maybe… let's go out for a walk together — I don't really know what I'd do with myself today anyway."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to make any active changes for now, and instead return to the familiar busyness to keep the days stable. I tell him: let's just keep things the way they've always been — I'll head to the shop first, and when I get back I'll tidy up around the house.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to actively respond to my partner's suggestion, converting the vague "let's go out for a walk together" into a concrete starting point for today — rather than each of us retreating into familiar busyness to avoid this blank space. I stand up and say: "Let's go — let's take that walk. When we get back, we can think about how to structure the time ahead."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to break the silence and reach toward him — not let this morning dissolve into each of us going our separate ways. I speak up: 'Let's go out for a walk. I don't really know what else to do either — but not knowing together seems a little better than not knowing apart.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay at the intersection — refusing to drive straight home and pretend everything is normal, but also refusing to completely fall apart — and instead try to treat these twenty years as a text that needs to be reread rather than a mistake that needs to be negated. I didn't start the engine. I turned my phone face-down on the passenger seat and said quietly to myself: 'This isn't a conclusion. This is just a chapter I haven't finished reading yet.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to go straight home — I give myself some time alone to absorb the impact of this — but I don't allow myself to fall apart completely, because falling apart solves nothing. I flip my phone face-down onto the passenger seat, start the car, and drive to a nearby parking lot. I sit there, staring at the dark windshield, and say quietly: "This variable can never be verified, so it shouldn't be grounds for invalidating the existing data." But I know I don't entirely believe it.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay where I am for now — neither falling apart nor forcing myself to calm down — and allow this tearing feeling to exist for a little while. I flip my phone face-down, take a slow deep breath, wait for the traffic light to change one more time, and then ease the car back toward home. Tonight, I go back. Everything else can wait.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay put for now — not go home right away, but not let myself completely fall apart either. Instead I try to convert this feeling of losing control into something that can be processed. I flip my phone face-down on the passenger seat, close my eyes, and tell myself: just list out tonight's tasks one by one. Everything else can wait until tomorrow.

options.label: A
options.content: I decide to tell my family — but I choose to bring it up in a calm, measured way, without amplifying the fear, laying out the facts clearly rather than making it sound catastrophic. Tonight, after the kids are asleep, I set down my phone, walk over to my partner, and say quietly: 'One of my results from today's check-up needs a follow-up — I don't know what it means yet, but I wanted you to know. We'll face it together.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep this to myself for now — to build the information framework first, and tell my family once the follow-up results are in. I picked up my phone, opened the calendar, and created a new reminder in tomorrow morning's open slot: 'Contact hospital — schedule follow-up — confirm timeline.' Then I turned the screen face-down on the desk.

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to hold off on telling family for now; prioritize completing the follow-up test and obtaining the official report before deciding on next steps. Pick up the phone immediately, call the doctor's office to schedule the earliest available follow-up appointment, and jot down in a notes app the specific marker questions that need to be confirmed.

options.label: D
options.content: Choosing to keep it to myself for now, but already wavering inside — deciding to go for a follow-up check alone first, and only telling the family once there's an official result. I flip the phone face-down on the desk, open the hospital app to look up the nearest available appointment for a follow-up, and murmur quietly: 'I'll say something once I know for sure.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I keep things vague for now — I'm not going to drop the worst of it on her yet. Once I pick up, I tell her: two of my markers came back elevated, the doctor wants me to get a follow-up as soon as possible, and the moment those results are in, you'll be the first to know.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to answer the call, but keep it vague — I don't mention the abnormal results for now, and plan to tell her everything honestly after the follow-up. I press accept and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Hey, I just got the results — a few things need a follow-up check, the doctor said it's pretty common. I've already made the appointment. Don't worry — when you're back Friday we'll talk it through properly.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to answer the call, but gloss over the checkup results, planning to tell the full truth after the follow-up. I press the answer button and say: 'The results came back — there are a few indicators that need to be confirmed again. Let me get through the follow-up next week and then I'll fill you in properly. How are things going on your end in Hangzhou?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to pick up the video call, but I gloss over the details of the report, saying only 'there are a couple of markers they want to recheck, the doctor said to come back in a few days,' pressing the real fear down beneath my voice. I answer, manage a small smile, and say: 'Hey — just walked out. There are two little indicators they want to follow up on, the doctor said to keep an eye on it for now, nothing major. How are things on your end?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay. I don't leave. First I pull her up off the floor, I hear her out, and we face this mess together. I bend down, uncurl her hands from my pant leg, and then hold them, and I say: 'Come on, get up — the floor's cold. We'll figure out the apartment thing, one step at a time. But from now on, you can't carry something like this alone.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold my emotions in check for now — no immediate decision to leave, no immediate forgiveness — and instead ask him to lay everything out. I crouch down and say, in a voice that's quiet but steady: 'Get up first. Then tell me everything — how much is still owed, who these people are, what the situation looks like right now. All of it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold back the emotional explosion for now, and ask them to stand up and lay out every piece of information in full — how much was borrowed, how much is left, whose accounts those are — first, contain the chaos inside a bounded container, then decide on the next step. I open my mouth and say: "Get up first. Sit down. Tell me all the numbers — every transaction, from the beginning."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to accept this explanation, and I'm not continuing as though things are the same between us. I gently moved her aside and said plainly that we're separating our finances for now — tomorrow we sit down and go through every account, every transaction, and where we each stand.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea clearly, but I don't agree to the competition on the spot. I walk through the approach step by step first, and then I tell the teacher that I'd like to go home and organize my thoughts before deciding whether to enter.

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to stand up and share my idea in front of everyone, but gave a vague, noncommittal response to the suggestion about the speech competition. I stood up and walked through my idea — the seesaw-and-elephant weighing method — and the words came easier and easier as I went. When the teacher brought up the competition at the end, I just smiled and said: "I'll think about it."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up and share my idea in front of everyone; the suggestion about the speech competition is set aside for now, neither accepted nor explicitly refused. I rise from my chair, my voice a little rushed at first but growing steadier as I go: 'It's just… you put an elephant on one end, then you stack people onto the other end one by one, and you count how many people it takes — because you can measure each person's weight ahead of time —'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to respond in a vague, retreating way — neither outright refusing the teacher nor actually standing up to perform — trying to shrink myself back into the crowd. I keep my head down and say in a very small voice: "Teacher… what I said just now wasn't very clear, it probably wasn't that good…" and give a small shake of my head, without standing up.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to give a definitive answer for now, and instead ask for more complete information and a concrete execution framework before deciding. I tell my friend: 'I'm taking this very seriously — but I need to see a more detailed financial forecast, a clear team breakdown, and a cash flow plan for the first eighteen months. Can you give me a week to work through all of that before I give you my answer?'

options.label: B
options.content: The impulse called forth by the flow state outweighed the gravity of the financial spreadsheet, and I chose to accept the invitation — but I tried to carve out a little breathing room for myself rather than committing fully on the spot. I told my friend: 'Honestly, I already know what I want — I'm in. But can you give me a week to wrap things up on this end?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to give an answer immediately, and instead ask for a full breakdown of the terms, the equity structure, and the exit mechanism — keeping the decision firmly in my own hands. I tell my friend: 'I need to see the complete terms and the equity agreement. Give me three days, and I'll give you an answer that's grounded in evidence — not one that was rushed out of me.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving a definitive answer and ask for a specific window of time to think — rather than refusing or agreeing on the spot. I say to my friend: "You know I've been following this direction seriously for a long time, and your invitation genuinely means a lot to me — I need a few days to really think it through. Can you give me a week? I don't want to give you an answer I haven't earned."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to confront her directly — to put the screenshot and the side group chat out in the open, no holding back and no dancing around it. I turn toward her, lower my voice, but speak with complete directness: "I saw that group chat. I saw what you said. Can you tell me — what exactly is going on?"

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to push down the urge to confront her, getting through this moment with silence and avoidance, not letting her see that I already know about the screenshot. I lower my head, force the tears back, and say quietly, "I'm fine, probably just didn't sleep well," then look away and pretend to flip through my textbook.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to avoid the confrontation and escape the scene for now — no calling her out, no scene. I push every last bit of the anger and hurt down. I pick up my bag, keep my head low, walk out of the classroom, find a corner where no one can find me, shove my earbuds in, and pretend the entire world doesn't exist.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold back for now, to bide my time — no confrontation in the moment, but inside I've already started building a framework for "how to handle this." I press my hand flat on the desk, eyes fixed straight ahead, and wait for them to finish laughing. I don't say a single word — but in my mind, I've logged every detail of what I saw today.

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to promise secrecy, and instead choose to be honest — telling her I will seek help from an adult, while doing everything I can to stay by her side and not leave. I say quietly: 'I can't make that promise — not because I don't care about you, but because I'm scared something will happen to you. Let's go find a teacher together right now. I'll be with you the whole time.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to make any promise on the spot. Instead, I hold space for his pain first, and then — gently but firmly — tell him we need to find an adult who can help. I crouch down and take his hand, and I say: 'I won't lie to you and tell you I promise, because I'm too scared of losing you — let's go find someone together, and I'll be right there with you the whole time.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to promise secrecy outright, but also not to immediately announce that I'm going to tell an adult. Instead, I steady her first, stay by her side, and then honestly tell her that I can't carry this alone. I say: 'I'm not going to run off and tell anyone right now — but I also can't promise you I'll keep this secret. Because what I saw, those wounds, that's not something I'm able to help you with on my own. I need you to think through this with me — how we find someone who can actually help you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I didn't promise to keep the secret on the spot. Instead I chose to steady her emotions first — neither exposing her nor flatly refusing — while inside I had already decided I had to find an adult who could help her. I held her hand, my voice trembling, and said: "I'm not going to go around telling people — but I need you to help me figure this out together, how we can get you to a place where you don't have to do this anymore. I can't carry this by myself. I need someone to help both of us."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to immediately declare the event cancelled. Instead, I propose a compromise: scale down the scope, redistribute the tasks, and first get a clear picture of what can still be pushed forward. I speak up: 'Let's not rush to say cancel — let's sit down and go through what absolutely has to get done, see if we can cut some of it, split the rest among ourselves, and if we really can't manage, then we go talk to the student union.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to take over all the preparation work the president left behind on my own, pushing the event forward through sheer willpower, refusing to agree to cancellation. I take a deep breath, voice trembling slightly but I speak anyway: 'We're not canceling. I'll take it from here. Help as much as you can — if you can't, I'll figure it out myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to take over the event, while simultaneously reassessing and trimming the workload to build an executable contingency plan. Say to the three members: 'I know what happened with the president. The event is not being canceled — but we're redividing the work. I'll put together a list: everyone is only responsible for their single most essential piece. I'll send it to you tonight, and you tell me whether you can do it.'

options.label: D
options.content: Refuse to cancel outright, but don't take on the entire mess alone either — instead, propose a scaled-down plan: cut the event to its most essential core, redistribute the few people still around, and only do what can actually be done. I looked up and said: 'We're not canceling — but we need to cut half the content. Everyone left takes ownership of one thing, and I'll keep things moving. Anyone who's still in, tell me right now.'

options.label: A
options.content: Setting aside all deliberation over the overseas opportunity, deciding in this moment to go home immediately and be with Dad — everything else can wait. Saying to Mom: "I'm booking the ticket right now — the earliest flight tonight or first thing tomorrow morning. Wait for me. I'm coming."

options.label: B
options.content: She chooses to immediately abandon the overseas assignment and go home to be with her father and handle whatever comes next with his medical care. Voice breaking over the phone, she says: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — first flight tomorrow morning. Wait for me.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to let go of the overseas opportunity and go home immediately to be by Dad's side and handle the medical arrangements. Say to Mom: 'Mom, I'll be back tomorrow — tell me which hospital Dad's in, and I'll take care of everything from there.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to set aside the immediate push on the overseas project and prioritize going home to handle Dad's medical arrangements, while also starting to break down the timelines of both things in search of a middle path. I begin making a list on my phone screen: the earliest train tomorrow morning, contact the attending physician, and at the same time send HR an email asking whether the overseas role allows for a deferred decision — first get the parts I can control into their boxes, then go home.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to confront him directly tonight — but I also refuse to play along. Instead, I let a weighted silence and a careful question hang the whole thing in the air, leaving room for a private conversation later tonight. I take the milk from him, glance down at it for a moment, and say quietly: 'Let's set the speech aside for now — did you actually review today? That problem you said you couldn't do before — can you work through it on your own yet?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold it in for now, steady my emotions, and wait until he's gone before finding the right moment to talk to him alone. I took the milk from him, glanced down at it for a moment, and said quietly: 'Let's talk about the speech later — go wash up first. I have something I want to say to you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold back and not expose everything in this moment, but I decide that tonight I will find a quiet moment alone with him to talk through this face to face — and to stop him from giving the speech at the commendation ceremony and from registering for the competition. I take the milk from him, set down what I'm holding, and say calmly: 'Let's put the speech aside for now. There's something important we need to talk about tonight — come find me in the study when you're done eating.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to confront him directly, right here and now——no dancing around it. I put the note on the table and make it clear that the competition and the awards ceremony have to stop. I set down the book in my hands, look him in the eyes, and say: 'Sit down. There's something I need to talk to you about. I found a note in your backpack——it's about your math midterm.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to make my position clear calmly but firmly — neither accepting the cash nor letting things explode into a scene. I push the wad of money back across the coffee table and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Uncle, I can't take this. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't about the cost of replacing a hard drive. Those files are gone, and that's that. I understand the kid didn't know any better, but I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse the money, say my piece briefly, and end this conversation as quickly as possible — without getting into a direct argument with my uncle, but without pretending everything is fine either. I push the stack of money back across the coffee table, my voice flat, and say only one thing: 'Keep the money. What was in there isn't a money matter.' Then I stand up to go get some water, and I don't look at them again.

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to take the money. In a tone that is tightly controlled but unmistakably clear, I explain what was actually lost — I don't break down in front of my uncle, but I don't pretend this is something we can just move past either. I push the stack of bills back, my voice level, every word bitten off clean: 'This isn't about money, Uncle. That drive had six months of my work on it. No backup. It's gone. I can't take this.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse the money and say outright that the loss cannot be compensated financially — my tone is restrained but my position is clear. I won't get into a heated confrontation with Uncle, but I will not accept this way of 'settling things.' I push the stack of bills back, my voice kept very low but very steady: 'Uncle, I can't take this money. What was on there is six months of my life — it can't be bought back. Please sit with the child for a moment; I need to think about how to handle this.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, while giving my colleague the opportunity to correct the error himself rather than going over his head directly. I tell him: 'I can't sign off on this data — but before I say anything to anyone above us, I'm giving you tonight. You go to the project team yourself and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the only concession I can make.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to report the data falsification to management honestly and to refuse to sign the confirmation form. I tell my colleague: 'I understand the position you're in, but I can't confirm this data. I'll be letting management know the full situation tonight.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to sign off confirming the data is valid, but I also won't formally report it to a superior — instead, I tell my colleague directly that I can't sign off on the data, and demand that he correct it or explain the situation himself before submitting. I say to him: 'I can't put my name on this data as confirmed. You know what I saw — you need to figure out how you're going to handle this before you submit tonight. I'm not stamping this for you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, and at the same time I tell my colleague directly, face-to-face, that he has to report or correct this himself — giving him one last window to make it right on his own terms, or I will report it accurately myself. I say to him: 'I can't sign off on this — just hear me out. Go to the project lead right now and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the last window you have any control over. I'm not trying to destroy you, but tomorrow morning I will not put my name behind these numbers.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to wait and watch — I won't accept my former colleague's invitation, and I'll prepare to tell my supervisor tomorrow that I'm willing to align with the company's current direction and that I want to keep my position. I reply to my former colleague: 'I support your decision, but I've got family pressure on my end — quitting isn't something I'm considering right now. I'll see how things play out here first.' Then I open my calendar and accept the supervisor's invite.

options.label: B
options.content: I decide to spend tonight systematically building a decision framework, then walk into tomorrow's conversation with my manager armed with clear analysis — rather than making any hasty declarations. I open my laptop, create a new document, type "Transition Path Assessment" as the title, and begin listing out the timeline milestones, resource requirements, risk items, and quantifiable evaluation criteria for both paths, while in a separate column I draft the agenda for tomorrow's conversation and note the key information I need to get from my manager.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to make any formal decision tonight, and instead follow my gut to explore which new direction genuinely excites me — treating tomorrow's conversation with my manager as a fact-finding session rather than a moment to commit. I sent my former colleague a voice message back: "That's a bold move you made. I'm still looking for the direction that truly lights me up — once I find it, let's talk. Just give me a little time."

options.label: D
options.content: I'm choosing to take the risk and make the transition — but I won't quit cold turkey right away. Instead, I'm going to lay my cards on the table proactively. Tonight I'll sign up for a course and put together a learning plan; tomorrow I'll tell my manager that I want to move into an AI-related role and ask about the possibility of an internal transition.

options.label: A
options.content: Accept the invitation — but also decide to have a real conversation with my partner that same night, and face whatever has actually gone wrong between us. I say to them: "I'd love to go, I've been wanting to catch this talk too — but there's something I need to tell you first, about where things have been between us lately."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation, but to be honest about why rather than brush it off with an excuse. I glanced down at the table for a moment, then looked up and said: 'I'm really grateful you said all of this — these past few months have meant something to me too — but I can't go. Not because I don't have time. It's because I know what it would mean for both of us, and I can't do that to you, and I can't do that to myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation, but respond in an offhand, understated way — without explicitly acknowledging what it means to me. I look up, let the corner of my mouth pull into something almost like a smile, and say: "Sure, I've been meaning to catch that author too. Send me the address."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation, but respond with sincerity rather than coldness — acknowledging what this partnership has meant, while drawing a clear line. I say to TA: 'These past few months working with you have been something I genuinely value. But Saturday… I can't go. Just the two of us — it wouldn't be right.'

options.label: A
options.content: Refuse to use the service, and stick to the original plan to complete the thesis independently. Push the phone back, and say calmly: 'My progress is fine right now — I have enough time following the plan. I'm not going to consider this.'

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to use the service and insist on completing the thesis independently. I shook my head, pushed the phone back, and said: "No thanks — this one I'll do myself. There's still so much about my own topic I haven't worked out yet; if I handed it off to someone else, I'd never be able to sleep."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to politely decline my roommate's suggestion, not use the service, and continue pushing forward with my thesis according to the original plan. I tell my roommate: "I took a look at it, and I think I'll pass — my progress is still fine, I should be able to finish it myself, and if something goes wrong with that kind of thing it's just not a situation you can easily get out of."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse the service — I'm not going to look into it further or reach out. I waved my roommate off and said: "Forget it, I'll figure it out myself — this kind of thing isn't for me."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold my ground and present the core evidence without directly escalating the conflict, while offering a limited compromise — and I do not voluntarily withdraw my materials. I speak up; my voice comes out quieter than I expected, but it doesn't waver: "Professor, I understand the pressure of the timeline, and I'm not trying to overturn the entire existing plan — what I want to ask is: would it be possible, while the main line of work moves forward, to allow me to run an alternative procedure as a controlled comparison at one specific stage, and document the risk point on record? That way, even if the final conclusion doesn't change, there's data showing we considered this issue."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to use the data as my lever and compromise as my form — holding firm on getting the alternative approach into the conversation, rather than going into full confrontation or backing down entirely. I speak evenly: 'I understand the time costs and resource constraints you're describing, and I'm not trying to overturn the entire direction. But those failure cases all point to the same single step — and my controlled data is right there. Could we allow ourselves, within the existing plan, to run just one controlled comparative test on that one critical step? Everything else I'll push forward exactly as originally planned, no milestones moved.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose neither full retreat nor direct confrontation, but instead try to shift the argument from "who is right and who is wrong" to "how do we reduce the risk"——carving out space for a limited trial of the alternative. I speak up: "I'm not trying to invalidate your system. What I want to ask is: can you allow me, within the existing framework, to run that critical step in parallel using two approaches for a period of time? The data will be right there for you to judge. If the results are the same, I'll follow the original plan and never bring this up again."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to propose a middle path — neither withdrawing the materials nor insisting on an immediate change of course — and instead ask that, while the existing plan moves forward, a small-scale parallel verification be kept in place. I speak up: 'I hear what you're saying, and I understand the pressure of the deadline. I'm not trying to overturn the whole direction — I just want to ask: would it be possible, while we push ahead with the main plan as it stands, to allow me to keep that parallel process as an additional verification? If the data shows no difference in the end, I will fully accept your judgment; but if a discrepancy does appear, we'll have a documented basis to work from. That's also a form of protection for your framework.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to give up my acceptance here and take the offer from the university up north — and tonight I give my partner a clear answer. I sent back a voice message: 'I'm coming to you. It's already decided. Wait for me.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to accept the offer from the northern university, and I give a clear answer tonight — no more stalling, no more vague non-responses. I send a voice message, my voice a little hoarse: 'I've made up my mind. I'm coming to you. We do this together. We'll find a new path over there — but you don't carry this alone anymore.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to stay at my current school, and tell her directly — no evasion, no sugarcoating. My reply to her tonight: 'I listened to every word you said. Staying here is the only honest choice I can make. If I went north carrying regret, that wouldn't be fair to you either. I want to talk about whether we can find another way forward — but I can't give you an answer I don't believe myself.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school — I won't switch institutions as a concession to save the relationship. I send a message explaining my decision, and I'm honest: if they can't continue under these circumstances, I respect that we part ways.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to express conditional interest while making it clear that I need structured information before I can make any judgment — neither an outright refusal nor a commitment. I say: 'I'm taking this seriously, I really am. But I need to see a few things before Wednesday — the current division of responsibilities on the team, the key milestones on the product roadmap, and a concrete definition of what the CTO role actually covers. Give me those, and I can give you a real answer.'

options.label: B
options.content: I made my strong interest and inclination clear on the spot — no attempt to hide it — but I didn't say 'I'm in' outright; instead I caught the ball and kept it in my own hands. I told my senior: "I don't need until Tuesday — I want to dig into the product right now. Walk me through this prototype; I've already got ideas forming."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to express enthusiasm and lean toward accepting, while carving out a small buffer rather than committing on the spot. I lean forward, meet my senior's eyes directly, and say: 'I don't need to wait until Tuesday — this direction feels right to me, and everything you said landed. Give me 48 hours. Not to hesitate, but to get my own side of things sorted out — and then we sit down and talk through the details for real.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to express genuine interest while making it clear that I needed to verify the information before giving any answer, keeping the decision in my own hands. I told the senior: "I listened carefully to everything you said, and I'm going to take this seriously. But before I give you an answer, I need to see the original letter of intent, and whatever user data the product has right now — if there is any. I'll give you a definitive reply before Tuesday."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to call emergency services and the police, leave the handbag exactly as it is on the ground, and stay on the scene to wait for help to arrive. I set the handbag down by the roadside and dialed 120, my voice pressing down against a tremor: 'Someone's been hit by a car — it's on that two-lane road at the edge of the city, near the abandoned tin shed. The injured person is beside the drainage ditch. Please come quickly.' Then I called 110 and told them everything I'd seen, including the black Audi and the driver who fled.

options.label: B
options.content: I drop the bag, immediately dial 120 and 110, then run toward the rider and try to help. I threw the bag down on the roadside, pulled out my phone and called 120, ran to the edge of the drainage ditch and crouched down: 'I'm here, the ambulance is coming, just hold on.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to call for help immediately and save the person, refuse to accept the bribe, and leave the bag where it is to be handled by the police. I set that bag down by the roadside without touching it, pull out my phone and dial 120 and 110, then walk over to the drainage ditch and crouch down, calling out to the rider: 'I'm here — don't move — the ambulance is on its way.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to immediately call 120 and 110, leave the money bag exactly where it is, and walk toward the injured person to assess their condition. I set the bag down by the roadside, dial 120, and say: 'Someone's been hit — they need emergency help, I'm at—' At the same time I crouch down beside the rider and tell him: 'Help is on the way. Hold on.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to return the bottle. I stay where I am and speak first, while also deciding I need to contact the resident advisor or a mental health crisis line. I close my hand around the pill bottle — I don't kick it back — and I turn to face his bed curtain and say: 'I'm not going anywhere tonight, because I'm right here. Over there — could you pull the curtain open a little? I want to see you.' At the same time, I quietly pick up my phone, ready to contact the resident advisor.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak up and respond to my roommate — using the bottle as a reason to stay, remaining right where I am in a clumsy but genuine way. I keep the bottle in my hand instead of kicking it back, turn around, and say: 'I don't feel like going out tonight. This bottle of pills… can you tell me about it?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to return the pill bottle. Instead, I stay and speak up to check on him — without immediately contacting the RA — and try to build a connection first. I crouch down, set the bottle in my palm rather than kicking it back, and say: 'I'm not going out tonight… I'd like to sit with you for a while, if that's okay.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to intervene directly — I don't return the pill bottle, and I immediately contact the resident advisor or call a mental health crisis line. I don't let this get handled internally within the dorm room. I keep the bottle in my hand, stand up, and walk to the curtain around his bed. In a steady voice, I say: 'I'm not giving this back to you. You need someone here right now — I'm going to contact the RA. If you don't want me to, tell me now.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to be upfront about the situation at home first, while proactively offering a compromise — trying not to completely let either side down. I speak up: 'Director, there's something I need to tell you — my dad has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend, and I've already promised my family I'd go with him. I know this is a critical moment, so here's what I'm thinking: I can work remotely from home on Saturday and push forward on the market benchmarking and the main body of the proposal. If I can get back Sunday morning, we could go through the final draft together Sunday afternoon or evening — does that work for you?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to explain the situation at home and hold firm on going back, while offering a compromise to soften the blow of saying no. I tell the director: 'My dad has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend — I promised three days ago that I'd go with my parents, so there's really no way I can change that. But I can put together a written document tonight with the competitive market analysis section and send it to you. Maybe you could find someone to work off that for the redesign? Or I could do a remote review of the final draft Sunday evening?'

options.label: C
options.content: I won't agree to being in the office both days of the weekend, but I'll propose a compromise and stay responsible for the core content. I'll say: I have to go back home to be with my father for his follow-up appointment, but I'll hand everything off tonight with a clear checklist, revise remotely over the weekend, and do a final online review of the draft Sunday evening.

options.label: D
options.content: Choose partial compromise while holding the line: make it clear to the director that the weekend trip home is not being canceled, but offer to put in extra hours tonight to push the project forward, provide remote support on the critical sections over the weekend, and join a video call Sunday evening for the final-draft review. Say directly: 'I have a family commitment this weekend — it was locked in three days ago and I can't cancel it. I can stay late tonight and I'm available remotely over the weekend, but coming into the office isn't something I can do. Tell me which parts are the biggest bottlenecks and let's work out the priorities together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to force myself to stay calm — no running, no fighting back hard — and in the most direct way possible I hold up my phone screen showing the work group chat, so everyone in the carriage and the livestream camera can see the evidence. I say loudly: 'Look carefully — what's on my phone is the work group chat. Screen facing up. Everyone can see it. I didn't photograph anyone.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to strike back loudly and immediately, actively turning this livestreamed scene into my own stage for clearing my name. I hold my phone screen up high, my voice cutting through the noise of the carriage, staring straight into the camera: 'Everyone, look closely — what's on my phone is a work group chat, check the timestamp yourself — I did not photograph anyone. This is a false accusation!'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to proactively present the evidence and use facts to restore order — no fleeing, no breaking down, no fighting fire with fire against the other person's emotions. I raise my phone screen high, turning it toward the cameras on all sides, my voice low but clear: 'Please look at the screen carefully — this is my company's work group chat, the timestamp is four in the morning, I was reading a new project brief, not the camera app, not the photo album. Anyone here is welcome to verify it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to actively present the evidence and break the spectator structure, rather than endure in silence or fight back aggressively. I held my phone screen up as high as I could and said, as loudly but as steadily as I could manage: 'Everyone look — this is my work group chat, messages sent at four in the morning, I've been reading this the whole time, my phone has been held at exactly this height, screen facing out — anyone can come and look.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to briefly flag the core risk rather than put forward the full alternative plan — leaving my judgment on record in a measured way, without pushing to change the direction. I speak up: 'My alternative proposal is still there. My main concern is that once the complexity ramps up later on, maintenance is going to get pretty painful — I just wanted to flag that risk. However you all want to move forward, I'll follow your lead.'

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal on the spot — stating the risk points plainly, not pushing for any particular outcome, just making sure the information got through in full. I said: 'I do have a few thoughts — I put together an alternative proposal, mainly with an eye toward the long-term scaling issues. The document's right here; everyone can take two minutes to look it over, and we can decide after that — no rush.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal in this moment, presenting it in a structured, evidence-based way rather than silently accepting the consensus. I turned my screen toward the room and said: 'There are a few other ideas I'd like to share — I've put together a specific proposal with the risk points laid out. I need three minutes. I think saying it now costs less than waiting until something goes wrong later.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to casually flag a single risk point without presenting the full alternative plan, leaving myself a way out. I speak up: 'No issue with the direction overall — just something to keep in mind: if the complexity ramps up later, the extensibility piece might get a bit strained, so just worth watching. As for dividing up the work, you all decide.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to actively respond to my partner's suggestion, converting the vague "let's go out for a walk together" into a concrete starting point for today — rather than each of us retreating into familiar busyness to avoid this blank space. I stand up and say: "Let's go — let's take that walk. When we get back, we can think about how to structure the time ahead."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond — to tuck my need inside a suggestion, phrased lightly, as if it just slipped out, not as if I'm scared. I lift my head, my voice steadier than I expected: "Maybe… let's go out for a walk together — I don't really know what I'd do with myself today anyway."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to break the silence and reach toward him — not let this morning dissolve into each of us going our separate ways. I speak up: 'Let's go out for a walk. I don't really know what else to do either — but not knowing together seems a little better than not knowing apart.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to sidestep this shared strangeness — to gently acknowledge that it exists, rather than each of us retreating back into the familiar busyness. I look down at the table and say: "This does feel strange. How about… we have a cup of coffee together first? No need to think about what comes next."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to set aside any direct response for now, letting confusion and silence stand in for any real position, while getting distracted by my phone. I looked up at her, stayed quiet for a few seconds, my voice coming out a little hoarse: 'All of this you're saying… I need to think about it.' Then, almost without realizing it, I glanced down at my phone screen as it lit up.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose neither to accept nor to reject — not yet. Instead, I demand that the act of 'redefining' itself be made clear and discussable, and I refuse to make any commitment without sufficient information. I set down my phone, looked at my partner, and said: 'I need you to tell me what you mean by flexibility, specifically — where are the boundaries, what are the rules. We need to spell this out. I can't say yes or no to a vague proposal.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak up immediately, to turn this into a direct, face-to-face confrontation, refusing silence and delay. I set down the dish towel and look straight at her: 'You said you've been thinking about this for a long time — well, I want to know now, when did you start thinking about it, because when did there get to be so much between us that you weren't telling me?'

options.label: D
options.content: Refuse the proposed redefinition of the relationship's boundaries; hold to the original commitment. I press the phone face-down first, then tell him plainly: 'I can't accept this kind of redefinition — we need to talk this through properly.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose a half-open confession — no complete answer, but no more pretending to be calm either. I squeeze her hand and say quietly: "I don't know what comes next, but I don't want to lie to you and say I have a plan — I'm really all over the place right now, but I'm not just going to give up."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell my partner the truth and to swallow my pride first — to go after whatever work I can get up to speed on quickly. I say quietly, 'I'm letting go of the old salary standard. Starting tomorrow, I'm applying to warehouse and customer service jobs too.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to be honest with my partner about the real situation and my inner fear, while also deciding not to lower my salary floor — and instead to consider a more aggressive path toward starting something of my own or a full pivot. I squeeze her hand and say: "I'm going to be straight with you — I'm scared right now, but I'm not going to go work in a call center. I want to talk to you about a much bigger bet."

options.label: D
options.content: Choose partial honesty — acknowledge the reality of the difficulty, but hold off on any complete statement about the future, because there is no executable plan yet to underpin any promise. Squeeze my partner's hand and say quietly: 'I know things are hard right now. I haven't figured out the way forward yet — but I'm working on it. Give me a little time. We'll figure it out together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to claim only the legitimate travel expenses, and I take the two personal receipts off the desk — they won't go into the report. I tuck the Japanese restaurant receipt and the scarf receipt back into my wallet, then neatly attach the high-speed rail ticket, the hotel invoice, and the business meal receipt, fill out the expense form, and submit it.

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to submit only the legitimate business travel expenses and left those two personal receipts in my wallet without turning them in. I tucked the Japanese restaurant receipt and the scarf receipt back into my pocket, and only attached the high-speed rail ticket, the hotel invoice, and the work-meal receipt to the reimbursement form before submitting it.

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to submit only the legitimate business travel expenses, slipping the two personal receipts back into my wallet and leaving them off the reimbursement form. I tucked those two receipts back into my wallet, filled out the form for the actual travel amount, got up, and went to print it.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to submit only the legitimate business travel expenses, leaving those two personal receipts folded in my wallet without turning them in. I fold the Japanese restaurant receipt and the scarf receipt and tuck them back into my wallet, then neatly attach the high-speed rail ticket, the hotel bill, and the work-meal receipt to the reimbursement form, fill in the total — a number more than four hundred yuan short of the ceiling — and push the form to the side, taking a slow, deep breath.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now, giving up any confrontation in the moment — but I haven't given up on recovering the deposit. I press that thought down into the deepest part of myself, to be dealt with another day. I lower my head, and in a voice that's barely audible, I say: 'I'm leaving now, but this isn't over.' Then I bend down and start picking up the clothes scattered across the floor, hands trembling, eyes never once lifting to look at him.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to comply for now in order to get out of immediate physical danger, but inwardly I resolve not to give up on recovering the deposit — the moment I leave, I'll contact someone who can help me (a tenant rights hotline, legal aid, or a friend). I lower my head and say, voice trembling but as steady as I can manage: 'Fine, I'll go for tonight — but we're not done talking about the deposit. I'll be handling this through the proper channels.' Then I bend down and start gathering the clothes from the floor, and I stop meeting his eyes.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to yield for now and leave without confronting them head-on — but inside I'm already calculating the next steps: memorize the landlord's information, photograph the scene, and once I'm out, pursue this through legal channels or whatever other means I can find. I lower my head and bend down to pick up the clothes scattered across the floor, keeping my voice very quiet: 'Fine, I'm leaving — but I'm taking my own things with me.' Then I stuff the clothes into my bag one by one, hoist my luggage onto my back, and walk out of that hallway.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now — but I am absolutely not giving up on recovering the deposit. As I retreat, I'm quietly memorizing details, preserving evidence, and mapping out the path for follow-up complaints. I bend down and start gathering the scattered clothes, and in a steady but clear voice I say: 'I'll leave tonight — but I'm taking the contract with me, and we'll settle the deposit through legal channels.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on and establish authority through data and framework, rather than deflecting or firing back emotionally. I hold his gaze, voice steady: 'Good question. I have the specifics on the unfinished module and the full quarterly schedule right here — let's go through them now. Everyone please turn to item one on the agenda.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to respond directly in a way that appears composed on the surface but is in fact an act of self-protection——neither pushing back hard nor conceding weakness, pulling the conversation back to the meeting itself. I take a deep breath and say: 'Whether I can handle it or not——we'll find out by doing it.
