I've already gone through that unfinished module, and that's exactly why we're here today, to align on the schedule. Everyone take a seat. Let's get started.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on with an open stance, and to actively reframe the senior team member's provocation as a topic for the whole group to work through together—establishing my rhythm as team lead rather than shutting him down. I smile, stand up straight, and speak at a volume that's calm but carries to every corner of the room: "Good question—that module is actually exactly what I want us to dig into today. Old Li, you've got the deepest experience on this one. When we meet in a bit, you go first, and we'll all figure out together how to move it forward."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on, defuse the pressure with a relaxed attitude, and kick the ball back to the whole team — so I don't end up as his target. I smiled and said: "Whether I can handle it or not, we won't know until we try — but actually, that question you just asked was a good one. That unfinished module? Let's put it on the table in today's meeting, take a look together at where it's stuck, and anyone who has ideas can speak up. That includes you."

options.label: A
options.content: Choosing to keep it to myself for now, but already wavering inside — deciding to go for a follow-up check alone first, and only telling the family once there's an official result. I flip the phone face-down on the desk, open the hospital app to look up the nearest available appointment for a follow-up, and murmur quietly: 'I'll say something once I know for sure.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to be honest as soon as possible, but without framing it as a foregone conclusion. I'll schedule a follow-up appointment first, then bring my family together and tell them: 'I've found something abnormal, and I need us to deal with it right away — together.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to hold off on telling family for now; prioritize completing the follow-up test and obtaining the official report before deciding on next steps. Pick up the phone immediately, call the doctor's office to schedule the earliest available follow-up appointment, and jot down in a notes app the specific marker questions that need to be confirmed.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to keep this to myself for now — to build the information framework first, and tell my family once the follow-up results are in. I picked up my phone, opened the calendar, and created a new reminder in tomorrow morning's open slot: 'Contact hospital — schedule follow-up — confirm timeline.' Then I turned the screen face-down on the desk.

options.label: A
options.content: Honestly express how strongly I want this academic opportunity, acknowledge the uncertainty, but make it clear that I want to seriously consider accepting the invitation — and that same evening, reply to my advisor's email with an initial expression of intent. I tell him: "I know this will change a lot of things, and I'm scared too. But when I found that notebook last night, I realized I haven't really been alive for a long time — I want to reply to that email, and we can figure out the rest together."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honestly express my longing for this academic opportunity, while framing the decision as a project that needs to be evaluated together — not as an emotional impulse. I turn to them and say: 'You're right, I need to think about this seriously. I want to start by laying out all the variables — the salary gap, the adjustment period, the actual impact on our lives — and go through them together, and then I'll reply to my advisor. But I need you to know: that email reminded me of something I thought I'd let go of, and I don't want to close the door on it before I've figured out what that means.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to honestly express how deeply I want this academic opportunity, while actively inviting my partner to work through the possibilities together, rather than pushing ahead on my own. I turn toward her, tilt the laptop screen so she can see it, and say: 'I'm not running away — I just haven't felt like this in a really long time. Can you let me tell you what this opportunity means to me? And then we can figure out what to do together.'

options.label: D
options.content: I'm honest about my longing for this academic opportunity, while acknowledging that I don't have a concrete plan yet — but I make it clear that I want to try. I say to her: 'I know you're worried, and I don't have all the answers — but that email made me feel something I haven't felt in a very long time. I want to write back to my advisor and tell him I'm interested. Let's just start with a conversation.'

options.label: A
options.content: I decide to do both — wake up early, go to school first to take care of my responsibilities, then head to the amusement park. I tell Mom: 'I'm in! But I have to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the keys, and I made a promise. I'll go early and be back early. If I leave at seven-thirty, I should make it in time!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to complete my school responsibility first, then negotiate with Mom about whether we can head over to join the party afterward — I will not abandon a commitment I've already made. I say to Mom: 'Mom, tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the key, and it's something I promised to do. How long do you think it'll take once I'm done? If there's still time, could we go to the amusement park to meet my cousin after?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to fulfill the responsibility I was entrusted with first, then make my way to the amusement park as soon as I can — resolving the conflict by doing both. I tell Mom: 'I'll go to school first thing tomorrow morning to water the plants and feed the tadpoles, and once that's done I'll head straight to find my cousin — I should still make it in time. Can you let Uncle know for me?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to go to school first to finish my responsibilities, then head to the birthday outing — I'll do both, and I'm not giving either one up. I tell Mom: 'I'm happy, but tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — it'll take half an hour, tops, and then we can head out. It won't hold us up for long.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to reply to the message, but I proactively narrow the emotional boundaries, steering the conversation back onto a professional and friendly track without leaving room for the other person to go deeper. I reply: 'Made it home. Glad that idea was useful to you. The bookstore is worth a visit if you get the chance — very quiet.' I put the phone down after sending it and go check the schedule for the kid's tutoring.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to reply to my colleague but deliberately narrow the boundary, using a polite yet clear-eyed tone to pull the connection back to a safe distance. I write: 'Made it home — thanks for checking in. That conversation the other day was really lovely; I'll have to recommend the bookstore to you sometime. Good night.' — Then I put down my phone, open my notes app, and add tomorrow's tutoring run with the kids to my schedule.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to reply, but keep the emotional register within a friendly, restrained boundary — not actively deepening things, but not coldly cutting them off either. I write back: "Made it home. The meeting today was actually pretty worthwhile. That bookstore sounds worth checking out sometime — it seems really quiet. Good night."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to reply to that message, in a tone that is light but genuine — not crossing any line, but not pretending to be indifferent either. I write back: "Made it home. I kept thinking about what you said during the meeting today. That bookstore sounds very quiet — we should go sometime. Good night."

options.label: A
options.content: Accept the overall direction on the surface, while fighting for a voice at the implementation level — trading 'I'll oversee the transition' for real room to preserve the process details that matter. I speak up: "Fine, I have no issue with the overall direction. But if I'm the one overseeing the transition, then I need to break this down according to my own understanding of how these workflows operate. I'll hold the pace you want — but when it comes to the specifics of what gets changed, I need you to give me the space to do that."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to outwardly go along with the overall direction, but use execution risks as my entry point to raise specific reservations, and push for a transitional buffer period — without directly challenging his authority. I slowly open the proposal and say: 'I understand the overall direction, and I'll own the execution. But there are a few junctures where switching too fast will cause problems — not a matter of old habits, but data migration and the time window for staff training. I need to flag those here. Which ones can you give a two-week buffer on?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to appear cooperative on the surface while quietly buying time — raising risk details as a way of creating friction without direct confrontation. I speak up: 'I hear you on the direction, and I'll follow through on execution — but there are a few risk points in the transition period you may not have seen yet. Let me write them up; take a look and decide whether we should go over them first, so if something does go wrong later, nobody's left scrambling to explain it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the overall direction while raising specific risk nodes on the spot, pushing for a transition buffer and written confirmation — rather than silently complying or confronting him head-on. I flip open the proposal, press my finger on the first timeline node, and keep my voice steady but unyielding: 'I accept the overall direction, but there are three risk points here that need to be confirmed before execution — if we don't get them written into the transition plan this week, I can't guarantee a problem won't affect the launch date, and I need you to give me a written record of the decision basis.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the missing model, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately switch to a no-model presentation. I say to the teacher: 'The model was left on the bus on the way to school. We'll complete the presentation using a verbal explanation — I know the material thoroughly, and we're ready to begin.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the missing model — I won't go along with the lie, but I also won't actively assign blame to anyone; I simply state the facts and ask to continue with a verbal presentation instead. I say quietly to the homeroom teacher: 'The model was lost on the way to school and didn't make it here — would it be alright if we just walked through the content verbally?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to go along with the lie, but I also don't throw my teammate under the bus on the spot — instead, I steer the conversation directly toward how to handle the presentation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but quickly: 'The model had an accident on the way here and didn't make it, but we can go up and present without it — I have all the content memorized.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell a softened version of the truth: acknowledge that the model wasn't brought, without actively naming who's responsible, while indicating that the group can complete the presentation through a verbal walkthrough. I say to the homeroom teacher: 'Ms. [Teacher], the model wasn't able to make it today — something came up — but we're fully prepared to walk through everything verbally. The content is all there. Please let us go up.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to take either side — I'm going to flee the scene first. I'll wrench my hands free from theirs, cry out 'I don't want to choose,' and then run back to my room and shut the door.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to freeze in place and stay silent as a way of refusing to make this choice, while instinctively trying to redirect my attention toward the concrete mess that can actually be dealt with. I crouch down and start picking up the shards of porcelain from the floor, and in a very small voice I say: "Don't go yet — there are pieces on the floor, you'll cut your feet."

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to make the choice either of them is demanding, and in a calm, direct way I hand the decision back to the adults. I speak up: 'This is between the two of you — it's not my business. I'm not going anywhere and I'm not staying for either of you. You decide.' Then I turn around, walk back to my room, and close the door.

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to choose between them, trying to break through this forced ultimatum with tears and silence. I crouch down, wrap my arms around my knees, lower my head, and let myself cry out loud — without saying a single word, just shaking my head over and over.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and share my idea in front of everyone; the suggestion about the speech competition is set aside for now, neither accepted nor explicitly refused. I rise from my chair, my voice a little rushed at first but growing steadier as I go: 'It's just… you put an elephant on one end, then you stack people onto the other end one by one, and you count how many people it takes — because you can measure each person's weight ahead of time —'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea clearly, but I don't agree to the competition on the spot. I walk through the approach step by step first, and then I tell the teacher that I'd like to go home and organize my thoughts before deciding whether to enter.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to respond in a vague, retreating way — neither outright refusing the teacher nor actually standing up to perform — trying to shrink myself back into the crowd. I keep my head down and say in a very small voice: "Teacher… what I said just now wasn't very clear, it probably wasn't that good…" and give a small shake of my head, without standing up.

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to stand up and share my idea in front of everyone, but gave a vague, noncommittal response to the suggestion about the speech competition. I stood up and walked through my idea — the seesaw-and-elephant weighing method — and the words came easier and easier as I went. When the teacher brought up the competition at the end, I just smiled and said: "I'll think about it."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse to use the answer sheet, and to hand it to the teacher or return it to where it came from, while gently turning down my best friend's request. I fold the paper and clutch it in my hand, shake my head at my friend, and say: 'We can't use this — I'm going to hand it in to the teacher. You shouldn't copy from it either. Let's go back and review; there's still time.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to refuse copying and return the answer sheet to where it was — neither reporting it nor using it. Say to Xin: 'We can't copy from this — put it back. Getting caught would be way worse than just bombing the test. You head out first, I'll be there once I finish sweeping up.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request and decide to hand the answer sheet over to the teacher or put it back where it belongs — I'm not keeping this risk around. I tell my friend: 'I can't copy from this, and you shouldn't either. This belongs to the teacher — I'm turning it in. You should go ahead.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse to use the answer sheet, and I won't let my friend copy it either — I'll return the paper or put it back where it was, and I won't take part in this gamble on luck. I tell my friend: 'I'm not using this, and you shouldn't copy it either — have you actually calculated what it costs if we get caught? I'm putting it back. What you do after that is your call.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell the truth — that the toy was not put in my bag by me — and refuse to take the fall for my friend, even though my voice is shaking. I keep my head down, but I still manage to say it out loud: 'This… I didn't put this here. I don't know how it got into my bag.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the truth and not take the blame for my friend — I state the facts directly to the security guard. I say: 'I didn't put that toy in there. My friend put it in my bag. I didn't know about it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the security guard the truth — that I didn't put the toy in my backpack — and refuse to take the blame. I say to the security guard: 'I didn't put this toy in there. I don't know how it ended up in my bag. I did not take it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take the blame for my friend, admitting that I 'wasn't thinking' when I took the toy. Head down, I tell the security guard: "I took it. I don't even know how it ended up in there. I'm sorry."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to give a definitive answer for now, and instead ask for more complete information and a concrete execution framework before deciding. I tell my friend: 'I'm taking this very seriously — but I need to see a more detailed financial forecast, a clear team breakdown, and a cash flow plan for the first eighteen months. Can you give me a week to work through all of that before I give you my answer?'

options.label: B
options.content: The impulse called forth by the flow state outweighed the gravity of the financial spreadsheet, and I chose to accept the invitation — but I tried to carve out a little breathing room for myself rather than committing fully on the spot. I told my friend: 'Honestly, I already know what I want — I'm in. But can you give me a week to wrap things up on this end?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving a definitive answer and ask for a specific window of time to think — rather than refusing or agreeing on the spot. I say to my friend: "You know I've been following this direction seriously for a long time, and your invitation genuinely means a lot to me — I need a few days to really think it through. Can you give me a week? I don't want to give you an answer I haven't earned."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the career change opportunity, but I ask for a little time to arrange a financial buffer rather than signing immediately. I tell my friend: 'I'm in — but give me two weeks. I need to wrap things up properly on this end. Not because I'm hesitating, but because I want to show up giving everything I've got.'

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to hold his emotions in check for the moment—letting the child set down the milk and leave first—then calls him back alone afterward and, calmly but directly, lays that note out on the table. He doesn't pick up the thread of the boy's cheerful talk; he waits until the glass is set down, then says: 'Sit down. There's something I need to talk to you about.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to confront him directly tonight — but I also refuse to play along. Instead, I let a weighted silence and a careful question hang the whole thing in the air, leaving room for a private conversation later tonight. I take the milk from him, glance down at it for a moment, and say quietly: 'Let's set the speech aside for now — did you actually review today? That problem you said you couldn't do before — can you work through it on your own yet?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold back and not expose everything in this moment, but I decide that tonight I will find a quiet moment alone with him to talk through this face to face — and to stop him from giving the speech at the commendation ceremony and from registering for the competition. I take the milk from him, set down what I'm holding, and say calmly: 'Let's put the speech aside for now. There's something important we need to talk about tonight — come find me in the study when you're done eating.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold it in for now, steady my emotions, and wait until he's gone before finding the right moment to talk to him alone. I took the milk from him, glanced down at it for a moment, and said quietly: 'Let's talk about the speech later — go wash up first. I have something I want to say to you.'

options.label: A
options.content: I've decided to file a formal complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name, as the lead complainant on record. I'll accept the neighbors' quiet assistance but won't pressure anyone to co-sign. And I'll hold no resentment toward them for stepping back — I understand it. I told the retired teacher: 'I hear everything you're saying. You don't have to sign anything. But I have to go — I'm the one who dug up the evidence, so I'll carry it there myself. If you have any materials, pass them to me. That's enough.'

options.label: B
options.content: I've decided to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name — but I'm making it clear to the neighbors: quiet support is fine, but I'm not out here taking all the risk for everyone for nothing. I'll keep everyone posted as things develop. To the retired teacher, I said: "I understand why you won't sign your names — but I've already done the digging, and I'm going to file this complaint. However you want to help, that's up to you. Just don't expect me to carry the whole thing by myself and call it done."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name alone — no waiting for a joint letter, no accepting the terms of 'private support' as a trade. I tell the neighbor directly: 'I'm not going to push you on the joint letter, but I will be submitting the complaint materials under my own name. The evidence is already organized. If you want to contribute anything, I'll take it; if not, that's fine too — I'll handle this myself.'

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to file a formal complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name, and to give the upstairs neighbor a direct but measured response — no accusations, but a clear statement of where I stand and what I will and won't accept. To the upstairs neighbor, I say: 'I understand everyone's concerns. I'll file the complaint myself. But I need the materials and information you all promised me — that's my only ask. Two million isn't a matter of a few thousand dollars per household. It's a matter of whether anyone is going to hold this process accountable.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to commit on the spot, and instead return the decision to the child rather than letting myself be swept along by the teacher's excitement or my own fear. I say to the teacher: 'Thank you — this means a great deal to us. I'd like to speak with him alone first, hear what he thinks, and then we'll make a decision.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to support my child in pursuing the professional path, while returning the decision to him — driving the conversation with information rather than emotion. I say to the teacher: 'Thank you for telling me. I'll go through everything in this brochure with him — the full picture, the pros and the cons — and let him decide for himself. But I'm not going to hide this option from him.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving a definitive answer and ask to gather information systematically before deciding. I say to the teacher: 'Thank you so much for your trust in us — this is genuinely important news. I'd like to have a real conversation with my child first, and at the same time get a clearer picture of the specialist program's curriculum and timeline. Would it be possible to get a detailed information packet? We'll get back to you as quickly as we can.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to politely decline the specialist track and hold firm on keeping my child in the regular education system, without applying for the talent recruitment program. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you so much — knowing that you see him that way, we as parents are truly grateful. But this is too big a decision for me to make on the spot. I need to go home and talk it through properly with the family. His schoolwork is stable right now, and we don't want to rush into anything.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to gently but directly let him know I've already seen the report, and invite him to tell me honestly what's going on — rather than smoothly handing the envelope back as if I hadn't. I set the envelope quietly on the table, look up at him, and say: 'I saw some of it. Would you be willing to tell me about it? I want to face this with you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to bring it out into the open directly — rather than going along with her and putting the envelope back, I speak up, gently but firmly. I set the envelope down softly on the table, look at her, and say: 'I saw it. Tell me what's going on — whatever it is, we'll face it together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to make it clear that I've already seen the report, and I ask my partner to be straight with me — I'm not going to follow his lead and let the subject drift away. I set the envelope down gently on the table, look at him, and say: 'I saw it. Can you tell me what this means?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to state directly that I've seen the contents of the report, and I ask my partner to tell me honestly what's going on — I won't accept evasion. I set the envelope on the table and speak, calm but clear: 'I saw it — it's a recent test report. Are you willing to tell me what it says?'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to take full ownership of the situation — no cancellation, no hesitation — and pulled all three club members out of their slump on the spot to redistribute the work. I set my phone down on the table, swept my eyes across all three of them, and said: 'Cancel? Not happening. The president bailed, but the event is still on. Come on — pass me that task breakdown sheet. We're redividing everything right now. Everyone picks up two items, and we start tonight.'

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to cancel outright, and instead propose a scaled-down plan: cut the high-difficulty elements, redistribute responsibilities, and reshape the event into a smaller but genuinely executable version. I speak up: "We're not canceling — but we do need to redesign this. The president's gone, so we turn it into something smaller. Each of you takes on one thing you can actually handle, I'll coordinate the whole thing, and we get through it together."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take over the organizing work, refuse to agree to canceling the event, but proactively scale back the goals and focus on pushing forward the most critical tasks. I say to the members: "Canceling is something I can't agree to — but I'm not going to force any of you either. I'll personally follow up on the most urgent items myself: confirming the sponsors, getting the telescope loan request submitted, and sorting out the volunteer schedule. Whoever has time can pitch in; if you don't, that's fine. As for what shape the event ends up in — we'll see when we get there."

options.label: D
options.content: I decide not to cancel the event, but I propose a compromise: split the remaining tasks, explicitly assign the parts that require external communication (confirming sponsors, contacting guest speakers) to whichever members are still willing to stay, and take on the parts I can handle independently myself (the venue layout plan, organizing the volunteer schedule). I speak up: 'We're not cancelling. Whoever can make phone calls, go make them — I'll handle the scheduling and the venue plan. We work separately. Ten days is enough.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to give a vague, minimizing response — neither directly identifying the person who brought the contraband, nor explicitly denying that I saw anything — in order to end this conversation as quickly as possible and get out of here. I say to the director: "I was reading at the time. I didn't notice who brought it — I really couldn't make it out."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the truth about who brought the contraband in, prioritizing my own protection and making clear I had no part in it. I'll quietly tell the director exactly who I saw and what happened — and I'll also let him know that person just threatened me.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the truth about who was wearing the badge, but I won't frame myself as anyone's follower. I say directly who I saw wearing it, and I also state plainly, right there in the room, that he just threatened me — don't count me in as an accomplice.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to state the facts I personally witnessed — only what I saw, no interpretation, not one word more or less on anyone's behalf. I open my mouth and say: 'What I saw is that the deck of cards was taken out of his bag. Beyond that, I don't know.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to campaign hard for votes and stand by my original plan — no merger. I tell my friend: 'No merging — my plan is my plan. Come on, this weekend we go talk to everyone one by one. I'm bringing the sketch. Let them see it for themselves.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to merge and not to campaign blindly—instead, I'm going to do a precise read of where the votes actually are. I'll identify the small number of people in the class who genuinely want the show to be good, not the ones who'd be happy just singing a few songs, and focus all my energy on convincing them. I tell my friend: 'Merging is off the table—if we do that, my concept is gone. Help me think: who in the class actually wants to put on a real show? Not the 'whatever, just sing' crowd—starting with those people, I'll go pitch my concept.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to proactively approach the other person about collaboration, merging the two proposals together, and abandon the route of campaigning solo for votes. Say to my friend: 'Let's go talk to her — her plan solves the rehearsal problem, my plan has the content, and the two things can be pieced together. The credit doesn't matter; what matters is that the plan actually gets made.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to step away from the competition — I won't go talk to her about merging, and I won't spend the weekend canvassing votes. I'll let the proposal lose on its own terms. I tell my friend: "Forget it — if everyone likes her proposal, they should go with it. I'll keep mine for myself."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to confront her directly — to put the screenshot and the side group chat out in the open, no holding back and no dancing around it. I turn toward her, lower my voice, but speak with complete directness: "I saw that group chat. I saw what you said. Can you tell me — what exactly is going on?"

options.label: B
options.content: I choose silence and retreat — no confrontation, no questions, every word swallowed back down, replacing the urge to explode with coldness and distance. I lower my head, shift my gaze away from her, and say quietly, "It's nothing, my eyes are just a little dry," then flip my textbook open and hold it in front of my face, not looking at her again.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation, not here, not yet. I'll wait until I've calmly worked out what outcome I actually want before deciding on a next step. I turn off my phone screen, look up, give her one expressionless glance, then drop my eyes and open my textbook.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to confront her directly, face to face — put the evidence I have out in the open, give her no room to keep brushing me off. I speak calmly: "I saw that screenshot. I saw the group chat too. You don't need to explain that it 'wasn't intentional' — I just want you to know that I know."

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to suppress the breakdown and force himself back into the rhythm of the everyday, smothering that crack under the weight of family obligation and routine. He flips the phone face-down on the passenger seat, puts the car in gear, and drives home — repeating quietly to himself: there's dinner tonight, there are things to do tomorrow. That's enough.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stay at the intersection — refusing to drive straight home and pretend everything is normal, but also refusing to completely fall apart — and instead try to treat these twenty years as a text that needs to be reread rather than a mistake that needs to be negated. I didn't start the engine. I turned my phone face-down on the passenger seat and said quietly to myself: 'This isn't a conclusion. This is just a chapter I haven't finished reading yet.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay where I am for now — neither falling apart nor forcing myself to calm down — and allow this tearing feeling to exist for a little while. I flip my phone face-down, take a slow deep breath, wait for the traffic light to change one more time, and then ease the car back toward home. Tonight, I go back. Everything else can wait.

options.label: D
options.content: He chooses to break the silence and reach out rather than suppress it alone or drive home and pretend everything is normal. He dials an old friend's number, his voice a little rough: 'I'm stuck at a red light right now — there's something I've been sitting on for a long time. Do you have a few minutes to talk?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to answer the call, but keep things vague — I don't mention the abnormal report, and I'll wait until the follow-up results come back before saying anything. I press accept and keep my voice as steady as I can: "Hey, just finished up, nothing major with the check-up — how are things on your end?"

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I gloss over everything — I downplay it with "it's fine, a couple of indicators came back slightly elevated, the doctor said to just keep an eye on it" — and I don't breathe a word about the line that says "recommend follow-up as soon as possible to rule out the possibility of early pathological changes." I pick up, manage to arrange my face into something that looks reasonably calm, and say: "Just got out — overall it's fine, a couple of minor indicators were a little high, the doctor said it's probably just been too hectic lately, told me to watch my diet a bit. Nothing serious."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I keep things vague for now — I'm not going to drop the worst of it on her yet. Once I pick up, I tell her: two of my markers came back elevated, the doctor wants me to get a follow-up as soon as possible, and the moment those results are in, you'll be the first to know.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to answer the video call and share the information honestly but precisely, separating the facts from any conclusions. I pick up and say, calmly: 'The report came back. Two markers are elevated — the doctor recommends a follow-up, and I'm already arranging it. There's nothing more to say until those results are in. You don't need to come back.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to outwardly go along with the overall direction, but use execution risks as my entry point to raise specific reservations, and push for a transitional buffer period — without directly challenging his authority. I slowly open the proposal and say: 'I understand the overall direction, and I'll own the execution. But there are a few junctures where switching too fast will cause problems — not a matter of old habits, but data migration and the time window for staff training. I need to flag those here. Which ones can you give a two-week buffer on?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to step up and engage directly, raising specific objections on the spot and working to embed my own judgment and buffer space within the execution framework — rather than offering surface compliance. I flip the proposal open, press my index finger against one of the timeline milestones, and say: 'I have an issue to raise here — not a question of whether it can be changed, but if this milestone goes the way you're describing, I can tell you right now exactly what gaps will open up in the transition period. Let's hear how you want to handle that.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to raise the specific risk points on the spot, grounding everything in operational facts — not challenging the overall direction, but pushing for adjustments on the nodes that have real problems. I open the proposal and point to two of the timeline entries: 'I have no objection to the big-picture direction — but here and here, the transition period is going to hit concrete problems at this pace. I can walk you through exactly what they are. Do you want to hear it?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to challenge the logical gaps in the proposal directly, on the spot — not attacking his authority as such, but forcing him to back it up with data. I flip the proposal open, point to the timeline, and say: 'I do have an objection — let's start with one: how were the transition-period timelines determined? If there's no actual measurement based on the existing process, I can't guarantee the rollout won't run into problems, and you won't get the results you're after either.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to keep it to myself for now, using 'waiting for the results to be confirmed' as my reason to move forward with the follow-up alone, leaving the decision of whether to tell my family suspended. I reply to the doctor's text to confirm the follow-up appointment, then flip my phone face-down on the desk, reopen the code documentation, and tell myself: wait until the results come in.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep this to myself for now, prioritize getting definitive information, and defer any emotional trigger until there are facts to back it up. I wake the phone screen, go straight to the hospital's appointment system, lock in the earliest available follow-up slot, then flip the phone face-down on the desk and open my work files.

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to hold off on telling family for now; prioritize completing the follow-up test and obtaining the official report before deciding on next steps. Pick up the phone immediately, call the doctor's office to schedule the earliest available follow-up appointment, and jot down in a notes app the specific marker questions that need to be confirmed.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell my family as soon as possible, but I'll hold the rhythm steady — I won't frame it as a catastrophe. Tonight I'll bring my partner in, be honest that the doctor has asked for a follow-up and that I'm worried there may be something to it, and we'll go together tomorrow to find out for sure.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation, but respond with sincerity rather than coldness — acknowledging what this partnership has meant, while drawing a clear line. I say to TA: 'These past few months working with you have been something I genuinely value. But Saturday… I can't go. Just the two of us — it wouldn't be right.'

options.label: B
options.content: Accept the invitation — but also decide to have a real conversation with my partner that same night, and face whatever has actually gone wrong between us. I say to them: "I'd love to go, I've been wanting to catch this talk too — but there's something I need to tell you first, about where things have been between us lately."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to gently decline the invitation, but without cutting off contact — giving myself time to face both this feeling and my existing relationship. I tell him: "I actually have something on this Saturday, so I probably can't make it — but that feeling of being understood that you mentioned, I treasure that too. It's just that right now I need to think some things through first."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation, but to be honest about why rather than brush it off with an excuse. I glanced down at the table for a moment, then looked up and said: 'I'm really grateful you said all of this — these past few months have meant something to me too — but I can't go. Not because I don't have time. It's because I know what it would mean for both of us, and I can't do that to you, and I can't do that to myself.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up and step in — using the pill bottle as the opening to walk directly toward my roommate, without flinching from this crisis signal. I keep the bottle in my hand instead of giving it back, stand up, and walk to the edge of his bed. I say quietly: 'I didn't go out tonight — good timing, I'll keep you company for a bit. This bottle — can you tell me what's going on?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene — I don't return the bottle, I walk toward the bed curtain and speak, while preparing to reach out for outside help. I grip the pill bottle in my hand, walk to his bedside, and say quietly: 'I'm not leaving — I just don't want to. I'm holding onto this bottle for now — how are you doing right now?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to intervene directly, with confirming the medication situation as the immediate priority, and contacting emergency services if necessary. I carry the bottle to the curtain around his bed and speak quietly, without any edge in my voice: 'I didn't go out. This bottle — how many did you take?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay. I don't return the pill bottle. I use his question as an opening and speak softly, trying to let him know that someone is here. I keep the bottle in my hand without moving, and say quietly: 'I didn't feel like going out tonight. Are you… doing okay over there?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell the truth — that the toy was not put in my bag by me — and refuse to take the fall for my friend, even though my voice is shaking. I keep my head down, but I still manage to say it out loud: 'This… I didn't put this here. I don't know how it got into my bag.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the security guard honestly that I didn't put the toy in my bag, while not directly pointing the finger at my friend — instead I say I have no idea how it got there and ask the guard to check the security footage. I look up at the guard and say: 'Sir, I really didn't put this toy in my bag. I don't know how it got in there. You have security cameras — could you check them?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the truth, refuse to take the fall for my friend, and state the facts directly to the security guard. I speak calmly: 'I didn't put that toy in there — he did. I don't know when he put it in, and I didn't take anything.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take the blame for my friend — using silence and a vague confession to protect him — and partly because somewhere deep down I've already decided that 'speaking up won't make anyone believe me anyway.' I lower my head, my voice barely above a whisper, and say to the security guard: 'It was me… I took it myself. I don't even know how it ended up in there. I'm sorry.'

options.label: A
options.content: She chooses to immediately abandon the overseas assignment and go home to be with her father and handle whatever comes next with his medical care. Voice breaking over the phone, she says: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — first flight tomorrow morning. Wait for me.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to let go of the overseas opportunity and go home immediately to be by Dad's side and handle the medical arrangements. Say to Mom: 'Mom, I'll be back tomorrow — tell me which hospital Dad's in, and I'll take care of everything from there.'

options.label: C
options.content: Setting aside all deliberation over the overseas opportunity, deciding in this moment to go home immediately and be with Dad — everything else can wait. Saying to Mom: "I'm booking the ticket right now — the earliest flight tonight or first thing tomorrow morning. Wait for me. I'm coming."

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to immediately set aside all preparations for the overseas project and return home as soon as possible to be with Dad through his medical care, putting the career decision on hold for now. Call Mom and say: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — I'll be there on the earliest flight tomorrow. Don't worry, I'll handle everything.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to reply to this colleague's message — not keeping a deliberate distance, but taking one step forward along this genuine connection. I write back: "Made it home. I kept thinking about what you said during the meeting today — that bookshop really does sound quiet. We should go sometime. Good night."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to reply to my colleague's message, but keep my wording restrained and maintain distance — not crossing any emotional boundary, responding to the feeling of being seen in a way that stays safe. I write back: "Made it home. That point you mentioned — I've always found it interesting too. That bookstore really is quiet; worth a visit sometime. Good night."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to reply, but keep the emotional register within a friendly, restrained boundary — not actively deepening things, but not coldly cutting them off either. I write back: "Made it home. The meeting today was actually pretty worthwhile. That bookstore sounds worth checking out sometime — it seems really quiet. Good night."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to reply to my colleague's message for now — cutting off the bud with self-attack, pressing the longing back down. I darken the phone screen, pick up my partner's message, reply with a single "okay," then close both chat windows and stare at the blurred night outside the car window, telling myself: you could never hold something like this together.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stall with a vague, positive-sounding response rather than actually commit — neither refusing nor promising, keeping a way out for myself. I smiled, pressed my hands against my knees to hide the trembling, and said: "I'm honestly so surprised you thought of me — this opportunity… I need to think about it seriously. Everything you said, I've taken it all in. I'll give you an answer by Tuesday for sure."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to express enthusiasm and lean toward accepting, while carving out a small buffer rather than committing on the spot. I lean forward, meet my senior's eyes directly, and say: 'I don't need to wait until Tuesday — this direction feels right to me, and everything you said landed. Give me 48 hours. Not to hesitate, but to get my own side of things sorted out — and then we sit down and talk through the details for real.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to politely but clearly communicate that I cannot accept the terms of full-time commitment, carving out space to step back rather than rejecting the opportunity outright. I tell my senior: "I genuinely find your project interesting, but the condition of going full-time and taking a leave of absence isn't something I can agree to right now — not because I don't believe in it, but because I know myself well enough to know I can't perform in a high-intensity collaborative environment like that, and that wouldn't be fair to you either. If there's ever a possibility of a technical advisor role or remote collaboration down the line, I'd be happy to talk."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to express conditional interest while making it clear that I need structured information before I can make any judgment — neither an outright refusal nor a commitment. I say: 'I'm taking this seriously, I really am. But I need to see a few things before Wednesday — the current division of responsibilities on the team, the key milestones on the product roadmap, and a concrete definition of what the CTO role actually covers. Give me those, and I can give you a real answer.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honestly express my assessment of the academic opportunity and to formally reply to my advisor accepting the invitation. I'll say: 'I've thought it through. This opportunity is the right call logically, and I'm going to take it. As for the life logistics — I'll handle them. But they're not a reason to walk away from this.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honestly express how deeply I want this academic opportunity, while actively inviting my partner to work through the possibilities together, rather than pushing ahead on my own. I turn toward her, tilt the laptop screen so she can see it, and say: 'I'm not running away — I just haven't felt like this in a really long time. Can you let me tell you what this opportunity means to me? And then we can figure out what to do together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to honestly express the longing inside me while acknowledging the uncertainty, and to ask that we have a serious conversation together — without rushing to reply to my advisor. I say to him: 'I really have been reading this email the whole time. Those research directions are something I hadn't thought about in a long time, but never truly let go of. I don't know whether going or not going is the right call — but I want to tell you how I feel first, and then we can think this through together before we decide how to respond to my advisor.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to honestly express my longing for this academic opportunity — not shying away from the uncertainty — while acknowledging that her concerns are real. Say to her: 'I know this will change a lot of things, and I'm scared too, but I can't keep pushing this down anymore — I want to reply to my advisor and have a real conversation about this opportunity.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up immediately, to turn this into a direct, face-to-face confrontation, refusing silence and delay. I set down the dish towel and look straight at her: 'You said you've been thinking about this for a long time — well, I want to know now, when did you start thinking about it, because when did there get to be so much between us that you weren't telling me?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to set aside any direct response for now, letting confusion and silence stand in for any real position, while getting distracted by my phone. I looked up at her, stayed quiet for a few seconds, my voice coming out a little hoarse: 'All of this you're saying… I need to think about it.' Then, almost without realizing it, I glanced down at my phone screen as it lit up.

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse the proposed redefinition of the relationship's boundaries; hold to the original commitment. I press the phone face-down first, then tell him plainly: 'I can't accept this kind of redefinition — we need to talk this through properly.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to set my phone aside for now, to shut out any outside distraction, and to ask him first — to understand what he's really feeling and what exactly he means — rather than immediately saying yes or no. I put down the dish cloth, sit across from him, and say quietly: 'When you say independent space… can you tell me — when did you first start feeling this way?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honor my father's wishes and decide on palliative care, stating that choice plainly without flinching. I say to the doctor: 'We're choosing palliative care — please initiate the comfort care protocol. He has made his choice, and we respect it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion — palliative care, letting go of that 20% chance. I look at the doctor, my voice a little hoarse, and say: 'We'll go with comfort care. Let him go with some dignity.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose palliative care, honor the exhaustion and wishes my father has expressed, and refuse to let a low-probability course of chemotherapy turn his remaining time into suffering. I speak to the doctor: 'Start the comfort-care protocol—let him spend whatever time he has left in peace.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose palliative care, honoring the genuine wish my father expressed through his exhaustion, rather than forcing him to endure suffering for the sake of a 20% chance. I turn to the doctor, my voice slightly unsteady but clear: 'We're choosing palliative care — so he can spend the time he has with us, without going through all of that pain.'

options.label: A
options.content: I decline the invitation and stay behind to keep observing the insect and the little purple flower, trying to document every detail with my phone or the notebook I have on me. I tell the kid: "Go on ahead without me — there's something here I haven't finished looking at. I've never seen this bug before in my life, and I need to figure out what it is first."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to accept the invitation and go play soccer, but at the same time I turn this strange bug into a 'surprise' I bring to the field — merging both things into one. I say to the kid: 'Let's go, let's go — but look at this first. Have you ever seen a bug like this? Its back is green and it actually glows! We'll come back and study it after the game — come on, let's go!'

options.label: C
options.content: He politely declines the invitation, tells the other kid to head back first, and stays to look a little longer before deciding anything — neither diving into research nor leaving right away. "You guys go ahead, I'll come check it out in a bit."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to politely decline the invitation and stay to keep observing, brushing him off in a short, direct way. I keep my eyes on the bug and say offhandedly: 'You guys go ahead — I'm watching this. I'm not coming.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to make any promise on the spot. Instead, I hold space for his pain first, and then — gently but firmly — tell him we need to find an adult who can help. I crouch down and take his hand, and I say: 'I won't lie to you and tell you I promise, because I'm too scared of losing you — let's go find someone together, and I'll be right there with you the whole time.'

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to promise secrecy, but don't leave immediately — choose to stay and explain my reasons to her first, then let her know I will be seeking help from an adult. I meet her eyes directly and say, in a steady voice: 'I can't promise you that — not because I don't care about you, but because this is something I can't carry alone, and you shouldn't have to carry it alone either. I'll go with you to find someone, but I'm not going to pretend I didn't see anything today.'

options.label: C
options.content: I didn't promise to keep it secret. Instead, I put the focus on just being there with her — trying to make sure she didn't feel abandoned, while leaving room to seek help later. I crouched down and said quietly: 'I'm not going to go around telling everyone — but I also can't promise to do nothing. Can you tell me what's actually been going on lately?'

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to give a clean, clear-cut promise of secrecy, but I insist on staying by her side — no running — trying to steady her first before we figure out what to do together. I take her hand, my voice coming out a little rough: 'I'm not going anywhere. I'm right here. But I can't promise you I'll do nothing — not because I don't care about you, but because I care too much. I can't carry this alone, and you shouldn't have to either. Let's figure it out together, okay?'

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to accept this unilaterally decided arrangement in the group chat. I choose to make my position explicitly clear in the family chat in writing, demanding that everyone renegotiate the specific division of responsibilities and cost-sharing — not a money-for-labor swap. I type into the group: "I wasn't part of this discussion. Caregiving isn't something one person should have to carry just because they live closest. I need everyone to sit down together and work out exactly how to divide this — both the time and the money need to be recalculated." Then I put down my phone and turn toward my partner.

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to accept the asymmetrical 'money for labor' arrangement, and raise in the group chat the need to renegotiate a caregiving division of responsibilities that names specific people. Reply in the group chat: 'Let's set the money aside for now — I can't keep going the way things are. I need everyone to be clear about who can come, when they can come, and what they'll be responsible for. Let's get on a video call this week.' Then put the phone down, walk over to my partner, and say: 'I know. Let's talk.'

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to silently accept the arrangement being decided in that group chat — but I choose to put the phone down first and turn to face my partner, to have this conversation between the two of us before anything else, rather than exploding in the chat right now. I press the phone face-down onto the couch, stand up, and walk toward her. 'Let's talk first,' I say. 'Just the two of us.'

options.label: D
options.content: I'm not accepting another round of carrying the bulk of the caregiving alone — but I'm not blowing things up in the open either. I reply in the group chat: both money and caregiving need to be shared on a rotating schedule; I'll cover the medication this time, and tomorrow I'll send out the list and the shift rotation. Then I turn and quietly tell my partner that we need to talk tonight — really talk.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — no taking the card, no explanation, no anger — cutting off this performance with silence and by walking away. I glanced down at the card for a moment, didn't reach for it, then raised my eyes and said calmly: 'No, thank you,' set my orange juice glass on the nearest table, and turned and walked toward the exit.

options.label: B
options.content: I decline the card, and in front of everyone I flip this so-called 'invitation' into a public counter-strike — letting every person in the room see exactly what is behind that business card. I don't reach out to take it. Instead, I smile slightly, my voice pitched just loud enough for two or three tables to catch every word: 'Generous enough? Twelve years ago you put your name on my proposal — now that was generous. Keep the assistant position. It's not for me — I've never been good at signing other people's names for them.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly — tone steady and clear, giving him no emotional leverage, letting no one around me see a single flicker of panic. I push the business card back toward him, voice pitched just right, loud enough for the people nearby to hear: 'Thanks, I'm good. The work I do now — I use my brain.' Then I turn away and go back to my orange juice.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to decline the card, but in a way that neutralizes his performance rather than meeting it with direct confrontation or an outburst of anger. A slight smile, I gently push the card back toward him, and in a voice pitched just right — not too loud, not too soft — I say: 'Thanks for remembering me — though the assistant track isn't really a direction I'm interested in. Your team's projects over the years, on the other hand, I've always thought there was something worth looking into there. Catch up sometime?' Then I turn and start a new conversation with the person beside me.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to go along with what he's saying for now — no exposing him on the spot — but I use one sentence to keep him here, laying the groundwork for a private conversation later tonight. I take the milk from him, lower my head and drink a sip, then look up at him and say: 'The speech can wait — sit down for a second. Dad has something he wants to talk to you about, just the two of us.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold back for now, not to expose him in this moment — but inwardly I've already decided that tonight I will sit down with him privately and talk this through. I take the milk from him and say quietly: "We can talk about the speech later. Go wash up first — tonight, let's have a real conversation."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to confront him directly, right here and now——no dancing around it. I put the note on the table and make it clear that the competition and the awards ceremony have to stop. I set down the book in my hands, look him in the eyes, and say: 'Sit down. There's something I need to talk to you about. I found a note in your backpack——it's about your math midterm.'

options.label: D
options.content: He chooses to hold his emotions in check for the moment—letting the child set down the milk and leave first—then calls him back alone afterward and, calmly but directly, lays that note out on the table. He doesn't pick up the thread of the boy's cheerful talk; he waits until the glass is set down, then says: 'Sit down. There's something I need to talk to you about.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to dodge the camping invitation with a vague excuse, buying time and space — neither outright refusing nor agreeing. Reply: 'It's been so long! Something just came up on our end this weekend, so we probably won't make it — can we plan something another time? Hope you guys have a blast! ～'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a plausible excuse, removing myself from the situation entirely — neither breaking the promise I made to him nor showing up to play a supporting role in this deception. Reply: 'There's a project at work that's hit a critical point right now, so I'm afraid I can't get away this weekend — you all go ahead and have fun, and we'll find another time to get together.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to decline the camping invitation with a personal excuse, without revealing anything about the secret, and use a brief, vague reason to quietly remove myself from the situation. Reply: 'I haven't been feeling great lately — I probably won't be able to make it this weekend. Hope you all have a great time, let's plan something again soon!'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the camping invitation and respond with surface-level enthusiasm, but leave myself a vague out — while quietly working to make sure the four of us never actually go. I write back: "That sounds amazing! The guesthouse must have such a beautiful view — let me just check my schedule on this end, you guys lock in the details first, and I'll get back to you as soon as I can!"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take over the organizing work, refuse to agree to canceling the event, but proactively scale back the goals and focus on pushing forward the most critical tasks. I say to the members: "Canceling is something I can't agree to — but I'm not going to force any of you either. I'll personally follow up on the most urgent items myself: confirming the sponsors, getting the telescope loan request submitted, and sorting out the volunteer schedule. Whoever has time can pitch in; if you don't, that's fine. As for what shape the event ends up in — we'll see when we get there."

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to take full ownership of the situation — no cancellation, no hesitation — and pulled all three club members out of their slump on the spot to redistribute the work. I set my phone down on the table, swept my eyes across all three of them, and said: 'Cancel? Not happening. The president bailed, but the event is still on. Come on — pass me that task breakdown sheet. We're redividing everything right now. Everyone picks up two items, and we start tonight.'

options.label: C
options.content: I decide not to cancel the event, but I propose a compromise: split the remaining tasks, explicitly assign the parts that require external communication (confirming sponsors, contacting guest speakers) to whichever members are still willing to stay, and take on the parts I can handle independently myself (the venue layout plan, organizing the volunteer schedule). I speak up: 'We're not cancelling. Whoever can make phone calls, go make them — I'll handle the scheduling and the venue plan. We work separately. Ten days is enough.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to cancel the event outright, and instead propose a compromise: scale things down, redistribute responsibilities, ask the remaining members to each take on whatever they can manage, and reach out to the student affairs committee to explain the situation and ask for support. I speak up: 'I think we shouldn't rush to say cancel just yet — can you hear me out for a second? We scale the event down, everyone only handles one or two of the most critical things, and I'll go coordinate the rest with the student affairs committee. That way nobody's finals get wrecked, and the event doesn't just disappear into nothing.'

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to file a real-name report in his own name alone, refusing to let the neighbors' retreat or his wife's pleas change his position — but he won't push anyone to join him either. He says calmly to the old man upstairs: 'I understand your difficulties. I won't press anyone on the joint letter — but I'll go to the Housing and Construction Bureau myself. I've already got the materials ready.'

options.label: B
options.content: I've decided to file a formal complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name, as the lead complainant on record. I'll accept the neighbors' quiet assistance but won't pressure anyone to co-sign. And I'll hold no resentment toward them for stepping back — I understand it. I told the retired teacher: 'I hear everything you're saying. You don't have to sign anything. But I have to go — I'm the one who dug up the evidence, so I'll carry it there myself. If you have any materials, pass them to me. That's enough.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to file the complaint in my own name, as an individual — but I make my disappointment and my boundaries clear to the neighbors who were 'full of advice but unwilling to sign.' I say to the retired teacher: 'I'll go file the report, and I'll put my name on it — but I need you all to promise me this: if the housing authority comes to verify the facts, you cannot deny that you knew. I'm not asking you to be out in front. But I can't stand there alone and lie by saying I discovered all of this by myself.'

options.label: D
options.content: I've decided to file a real-name report with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name, and to make my position clear to the neighbor face-to-face: if you support this, that support needs to be real action. I understand if you're not willing to co-sign, but don't use 'why bother' to strip this thing of its meaning. I say to the old neighbor: 'Brother, I'm going to file the report — putting my name down alone is fine. But I want you to remember: tonight, you said with your own mouth that none of this was a problem. If anyone asks about it down the road, I hope you'll tell the truth. Two million yuan is not a small thing. I'm not doing this for a few thousand bucks — I just don't want the people in this complex to think that if someone steals, they steal, and nobody does a damn thing about it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to proactively approach her and propose merging the two plans into a single executable version, rather than fighting it out at the ballot. I tell my friend: "I'll go talk to her — keep the time-tunnel framework, fold her free-party elements in. That way the plan can actually run, and it's more solid than trying to pull votes."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to campaign independently, stand by the original proposal, and let the idea's own appeal win people over — rather than merging it with another plan just to play it safe. I tell my friend: "I want to try on my own first. This weekend I'll go find people, show them the sketches, let them really picture that space — and if I've made my case and still lose, I'll accept it. But I don't want to gut the whole thing before I've even had a chance to show it."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to step away from the competition — I won't go talk to her about merging, and I won't spend the weekend canvassing votes. I'll let the proposal lose on its own terms. I tell my friend: "Forget it — if everyone likes her proposal, they should go with it. I'll keep mine for myself."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to campaign hard for votes and stand by my original plan — no merger. I tell my friend: 'No merging — my plan is my plan. Come on, this weekend we go talk to everyone one by one. I'm bringing the sketch. Let them see it for themselves.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to pull back for now, avoid a direct confrontation, but keep the alternative proposal alive — angling for one small opening to compromise rather than giving everything up entirely. I lowered my head slightly and said: 'I understand the timeline math you're talking about, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — I'll get the required materials submitted. But could we hold onto that comparison data for now? Once the phase materials are cleared, I'd like to come back and ask you to take another look at it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold my position in person, not withdraw the materials, but proactively offer a middle path — trying to shift the conversation from confrontation toward joint problem-solving. I say: 'Professor, I understand your concerns, and I'm well aware of the time pressure. But I can't pretend those control data don't exist — I'm not trying to invalidate your framework; I'm asking you to look at where the specific risk points are. Could you give me ten minutes? Let's look together and see whether there's any possibility of making partial adjustments within the current conditions.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand by the evidence and the alternative approach directly, explicitly refusing the suggestion to put away my materials and proceed with the original plan, while keeping my tone focused on the issue rather than emotionally confrontational. I speak up: 'I understand your judgment, but I can't put these materials away — I ran a controlled experiment, the data is right here, and I can go through it point by point right now. If you can show me where my methodology is flawed, I'll correct it immediately. But if the data itself holds up, I need you to seriously consider adjusting the approach. I'm willing to take the risk of a delay on myself.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold my ground and present the core evidence without directly escalating the conflict, while offering a limited compromise — and I do not voluntarily withdraw my materials. I speak up; my voice comes out quieter than I expected, but it doesn't waver: "Professor, I understand the pressure of the timeline, and I'm not trying to overturn the entire existing plan — what I want to ask is: would it be possible, while the main line of work moves forward, to allow me to run an alternative procedure as a controlled comparison at one specific stage, and document the risk point on record? That way, even if the final conclusion doesn't change, there's data showing we considered this issue."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note — I shield it with my body, fold it quickly, and stuff it back into my textbook, then brush off my best friend with a lie. As for whether to show up after school, I haven't decided yet, but I'm leaning toward not going, or putting off the decision until the very last second. I press my textbook down over the note and look up at her with a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking to borrow my notes.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to quickly hide the note so my best friend can't see it, and I decide to go to the meeting alone after school. I close my fingers around the note, palm it away, and smile at her: 'It's nothing — a classmate asking me for a favor, really boring stuff. Did you finish your homework?'

options.label: C
options.content: On instinct I flip the note face-down on the desk to cover it, vaguely change the subject, and quietly decide to slip away to the meeting after school — without telling her who asked me to come. I press the note under my textbook and say with a smile: 'Oh, it's nothing — just some random classmate, no big deal — did you finish your homework yet?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note and refuse to let my friend see it; at the same time, I'm deeply resistant to the idea of showing up at all, and I'm leaning toward not going. I flip my textbook over, press the note underneath it, look up, and give a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking about homework. Nothing interesting.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up and name the true weight of what was lost, to refuse the money — but to say it in as steady a voice as I can manage rather than letting it become an explosion. Not because I expect my uncle to understand, but because I can't let myself stay silent. I push the stack of bills back toward him, my voice a little dry but not trembling: 'Uncle, I can't take this. What was on that drive was six months of my work — it's not something a few bills can bring back.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to set aside any emotional confrontation and prioritize dealing with the actual loss — the drive itself. I politely but directly push the money back and ask for the drive. I say to my uncle: 'No need for the money — there are some work files on the drive, I need to take it and see if anything can be recovered. Please, make yourself comfortable.' I casually push the stack of bills back onto the coffee table, pick up the drive and the screw, and head to another room to look up data recovery services.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse the money, say my piece briefly, and end this conversation as quickly as possible — without getting into a direct argument with my uncle, but without pretending everything is fine either. I push the stack of money back across the coffee table, my voice flat, and say only one thing: 'Keep the money. What was in there isn't a money matter.' Then I stand up to go get some water, and I don't look at them again.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to express my anger directly, on the spot — refuse the money, say what needs to be said, and give my uncle no opening to wrap this up with a "kids don't know any better." I push the stack of cash back toward him, voice controlled but clear: "Uncle, I don't want the money. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't something you can make right by replacing a few hard drives. I need you to understand how serious this is. I'm not trying to make things difficult for you, but I can't just let this go."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and express my disagreement, but I frame it as offering supplementary information rather than a direct refutation, leaving the teacher as much room as possible. I rise, open my notebook, and say: 'Professor, I came across an academic work last week that draws on multiple primary sources, and it presents this causal relationship somewhat differently. I'd like to ask — is there something about the gap between that account and the framework you've described that I'm simply not understanding correctly?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly but without aggression, holding out my notebook as evidence. I stand and say: 'Teacher, last week I read an academic book that draws on primary archival sources, and the way it describes this issue is a bit different from what you've explained — I have notes on it right here. Would it be alright if I shared them?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly — respectful in tone, but clear in position — and actively bring the arguments and primary sources from my outside reading into the classroom discussion. I stand and say: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history in the library — it cites several primary-source documents and offers a different interpretation of the causal relationship here. I'd love to hear your thoughts on that reading.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up, but only to state the facts — no drawn-out argument — and sit back down the moment I'm done. I rise, my voice a little dry but as steady as I can keep it: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history, and the primary documents it cites describe this causal relationship differently — there's one point that doesn't quite match what you've said. I'm not sure which account is more accurate, but I wanted to raise it.' Then I lower my head and sit down, one hand pressed flat on my notebook.

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to stay at my current school, and tell her directly — no evasion, no sugarcoating. My reply to her tonight: 'I listened to every word you said. Staying here is the only honest choice I can make. If I went north carrying regret, that wouldn't be fair to you either. I want to talk about whether we can find another way forward — but I can't give you an answer I don't believe myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school, and I accept that this may bring the relationship to an end. I text her back, tell her I'll be staying in my own program, and am honest with her: I love her, but I can't sustain what we have by rewriting the direction of my own life.

options.label: C
