options.content: I choose to accept the offer from the university up north, giving up the opportunity to continue here, and I'm giving her my answer tonight. I call her right now, and I say: 'I've made up my mind — I'm coming to you. I'll mail back the reply form the day after tomorrow. Wait for me.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept my own school's offer and not change course just to hold the relationship together. I write back to her: 'I've thought about it seriously — I'm staying at my school. If you're willing, let's talk through what the future looks like for us; if you're not, I'll accept that too.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene and offer real, concrete protection — but on my own terms, through deliberate action rather than impulsive confrontation. I tell Lin Xiaoyu: 'Don't go to that meeting alone tomorrow — I'll figure something out. I'll use project coordination as a reason to get myself inserted into that meeting arrangement, so you won't have to face him by yourself. As for reporting it, we can take that one step at a time — but tonight, write down the times, the places, and what happened, and send it to me. Get it on record.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively, prioritizing information-gathering and concrete action over simply offering emotional support. I tell her: 'Start by walking me through everything he said and did — whatever you can remember, the more specific the better. And tomorrow's meeting — you don't have to walk in there alone.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to intervene directly, commit to offering concrete support, and guide her toward the formal reporting channels. I take her hand and say: 'Coming to find me tonight — that was the right thing to do. You don't have to walk into that meeting alone tomorrow. I'll be there with you, and we'll figure out the next step together — if you're willing, I'll help you push this to where it needs to go.'

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to step in and accept the professional risk — to help Lin Xiaoyu move this forward in a structured way, rather than stopping at simply listening. I tell her: "The moment you came to me today, this stopped being just your problem alone. Don't go to that meeting tomorrow by yourself — I'll figure something out. First, let's get everything you've experienced down in writing: the dates, the locations, exactly what happened each time, laid out point by point. That's the foundation for whatever we do next."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to throw myself fully into an impromptu speech, using the surprise of winning as my best material, and drawing the whole room in with a light, humorous touch. I lean toward the microphone with a grin and say: "Honestly? I didn't even proofread this piece for typos — so I think what the judges are really telling us today is: inspiration matters more than perfection. Thank you to everyone here — your applause makes me feel like even something you dashed off can carry real weight!"

options.label: B
options.content: I chose to muddle through and get out as fast as possible. I gripped the microphone, kept my head down, and mumbled a few words — 'I just wrote it casually, didn't think too much about it' — then tucked the trophy and certificate under my arm and practically fled off the stage, sinking back into my seat and folding myself into the crowd.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak, but I use self-deprecation and deliberate understatement to preemptively defuse any possible doubt — pushing the honor away from myself to keep myself safe. I lean into the microphone and say: 'Honestly… I didn't expect this myself. I wrote this piece pretty casually, so maybe the judges saw something in it that I didn't — thank you all.' Then I lower my head, take a small step back, and return the microphone to the host.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to force myself to open my mouth anyway, but I squeeze out only a handful of words and make a hasty exit — unable to truly share anything at all. I lean into the microphone and say quietly: 'It's just… something I wrote on a whim. Thank you.' Then I hand the microphone back to Mr. Zhao and retreat to the side, head down.

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to make the promise of secrecy — but not with coldness. I tell her clearly that I can't agree to this one thing, because I'm too afraid of losing her. I look down, my voice a little hoarse: 'I can't promise you that… not because I don't care about you — because I do care about you. I can't carry this alone. We need to find someone who can actually help you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to make any promise on the spot. Instead, I hold space for his pain first, and then — gently but firmly — tell him we need to find an adult who can help. I crouch down and take his hand, and I say: 'I won't lie to you and tell you I promise, because I'm too scared of losing you — let's go find someone together, and I'll be right there with you the whole time.'

options.label: C
options.content: I didn't promise to keep it secret. Instead, I put the focus on just being there with her — trying to make sure she didn't feel abandoned, while leaving room to seek help later. I crouched down and said quietly: 'I'm not going to go around telling everyone — but I also can't promise to do nothing. Can you tell me what's actually been going on lately?'

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to give a clean, clear-cut promise of secrecy, but I insist on staying by her side — no running — trying to steady her first before we figure out what to do together. I take her hand, my voice coming out a little rough: 'I'm not going anywhere. I'm right here. But I can't promise you I'll do nothing — not because I don't care about you, but because I care too much. I can't carry this alone, and you shouldn't have to either. Let's figure it out together, okay?'

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to express genuine interest while making it clear that I needed to verify the information before giving any answer, keeping the decision in my own hands. I told the senior: "I listened carefully to everything you said, and I'm going to take this seriously. But before I give you an answer, I need to see the original letter of intent, and whatever user data the product has right now — if there is any. I'll give you a definitive reply before Tuesday."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to politely but clearly communicate that I cannot accept the terms of full-time commitment, carving out space to step back rather than rejecting the opportunity outright. I tell my senior: "I genuinely find your project interesting, but the condition of going full-time and taking a leave of absence isn't something I can agree to right now — not because I don't believe in it, but because I know myself well enough to know I can't perform in a high-intensity collaborative environment like that, and that wouldn't be fair to you either. If there's ever a possibility of a technical advisor role or remote collaboration down the line, I'd be happy to talk."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to express enthusiasm and lean toward accepting, while carving out a small buffer rather than committing on the spot. I lean forward, meet my senior's eyes directly, and say: 'I don't need to wait until Tuesday — this direction feels right to me, and everything you said landed. Give me 48 hours. Not to hesitate, but to get my own side of things sorted out — and then we sit down and talk through the details for real.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to politely decline the invitation while buying myself some breathing room — I won't agree on the spot, but I won't burn the bridge either; I'll use 'I need to think it over seriously' to extract myself from this pressure cooker first. I pick up my Americano, take a sip, set it back down, and look up at him: 'I hear what you're saying, and I've taken it all in — but this is too big a decision for me to make right now. Let me go away and think it through properly. I'll give you an answer by Tuesday.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lend him three thousand dollars on the spot and put the loss of the trip aside for later. I stand up and extend my hand toward him: 'Get up — come with me to the ATM. I'll lend it to you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lend him the three thousand yuan, but I don't want to talk more than necessary — I want to get it done as fast as possible and go. I hold out my phone so he can see the balance, and say quietly: 'I'll transfer it to you. Give me your payment code. Doesn't matter whether you write an IOU or not.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to lend the money, but with conditions attached — some way to keep a sense of control. I pulled out my phone, my voice coming out harder than I intended, and said: 'Write an IOU in your phone's notes app, screenshot it and send it to me, then give me your phone number. I'll lend you the three thousand, but I need your real information.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan immediately, without hesitating to press for too many details. I pull out my phone and open the transfer screen, and I tell him: 'I'm sending it to you now — go pay the deposit. You don't need to write an IOU. Save him first.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to deliver the evidence asynchronously, hand the decision back to the roommate, and keep myself out of any direct confrontation. Quietly screenshot and save the search results, close his laptop, and once he walks in, send him a message: 'I accidentally saw your section just now — ran a search, sending you the screenshots. It's due the day after tomorrow. You decide what to do with it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to confront my roommate the moment he walks in the door — bring it up directly, no dancing around it, no going to the professor behind his back. I stand up and move toward the doorway, and I say: 'I happened to see your document just now, and I searched a few of the passages — you know what I found. It's due the day after tomorrow. We need to figure out what we're going to do.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to confront him directly — to tell my roommate what I found and demand that he deal with it before submission, but I have no intention of contacting the professor. Before the footsteps reach the door, I turn his laptop screen back to where it was, take a deep breath, and wait for him to push the door open. Then I say: 'I accidentally saw what was on your screen just now — I searched a few of the passages, you know what I mean. It's due the day after tomorrow, so you need to figure out what you're going to do. I'm not carrying this for you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell my roommate directly, face-to-face — lay out the facts and require him to fix it before the deadline, without any emotional judgment, and without going around him to contact the professor. Once he comes in and sits down, I say calmly: 'I accidentally saw your section just now. I searched a few passages, and they're word-for-word matches with articles online. It's due the day after tomorrow — you need to fix it, or the whole group is done for.'

options.label: A
options.content: I don't agree outright to being on-site the entire weekend. Instead, I propose a division of labor and a remote-coverage plan. I explain that I need to go back home to accompany my parents for Dad's follow-up appointment, but that I'll start tonight by drawing up a list of gaps, coordinate with colleagues on the market side to share the load, and complete my portion of the work remotely over the weekend.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honestly explain the situation at home while proposing a middle-ground arrangement — such as handling part of the work remotely — rather than simply agreeing or outright refusing. I keep my eyes slightly down, voice low but as steady as I can manage: 'Director, my father has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend — I've already promised my family I'd go back to be with him. Could we do it this way: I push as far as I can tonight and before I leave tomorrow morning, I stay reachable remotely on the way there, and for the final draft Sunday night I'll do my best to either get back in time or go through it with you online. Would that work?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to propose a compromise on the spot — neither agreeing outright nor refusing — and proactively explain my family situation while pushing for a flexible arrangement. I say to the director: 'I will absolutely carry this project — but there's something at home I need to be upfront about. My dad has a follow-up appointment this weekend, and I promised to go with him. I'll head back Saturday to take care of that, then come back the same evening or first thing Sunday morning. Whatever time I have left, I'll pour everything into the proposal — submitting Sunday night is no problem. Does that work for you?'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose partial compromise while holding the line: make it clear to the director that the weekend trip home is not being canceled, but offer to put in extra hours tonight to push the project forward, provide remote support on the critical sections over the weekend, and join a video call Sunday evening for the final-draft review. Say directly: 'I have a family commitment this weekend — it was locked in three days ago and I can't cancel it. I can stay late tonight and I'm available remotely over the weekend, but coming into the office isn't something I can do. Tell me which parts are the biggest bottlenecks and let's work out the priorities together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to give a clear rejection on the spot, and I decide to tell my current partner that same day about everything that happened at this meeting. I look my ex in the eye and say: 'I'm grateful you told me all of this, but the question of whether I've moved on — the answer is yes, I have. I'm in a serious relationship right now, and I'm not going to leave it hanging here. I wish you well.' Then that evening I reach out to my partner first and lay out everything that happened today, exactly as it was.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to turn my ex down — gently but clearly, leaving no room for ambiguity — and at the same time I decide to tell my current partner honestly what this meeting was about. I speak up: "I haven't completely forgotten what we had, but I'm in a new relationship now, and I'm not going back. Thank you for telling me all of this." When I get home, I'll tell my current partner directly what happened today.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to turn down getting back together — gently but clearly — while acknowledging that I did waver inside, and I decide to tell my current partner about this meeting. I say to my ex: "I won't lie to you and say there's nothing there, but I'm with someone now, and I don't want to let him down — and I don't want to keep you waiting on an answer that's never going to come. This is where we stop." When I get home, I bring it up myself: I tell my current partner that I saw my ex today, and what my ex said.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to turn down my ex's request to get back together, but my response is vague and riddled with self-attacking apologies — and I decide to hide the details of this meeting from my current boyfriend. I say to my ex: "We can't go back to what we were. You deserve better. I'm sorry for making you wait so long" — then I lower my head, unable to meet his eyes, while inside I've already started running through how I'm going to explain to my current boyfriend why I'm coming home late tonight.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now — but I am absolutely not giving up on recovering the deposit. As I retreat, I'm quietly memorizing details, preserving evidence, and mapping out the path for follow-up complaints. I bend down and start gathering the scattered clothes, and in a steady but clear voice I say: 'I'll leave tonight — but I'm taking the contract with me, and we'll settle the deposit through legal channels.'

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to leave in silence — instead, force the confrontation into the open, buying time and drawing witnesses. Say out loud: 'Fine, I'm not going anywhere. You're standing on my stuff while you talk to me about a contract — there are plenty of doors in this hallway, and everyone can hear us right now. I'm calling 12345 this second. You say you know people at the precinct? Perfect — then we'll wait for them together, right here in front of everyone, and sort out this contract and this deposit where the whole world can see.' At the same time, pull out the phone and get ready to dial.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now, without confronting anyone on the spot — but I make a silent decision to pursue the deposit through legal channels afterward. I keep my head down, my voice steady but very quiet: 'I'm leaving now, but I'm keeping this contract, and we'll settle the deposit matter another time.' Then I bend down to gather the clothes scattered on the floor, pick up my suitcase, and walk out of the hallway.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now, giving up any confrontation in the moment — but I haven't given up on recovering the deposit. I press that thought down into the deepest part of myself, to be dealt with another day. I lower my head, and in a voice that's barely audible, I say: 'I'm leaving now, but this isn't over.' Then I bend down and start picking up the clothes scattered across the floor, hands trembling, eyes never once lifting to look at him.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up and respond to my roommate — using the bottle as a reason to stay, remaining right where I am in a clumsy but genuine way. I keep the bottle in my hand instead of kicking it back, turn around, and say: 'I don't feel like going out tonight. This bottle of pills… can you tell me about it?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene directly — I don't return the pill bottle, and I immediately contact the resident advisor or call a mental health crisis line. I don't let this get handled internally within the dorm room. I keep the bottle in my hand, stand up, and walk to the curtain around his bed. In a steady voice, I say: 'I'm not giving this back to you. You need someone here right now — I'm going to contact the RA. If you don't want me to, tell me now.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak up and step in — using the pill bottle as the opening to walk directly toward my roommate, without flinching from this crisis signal. I keep the bottle in my hand instead of giving it back, stand up, and walk to the edge of his bed. I say quietly: 'I didn't go out tonight — good timing, I'll keep you company for a bit. This bottle — can you tell me what's going on?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to intervene — I don't return the bottle, I walk toward the bed curtain and speak, while preparing to reach out for outside help. I grip the pill bottle in my hand, walk to his bedside, and say quietly: 'I'm not leaving — I just don't want to. I'm holding onto this bottle for now — how are you doing right now?'

options.label: A
options.content: I'm choosing to get the follow-up done as soon as possible, and to tell my family about the risk first — no more hiding it. I'll book the appointment first, then tell everyone at home that I saw something abnormal and need to go in right away.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep this to myself for now, prioritize getting definitive information, and defer any emotional trigger until there are facts to back it up. I wake the phone screen, go straight to the hospital's appointment system, lock in the earliest available follow-up slot, then flip the phone face-down on the desk and open my work files.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to keep it to myself for now, using 'waiting for the results to be confirmed' as my reason to move forward with the follow-up alone, leaving the decision of whether to tell my family suspended. I reply to the doctor's text to confirm the follow-up appointment, then flip my phone face-down on the desk, reopen the code documentation, and tell myself: wait until the results come in.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell my family as soon as possible, but I'll hold the rhythm steady — I won't frame it as a catastrophe. Tonight I'll bring my partner in, be honest that the doctor has asked for a follow-up and that I'm worried there may be something to it, and we'll go together tomorrow to find out for sure.

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal in this moment, presenting it in a structured, evidence-based way rather than silently accepting the consensus. I turned my screen toward the room and said: 'There are a few other ideas I'd like to share — I've put together a specific proposal with the risk points laid out. I need three minutes. I think saying it now costs less than waiting until something goes wrong later.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to drop the idea of raising an alternative proposal, fall in line with the current consensus, and not create friction at this moment. I shook my head and said: 'Nothing from me — let's go with the plan as it stands. I'll work with whatever assignments come my way.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to raise the alternative plan on the spot — to open things up rather than quietly go along. I turn my screen toward the group and say: 'Actually, yes — I've put together a different approach over here. Give it two minutes; if everyone feels it's not worth discussing, we'll get straight back to dividing up tasks — but I want this plan to be seen at least once.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up briefly — raising my concern without trying to hard-sell my alternative — and leave the file as a written supplement for them to decide on. I say: 'I still think there could be some strain when it comes to scaling later on. I'll write up the plan and send it to everyone — whichever way you go is up to you.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to strike back loudly and immediately, actively turning this livestreamed scene into my own stage for clearing my name. I hold my phone screen up high, my voice cutting through the noise of the carriage, staring straight into the camera: 'Everyone, look closely — what's on my phone is a work group chat, check the timestamp yourself — I did not photograph anyone. This is a false accusation!'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to proactively present the evidence and use facts to cut through the chaos, rather than argue or silently endure. I raise my phone screen high and turn it toward the phone that's livestreaming, and say in a steady voice: 'Everyone, take a good look at this screen — this is our department's work group chat. A new request came in at four in the morning, and I was just reading the message. The timestamp is right here.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to break the situation with physical evidence rather than verbal argument, forcing myself to speak but taking the shortest possible path straight to the proof. I raise my phone screen high and turn it toward the people filming around me, and with everything I have I squeeze out one sentence: 'Look at the screen — work group chat, timestamp, I was scrolling the group, I wasn't photographing you. See for yourselves.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to go on the offensive — counter with evidence directly, no explanations, no apologies, no showing weakness. I snapped my phone screen around toward the live-stream camera, my voice calm but unmistakably clear: 'You're live-streaming? Good. Let everyone get a good look at this screen — this is the work group chat, the timestamp is right there, my phone was never pointed at you.' Then I pulled my collar free from their grip and held their gaze.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak — to tell her part of the truth, to acknowledge how hard things are, while setting aside for now the most drastic idea (scrapping everything to go independent) and focusing on steadying what's right in front of us. I turned my hand over and held hers back, and said quietly: 'The options really are narrow right now. I haven't figured it out yet — but I don't want to hide it from you. It's harder than I expected.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to be honest with my partner about the real situation and my inner fear, while also deciding not to lower my salary floor — and instead to consider a more aggressive path toward starting something of my own or a full pivot. I squeeze her hand and say: "I'm going to be straight with you — I'm scared right now, but I'm not going to go work in a call center. I want to talk to you about a much bigger bet."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell my partner the truth and to swallow my pride first — to go after whatever work I can get up to speed on quickly. I say quietly, 'I'm letting go of the old salary standard. Starting tomorrow, I'm applying to warehouse and customer service jobs too.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to be honest with my partner about the reality of our situation and my own sense of being lost — setting aside, for now, any concrete discussion of job-search limits — and to make sure this night doesn't turn into the two of us retreating into separate silences. I tighten my grip on her hand and say quietly: 'I honestly don't know what the next step looks like right now, but I don't want to carry this alone — what you just said, let's figure it out together.'

options.label: A
options.content: Honestly express how strongly I want this academic opportunity, acknowledge the uncertainty, but make it clear that I want to seriously consider accepting the invitation — and that same evening, reply to my advisor's email with an initial expression of intent. I tell him: "I know this will change a lot of things, and I'm scared too. But when I found that notebook last night, I realized I haven't really been alive for a long time — I want to reply to that email, and we can figure out the rest together."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honestly express my assessment of the academic opportunity and to formally reply to my advisor accepting the invitation. I'll say: 'I've thought it through. This opportunity is the right call logically, and I'm going to take it. As for the life logistics — I'll handle them. But they're not a reason to walk away from this.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to honestly express my longing for this academic opportunity — not shying away from the uncertainty — while acknowledging that her concerns are real. Say to her: 'I know this will change a lot of things, and I'm scared too, but I can't keep pushing this down anymore — I want to reply to my advisor and have a real conversation about this opportunity.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honestly express how deeply I want this academic opportunity, while actively inviting my partner to work through the possibilities together, rather than pushing ahead on my own. I turn toward her, tilt the laptop screen so she can see it, and say: 'I'm not running away — I just haven't felt like this in a really long time. Can you let me tell you what this opportunity means to me? And then we can figure out what to do together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to reply to my colleague's message, but keep my wording restrained and maintain distance — not crossing any emotional boundary, responding to the feeling of being seen in a way that stays safe. I write back: "Made it home. That point you mentioned — I've always found it interesting too. That bookstore really is quiet; worth a visit sometime. Good night."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to reply to my colleague but deliberately narrow the boundary, using a polite yet clear-eyed tone to pull the connection back to a safe distance. I write: 'Made it home — thanks for checking in. That conversation the other day was really lovely; I'll have to recommend the bookstore to you sometime. Good night.' — Then I put down my phone, open my notes app, and add tomorrow's tutoring run with the kids to my schedule.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to reply to this colleague's message — not keeping a deliberate distance, but taking one step forward along this genuine connection. I write back: "Made it home. I kept thinking about what you said during the meeting today — that bookshop really does sound quiet. We should go sometime. Good night."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to reply to the message, but I proactively narrow the emotional boundaries, steering the conversation back onto a professional and friendly track without leaving room for the other person to go deeper. I reply: 'Made it home. Glad that idea was useful to you. The bookstore is worth a visit if you get the chance — very quiet.' I put the phone down after sending it and go check the schedule for the kid's tutoring.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up directly, without sidestepping the conflict — but I position myself as his ally, not his opponent, opening the conversation through emotional connection rather than a frontal rebuttal. I set the PPT aside, lean forward, and look him straight in the eyes: 'Man, I'm not jealous of you. I'm scared for you. You've put 800,000 in — I'm not in any position to regret that for you. But if I don't say something today, then what kind of friend have I even been? Tell me honestly: can you afford to lose this money?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak up directly and urge him to stop — without backing down because of his emotional reaction or the risk to our friendship — using facts and logic as my tools, refusing to trade silence for a surface-level peace. I flip the PowerPoint back to the first page, look him in the eye, and say: 'I'm not jealous of you. I've just seen too many people caught up in this exact structure and watched how it ends. Your income right now comes from the people you recruit, not from any product or service — that's the legal definition of a pyramid scheme, not my opinion, the law's definition. Eight hundred thousand is already gone, but you're still pulling more people in — that's what I'm actually worried about.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak up directly and not shy away from the conflict, even at the risk of losing this friendship. I set down the PPT and look up at him, my voice low but steady: 'I'm not jealous of you — I'm scared for you. This is a pyramid scheme. I mean that in the most literal sense — recruiting heads, tiered commissions, fabricated returns. The eight hundred thousand you put in is almost certainly gone, and so is the money of every one of those dozen-odd people you brought in. You can be angry with me, but I can't stand here and watch you keep walking deeper into this.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak my honest assessment gently but directly — not sidestepping the pyramid-scheme structure for what it is, but keeping the focus on my friend's state of mind rather than on who's right or wrong, and refusing to let his defensive attacks pull me off course. I set down the PowerPoint, look at him, and say: "I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're stupid — I just saw some things in this material that made me genuinely worried about you, and I want to talk about it seriously, not to tear you down, but because you're someone I care about."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to sidestep this shared strangeness — to gently acknowledge that it exists, rather than each of us retreating back into the familiar busyness. I look down at the table and say: "This does feel strange. How about… we have a cup of coffee together first? No need to think about what comes next."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to take the initiative and break the deadlock — pulling my housemate out the door with me, filling this strange morning with movement. I stand up, give the table a light slap, and say with a smile: 'Come on, let's go for a walk — just the two of us. We can figure out what comes next as we go. Beats sitting here staring into space.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to respond — to tuck my need inside a suggestion, phrased lightly, as if it just slipped out, not as if I'm scared. I lift my head, my voice steadier than I expected: "Maybe… let's go out for a walk together — I don't really know what I'd do with myself today anyway."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to make any active changes for now, and instead return to the familiar busyness to keep the days stable. I tell him: let's just keep things the way they've always been — I'll head to the shop first, and when I get back I'll tidy up around the house.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose neither to accept nor to reject — not yet. Instead, I demand that the act of 'redefining' itself be made clear and discussable, and I refuse to make any commitment without sufficient information. I set down my phone, looked at my partner, and said: 'I need you to tell me what you mean by flexibility, specifically — where are the boundaries, what are the rules. We need to spell this out. I can't say yes or no to a vague proposal.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to set aside any direct response for now, letting confusion and silence stand in for any real position, while getting distracted by my phone. I looked up at her, stayed quiet for a few seconds, my voice coming out a little hoarse: 'All of this you're saying… I need to think about it.' Then, almost without realizing it, I glanced down at my phone screen as it lit up.

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse the proposed redefinition of the relationship's boundaries; hold to the original commitment. I press the phone face-down first, then tell him plainly: 'I can't accept this kind of redefinition — we need to talk this through properly.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up immediately, to turn this into a direct, face-to-face confrontation, refusing silence and delay. I set down the dish towel and look straight at her: 'You said you've been thinking about this for a long time — well, I want to know now, when did you start thinking about it, because when did there get to be so much between us that you weren't telling me?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to cooperate with the vice principal to a limited degree — I'll say who brought the cards in, but I won't volunteer anything extra or embellish. I keep my head down and speak: 'It was Chen in the back row — he brought them in. I saw it, but I wasn't part of it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the truth about who was wearing the badge, but I won't frame myself as anyone's follower. I say directly who I saw wearing it, and I also state plainly, right there in the room, that he just threatened me — don't count me in as an accomplice.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the truth about who brought the contraband in, prioritizing my own protection and making clear I had no part in it. I'll quietly tell the director exactly who I saw and what happened — and I'll also let him know that person just threatened me.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to give a vague, minimizing response — neither directly identifying the person who brought the contraband, nor explicitly denying that I saw anything — in order to end this conversation as quickly as possible and get out of here. I say to the director: "I was reading at the time. I didn't notice who brought it — I really couldn't make it out."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to outwardly go along with the overall direction, but use execution risks as my entry point to raise specific reservations, and push for a transitional buffer period — without directly challenging his authority. I slowly open the proposal and say: 'I understand the overall direction, and I'll own the execution. But there are a few junctures where switching too fast will cause problems — not a matter of old habits, but data migration and the time window for staff training. I need to flag those here. Which ones can you give a two-week buffer on?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to step up and engage directly, raising specific objections on the spot and working to embed my own judgment and buffer space within the execution framework — rather than offering surface compliance. I flip the proposal open, press my index finger against one of the timeline milestones, and say: 'I have an issue to raise here — not a question of whether it can be changed, but if this milestone goes the way you're describing, I can tell you right now exactly what gaps will open up in the transition period. Let's hear how you want to handle that.'

options.label: C
options.content: Accept the overall direction on the surface, while fighting for a voice at the implementation level — trading 'I'll oversee the transition' for real room to preserve the process details that matter. I speak up: "Fine, I have no issue with the overall direction. But if I'm the one overseeing the transition, then I need to break this down according to my own understanding of how these workflows operate. I'll hold the pace you want — but when it comes to the specifics of what gets changed, I need you to give me the space to do that."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the overall direction while raising specific risk nodes on the spot, pushing for a transition buffer and written confirmation — rather than silently complying or confronting him head-on. I flip open the proposal, press my finger on the first timeline node, and keep my voice steady but unyielding: 'I accept the overall direction, but there are three risk points here that need to be confirmed before execution — if we don't get them written into the transition plan this week, I can't guarantee a problem won't affect the launch date, and I need you to give me a written record of the decision basis.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the missing model, refuse to go along with any lie, and proactively ask to go on stage immediately and deliver the presentation through words alone. I say directly to the homeroom teacher: 'Ms. Li, the model was lost on the bus — that's our mistake — but we have our content ready. Please let us go on. I'll present it. We don't need the model.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the missing model — I won't go along with the lie, but I also won't actively assign blame to anyone; I simply state the facts and ask to continue with a verbal presentation instead. I say quietly to the homeroom teacher: 'The model was lost on the way to school and didn't make it here — would it be alright if we just walked through the content verbally?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the model being lost, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately pivot to figuring out how to complete the presentation without it. Looking the homeroom teacher in the eye: 'The model was lost on the way to school — he left it on the bus. Without the model, we'll complete the presentation using a verbal explanation and the board. I'll take the lead.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to go along with the lie, but I also don't throw my teammate under the bus on the spot — instead, I steer the conversation directly toward how to handle the presentation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but quickly: 'The model had an accident on the way here and didn't make it, but we can go up and present without it — I have all the content memorized.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to complete my school responsibility first, then negotiate with Mom about whether we can head over to join the party afterward — I will not abandon a commitment I've already made. I say to Mom: 'Mom, tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the key, and it's something I promised to do. How long do you think it'll take once I'm done? If there's still time, could we go to the amusement park to meet my cousin after?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to go to school first to finish my responsibilities, then head to the birthday outing — I'll do both, and I'm not giving either one up. I tell Mom: 'I'm happy, but tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — it'll take half an hour, tops, and then we can head out. It won't hold us up for long.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to do both, handling them in sequence by planning the timing in advance so I can honor my responsibility and still make it to the outing. I tell Mom: 'I'm excited, I really am! But tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the keys, and I promised her I'd do it. Once I'm done we'll head straight out; we should still make it in time. Can you let Uncle know we'll be there a little bit later?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take care of the watering and tadpole-feeding at school first, then decide afterward whether to head over to the gathering — but I'm not committing to showing up. I tell Mom: 'Tomorrow I have to go to school first to water the plants. The teacher gave me the keys — the mimosa was just repotted and it can't wait. Once I'm done I'll see if I can make it over, but I might not be in time. Can you let Uncle know for me?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose silence and freeze in place, refusing to open my mouth or make any choice at all, letting the stillness of my body stand in for words. I sink down into a crouch, bury my face in my knees, and can't make a single sound — only shaking.

options.label: B
options.content: I refuse to make a choice. Instead I try to break the deadlock with my voice and my body — pulling both of their attention away from "waiting for me to take a side." I lunge forward and wrap my arms around the waist of the one crouching by the door, then spin back and scream at the other one through my tears: "You can't both leave! Nobody is allowed to leave! I won't let you!"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay, expressing through the rigid stillness of my body a despair that cannot make any choice, while instinctively leaning toward the 'stay' side. I drew my shoulders in tight, my feet didn't move, and in a voice barely above a whisper I said: "I'm not leaving… I'm not going anywhere…" Then I crouched down, covered my face, and cried out loud.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to take either side — I'm going to flee the scene first. I'll wrench my hands free from theirs, cry out 'I don't want to choose,' and then run back to my room and shut the door.

options.label: A
options.content: I've decided to file a named complaint with the Housing and Construction Bureau in my own name, accept the neighbors' quiet support without pressing them for a joint signature, and calmly let them know where I stand. I'll tell them: 'The materials are solid enough — my name alone will do. If you're willing to help track down more documents, I'll take that; I'm not going to push the joint-signature angle — but I am going to file the complaint.'

options.label: B
options.content: He chooses to file a real-name report in his own name alone, refusing to let the neighbors' retreat or his wife's pleas change his position — but he won't push anyone to join him either. He says calmly to the old man upstairs: 'I understand your difficulties. I won't press anyone on the joint letter — but I'll go to the Housing and Construction Bureau myself. I've already got the materials ready.'

options.label: C
options.content: I've decided to file a real-name report with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name, and to make my position clear to the neighbor face-to-face: if you support this, that support needs to be real action. I understand if you're not willing to co-sign, but don't use 'why bother' to strip this thing of its meaning. I say to the old neighbor: 'Brother, I'm going to file the report — putting my name down alone is fine. But I want you to remember: tonight, you said with your own mouth that none of this was a problem. If anyone asks about it down the road, I hope you'll tell the truth. Two million yuan is not a small thing. I'm not doing this for a few thousand bucks — I just don't want the people in this complex to think that if someone steals, they steal, and nobody does a damn thing about it.'

options.label: D
options.content: I've decided to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name — but I'm making it clear to the neighbors: quiet support is fine, but I'm not out here taking all the risk for everyone for nothing. I'll keep everyone posted as things develop. To the retired teacher, I said: "I understand why you won't sign your names — but I've already done the digging, and I'm going to file this complaint. However you want to help, that's up to you. Just don't expect me to carry the whole thing by myself and call it done."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to go along with what he's saying for now — no exposing him on the spot — but I use one sentence to keep him here, laying the groundwork for a private conversation later tonight. I take the milk from him, lower my head and drink a sip, then look up at him and say: 'The speech can wait — sit down for a second. Dad has something he wants to talk to you about, just the two of us.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold back and not expose everything in this moment, but I decide that tonight I will find a quiet moment alone with him to talk through this face to face — and to stop him from giving the speech at the commendation ceremony and from registering for the competition. I take the milk from him, set down what I'm holding, and say calmly: 'Let's put the speech aside for now. There's something important we need to talk about tonight — come find me in the study when you're done eating.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold back for now, not to expose him in this moment — but inwardly I've already decided that tonight I will sit down with him privately and talk this through. I take the milk from him and say quietly: "We can talk about the speech later. Go wash up first — tonight, let's have a real conversation."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — not to expose him on the spot — but to use a single question to plant a crack inside him, leaving room for a deeper conversation later. I take the milk from him, look down at it for a moment, then lift my head and say calmly: 'Let's set the speech aside for now. I have a question I want to ask you — this exam, do you feel like you were truly ready for it? Can you walk me through how you studied for the math?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to make clear that I've seen the report and ask my partner to be honest with me about what's going on — I won't follow his lead into avoidance. I set the envelope down gently on the table, didn't hand it back to him, and said quietly: 'I saw it. Can you tell me what's going on?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lay it all on the table. Instead of going along with her and putting the envelope away, I gently but clearly let her know I've seen what's in the report and ask her to be honest with me. I set the envelope softly on the table, look at her, and say: 'I saw it — whatever it says, I want to know together with you.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to bring it out into the open directly — rather than going along with her and putting the envelope back, I speak up, gently but firmly. I set the envelope down softly on the table, look at her, and say: 'I saw it. Tell me what's going on — whatever it is, we'll face it together.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to gently but directly let him know I've already seen the report, and invite him to tell me honestly what's going on — rather than smoothly handing the envelope back as if I hadn't. I set the envelope quietly on the table, look up at him, and say: 'I saw some of it. Would you be willing to tell me about it? I want to face this with you.'

options.label: A
options.content: She chooses to stand up and share her idea in front of the class, giving a vague, positive response to the suggestion about the speech competition rather than refusing outright. She rises slowly, her voice a little soft but pushing herself to speak clearly: "It's just… using the principle of a seesaw — you put the elephant on one side, then add people one by one to the other side, and by counting the people you can figure out how much the elephant weighs…" When she finishes, she gives the teacher a small nod: "The competition… I — I'll think about it."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea concisely, but politely decline the invitation to join the speech competition. I'll say: 'Teacher, I can walk through this method, but I'd rather not join the speech competition — I don't think it's necessary.' Then I'll lay out the logic of the seesaw weighing method in the most straightforward way I can, without dragging it out, and without caring about the skeptical looks.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up without a moment's hesitation, lay out the full explanation in front of everyone, and readily agree to enter the speech competition. I rise to my feet, sweep my eyes across the whole class, and say with a smile: "The principle of the seesaw is simple — put the elephant on one end, then keep stacking people onto the other end until the two sides balance. Count how many people there are and roughly how much each one weighs, add it all up, and that's the elephant's weight. I'll go to the speech competition — thank you, Teacher!"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to quietly decline standing up to explain, and respond to the suggestion about the speech competition with a vague, evasive shake of my head. I lower my gaze and say in a very small voice: "I… I can't really explain it well — teacher, you go ahead and tell them."

options.label: A
options.content: Refuse to promise secrecy, but don't leave immediately — choose to stay and explain my reasons to her first, then let her know I will be seeking help from an adult. I meet her eyes directly and say, in a steady voice: 'I can't promise you that — not because I don't care about you, but because this is something I can't carry alone, and you shouldn't have to carry it alone either. I'll go with you to find someone, but I'm not going to pretend I didn't see anything today.'

options.label: B
options.content: I didn't promise to keep the secret on the spot. Instead I chose to steady her emotions first — neither exposing her nor flatly refusing — while inside I had already decided I had to find an adult who could help her. I held her hand, my voice trembling, and said: "I'm not going to go around telling people — but I need you to help me figure this out together, how we can get you to a place where you don't have to do this anymore. I can't carry this by myself. I need someone to help both of us."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to make any promise on the spot. Instead, I hold space for his pain first, and then — gently but firmly — tell him we need to find an adult who can help. I crouch down and take his hand, and I say: 'I won't lie to you and tell you I promise, because I'm too scared of losing you — let's go find someone together, and I'll be right there with you the whole time.'

options.label: D
options.content: I didn't promise to keep it secret. Instead, I put the focus on just being there with her — trying to make sure she didn't feel abandoned, while leaving room to seek help later. I crouched down and said quietly: 'I'm not going to go around telling everyone — but I also can't promise to do nothing. Can you tell me what's actually been going on lately?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly — respectful in tone, but clear in position — and actively bring the arguments and primary sources from my outside reading into the classroom discussion. I stand and say: 'Professor, last week I came across an academic monograph on this period of history in the library — it cites several primary-source documents and offers a different interpretation of the causal relationship here. I'd love to hear your thoughts on that reading.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and voice my disagreement directly but without aggression, holding out my notebook as evidence. I stand and say: 'Teacher, last week I read an academic book that draws on primary archival sources, and the way it describes this issue is a bit different from what you've explained — I have notes on it right here. Would it be alright if I shared them?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up and, framing it as 'seeking guidance' rather than 'making a correction,' gently raise the different account I came across in my outside reading, packaging the discrepancy as a question. I stand up and say quietly: 'Teacher, I was reading a book last week that cited some primary archival sources, and the way it described this period of history differs a little from what you've explained. I'm not entirely sure whether I've misunderstood something on my end — could you help clarify it for me?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up directly and point out, calmly and clearly, the contradiction between the teacher's account and the primary sources — no hedging, no softening of language. I open my notebook and point to the page numbers I've recorded, and say: 'Sir, the way you just described this historical event is noticeably inconsistent with several primary documents I came across at the library last week. Page X of this book cites firsthand sources, and the conclusion it reaches is different from what you've said — I'd like to ask you about that.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and hold off on the after-school plan for now, putting my friend's feelings ahead of my own. I fold the note casually and tuck it back into my textbook, then smile over at her and say: "It's nothing — someone from the next class asking to borrow my notes. Keep working on your homework."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to quickly hide the note so my best friend can't see it, and I decide to go to the meeting alone after school. I close my fingers around the note, palm it away, and smile at her: 'It's nothing — a classmate asking me for a favor, really boring stuff. Did you finish your homework?'

options.label: C
options.content: On instinct I flip the note face-down on the desk to cover it, vaguely change the subject, and quietly decide to slip away to the meeting after school — without telling her who asked me to come. I press the note under my textbook and say with a smile: 'Oh, it's nothing — just some random classmate, no big deal — did you finish your homework yet?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note and brush off my best friend for now, while inwardly I've already decided to go to the meeting alone after school — but I need to deal with these two things separately first. I shoved the note into my textbook, looked up, and gave my best friend a smile: 'It's nothing, someone just asking to borrow my notes — totally boring.' My voice was steady, my eyes didn't waver.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation, not here, not yet. I'll wait until I've calmly worked out what outcome I actually want before deciding on a next step. I turn off my phone screen, look up, give her one expressionless glance, then drop my eyes and open my textbook.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to avoid the confrontation and escape the scene for now — no calling her out, no scene. I push every last bit of the anger and hurt down. I pick up my bag, keep my head low, walk out of the classroom, find a corner where no one can find me, shove my earbuds in, and pretend the entire world doesn't exist.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to push down the urge to confront her, getting through this moment with silence and avoidance, not letting her see that I already know about the screenshot. I lower my head, force the tears back, and say quietly, "I'm fine, probably just didn't sleep well," then look away and pretend to flip through my textbook.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to confront her directly, face to face — put the evidence I have out in the open, give her no room to keep brushing me off. I speak calmly: "I saw that screenshot. I saw the group chat too. You don't need to explain that it 'wasn't intentional' — I just want you to know that I know."

options.label: A
options.content: I allow myself a brief breakdown at the intersection, but in the end I use self-attack to seal off any genuine questioning, substituting "pretending to accept" for truly facing it. I flip my phone face-down, take a deep breath, and murmur to myself: "You didn't have a choice back then — this is just who you are" — then I put the car in gear and drive home.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to go straight home — I give myself some time alone to absorb the impact of this — but I don't allow myself to fall apart completely, because falling apart solves nothing. I flip my phone face-down onto the passenger seat, start the car, and drive to a nearby parking lot. I sit there, staring at the dark windshield, and say quietly: "This variable can never be verified, so it shouldn't be grounds for invalidating the existing data." But I know I don't entirely believe it.

options.label: C
options.content: He chooses to break the silence and reach out rather than suppress it alone or drive home and pretend everything is normal. He dials an old friend's number, his voice a little rough: 'I'm stuck at a red light right now — there's something I've been sitting on for a long time. Do you have a few minutes to talk?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stop at the intersection for a few minutes and give myself a moment alone — neither falling apart nor pretending nothing happened, just letting the feeling settle before I drive home. I turn my phone face-down, close my eyes, take a slow breath, and tell myself: 'Go home first. Just go home tonight. I'll deal with this later.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to turn my ex down — gently but without ambiguity — and decide to tell my current partner about the encounter that same day. To my ex, I say: 'I never pretended during those two years, but I'm in a new relationship now, and I can't give you a reason to keep waiting — that wouldn't be fair to you, and it wouldn't be fair to him. I genuinely hope you find happiness, but there's no going back for us.' Then, when I get home, I bring it up myself and tell my current partner everything that happened today.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to turn my ex down — gently but clearly, leaving no room for ambiguity — and at the same time I decide to tell my current partner honestly what this meeting was about. I speak up: "I haven't completely forgotten what we had, but I'm in a new relationship now, and I'm not going back. Thank you for telling me all of this." When I get home, I'll tell my current partner directly what happened today.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to turn down my ex's request to get back together, but my response is vague and riddled with self-attacking apologies — and I decide to hide the details of this meeting from my current boyfriend. I say to my ex: "We can't go back to what we were. You deserve better. I'm sorry for making you wait so long" — then I lower my head, unable to meet his eyes, while inside I've already started running through how I'm going to explain to my current boyfriend why I'm coming home late tonight.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to directly turn down my ex's request to get back together, and I decide to tell my current partner honestly about how this meeting went. I say to my ex: 'I hear what you're saying. That time was real for me too — but the problems we couldn't solve back then are still there now. I have no way to verify that you've changed, and I have my own life. I'm not going to give you a reason to keep waiting — that's not fair to you, and it's not fair to the relationship I'm in.' When I get home, I'll bring it up with my current partner myself and tell him everything that happened today.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to sign off confirming the data as valid, but rather than going directly over my colleague's head to report it to his supervisor, I give him one last chance to come forward on his own. I say to him: 'I can't sign off on this data. Tonight I'm going to reply to the system honestly and say there's a problem with the data that needs to be reviewed — you still have time. Before I submit that, go tell your supervisor yourself. I'll go with you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to report the data falsification to my superiors truthfully and to refuse to sign off on the data. I tell my colleague: 'I understand the position you're in, but I can't sign off on this data, and I can't hide this from our superiors. I'll be reporting it honestly tonight.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to report the data issue honestly to my supervisor and did not sign off on the confirmation email. I replied to the project management system's notification, flagging the data as anomalous and requiring review, and that evening I reached out to the project lead to explain the situation — without naming anyone directly, but making clear that the core metrics needed to be re-verified before the briefing.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to sign off confirming the data is valid, but I also won't formally report it to a superior — instead, I tell my colleague directly that I can't sign off on the data, and demand that he correct it or explain the situation himself before submitting. I say to him: 'I can't put my name on this data as confirmed. You know what I saw — you need to figure out how you're going to handle this before you submit tonight. I'm not stamping this for you.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to keep trying to talk him out of it, but instead of flat-out rejecting what he's saying, I use self-disclosure and emotional connection to try to get around his defenses. I set down the slides and say quietly: 'I'm not jealous of you. I'm just scared. Scared that three months from now you'll call me to say the money's gone — and that today I said nothing.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to say what I actually think — gently but clearly, without dodging and without accusation — say it all the way through, and leave the ball in his court. I set down the PPT, looked up, and met his eyes: 'I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're stupid. It's precisely because you're my closest friend that I have to tell you — this structure, I've seen it before. It's called a pyramid scheme. I don't want your money. I just don't want to be in a position, three months from now when you call me, where I never said this.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to keep trying to dissuade him — I'll lay out the factual hallmarks of a pyramid scheme structure directly, decline the investment invitation, and not dignify the 'jealousy' accusation with a response. I say: 'I'm not putting money in, and it's not because I'm jealous. This model of yours — recruiting downlines, tiered profit-sharing, returns that depend on the people below you rather than on any actual product — that's the textbook structure of a pyramid scheme. There's nothing I can do about the eight hundred thousand that's already gone, but don't ask me to hand over this hundred and fifty thousand.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up directly and urge him to stop — without backing down because of his emotional reaction or the risk to our friendship — using facts and logic as my tools, refusing to trade silence for a surface-level peace. I flip the PowerPoint back to the first page, look him in the eye, and say: 'I'm not jealous of you. I've just seen too many people caught up in this exact structure and watched how it ends. Your income right now comes from the people you recruit, not from any product or service — that's the legal definition of a pyramid scheme, not my opinion, the law's definition. Eight hundred thousand is already gone, but you're still pulling more people in — that's what I'm actually worried about.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up proactively, with a gentle and non-judgmental tone, while also responding to my colleague's private message without overstepping. First, I reply to my colleague: "I have a few thoughts too — want to talk it over together?" Then I send the younger colleague a private message: "Your project really resonated with me — I did something similar once, and there's one small detail I'd love to share with you. It might not even apply, just take it as a reference — would you be up for a quick chat when you have a moment?"

options.label: B
options.content: I've decided to reach out directly via private message, pointing out the specific structural risk points in the process — no emotional preamble, no 'just for your reference' disclaimer tacked on at the end. And I'll reply to my colleague: 'I'll say it directly. You don't need to get involved.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to use my colleague's private message as the entry point — passing along a word of caution from experience indirectly rather than directly, so it doesn't come across as a forceful intervention through a public comment. My reply to my colleague: "I saw it too, and your instinct might be right — if you're open to it, we could talk. I once ran a project with a very similar structure, and there are a few wrong turns that might be worth mentioning."

options.label: D
options.content: Choose a light-touch intervention over silence — leave a door open through open-ended questions rather than pouring experience over him. Send the young colleague a private message: 'Saw your project — there's a detail that brought some interesting questions to mind for me. If you ever feel like talking through certain nodes in the process architecture, I'm around whenever — purely out of curiosity, not advice.' And reply to my colleague: 'I feel the same way, but this is his exploration. We can just be here when he wants to talk.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on, respond with facts, neither dodging nor snapping back, and reclaim control of the meeting's rhythm with a steady tone. I hold his gaze, keep my voice even: 'I can handle it. I went through the unfinished module from last quarter over the weekend — we'll slot it into today's team meeting. If you have any specific concerns, say them now and we'll go through them together.' Then I turn to the rest of the team and signal everyone to sit down and get started.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on and establish authority through data and framework, rather than deflecting or firing back emotionally. I hold his gaze, voice steady: 'Good question. I have the specifics on the unfinished module and the full quarterly schedule right here — let's go through them now. Everyone please turn to item one on the agenda.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to respond directly in a way that appears composed on the surface but is in fact an act of self-protection——neither pushing back hard nor conceding weakness, pulling the conversation back to the meeting itself. I take a deep breath and say: 'Whether I can handle it or not——we'll find out by doing it. I've already gone through that unfinished module, and that's exactly why we're here today, to align on the schedule. Everyone take a seat. Let's get started.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to meet the challenge head-on with an open stance, and to actively reframe the senior team member's provocation as a topic for the whole group to work through together—establishing my rhythm as team lead rather than shutting him down. I smile, stand up straight, and speak at a volume that's calm but carries to every corner of the room: "Good question—that module is actually exactly what I want us to dig into today. Old Li, you've got the deepest experience on this one. When we meet in a bit, you go first, and we'll all figure out together how to move it forward."

options.label: A
options.content: I decide to tell my family — but I choose to bring it up in a calm, measured way, without amplifying the fear, laying out the facts clearly rather than making it sound catastrophic. Tonight, after the kids are asleep, I set down my phone, walk over to my partner, and say quietly: 'One of my results from today's check-up needs a follow-up — I don't know what it means yet, but I wanted you to know. We'll face it together.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep this to myself for now, prioritize getting definitive information, and defer any emotional trigger until there are facts to back it up. I wake the phone screen, go straight to the hospital's appointment system, lock in the earliest available follow-up slot, then flip the phone face-down on the desk and open my work files.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to keep this to myself for now — to build the information framework first, and tell my family once the follow-up results are in. I picked up my phone, opened the calendar, and created a new reminder in tomorrow morning's open slot: 'Contact hospital — schedule follow-up — confirm timeline.' Then I turned the screen face-down on the desk.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to be honest as soon as possible, but without framing it as a foregone conclusion. I'll schedule a follow-up appointment first, then bring my family together and tell them: 'I've found something abnormal, and I need us to deal with it right away — together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honestly express how deeply I want this academic opportunity, while actively inviting my partner to work through the possibilities together, rather than pushing ahead on my own. I turn toward her, tilt the laptop screen so she can see it, and say: 'I'm not running away — I just haven't felt like this in a really long time. Can you let me tell you what this opportunity means to me? And then we can figure out what to do together.'

options.label: B
options.content: She chooses to honestly express her genuine longing for this academic opportunity, while sincerely receiving his concerns and inviting him into a shared conversation rather than making a unilateral decision; she holds off on replying to her advisor's email for now, and first opens up to the person beside her. She gently takes his hand and says: "I've been staring at this email because I really do want to go — I know it would change a lot of things, and I know you have reservations, and I want to listen to all of them seriously. Can we talk tonight — not me trying to convince you, but the two of us figuring it out together?"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to honestly express my assessment of the academic opportunity and to formally reply to my advisor accepting the invitation. I'll say: 'I've thought it through. This opportunity is the right call logically, and I'm going to take it. As for the life logistics — I'll handle them. But they're not a reason to walk away from this.'

options.label: D
options.content: Honestly express how strongly I want this academic opportunity, acknowledge the uncertainty, but make it clear that I want to seriously consider accepting the invitation — and that same evening, reply to my advisor's email with an initial expression of intent. I tell him: "I know this will change a lot of things, and I'm scared too. But when I found that notebook last night, I realized I haven't really been alive for a long time — I want to reply to that email, and we can figure out the rest together."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to be honest with my partner about the real situation and my inner fear, while also deciding not to lower my salary floor — and instead to consider a more aggressive path toward starting something of my own or a full pivot. I squeeze her hand and say: "I'm going to be straight with you — I'm scared right now, but I'm not going to go work in a call center. I want to talk to you about a much bigger bet."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to be honest with my partner about the reality of our situation and my own sense of being lost — setting aside, for now, any concrete discussion of job-search limits — and to make sure this night doesn't turn into the two of us retreating into separate silences. I tighten my grip on her hand and say quietly: 'I honestly don't know what the next step looks like right now, but I don't want to carry this alone — what you just said, let's figure it out together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to be honest about the real situation and recalibrate my job-search floor — no soothing platitudes to placate my partner, but no emotional breakdown either. I squeeze her hand and speak: 'I'll be straight with you — the market right now isn't kind to people our age, but I've done the math: the severance can last eight months. I'll adjust my expectations, but I won't just take anything. Let's get the numbers clear first, and your dad's appointment this month — we'll go together.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak — to tell her part of the truth, to acknowledge how hard things are, while setting aside for now the most drastic idea (scrapping everything to go independent) and focusing on steadying what's right in front of us. I turned my hand over and held hers back, and said quietly: 'The options really are narrow right now. I haven't figured it out yet — but I don't want to hide it from you. It's harder than I expected.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to reply, but with a tone that is warm yet gently distancing — drawing this connection softly to a close without rejecting him outright or letting it go any deeper. I write: "Made it home. I found myself thinking about some of what we talked about that evening during the meeting today. That bookstore really is wonderfully quiet — you should go sometime when you get the chance. Good night."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to reply to the message, but I proactively narrow the emotional boundaries, steering the conversation back onto a professional and friendly track without leaving room for the other person to go deeper. I reply: 'Made it home. Glad that idea was useful to you. The bookstore is worth a visit if you get the chance — very quiet.' I put the phone down after sending it and go check the schedule for the kid's tutoring.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to reply to my colleague but deliberately narrow the boundary, using a polite yet clear-eyed tone to pull the connection back to a safe distance. I write: 'Made it home — thanks for checking in. That conversation the other day was really lovely; I'll have to recommend the bookstore to you sometime. Good night.' — Then I put down my phone, open my notes app, and add tomorrow's tutoring run with the kids to my schedule.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to reply to this colleague's message — not keeping a deliberate distance, but taking one step forward along this genuine connection. I write back: "Made it home. I kept thinking about what you said during the meeting today — that bookshop really does sound quiet. We should go sometime. Good night."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to answer the call but brush it off vaguely — "everything's fine, nothing major" — and leave it at that until the follow-up results come in. I picked up, keeping my voice as steady as I could: "Nothing to worry about, just a couple of minor indicators a little off — the doctor said to just keep an eye on it. Don't stress, we'll talk when I'm back Friday."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to answer the call, but I gloss over the test results and wait until the follow-up gives me something definitive before saying more. I pick up the video call and, keeping my voice steady, say: 'The results came in — two markers need a follow-up check. The doctor said it's just routine screening, probably nothing serious. I've already started making arrangements. Don't worry about it on your end; just focus on the trip.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I keep things vague for now — I'm not going to drop the worst of it on her yet. Once I pick up, I tell her: two of my markers came back elevated, the doctor wants me to get a follow-up as soon as possible, and the moment those results are in, you'll be the first to know.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to pick up the video call, but I gloss over the details of the report, saying only 'there are a couple of markers they want to recheck, the doctor said to come back in a few days,' pressing the real fear down beneath my voice. I answer, manage a small smile, and say: 'Hey — just walked out. There are two little indicators they want to follow up on, the doctor said to keep an eye on it for now, nothing major. How are things on your end?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to challenge the logical gaps in the proposal directly, on the spot — not attacking his authority as such, but forcing him to back it up with data. I flip the proposal open, point to the timeline, and say: 'I do have an objection — let's start with one: how were the transition-period timelines determined? If there's no actual measurement based on the existing process, I can't guarantee the rollout won't run into problems, and you won't get the results you're after either.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lead with pragmatic cooperation while carving out room to embed buffer space in the execution details, rather than directly challenging the overall direction. I open the proposal and say, calmly: 'I'm on board with moving forward — I'll keep a close eye on the new process. There are a few specific risk points in the transition period I want to walk you through right now.
