It's not about stalling; I just don't want problems cropping up later and throwing off your timeline — can I run you through them?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to appear cooperative on the surface while quietly buying time — raising risk details as a way of creating friction without direct confrontation. I speak up: 'I hear you on the direction, and I'll follow through on execution — but there are a few risk points in the transition period you may not have seen yet. Let me write them up; take a look and decide whether we should go over them first, so if something does go wrong later, nobody's left scrambling to explain it.'

options.label: D
options.content: Accept the overall direction on the surface, while fighting for a voice at the implementation level — trading 'I'll oversee the transition' for real room to preserve the process details that matter. I speak up: "Fine, I have no issue with the overall direction. But if I'm the one overseeing the transition, then I need to break this down according to my own understanding of how these workflows operate. I'll hold the pace you want — but when it comes to the specifics of what gets changed, I need you to give me the space to do that."

options.label: A
options.content: He politely declines the invitation, tells the other kid to head back first, and stays to look a little longer before deciding anything — neither diving into research nor leaving right away. "You guys go ahead, I'll come check it out in a bit."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to decline the invitation outright and stay to keep observing the beetle and the purple flower, without offering much explanation. I glanced up at him briefly and said, calmly: 'I'm not coming — I'm watching a beetle I've never seen before. You guys go ahead.' Then I looked back down and continued observing, starting to document the insect's physical features with my phone or a twig I found nearby.

options.label: C
options.content: I decline the invitation and stay behind to keep observing the insect and the little purple flower, trying to document every detail with my phone or the notebook I have on me. I tell the kid: "Go on ahead without me — there's something here I haven't finished looking at. I've never seen this bug before in my life, and I need to figure out what it is first."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to soften the moment gently — neither flatly refusing the invitation nor immediately giving up my observation — trying to buy myself a little time. I stay crouched where I am, look up, and give him a small smile: "Go ahead and warm up without me — I just need two more minutes. This bug is seriously weird, I've never seen anything like it — I'll be right there!"

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to take the fall for my friend temporarily — but I didn't fully confess. Instead I kept things vague to buy some breathing room. I lowered my head and said to the security guard: 'It was me… I don't know how it ended up in there, I didn't mean to…' My voice was barely above a whisper, my hands were shaking, and I couldn't stop the corners of my eyes from going red.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to state the facts truthfully, not to take the blame for my friend, but in a calm and measured tone — not actively accusing anyone, just making it clear that I didn't put the toy in the bag. I say to the security guard: 'I didn't put this toy in there. I don't know how it got into my bag. You can check the security footage.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the truth — that the toy was not put in my bag by me — and refuse to take the fall for my friend, even though my voice is shaking. I keep my head down, but I still manage to say it out loud: 'This… I didn't put this here. I don't know how it got into my bag.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the truth, refuse to take the fall for my friend, and state the facts directly to the security guard. I speak calmly: 'I didn't put that toy in there — he did. I don't know when he put it in, and I didn't take anything.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to state plainly that I've already seen the report and ask my partner to be honest with me about what's actually going on — no dancing around it. I set the envelope down on the table and say, calmly: 'I saw it. Go ahead and tell me — what does the report say, and how are things right now?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to gently but directly let him know that I've seen the report — to invite him to face it together, rather than follow his lead and sidestep it. I quietly set the envelope down on the table between us and say softly: 'I saw it. It's okay. Tell me. We'll get through it together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to make it clear that I've seen the report and demand that my partner be honest with me — I can't pretend nothing happened. I hold the envelope up, my voice a little unsteady but without backing down: "I saw it. Can you tell me — what does it mean?"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to lay it all out in the open — to ask my partner to honestly explain what the report says, and to refuse to play along with his attempts to change the subject. I set the envelope flat on the table, look at him, and say: 'I saw it. It's the results of a medical check-up. You can tell me now — what does it say.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take the microphone and speak sincerely — to get through an impromptu acceptance speech by looking after the mood of the room, rather than retreating into silence. I gave a small smile into the microphone, voice a little unsteady but still finding the words: 'Thank you to the teacher and the judges. When I wrote this piece, I really just wanted to put down on paper a question that had been rattling around in my head and wouldn't go away… I never expected anyone to like it. I think maybe — the kind of confusion the story is about — maybe most of us have felt something like that at some point.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to speak honestly, treating this unexpected win itself as an interesting 'counter-intuitive phenomenon' to share, and naturally weaving in all the chaotic associations and questions I had while writing. I lean into the microphone and say: "Honestly, I didn't even proofread for typos before I submitted — so I think that fact is more interesting than my story itself: maybe the ideas that look 'not serious enough' are actually hiding something worth exploring."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak off the cuff, honestly laying bare how I actually create, without pretending to be a hardworking student who prepared meticulously. I smiled into the mic and said: 'Honestly, I wrote this piece one afternoon when the feeling just hit me——didn't even proofread it. I think inspiration is something you can't plan for; you just have to wait for it to come find you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I chose to speak directly, describing my creative process in the most concise and honest way I could — no performed gratitude, no studied humility. Into the microphone I said: 'I wrote this piece off the top of my head three weeks ago. I just wanted to work out a story with a coherent internal logic. Winning means the judges found that logic convincing, and honestly, that's a better outcome than I expected.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to make my position clear calmly but firmly — neither accepting the cash nor letting things explode into a scene. I push the wad of money back across the coffee table and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Uncle, I can't take this. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't about the cost of replacing a hard drive. Those files are gone, and that's that. I understand the kid didn't know any better, but I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to set aside any emotional confrontation and prioritize dealing with the actual loss — the drive itself. I politely but directly push the money back and ask for the drive. I say to my uncle: 'No need for the money — there are some work files on the drive, I need to take it and see if anything can be recovered. Please, make yourself comfortable.' I casually push the stack of bills back onto the coffee table, pick up the drive and the screw, and head to another room to look up data recovery services.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to decline the money gently but clearly, to speak the true weight of what was lost, without letting it escalate into a direct confrontation. I push the stack of bills back toward him, my voice slightly unsteady but without tears, and say: 'Uncle, I can't take the money — that hard drive had six months of my work on it, and there's no replacing it. I just need a little time to get through this.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse the money outright, calmly but clearly laying out the real loss, and giving them no opening to wrap this up with a 'kids overreact' shrug. I push the money back across the table, voice steady: 'This isn't enough. That hard drive held six months of my work — not a few songs. If you want to have a serious conversation about compensation, I'm willing to do that, but this number isn't something I can accept.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold my position in person, not withdraw the materials, but proactively offer a middle path — trying to shift the conversation from confrontation toward joint problem-solving. I say: 'Professor, I understand your concerns, and I'm well aware of the time pressure. But I can't pretend those control data don't exist — I'm not trying to invalidate your framework; I'm asking you to look at where the specific risk points are. Could you give me ten minutes? Let's look together and see whether there's any possibility of making partial adjustments within the current conditions.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand by the evidence and the alternative approach directly, explicitly refusing the suggestion to put away my materials and proceed with the original plan, while keeping my tone focused on the issue rather than emotionally confrontational. I speak up: 'I understand your judgment, but I can't put these materials away — I ran a controlled experiment, the data is right here, and I can go through it point by point right now. If you can show me where my methodology is flawed, I'll correct it immediately. But if the data itself holds up, I need you to seriously consider adjusting the approach. I'm willing to take the risk of a delay on myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to propose a middle path — neither withdrawing the materials nor insisting on an immediate change of course — and instead ask that, while the existing plan moves forward, a small-scale parallel verification be kept in place. I speak up: 'I hear what you're saying, and I understand the pressure of the deadline. I'm not trying to overturn the whole direction — I just want to ask: would it be possible, while we push ahead with the main plan as it stands, to allow me to keep that parallel process as an additional verification? If the data shows no difference in the end, I will fully accept your judgment; but if a discrepancy does appear, we'll have a documented basis to work from. That's also a form of protection for your framework.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to pull back for now, avoid a direct confrontation, but keep the alternative proposal alive — angling for one small opening to compromise rather than giving everything up entirely. I lowered my head slightly and said: 'I understand the timeline math you're talking about, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — I'll get the required materials submitted. But could we hold onto that comparison data for now? Once the phase materials are cleared, I'd like to come back and ask you to take another look at it.'

options.label: A
options.content: Choose to let go of the overseas opportunity and return home immediately to be with Dad and handle the follow-up medical arrangements. Say to Mom: 'Mom, wait for me — I'll be back first thing tomorrow morning. You don't have to carry this alone.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to go back immediately to handle my father's medical affairs, while simultaneously reaching out to the company to negotiate a delay or explore remote options for the overseas opportunity. This isn't a pure emotional sacrifice — I'm treating it as a project that requires my on-site presence. I tell my mother: 'I'll book a ticket tonight and be there tomorrow. In the meantime, have the doctors pull together all the test results — when I arrive, I'll go straight to the attending physician and talk it through myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to go home immediately to deal with Dad's illness, setting the overseas project decision aside for now, and will reach out to the company once the situation becomes clearer. I tell my mother: 'I understand — tell me which hospital Dad is in. I'll look up tickets tonight and take the earliest flight out. You stay calm for now. Once I'm there, I'll go talk to the doctors myself.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to set aside the immediate push on the overseas project and prioritize going home to handle Dad's medical arrangements, while also starting to break down the timelines of both things in search of a middle path. I begin making a list on my phone screen: the earliest train tomorrow morning, contact the attending physician, and at the same time send HR an email asking whether the overseas role allows for a deferred decision — first get the parts I can control into their boxes, then go home.

options.label: A
options.content: She chooses to stand up and share her idea in front of the class, giving a vague, positive response to the suggestion about the speech competition rather than refusing outright. She rises slowly, her voice a little soft but pushing herself to speak clearly: "It's just… using the principle of a seesaw — you put the elephant on one side, then add people one by one to the other side, and by counting the people you can figure out how much the elephant weighs…" When she finishes, she gives the teacher a small nod: "The competition… I — I'll think about it."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea concisely, but politely decline the invitation to join the speech competition. I'll say: 'Teacher, I can walk through this method, but I'd rather not join the speech competition — I don't think it's necessary.' Then I'll lay out the logic of the seesaw weighing method in the most straightforward way I can, without dragging it out, and without caring about the skeptical looks.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stand up without a moment's hesitation, lay out the full explanation in front of everyone, and readily agree to enter the speech competition. I rise to my feet, sweep my eyes across the whole class, and say with a smile: "The principle of the seesaw is simple — put the elephant on one end, then keep stacking people onto the other end until the two sides balance. Count how many people there are and roughly how much each one weighs, add it all up, and that's the elephant's weight. I'll go to the speech competition — thank you, Teacher!"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to quietly decline standing up to explain, and respond to the suggestion about the speech competition with a vague, evasive shake of my head. I lower my gaze and say in a very small voice: "I… I can't really explain it well — teacher, you go ahead and tell them."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to take the initiative and talk to her about collaborating, to try to merge the two proposals, putting the harmony of our relationship ahead of fighting over credit. I tell my friend: "I'll go have a chat with her and see if we can piece the two proposals together — credit can wait, as long as this doesn't blow up into a whole thing in class."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand by my own proposal, campaign independently over the weekend, and not seek a merger with her. I tell my friend: "Forget the merger — I'm not doing anything where the credit can't be sorted out. I'll go talk to people myself this weekend, bring the sketches along, and let them see for themselves."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to campaign independently, stand by the original proposal, and let the idea's own appeal win people over — rather than merging it with another plan just to play it safe. I tell my friend: "I want to try on my own first. This weekend I'll go find people, show them the sketches, let them really picture that space — and if I've made my case and still lose, I'll accept it. But I don't want to gut the whole thing before I've even had a chance to show it."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to merge and not to campaign blindly—instead, I'm going to do a precise read of where the votes actually are. I'll identify the small number of people in the class who genuinely want the show to be good, not the ones who'd be happy just singing a few songs, and focus all my energy on convincing them. I tell my friend: 'Merging is off the table—if we do that, my concept is gone. Help me think: who in the class actually wants to put on a real show? Not the 'whatever, just sing' crowd—starting with those people, I'll go pitch my concept.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to appear outwardly compliant for now in order to remove myself from the immediate threat of violence, while quietly initiating evidence collection and legal proceedings in the background — I will not give up the deposit under any circumstances. I say to him: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight — but the contract and the deposit, we're not done with that. You'd better take a good hard look at those contract terms, because I'm going to have someone verify them.' At the same time, I quietly start recording on my phone.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to comply for now and leave without arguing over the deposit on the spot, but I quietly note every key detail for pursuing my rights afterward. I bend down to pick up my ID and phone from the floor, and say calmly: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight. But the matter of the contract — we'll settle that later.' Then I pick up my luggage and walk out.

options.label: C
options.content: Refuse to leave in silence — instead, force the confrontation into the open, buying time and drawing witnesses. Say out loud: 'Fine, I'm not going anywhere. You're standing on my stuff while you talk to me about a contract — there are plenty of doors in this hallway, and everyone can hear us right now. I'm calling 12345 this second. You say you know people at the precinct? Perfect — then we'll wait for them together, right here in front of everyone, and sort out this contract and this deposit where the whole world can see.' At the same time, pull out the phone and get ready to dial.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to comply for now in order to get out of immediate physical danger, but inwardly I resolve not to give up on recovering the deposit — the moment I leave, I'll contact someone who can help me (a tenant rights hotline, legal aid, or a friend). I lower my head and say, voice trembling but as steady as I can manage: 'Fine, I'll go for tonight — but we're not done talking about the deposit. I'll be handling this through the proper channels.' Then I bend down and start gathering the clothes from the floor, and I stop meeting his eyes.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to go along with the lie, but I step up and take control of the narrative myself — using language that is vague yet truthful, leaving my teammate some breathing room, while quickly proposing a no-model fallback plan for our presentation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say: 'Ms. Chen, the model had an accident on the way to school and couldn't make it here — that's our group's fault. But we've prepared a full verbal explanation, and we're asking for the chance to go up and present.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the model being lost, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately pivot to figuring out how to complete the presentation without it. Looking the homeroom teacher in the eye: 'The model was lost on the way to school — he left it on the bus. Without the model, we'll complete the presentation using a verbal explanation and the board. I'll take the lead.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell the homeroom teacher the truth about the missing model, refuse to go along with the lie, and immediately switch to a no-model presentation. I say to the teacher: 'The model was left on the bus on the way to school. We'll complete the presentation using a verbal explanation — I know the material thoroughly, and we're ready to begin.'

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to go along with the lie, choose to tell the homeroom teacher honestly that the model was lost, and immediately pivot to completing the presentation through verbal, impromptu explanation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but clearly: 'The model was left on the bus on the way to school. We don't have a prop, but we can just talk through it — I'll describe how the solar system works. We don't need the model.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to be honest with my partner about the real situation and my inner fear, while also deciding not to lower my salary floor — and instead to consider a more aggressive path toward starting something of my own or a full pivot. I squeeze her hand and say: "I'm going to be straight with you — I'm scared right now, but I'm not going to go work in a call center. I want to talk to you about a much bigger bet."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to be honest with my partner about the reality of our situation and my own sense of being lost — setting aside, for now, any concrete discussion of job-search limits — and to make sure this night doesn't turn into the two of us retreating into separate silences. I tighten my grip on her hand and say quietly: 'I honestly don't know what the next step looks like right now, but I don't want to carry this alone — what you just said, let's figure it out together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to be honest about the real situation and recalibrate my job-search floor — no soothing platitudes to placate my partner, but no emotional breakdown either. I squeeze her hand and speak: 'I'll be straight with you — the market right now isn't kind to people our age, but I've done the math: the severance can last eight months. I'll adjust my expectations, but I won't just take anything. Let's get the numbers clear first, and your dad's appointment this month — we'll go together.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell my partner the truth and to swallow my pride first — to go after whatever work I can get up to speed on quickly. I say quietly, 'I'm letting go of the old salary standard. Starting tomorrow, I'm applying to warehouse and customer service jobs too.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to propose a middle path — neither withdrawing the materials nor insisting on an immediate change of course — and instead ask that, while the existing plan moves forward, a small-scale parallel verification be kept in place. I speak up: 'I hear what you're saying, and I understand the pressure of the deadline. I'm not trying to overturn the whole direction — I just want to ask: would it be possible, while we push ahead with the main plan as it stands, to allow me to keep that parallel process as an additional verification? If the data shows no difference in the end, I will fully accept your judgment; but if a discrepancy does appear, we'll have a documented basis to work from. That's also a form of protection for your framework.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand by the evidence and the alternative approach directly, explicitly refusing the suggestion to put away my materials and proceed with the original plan, while keeping my tone focused on the issue rather than emotionally confrontational. I speak up: 'I understand your judgment, but I can't put these materials away — I ran a controlled experiment, the data is right here, and I can go through it point by point right now. If you can show me where my methodology is flawed, I'll correct it immediately. But if the data itself holds up, I need you to seriously consider adjusting the approach. I'm willing to take the risk of a delay on myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose neither full retreat nor direct confrontation, but instead try to shift the argument from "who is right and who is wrong" to "how do we reduce the risk"——carving out space for a limited trial of the alternative. I speak up: "I'm not trying to invalidate your system. What I want to ask is: can you allow me, within the existing framework, to run that critical step in parallel using two approaches for a period of time? The data will be right there for you to judge. If the results are the same, I'll follow the original plan and never bring this up again."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to pull back for now, avoid a direct confrontation, but keep the alternative proposal alive — angling for one small opening to compromise rather than giving everything up entirely. I lowered my head slightly and said: 'I understand the timeline math you're talking about, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — I'll get the required materials submitted. But could we hold onto that comparison data for now? Once the phase materials are cleared, I'd like to come back and ask you to take another look at it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to reject this boundary adjustment and explicitly uphold the original commitment. I'll silence my phone first, then look him in the eye and say: 'I don't accept this kind of relationship. If this is what you need, then let's have a real conversation about whether we're still doing this at all.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to set my phone aside for now, to shut out any outside distraction, and to ask him first — to understand what he's really feeling and what exactly he means — rather than immediately saying yes or no. I put down the dish cloth, sit across from him, and say quietly: 'When you say independent space… can you tell me — when did you first start feeling this way?'

options.label: C
options.content: He chooses to reject his partner's proposal, with preserving the existing relationship framework as his immediate priority, and sets his phone aside for the moment. I put down my phone, looked at her, and said, my voice a little tight: 'I don't understand what you mean by that kind of flexibility, but I know I don't want to change the way things are between us right now — if you feel like something isn't enough, just tell me directly, and we can talk about it, but not like this.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to set aside any direct response for now, letting confusion and silence stand in for any real position, while getting distracted by my phone. I looked up at her, stayed quiet for a few seconds, my voice coming out a little hoarse: 'All of this you're saying… I need to think about it.' Then, almost without realizing it, I glanced down at my phone screen as it lit up.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse his 'generosity' right there in front of everyone, and to push back against this humiliation with a cutting remark. I slide the business card back toward him, and with a cold smile I say: 'I appreciate the thought — but I'm much more used to being my own boss. That assistant position of yours? Save it for someone who needs their life managed for them.' Then I turn and walk out of the reception.

options.label: B
options.content: I decline the card, and in front of everyone I flip this so-called 'invitation' into a public counter-strike — letting every person in the room see exactly what is behind that business card. I don't reach out to take it. Instead, I smile slightly, my voice pitched just loud enough for two or three tables to catch every word: 'Generous enough? Twelve years ago you put your name on my proposal — now that was generous. Keep the assistant position. It's not for me — I've never been good at signing other people's names for them.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse him calmly, in front of everyone — not taking the card, not tearing into him directly, but making sure every person in earshot hears the 'no' clearly. I step back half a pace, leaving his hand suspended in the air, and say in a voice that is neither raised nor lowered: 'I appreciate the thought, but I'm very happy where I am right now — no need for a change.' Then I pick up my orange juice and walk toward my friend.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse him publicly, giving him no emotional reaction to exploit, letting the whole performance collapse in plain sight with nothing but calm. I glanced down at the card, then looked up, my voice as level as if I were stating a simple fact: 'No need. For the assistant position, I'd suggest finding someone who doesn't know how you got to where you are — it'll save you a lot of headaches.' Then I picked up my orange juice and walked away.

options.label: A
options.content: I've decided to file a real-name report with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name, and to make my position clear to the neighbor face-to-face: if you support this, that support needs to be real action. I understand if you're not willing to co-sign, but don't use 'why bother' to strip this thing of its meaning. I say to the old neighbor: 'Brother, I'm going to file the report — putting my name down alone is fine. But I want you to remember: tonight, you said with your own mouth that none of this was a problem. If anyone asks about it down the road, I hope you'll tell the truth. Two million yuan is not a small thing. I'm not doing this for a few thousand bucks — I just don't want the people in this complex to think that if someone steals, they steal, and nobody does a damn thing about it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau as an individual, while responding honestly to my neighbors' stance — accepting their behind-the-scenes support, but not hiding my disappointment in this collective silence. I say to the teacher upstairs: "I understand about the joint letter — I'm not going to force your names onto it. But I want you to know: this isn't just my business, it's all of our money. I'm going to file the complaint; help me fill in the supporting materials, but I won't be stopping this."

options.label: C
options.content: I have decided to file a formal, named complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name, while making clear to my neighbors that I understand their reluctance but will not accept the advice to 'just let it go.' I said to the teacher upstairs: 'I understand your difficulties completely, and I'm not going to drag you into this. I'll handle it myself. If you're willing to help me track down materials, I'm grateful; if not, I won't hold it against you. But this name on the complaint — that has to be mine.'

options.label: D
options.content: I've decided to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name — but I'm making it clear to the neighbors: quiet support is fine, but I'm not out here taking all the risk for everyone for nothing. I'll keep everyone posted as things develop. To the retired teacher, I said: "I understand why you won't sign your names — but I've already done the digging, and I'm going to file this complaint. However you want to help, that's up to you. Just don't expect me to carry the whole thing by myself and call it done."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to actively respond to his suggestion of "going out for a walk together" — to break the stalemate and keep us from drifting apart. I gave a small, quiet smile and said: "Let's go. You mentioned going out for a walk just now — I think that's a good idea. Let's go together."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to be the one who breaks the stalemate — to acknowledge that this strangeness is real, and to suggest we go out for a walk together. I say: 'Come on, let's go get some air — sitting here isn't going to bring any answers anyway.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to respond — to tuck my need inside a suggestion, phrased lightly, as if it just slipped out, not as if I'm scared. I lift my head, my voice steadier than I expected: "Maybe… let's go out for a walk together — I don't really know what I'd do with myself today anyway."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to sidestep this shared strangeness — to gently acknowledge that it exists, rather than each of us retreating back into the familiar busyness. I look down at the table and say: "This does feel strange. How about… we have a cup of coffee together first? No need to think about what comes next."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to go straight home — I give myself some time alone to absorb the impact of this — but I don't allow myself to fall apart completely, because falling apart solves nothing. I flip my phone face-down onto the passenger seat, start the car, and drive to a nearby parking lot. I sit there, staring at the dark windshield, and say quietly: "This variable can never be verified, so it shouldn't be grounds for invalidating the existing data." But I know I don't entirely believe it.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stay at the intersection — refusing to drive straight home and pretend everything is normal, but also refusing to completely fall apart — and instead try to treat these twenty years as a text that needs to be reread rather than a mistake that needs to be negated. I didn't start the engine. I turned my phone face-down on the passenger seat and said quietly to myself: 'This isn't a conclusion. This is just a chapter I haven't finished reading yet.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stop at the intersection for a few minutes and give myself a moment alone — neither falling apart nor pretending nothing happened, just letting the feeling settle before I drive home. I turn my phone face-down, close my eyes, take a slow breath, and tell myself: 'Go home first. Just go home tonight. I'll deal with this later.'

options.label: D
options.content: He chooses to break the silence and reach out rather than suppress it alone or drive home and pretend everything is normal. He dials an old friend's number, his voice a little rough: 'I'm stuck at a red light right now — there's something I've been sitting on for a long time. Do you have a few minutes to talk?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to handle the medical decision remotely, then attend the launch as planned. First I press the doctors to spell out the risks clearly over the phone and confirm the treatment plan directly; then I notify the CEO that I'll be there on time.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to go to the hospital immediately, to give up attending the launch, and to place my father's life before any professional cost. Choking up on the CEO's video call, I say: "I'm so sorry — my father is on the operating table right now, I have to get to the hospital to sign the consent forms. Please find a way to manage the launch without me. I am truly, deeply sorry."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to go to the hospital immediately, giving up hosting the launch in person, while quickly mobilizing resources to arrange a substitute. I call the CEO back, my voice faster and louder than usual: 'My father is on the operating table — I have to get to the hospital to sign the forms, I'm leaving now. Put the deputy director on, I'll direct remotely from the car. The launch cannot stop, but this signature can only come from me.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to abandon the launch event and go to the hospital immediately to sign the medical authorization, while activating the emergency contingency plan to have the team take over as the main presenter. I call the CEO and say: 'My father has gone into a critical situation during surgery — I need to be there in person to sign right now. Please arrange a backup presenter. I'll provide remote support if at all possible. I'm sorry.' Then I hang up and tell the driver to go straight to the hospital.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to reply to my colleague's message, but keep my wording restrained and maintain distance — not crossing any emotional boundary, responding to the feeling of being seen in a way that stays safe. I write back: "Made it home. That point you mentioned — I've always found it interesting too. That bookstore really is quiet; worth a visit sometime. Good night."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to reply, but with a tone that is warm yet gently distancing — drawing this connection softly to a close without rejecting him outright or letting it go any deeper. I write: "Made it home. I found myself thinking about some of what we talked about that evening during the meeting today. That bookstore really is wonderfully quiet — you should go sometime when you get the chance. Good night."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to reply to the message, but I proactively narrow the emotional boundaries, steering the conversation back onto a professional and friendly track without leaving room for the other person to go deeper. I reply: 'Made it home. Glad that idea was useful to you. The bookstore is worth a visit if you get the chance — very quiet.' I put the phone down after sending it and go check the schedule for the kid's tutoring.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to reply to my colleague's message for now — cutting off the bud with self-attack, pressing the longing back down. I darken the phone screen, pick up my partner's message, reply with a single "okay," then close both chat windows and stare at the blurred night outside the car window, telling myself: you could never hold something like this together.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to answer the call but brush it off vaguely — "everything's fine, nothing major" — and leave it at that until the follow-up results come in. I picked up, keeping my voice as steady as I could: "Nothing to worry about, just a couple of minor indicators a little off — the doctor said to just keep an eye on it. Don't stress, we'll talk when I'm back Friday."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to answer the call, but I gloss over the test results and wait until the follow-up gives me something definitive before saying more. I pick up the video call and, keeping my voice steady, say: 'The results came in — two markers need a follow-up check. The doctor said it's just routine screening, probably nothing serious. I've already started making arrangements. Don't worry about it on your end; just focus on the trip.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I keep things vague for now — I'm not going to drop the worst of it on her yet. Once I pick up, I tell her: two of my markers came back elevated, the doctor wants me to get a follow-up as soon as possible, and the moment those results are in, you'll be the first to know.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to answer the call, but gloss over the checkup results, planning to tell the full truth after the follow-up. I press the answer button and say: 'The results came back — there are a few indicators that need to be confirmed again. Let me get through the follow-up next week and then I'll fill you in properly. How are things going on your end in Hangzhou?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to put down my chopsticks, leave my friends' table, and walk toward the new girl in the corner. I carry my tray over and sit down across from her, saying: 'My name's Xiaopéngyǒu — are you eating alone? I came to keep you company.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table for the moment, pick up my tray, and walk over to the new student sitting alone in the corner. I tell my friends, 'Go ahead — I'll come find you in a bit,' then make my way to that little table against the wall and sit down across from her, saying softly: 'Is it okay if I sit here? My name is… what's yours?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table and walk over to the corner to talk to the new student — no emotional performance, just information. I pick up my tray, stand up, and tell my friends, 'You guys go ahead, I'm going to sit over there for a bit,' then walk to the corner and sit down across from her, and ask directly: 'I'm guessing you don't recognize most of the dishes here — let me tell you which ones are actually worth eating.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to leave the lively crowded table, pick up my tray, and walk over to the new student sitting alone in the corner — not forcing myself to seem warm or outgoing, just going over and sitting down. I carry my tray to that small table, set it down gently, and say quietly: 'Is it okay if I sit here?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to proactively approach her about merging our plans, with preserving the creative core as the priority, and letting go of my fixation on who gets the credit. I walk back into the classroom, find her, and say with a smile: 'Your concept is really fun — I wanted to chat and see if we could combine the two, make something that has a strong theme but still feels light and easy.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose to proactively approach the other person about collaboration, merging the two proposals together, and abandon the route of campaigning solo for votes. Say to my friend: 'Let's go talk to her — her plan solves the rehearsal problem, my plan has the content, and the two things can be pieced together. The credit doesn't matter; what matters is that the plan actually gets made.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to step away from the competition — I won't go talk to her about merging, and I won't spend the weekend canvassing votes. I'll let the proposal lose on its own terms. I tell my friend: "Forget it — if everyone likes her proposal, they should go with it. I'll keep mine for myself."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to campaign independently, stand by the original proposal, and let the idea's own appeal win people over — rather than merging it with another plan just to play it safe. I tell my friend: "I want to try on my own first. This weekend I'll go find people, show them the sketches, let them really picture that space — and if I've made my case and still lose, I'll accept it. But I don't want to gut the whole thing before I've even had a chance to show it."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to honestly express my assessment of the academic opportunity and to formally reply to my advisor accepting the invitation. I'll say: 'I've thought it through. This opportunity is the right call logically, and I'm going to take it. As for the life logistics — I'll handle them. But they're not a reason to walk away from this.'

options.label: B
options.content: She chooses to honestly express her genuine longing for this academic opportunity, while sincerely receiving his concerns and inviting him into a shared conversation rather than making a unilateral decision; she holds off on replying to her advisor's email for now, and first opens up to the person beside her. She gently takes his hand and says: "I've been staring at this email because I really do want to go — I know it would change a lot of things, and I know you have reservations, and I want to listen to all of them seriously. Can we talk tonight — not me trying to convince you, but the two of us figuring it out together?"

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to honestly share my genuine assessment of the academic opportunity, while bringing the question of impact on our family into the decision-making framework, and proposing that we map out the specific variables together before drawing any conclusions. I say to him: 'I really am thinking seriously about this. The salary, the pace, the effect on our life — those are all real questions. We can lay them out one by one and look at them together. There's no rush to decide right now, but I want to get the full picture first before I write back to my advisor.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honestly express how deeply I want this academic opportunity, while actively inviting my partner to work through the possibilities together, rather than pushing ahead on my own. I turn toward her, tilt the laptop screen so she can see it, and say: 'I'm not running away — I just haven't felt like this in a really long time. Can you let me tell you what this opportunity means to me? And then we can figure out what to do together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to keep it to myself for now, go to the follow-up appointment alone to confirm the results, and only decide whether to tell my family once I have more information. I press the phone screen dark, quietly note the follow-up appointment time in my memo app, then walk out of the study and tell my family I'm a little tired tonight and am turning in early.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to be honest as soon as possible, but without framing it as a foregone conclusion. I'll schedule a follow-up appointment first, then bring my family together and tell them: 'I've found something abnormal, and I need us to deal with it right away — together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to keep this to myself for now, prioritize getting definitive information, and defer any emotional trigger until there are facts to back it up. I wake the phone screen, go straight to the hospital's appointment system, lock in the earliest available follow-up slot, then flip the phone face-down on the desk and open my work files.

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to tell my family — but I choose to bring it up in a calm, measured way, without amplifying the fear, laying out the facts clearly rather than making it sound catastrophic. Tonight, after the kids are asleep, I set down my phone, walk over to my partner, and say quietly: 'One of my results from today's check-up needs a follow-up — I don't know what it means yet, but I wanted you to know. We'll face it together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold off on giving a definitive answer, buying time on the grounds that I need to sit down properly with my family and my child, and drawing everyone involved into the conversation. I say to the teacher: 'Thank you so much — it truly means a great deal that you see my child this way. I'd like to sit down with my child and the rest of the family first, hear what they think, and then make a decision. Could you let me know the latest date by which you'd need to hear back from me?'

options.label: B
options.content: I decided not to express immediate support or opposition to the special recruitment, and instead to return the decision to my child first, while buying time for the whole family to discuss it together. I said to the teacher: 'Thank you — this is very significant news for us. Before we make any decision, I want to genuinely hear what my child himself has to say. This is ultimately his path to walk, and I want him to walk it because he truly wants to, not because of our expectations. Could you give us a little time?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to support my child in pursuing the professional path, while returning the decision to him — driving the conversation with information rather than emotion. I say to the teacher: 'Thank you for telling me. I'll go through everything in this brochure with him — the full picture, the pros and the cons — and let him decide for himself. But I'm not going to hide this option from him.'

options.label: D
options.content: I've made up my mind to support the professional path — clearly and without reservation — while insisting that we call my kid out here right now for a three-way conversation, so he can say what he wants in his own words. I tell the teacher: "Personally, I'm in favor — but we need to ask him directly, right now. Can we bring him out here so the three of us can talk this through together?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to confront the conflict head-on, right here and now — not suppressing my emotions, not running from the relationship — staying to face it together, even with anger and tenderness pulling at me at the same time. I crouch down, lift her hands away from my pant leg, and hold them. My voice comes out a little rough: 'Get up. I can't hear you when you're kneeling like that. Then I want you to tell me everything, from the beginning, one thing at a time. I want to know all of it. I'm angry — but I'm not going anywhere.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold my emotions in check for now — no immediate decision to leave, no immediate forgiveness — and instead ask him to lay everything out. I crouch down and say, in a voice that's quiet but steady: 'Get up first. Then tell me everything — how much is still owed, who these people are, what the situation looks like right now. All of it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to accept this explanation, and I'm not continuing as though things are the same between us. I gently moved her aside and said plainly that we're separating our finances for now — tomorrow we sit down and go through every account, every transaction, and where we each stand.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold down the anger for now and catch my partner in his collapse first — but I make it clear that everything about this has to be laid out in full, and we can't just let it pass. I bend down and pull him up from the floor, my voice trembling but I say it anyway: "Get up first. Sit down. Tell me everything from the beginning — how much is still owed, whether those people are going to come back, how your parents are doing right now. I'm in a lot of pain, but I need to know what we're actually facing first."

options.label: A
options.content: I didn't promise to keep it secret. Instead, I put the focus on just being there with her — trying to make sure she didn't feel abandoned, while leaving room to seek help later. I crouched down and said quietly: 'I'm not going to go around telling everyone — but I also can't promise to do nothing. Can you tell me what's actually been going on lately?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to promise secrecy—but not by turning away and betraying her. Instead, I stay by her side and tell her honestly that I can't make that promise, while trying to think through with her how to find help. I crouch down, hold her hands, voice trembling but not running: 'I can't promise you that—not because I don't care about you, but because I care too much. Let's find someone who can actually help you, together—just the two of us figuring out how to do it first, okay?'

options.label: C
options.content: I didn't promise to keep the secret on the spot. Instead I chose to steady her emotions first — neither exposing her nor flatly refusing — while inside I had already decided I had to find an adult who could help her. I held her hand, my voice trembling, and said: "I'm not going to go around telling people — but I need you to help me figure this out together, how we can get you to a place where you don't have to do this anymore. I can't carry this by myself. I need someone to help both of us."

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to promise secrecy, and instead choose to be honest — telling her I will seek help from an adult, while doing everything I can to stay by her side and not leave. I say quietly: 'I can't make that promise — not because I don't care about you, but because I'm scared something will happen to you. Let's go find a teacher together right now. I'll be with you the whole time.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to express conditional interest, but explicitly refuse to be pushed into a position by time pressure when I don't have enough information. I say directly: 'I'm genuinely interested in taking a serious look at the project — but I need to see proof that the angel-round funding has closed, a complete list of the core team, and your answer to why the CTO has to be me specifically. I'll give you my answer by Tuesday, but that answer is contingent on you getting me that information by tomorrow.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to politely decline the invitation while buying myself some breathing room — I won't agree on the spot, but I won't burn the bridge either; I'll use 'I need to think it over seriously' to extract myself from this pressure cooker first. I pick up my Americano, take a sip, set it back down, and look up at him: 'I hear what you're saying, and I've taken it all in — but this is too big a decision for me to make right now. Let me go away and think it through properly. I'll give you an answer by Tuesday.'

options.label: C
options.content: I made my strong interest and inclination clear on the spot — no attempt to hide it — but I didn't say 'I'm in' outright; instead I caught the ball and kept it in my own hands. I told my senior: "I don't need until Tuesday — I want to dig into the product right now. Walk me through this prototype; I've already got ideas forming."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay open and actively seek more information, rather than committing or declining on the spot. I say: 'Everything you're describing genuinely excites me — but there are a few things I need to get clear on first. What's your core thesis about the education space, and what are the potholes you've already hit behind this product logic? Give me a few days. I want to actually understand this, not just get swept along by an equity number.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to go to school first to finish my responsibilities, then head to the birthday outing — I'll do both, and I'm not giving either one up. I tell Mom: 'I'm happy, but tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — it'll take half an hour, tops, and then we can head out. It won't hold us up for long.'

options.label: B
options.content: I decide to do both — wake up early, go to school first to take care of my responsibilities, then head to the amusement park. I tell Mom: 'I'm in! But I have to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the keys, and I made a promise. I'll go early and be back early. If I leave at seven-thirty, I should make it in time!'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to do both, planning to go to school first thing Saturday morning to water the mimosa and feed the tadpoles, then head over to the amusement park for my cousin's birthday party. I tell Mom: 'I really want to go! But the teacher gave me the key — the mimosa needs watering and the tadpoles need feeding. I'll go to school first thing tomorrow morning and get it all done. I should be there by nine-thirty at the latest — that's plenty of time. How about you all head out first and I'll catch up?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take care of the watering and tadpole-feeding at school first, then decide afterward whether to head over to the gathering — but I'm not committing to showing up. I tell Mom: 'Tomorrow I have to go to school first to water the plants. The teacher gave me the keys — the mimosa was just repotted and it can't wait. Once I'm done I'll see if I can make it over, but I might not be in time. Can you let Uncle know for me?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stay at my current school and hold onto the most stable path forward for my graduate studies. I send her a message saying, 'I'm not going north — and I don't want to keep stringing you along with empty words.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to give up my acceptance here and take the offer from the university up north — and tonight I give my partner a clear answer. I sent back a voice message: 'I'm coming to you. It's already decided. Wait for me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school — I won't switch institutions as a concession to save the relationship. I send a message explaining my decision, and I'm honest: if they can't continue under these circumstances, I respect that we part ways.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the offer at my own school — I won't redirect north for the relationship. I text her back and tell her: I've thought about this seriously. I love you, but I can't prove that love by changing course.

options.label: A
options.content: Choose partial compromise while holding the line: make it clear to the director that the weekend trip home is not being canceled, but offer to put in extra hours tonight to push the project forward, provide remote support on the critical sections over the weekend, and join a video call Sunday evening for the final-draft review. Say directly: 'I have a family commitment this weekend — it was locked in three days ago and I can't cancel it. I can stay late tonight and I'm available remotely over the weekend, but coming into the office isn't something I can do. Tell me which parts are the biggest bottlenecks and let's work out the priorities together.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to explain the situation at home and hold firm on going back, while offering a compromise to soften the blow of saying no. I tell the director: 'My dad has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend — I promised three days ago that I'd go with my parents, so there's really no way I can change that. But I can put together a written document tonight with the competitive market analysis section and send it to you. Maybe you could find someone to work off that for the redesign? Or I could do a remote review of the final draft Sunday evening?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to propose a compromise on the spot — neither agreeing outright nor refusing — and proactively explain my family situation while pushing for a flexible arrangement. I say to the director: 'I will absolutely carry this project — but there's something at home I need to be upfront about. My dad has a follow-up appointment this weekend, and I promised to go with him. I'll head back Saturday to take care of that, then come back the same evening or first thing Sunday morning. Whatever time I have left, I'll pour everything into the proposal — submitting Sunday night is no problem. Does that work for you?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to agree immediately, but also not to refuse outright — instead I try to propose a middle-ground arrangement, asking whether I could handle part of the work remotely or negotiate with the director to have certain modules shared with a colleague. I say: 'Director, I do have something I need to take care of at home this weekend — my father has a follow-up appointment, and I promised I'd go with him. I wanted to ask: would it be possible for me to pull together the market benchmarking section tonight, work remotely on Saturday, and then come into the office Sunday to go over the final draft? Or is there any chance a colleague could take on the slide redesign portion?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to engage by asking questions — approaching this with curiosity rather than direct confrontation or silent compliance — in order to open up space for real dialogue within the execution framework. I say: 'My objection isn't that we can't change things — but I want to ask you first: what specific observations about the existing process is this proposal based on? Because some of those steps were designed the way they were to solve particular problems. If you know which problems, we can figure out together whether the new process actually covers them.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to appear cooperative on the surface while quietly buying time — raising risk details as a way of creating friction without direct confrontation. I speak up: 'I hear you on the direction, and I'll follow through on execution — but there are a few risk points in the transition period you may not have seen yet. Let me write them up; take a look and decide whether we should go over them first, so if something does go wrong later, nobody's left scrambling to explain it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to challenge the logical gaps in the proposal directly, on the spot — not attacking his authority as such, but forcing him to back it up with data. I flip the proposal open, point to the timeline, and say: 'I do have an objection — let's start with one: how were the transition-period timelines determined? If there's no actual measurement based on the existing process, I can't guarantee the rollout won't run into problems, and you won't get the results you're after either.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose conditional cooperation — working within the new direction while using the execution details to carve out some breathing room. I won't challenge the big-picture direction head-on, but I'll lay out every key risk point, one by one. I flip open the proposal and speak calmly: 'I understand the overall direction, and I'll personally oversee the transition. But there are a few specific points I want to walk through — it's not a question of whether we can make the changes, it's about which parts of the rollout are going to break first if we push forward this way. We need to think through how we're going to catch those before they happen.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up, but in a private, peer-to-peer way rather than as a public critique. First, I reply to my colleague's message: 'I feel the same way — let's think together about how to bring it up.' Then I send the young colleague a private message: 'Seeing your project update, a few details reminded me of something from my own early career. If you'd ever like to talk it through, I'm happy to share — though of course it's just one perspective; you know your situation far better than I do.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose a light-touch intervention over silence — leave a door open through open-ended questions rather than pouring experience over him. Send the young colleague a private message: 'Saw your project — there's a detail that brought some interesting questions to mind for me. If you ever feel like talking through certain nodes in the process architecture, I'm around whenever — purely out of curiosity, not advice.' And reply to my colleague: 'I feel the same way, but this is his exploration. We can just be here when he wants to talk.'

options.label: C
options.content: I've decided to reach out directly via private message, pointing out the specific structural risk points in the process — no emotional preamble, no 'just for your reference' disclaimer tacked on at the end. And I'll reply to my colleague: 'I'll say it directly. You don't need to get involved.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to avoid commenting publicly, and instead respond to my colleague through the relatively safer channel of a private message, without directly engaging with the younger colleague's post. Private message reply: 'I saw it too — I'm a little worried, but it doesn't feel right to say something outright. If you think there's a problem, do you want to talk it through first?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stall and respond vaguely to dodge the camping invitation — neither accepting nor explicitly declining — buying myself some time. I reply: 'It sounds absolutely gorgeous! I just have something on my end this week that I need to check the timing on — I'll let you know for sure in a day or two, you guys go ahead and make your plans in the meantime~ '

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to politely decline the camping invitation, deflecting with a specific but not over-explained work excuse, while keeping a normal, friendly tone — no cracks, no unnecessary distance. My reply: 'Oh that sounds absolutely gorgeous! I've got a project milestone I need to wrap up this weekend and I'm worried I won't be able to get away last-minute — you all go and have fun, and make sure to include me next time!'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to make up an excuse and politely decline the camping invitation — neither giving away the secret nor getting dragged into a situation that feels suffocating. Reply to her: 'We've got a stock inventory at the shop this weekend, so I'm afraid I can't get away — next time for sure! Hope you all have a blast, let's plan something soon!'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to accept the camping invitation and respond with surface-level enthusiasm, but leave myself a vague out — while quietly working to make sure the four of us never actually go. I write back: "That sounds amazing! The guesthouse must have such a beautiful view — let me just check my schedule on this end, you guys lock in the details first, and I'll get back to you as soon as I can!"

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to hold his emotions in check for the moment—letting the child set down the milk and leave first—then calls him back alone afterward and, calmly but directly, lays that note out on the table. He doesn't pick up the thread of the boy's cheerful talk; he waits until the glass is set down, then says: 'Sit down. There's something I need to talk to you about.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold back and not expose everything in this moment, but I decide that tonight I will find a quiet moment alone with him to talk through this face to face — and to stop him from giving the speech at the commendation ceremony and from registering for the competition. I take the milk from him, set down what I'm holding, and say calmly: 'Let's put the speech aside for now. There's something important we need to talk about tonight — come find me in the study when you're done eating.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold it in for now, steady my emotions, and wait until he's gone before finding the right moment to talk to him alone. I took the milk from him, glanced down at it for a moment, and said quietly: 'Let's talk about the speech later — go wash up first. I have something I want to say to you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — not to expose him on the spot — but to use a single question to plant a crack inside him, leaving room for a deeper conversation later. I take the milk from him, look down at it for a moment, then lift my head and say calmly: 'Let's set the speech aside for now. I have a question I want to ask you — this exam, do you feel like you were truly ready for it? Can you walk me through how you studied for the math?'

options.label: A

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to use the answer sheet, and I turn down my friend's request — putting the paper back where it came from or handing it to the teacher. I shake my head at my friend and say: "We can't use this — put it back. If anyone finds out, we're in serious trouble. Let's just go study on our own."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend and either hand the paper to the teacher or put it back where it came from. I whisper to her, "We can't use this — we'll get caught," and then, while she's not paying attention, I slip the paper back into the crack in the desk or hand it to the teacher, pretending I just happened to find it by accident.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to use the answer sheet, and I turn down my friend's request as well, deciding to return the paper to the class rep or put it back where I found it. I tell my friend: 'This isn't ours — I'm going to give it back. If you want to study together, we could go to the library after school. options.label: A

options.label: B

options.label: C

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to answer the call but brush it off vaguely — "everything's fine, nothing major" — and leave it at that until the follow-up results come in. I picked up, keeping my voice as steady as I could: "Nothing to worry about, just a couple of minor indicators a little off — the doctor said to just keep an eye on it. Don't stress, we'll talk when I'm back Friday."

options.label: A

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and express my disagreement, but I frame it as offering supplementary information rather than a direct refutation, leaving the teacher as much room as possible. options.label: C
options.content: I chose to defer to authority and avoid dissent, responding to the teacher's call on me in a vague, noncommittal way rather than expressing what I actually thought. I stood up, head lowered, and said: 'Teacher, I… I understand, no questions.' Then I slowly sat back down and quietly closed my notebook, sliding it under my textbook.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up and respond, but I wrap my disagreement in an extremely cautious, deferential register — framing the challenge inside a shell of 'I may have misunderstood' to reduce the risk of being attacked. I stand, my voice slightly trembling, eyes still lowered: 'Professor, I came across a book about this period of history last week — it cited some primary source documents, and it seemed like… there might be a small discrepancy with the conclusion you mentioned. Is it possible I've misread it? Could you help me work through it?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to tell the truth about who was wearing the badge, but I won't frame myself as anyone's follower. I say directly who I saw wearing it, and I also state plainly, right there in the room, that he just threatened me — don't count me in as an accomplice.

options.label: B
options.content: He chooses to tell the truth, refusing to cover for the one who brought the cards, and ignores the threat entirely. Looking the director straight in the eye, he says: 'He's the one who brought them in. I saw it. That's all there is to it — it has nothing to do with me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to state the facts I personally witnessed — only what I saw, no interpretation, not one word more or less on anyone's behalf. I open my mouth and say: 'What I saw is that the deck of cards was taken out of his bag. Beyond that, I don't know.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the truth about who brought the cards, but only the part I personally witnessed. I'll quietly give the director the name and walk him through what happened, and I'll also tell him that the other student just threatened me.

options.label: A

Whoever has time can pitch in; if you don't, that's fine. As for what shape the event ends up in — we'll see when we get there."

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to cancel outright, and instead propose a scaled-down plan: cut the high-difficulty elements, redistribute responsibilities, and reshape the event into a smaller but genuinely executable version. I speak up: "We're not canceling — but we do need to redesign this. The president's gone, so we turn it into something smaller. Each of you takes on one thing you can actually handle, I'll coordinate the whole thing, and we get through it together."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take over and re-break down the tasks, pushing the event forward with minimal manpower, while making each member's baseline responsibilities explicit. I speak up: 'Canceling isn't the optimal solution — we can't just let everything we've already put in go to zero. I'll coordinate what's left, but each of you has to claim at least one concrete task; I can't have it all fall on me alone. Tell me — which one of you has time tonight to check in with the sponsors and confirm their status?'

options.label: D
options.content: I decide not to cancel the event, but I propose a compromise: split the remaining tasks, explicitly assign the parts that require external communication (confirming sponsors, contacting guest speakers) to whichever members are still willing to stay, and take on the parts I can handle independently myself (the venue layout plan, organizing the volunteer schedule). I speak up: 'We're not cancelling. Whoever can make phone calls, go make them — I'll handle the scheduling and the venue plan. We work separately. Ten days is enough.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to state the facts I personally witnessed — only what I saw, no interpretation, not one word more or less on anyone's behalf. I open my mouth and say: 'What I saw is that the deck of cards was taken out of his bag. Beyond that, I don't know.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to cooperate with the vice principal to a limited degree — I'll say who brought the cards in, but I won't volunteer anything extra or embellish. I keep my head down and speak: 'It was Chen in the back row — he brought them in. I saw it, but I wasn't part of it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to tell the dean of discipline the truth — I would not lie for anyone — and I kept to the facts as much as possible without adding any judgment of my own. I spoke steadily: 'It was the student in the back row who brought them in. I saw it. I wasn't part of the card game.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the truth about who brought the cards, but only the part I personally witnessed. I'll quietly give the director the name and walk him through what happened, and I'll also tell him that the other student just threatened me.

options.label: A

I flip the phone face-down on the desk, open the hospital app to look up the nearest available appointment for a follow-up, and murmur quietly: 'I'll say something once I know for sure.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep it to myself for now, go to the follow-up appointment alone to confirm the results, and only decide whether to tell my family once I have more information. I press the phone screen dark, quietly note the follow-up appointment time in my memo app, then walk out of the study and tell my family I'm a little tired tonight and am turning in early.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to be honest as soon as possible, but without framing it as a foregone conclusion. I'll schedule a follow-up appointment first, then bring my family together and tell them: 'I've found something abnormal, and I need us to deal with it right away — together.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to keep this to myself for now, prioritize getting definitive information, and defer any emotional trigger until there are facts to back it up. I wake the phone screen, go straight to the hospital's appointment system, lock in the earliest available follow-up slot, then flip the phone face-down on the desk and open my work files.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to step in — but in a way that puts protecting her first, without pushing for a formal report just yet. I place my hand over hers, my voice shaking a little, but I speak anyway: 'You don't have to carry this alone tonight. That meeting tomorrow — I'm going to figure out a way to make sure it doesn't happen. But first, tell me everything he's done.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to intervene directly, to take on whatever professional risk that entails, and not to push the burden back onto Lin Xiaoyu to carry alone. I take her hand and say: 'You're not in this by yourself. You don't have to walk into that meeting tomorrow — I'll figure something out. We'll find a safe way to handle this together.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to take a stand and commit to getting involved — but to move carefully rather than charge straight into a confrontation. I set down my teacup and looked at her: 'The fact that you came to me today means you trust me. That meeting tomorrow — you're not going alone. I'll figure something out. We'll document this first, and then we take it one step at a time.'

options.label: D
options.content: I decide to step in and accept the professional risk — to help Lin Xiaoyu move this forward in a structured way, rather than stopping at simply listening. I tell her: "The moment you came to me today, this stopped being just your problem alone. Don't go to that meeting tomorrow by yourself — I'll figure something out. First, let's get everything you've experienced down in writing: the dates, the locations, exactly what happened each time, laid out point by point. That's the foundation for whatever we do next."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and go alone — I fold it shut quickly and press it under my textbook, smile at my friend and say 'It's nothing, just some random scribbling,' and after school I head to the big banyan tree on the field by myself.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hide what the note says and avoid showing up — just push the chaos down for now. I shoved the note into my textbook and smiled at my best friend: 'It's nothing — someone from the next class asking to borrow my notes.'
