options.label: C
options.content: I choose to quickly hide the note so my best friend can't see it, and I decide to go to the meeting alone after school. I close my fingers around the note, palm it away, and smile at her: 'It's nothing — a classmate asking me for a favor, really boring stuff. Did you finish your homework?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hide the contents of the note and brush off my best friend for now, while inwardly I've already decided to go to the meeting alone after school — but I need to deal with these two things separately first. I shoved the note into my textbook, looked up, and gave my best friend a smile: 'It's nothing, someone just asking to borrow my notes — totally boring.' My voice was steady, my eyes didn't waver.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand by the evidence and the alternative approach directly, explicitly refusing the suggestion to put away my materials and proceed with the original plan, while keeping my tone focused on the issue rather than emotionally confrontational. I speak up: 'I understand your judgment, but I can't put these materials away — I ran a controlled experiment, the data is right here, and I can go through it point by point right now. If you can show me where my methodology is flawed, I'll correct it immediately. But if the data itself holds up, I need you to seriously consider adjusting the approach. I'm willing to take the risk of a delay on myself.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to pull back for now, avoid a direct confrontation, but keep the alternative proposal alive — angling for one small opening to compromise rather than giving everything up entirely. I lowered my head slightly and said: 'I understand the timeline math you're talking about, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — I'll get the required materials submitted. But could we hold onto that comparison data for now? Once the phase materials are cleared, I'd like to come back and ask you to take another look at it.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose a middle path — neither withdrawing the brief entirely nor pushing back in a direct, forceful confrontation — and instead request a small-scale partial verification at minimal cost. Say aloud: 'I understand your concerns; time and conditions are real constraints. But would you allow me, while moving the existing plan forward, to run a parallel comparison on one very small sub-component — it won't affect the main timeline, and if the data shows no difference, I'll drop this matter completely.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold my ground on the core issue while proactively offering a middle path — neither full confrontation nor complete retreat. I speak up: 'I hear what you're saying about the timeline and the conditions, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — but the failure points in those cases overlap heavily with our own critical steps, and I can't look the other way. Could you give me one chance — just that one step — to run a round with my control approach and let the data speak? I'll keep pushing forward on everything else according to the original direction.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to push down the urge to confront her, getting through this moment with silence and avoidance, not letting her see that I already know about the screenshot. I lower my head, force the tears back, and say quietly, "I'm fine, probably just didn't sleep well," then look away and pretend to flip through my textbook.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose silence and suppression — no confrontation, no questions. I lower my head and stare at the desk to get through this moment. I look away, eyes fixed on the tabletop, saying nothing, my hand wrapped around my phone resting on my lap, waiting for her to finish laughing.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation, not here, not yet. I'll wait until I've calmly worked out what outcome I actually want before deciding on a next step. I turn off my phone screen, look up, give her one expressionless glance, then drop my eyes and open my textbook.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to confront her directly — to put the screenshot and the side group chat out in the open, no holding back and no dancing around it. I turn toward her, lower my voice, but speak with complete directness: "I saw that group chat. I saw what you said. Can you tell me — what exactly is going on?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse to put the colleague who just returned from maternity leave on the list — I won't go along with my superior's arrangement, and I'll bear whatever consequences come. I kept my head down and stayed silent for a moment, then spoke: 'I can't write this list — her performance review is a B+, I have no grounds to put her on it, and I can't sign off on this.'

options.label: B
options.content: Refuse to include the colleague on maternity leave in the list, and state my reasons directly — no confrontation, no compromise. I tell my supervisor: 'I can't write that her performance failed to meet expectations on the list. Her evaluation is a B+ — that's the fact on record, and I can't sign off on something that contradicts it. I understand the situation with the relative, but for the other slot I need to go by actual performance. If you can point me to an employee rated C, I'll handle the paperwork for that.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose not to write my colleague — the one who just returned from maternity leave — onto the list, but I didn't confront my boss head-on either. Instead, I used delay and vagueness to buy myself time. I told him: 'I need to go back through the complete performance review records. I'll send you my thoughts before tonight — but I can't confirm this list right now.'

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to write the colleague who just returned from maternity leave onto the list, and I choose to voice my dissent directly rather than comply in silence. I meet my superior's eyes and say: 'I can't write this list the way you're asking. If someone has to go, I need a real reason — not this one.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to politely decline the invitation while buying myself some breathing room — I won't agree on the spot, but I won't burn the bridge either; I'll use 'I need to think it over seriously' to extract myself from this pressure cooker first. I pick up my Americano, take a sip, set it back down, and look up at him: 'I hear what you're saying, and I've taken it all in — but this is too big a decision for me to make right now. Let me go away and think it through properly. I'll give you an answer by Tuesday.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stay open and actively seek more information, rather than committing or declining on the spot. I say: 'Everything you're describing genuinely excites me — but there are a few things I need to get clear on first. What's your core thesis about the education space, and what are the potholes you've already hit behind this product logic? Give me a few days. I want to actually understand this, not just get swept along by an equity number.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to express genuine interest while making it clear that I needed to verify the information before giving any answer, keeping the decision in my own hands. I told the senior: "I listened carefully to everything you said, and I'm going to take this seriously. But before I give you an answer, I need to see the original letter of intent, and whatever user data the product has right now — if there is any. I'll give you a definitive reply before Tuesday."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to express enthusiasm and lean toward accepting, while carving out a small buffer rather than committing on the spot. I lean forward, meet my senior's eyes directly, and say: 'I don't need to wait until Tuesday — this direction feels right to me, and everything you said landed. Give me 48 hours. Not to hesitate, but to get my own side of things sorted out — and then we sit down and talk through the details for real.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lend him the three thousand yuan, but I don't want to talk more than necessary — I want to get it done as fast as possible and go. I hold out my phone so he can see the balance, and say quietly: 'I'll transfer it to you. Give me your payment code. Doesn't matter whether you write an IOU or not.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan, accept the loss of a trip that may now never happen, and act on the strongest feeling of the present moment. I pull my phone out of my pocket, open Alipay, and say to him: 'Show me your payment code — I'll transfer it to you right now. You don't need to write me an IOU.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan, accepting the risk that it may never be returned, and I put the travel plans on hold. I pull out my phone to transfer the money — or dig out the cash — and crouch down beside him: 'Go pay the deposit first. You don't need to write an IOU — we'll sort it out once your father is out.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to lend the money, but with conditions attached — some way to keep a sense of control. I pulled out my phone, my voice coming out harder than I intended, and said: 'Write an IOU in your phone's notes app, screenshot it and send it to me, then give me your phone number. I'll lend you the three thousand, but I need your real information.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene — I don't return the bottle, I walk toward the bed curtain and speak, while preparing to reach out for outside help. I grip the pill bottle in my hand, walk to his bedside, and say quietly: 'I'm not leaving — I just don't want to. I'm holding onto this bottle for now — how are you doing right now?'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to return the pill bottle. Instead, I stay and speak up to check on him — without immediately contacting the RA — and try to build a connection first. I crouch down, set the bottle in my palm rather than kicking it back, and say: 'I'm not going out tonight… I'd like to sit with you for a while, if that's okay.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak up and respond to my roommate — using the bottle as a reason to stay, remaining right where I am in a clumsy but genuine way. I keep the bottle in my hand instead of kicking it back, turn around, and say: 'I don't feel like going out tonight. This bottle of pills… can you tell me about it?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to use the question he asked — the first thing he's said on his own — as my way in. I stay where I am, respond quietly, and don't kick the bottle back. I let my simple presence tell him that someone is listening. I set the bottle on my own desk, turn my chair to face his side of the room, and say calmly: "Didn't feel like going out tonight. You doing… okay over there?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to accept the offer at my own school — I won't redirect north for the relationship. I text her back and tell her: I've thought about this seriously. I love you, but I can't prove that love by changing course.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to give up my acceptance here and take the offer from the university up north — and tonight I give my partner a clear answer. I sent back a voice message: 'I'm coming to you. It's already decided. Wait for me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay at my current school and hold onto the most stable path forward for my graduate studies. I send her a message saying, 'I'm not going north — and I don't want to keep stringing you along with empty words.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay at my own school — I won't switch institutions as a concession to save the relationship. I send a message explaining my decision, and I'm honest: if they can't continue under these circumstances, I respect that we part ways.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold my ground and present the core evidence without directly escalating the conflict, while offering a limited compromise — and I do not voluntarily withdraw my materials. I speak up; my voice comes out quieter than I expected, but it doesn't waver: "Professor, I understand the pressure of the timeline, and I'm not trying to overturn the entire existing plan — what I want to ask is: would it be possible, while the main line of work moves forward, to allow me to run an alternative procedure as a controlled comparison at one specific stage, and document the risk point on record? That way, even if the final conclusion doesn't change, there's data showing we considered this issue."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to pull back for now, avoid a direct confrontation, but keep the alternative proposal alive — angling for one small opening to compromise rather than giving everything up entirely. I lowered my head slightly and said: 'I understand the timeline math you're talking about, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — I'll get the required materials submitted. But could we hold onto that comparison data for now? Once the phase materials are cleared, I'd like to come back and ask you to take another look at it.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold my ground on the core issue while proactively offering a middle path — neither full confrontation nor complete retreat. I speak up: 'I hear what you're saying about the timeline and the conditions, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — but the failure points in those cases overlap heavily with our own critical steps, and I can't look the other way. Could you give me one chance — just that one step — to run a round with my control approach and let the data speak? I'll keep pushing forward on everything else according to the original direction.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold my position in person, not withdraw the materials, but proactively offer a middle path — trying to shift the conversation from confrontation toward joint problem-solving. I say: 'Professor, I understand your concerns, and I'm well aware of the time pressure. But I can't pretend those control data don't exist — I'm not trying to invalidate your framework; I'm asking you to look at where the specific risk points are. Could you give me ten minutes? Let's look together and see whether there's any possibility of making partial adjustments within the current conditions.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to let him stay for now — close the door, keep out of sight, stay on high alert, and make no move to open the door and confront the debt collectors. I drop my voice to a low murmur and tell him: 'Don't make a sound — don't touch the lights either. We wait until they're gone, and then you're going to tell me exactly how deep this goes.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to let him stay, while quickly and quietly working out a plan together for if someone knocks. I pull him inside, lock the door behind us, and say in a hushed voice: 'Get into the back room, turn the lights off — if anyone knocks I'll say there's no one else here. But you have to tell me: do they know you know me?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose without a moment's hesitation to let him stay, while readying myself to handle a knock at the door that could come at any second. I push him into the back room and drop my voice: 'Get in there, keep the lights off, I'll answer the door — I'll tell them there's only me here.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to take him in without a second's hesitation, and instead of waiting passively for a knock, I move to get ahead of it. I pull him inside, drop my voice low, and say: 'Get in, lock the door — now tell me exactly how much you owe and how many of them there are. We figure this out right now.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to be honest with my partner about the real situation and my inner fear, while also deciding not to lower my salary floor — and instead to consider a more aggressive path toward starting something of my own or a full pivot. I squeeze her hand and say: "I'm going to be straight with you — I'm scared right now, but I'm not going to go work in a call center. I want to talk to you about a much bigger bet."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell my partner the truth and to swallow my pride first — to go after whatever work I can get up to speed on quickly. I say quietly, 'I'm letting go of the old salary standard. Starting tomorrow, I'm applying to warehouse and customer service jobs too.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to be honest about where things stand, while laying out a concrete near-term action framework — neither dodging the difficulty nor inflating it into a crisis. I hold her hand and say: 'This is the situation, and I'll be straight with you — over the next three months I'm going to look seriously, and I can lower my standards if I have to, but give me a little time first to get a clear picture of the options. Dad's follow-up appointment — we'll go together this month. The apartment can wait; we're not there yet.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose partial honesty — acknowledge the reality of the difficulty, but hold off on any complete statement about the future, because there is no executable plan yet to underpin any promise. Squeeze my partner's hand and say quietly: 'I know things are hard right now. I haven't figured out the way forward yet — but I'm working on it. Give me a little time. We'll figure it out together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose not to make any active changes for now, and instead return to the familiar busyness to keep the days stable. I tell him: let's just keep things the way they've always been — I'll head to the shop first, and when I get back I'll tidy up around the house.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to break the silence and take the initiative to suggest we go out for a walk together, rather than each retreating back into our familiar routines. I look up at him and say: 'Let's go — just go for a walk, no need to decide where, just walk.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to respond — to tuck my need inside a suggestion, phrased lightly, as if it just slipped out, not as if I'm scared. I lift my head, my voice steadier than I expected: "Maybe… let's go out for a walk together — I don't really know what I'd do with myself today anyway."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose not to sidestep this shared strangeness — to gently acknowledge that it exists, rather than each of us retreating back into the familiar busyness. I look down at the table and say: "This does feel strange. How about… we have a cup of coffee together first? No need to think about what comes next."

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to report the data falsification to my supervisor and did not sign off on the confirmation email. I sent the project lead a brief message stating that there were anomalies in the data that needed to be re-verified before the presentation, and I attached a record of the specific issues I had found.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to report the data falsification to my superiors truthfully and to refuse to sign off on the data. I tell my colleague: 'I understand the position you're in, but I can't sign off on this data, and I can't hide this from our superiors. I'll be reporting it honestly tonight.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to sign off on the data, while giving my colleague the opportunity to correct the error himself rather than going over his head directly. I tell him: 'I can't sign off on this data — but before I say anything to anyone above us, I'm giving you tonight. You go to the project team yourself and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the only concession I can make.'

options.label: D
options.content: Choose to report the data falsification truthfully to my supervisor, and refuse to sign off on the fabricated data. Reply directly in the project management system, flag the data as anomalous and unverifiable, and inform my supervisor of the situation.

options.label: A
options.content: I allow myself a brief breakdown at the intersection, but in the end I use self-attack to seal off any genuine questioning, substituting "pretending to accept" for truly facing it. I flip my phone face-down, take a deep breath, and murmur to myself: "You didn't have a choice back then — this is just who you are" — then I put the car in gear and drive home.

options.label: B
options.content: He chooses to suppress the breakdown and force himself back into the rhythm of the everyday, smothering that crack under the weight of family obligation and routine. He flips the phone face-down on the passenger seat, puts the car in gear, and drives home — repeating quietly to himself: there's dinner tonight, there are things to do tomorrow. That's enough.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay at the intersection — refusing to drive straight home and pretend everything is normal, but also refusing to completely fall apart — and instead try to treat these twenty years as a text that needs to be reread rather than a mistake that needs to be negated. I didn't start the engine. I turned my phone face-down on the passenger seat and said quietly to myself: 'This isn't a conclusion. This is just a chapter I haven't finished reading yet.'

options.label: D
options.content: He chooses to break the silence and reach out rather than suppress it alone or drive home and pretend everything is normal. He dials an old friend's number, his voice a little rough: 'I'm stuck at a red light right now — there's something I've been sitting on for a long time. Do you have a few minutes to talk?'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to cooperate with the vice principal to a limited degree — I'll say who brought the cards in, but I won't volunteer anything extra or embellish. I keep my head down and speak: 'It was Chen in the back row — he brought them in. I saw it, but I wasn't part of it.'

options.label: B
options.content: He chooses to tell the truth, refusing to cover for the one who brought the cards, and ignores the threat entirely. Looking the director straight in the eye, he says: 'He's the one who brought them in. I saw it. That's all there is to it — it has nothing to do with me.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay vague and refuse to cooperate with either side — deflecting with 'I didn't notice' and 'I don't know' to keep myself out of it, neither informing on anyone nor taking the blame for anyone. I keep my head down and say: 'I was reading at the time — whatever they were doing in the back, I honestly didn't notice. I don't know whose cards those were either.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell the truth — to state the facts, to not be swayed by threats, and to make no unnecessary show of emotion. I look at the director calmly and say: 'It was the student in the back row near the corner who brought them in. I turned around because I heard a noise, but I wasn't involved.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to politely decline the soccer invitation and stay to keep observing the bug, but I explain it in a friendly way so he doesn't feel put out. I smiled at him and said: 'You guys go ahead — there's this super weird bug here I want to watch a little longer. Once I'm done, maybe I'll come find you!'

options.label: B
options.content: I decline the invitation and stay behind to keep observing the insect and the little purple flower, trying to document every detail with my phone or the notebook I have on me. I tell the kid: "Go on ahead without me — there's something here I haven't finished looking at. I've never seen this bug before in my life, and I need to figure out what it is first."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to politely decline the invitation for now, finish observing and mentally recording the insect and the plant, and then consider heading to the field. I tell that boy: 'Go ahead and start without me — I'll come over as soon as I'm done looking at this. If you're short a player, just give me a few more minutes; I'll be there once I've got it all noted down.'

options.label: D
options.content: He politely declines the invitation, tells the other kid to head back first, and stays to look a little longer before deciding anything — neither diving into research nor leaving right away. "You guys go ahead, I'll come check it out in a bit."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to support the child wholeheartedly in taking this unconventional route, without using my own experience as a reason to put up obstacles. I slide the report card back across the table toward them and say: 'Let me take a look. The fact that you've been teaching yourself since middle school — that already tells me everything I need to know. Go ahead and apply. I'm behind you.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose not to oppose him outright, but I can't bring myself to offer real support either — I replace a clear answer with the hedged worry of someone who's 'been through it' and a conditional softening. I say: 'You're telling me you've been at this since middle school… It's not that I won't let you try, but you need to think it through — if it doesn't pan out, do you have a fallback?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to support the kid — pushing down every worry about employment and income, refusing to echo the dismissals of the generation before me. I was silent for a moment, then I spoke: "You said you've loved this since middle school, that you've been teaching yourself all along — I believe you. Go and try. I won't stand in your way."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to support my child in pursuing this path — I voice my worries, but I don't use them to invalidate their choice. I say: 'I hear you. You've been preparing for so long, and I have no reason to say no. I have my fears, but those are my homework to do — not a chain around your neck. Let's figure out together how to make this path as solid as it can be.'

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to file a real-name report in his own name alone, refusing to let the neighbors' retreat or his wife's pleas change his position — but he won't push anyone to join him either. He says calmly to the old man upstairs: 'I understand your difficulties. I won't press anyone on the joint letter — but I'll go to the Housing and Construction Bureau myself. I've already got the materials ready.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to file the complaint in my own name, as an individual — but I make my disappointment and my boundaries clear to the neighbors who were 'full of advice but unwilling to sign.' I say to the retired teacher: 'I'll go file the report, and I'll put my name on it — but I need you all to promise me this: if the housing authority comes to verify the facts, you cannot deny that you knew. I'm not asking you to be out in front. But I can't stand there alone and lie by saying I discovered all of this by myself.'

options.label: C
options.content: I've decided to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name — but I'm making it clear to the neighbors: quiet support is fine, but I'm not out here taking all the risk for everyone for nothing. I'll keep everyone posted as things develop. To the retired teacher, I said: "I understand why you won't sign your names — but I've already done the digging, and I'm going to file this complaint. However you want to help, that's up to you. Just don't expect me to carry the whole thing by myself and call it done."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau as an individual, while responding honestly to my neighbors' stance — accepting their behind-the-scenes support, but not hiding my disappointment in this collective silence. I say to the teacher upstairs: "I understand about the joint letter — I'm not going to force your names onto it. But I want you to know: this isn't just my business, it's all of our money. I'm going to file the complaint; help me fill in the supporting materials, but I won't be stopping this."

options.label: A
options.content: I'm choosing to get the follow-up done as soon as possible, and to tell my family about the risk first — no more hiding it. I'll book the appointment first, then tell everyone at home that I saw something abnormal and need to go in right away.

options.label: B

I wake the phone screen, go straight to the hospital's appointment system, lock in the earliest available follow-up slot, then flip the phone face-down on the desk and open my work files.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to keep it to myself for now, using 'waiting for the results to be confirmed' as my reason to move forward with the follow-up alone, leaving the decision of whether to tell my family suspended. I reply to the doctor's text to confirm the follow-up appointment, then flip my phone face-down on the desk, reopen the code documentation, and tell myself: wait until the results come in.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to keep it to myself for now, go to the follow-up appointment alone to confirm the results, and only decide whether to tell my family once I have more information. I press the phone screen dark, quietly note the follow-up appointment time in my memo app, then walk out of the study and tell my family I'm a little tired tonight and am turning in early.

options.label: A
options.content: I chose not to write my colleague — the one who just returned from maternity leave — onto the list, but I didn't confront my boss head-on either. I'll send you my thoughts before tonight — but I can't confirm this list right now.'

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to directly write that colleague's name — the one who just returned from maternity leave — on the list, and I try to ask my boss whether another solution might be possible. I say quietly: "I understand the position you're in, but this colleague just came back and her performance is already recovering — I'd like to think a little longer about whether there's another way. Could you give me a bit of time?"

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to include the colleague who just returned from maternity leave on the layoff list, and I name the real nature of what my superior is asking directly, without offering any cooperative cover. I meet his eyes and say: 'The arrangement you're describing isn't something I can carry out — putting a B+ employee on record as failing to meet expectations while keeping someone who's been C two quarters running is a list I won't sign. If there's a second round of names, I'll accept being on it.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to intervene proactively — walk over to the new student and break the ice in a task-oriented way. I pick up my tray, stand up, tell my friends 'Go ahead, I'll catch up,' then walk over to the table in the corner and sit down, saying to the new student: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm — . It's your first day — if there's anything you're not sure about, feel free to ask me.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to set down my chopsticks and walk over to the corner to strike up a conversation with the new classmate, instead of heading to the playground with my friends. I carry my tray over to that little table against the wall, sit down with a smile, and say: 'Is it okay if I sit here? What's your name? I'm — . Let's eat together — it's so loud in this cafeteria, eating alone is no fun!'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to leave my original table, carry my tray over to the new student sitting alone in the corner, and skip going to the playground with my friends. I walk over with my tray and sit down across from her, saying quietly: 'Is it okay if I sit here? My name is — what's yours?'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to leave my friends' table and walk over to the new classmate sitting alone in the corner — curiosity and empathy driving the decision in equal measure. I pick up my tray, stand, and tell my friends, "Go ahead, I'll catch up," then walk to that corner table, sit down beside her, and ask: "Where are you from? What's fun to do over there?"

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold off on making any decision about a career transition, and instead prioritize gathering more internal information through tomorrow's one-on-one with my supervisor, while giving my former colleague a noncommittal reply in the meantime. I write back to him: "I think you make a fair point — I'm still turning it over. Let me talk to my supervisor tomorrow and see how that goes, then we can catch up after."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to wait and watch — I won't accept my former colleague's invitation, and I'll prepare to tell my supervisor tomorrow that I'm willing to align with the company's current direction and that I want to keep my position. I reply to my former colleague: 'I support your decision, but I've got family pressure on my end — quitting isn't something I'm considering right now. I'll see how things play out here first.' Then I open my calendar and accept the supervisor's invite.

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to proactively transition, while treating tomorrow's manager meeting as an intelligence-gathering opportunity rather than a moment to seek validation. Tonight, reply to my former colleague: 'I'm already looking into this — I don't need a bundled bootcamp, but we're pointing in the same direction, so let's keep each other posted on progress'; at the same time, put together my own transition timeline to use during tomorrow's conversation as a way of testing what the company's real expectations are for traditional roles.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to actively embrace the transition: I reply to my former colleague that same evening to confirm I'm in, and I prepare to go on the offensive in tomorrow's talk with my supervisor. I send my former colleague a voice message: "I'm in — let's do this together. Tomorrow I'll get everything straight with my supervisor first, then wait for my message next week."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to push down the urge to confront her, getting through this moment with silence and avoidance, not letting her see that I already know about the screenshot. I lower my head, force the tears back, and say quietly, "I'm fine, probably just didn't sleep well," then look away and pretend to flip through my textbook.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation, not here, not yet. I'll wait until I've calmly worked out what outcome I actually want before deciding on a next step. I turn off my phone screen, look up, give her one expressionless glance, then drop my eyes and open my textbook.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to confront her directly — to put the screenshot and the side group chat out in the open, no holding back and no dancing around it. I turn toward her, lower my voice, but speak with complete directness: "I saw that group chat. I saw what you said. Can you tell me — what exactly is going on?"

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold back the urge to confront her — no face-to-face showdown right now — but I don't pretend everything is fine either. Instead, I use silence and withdrawal to draw a line. I slide my phone into my desk drawer, don't look at her, don't respond to what she just said — I just lower my head and turn my eyes toward the window.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse the money and, in a trembling but clear voice, speak the true weight of what was lost — not to demand compensation, but to keep myself from disappearing into silence. I push the stack of bills back across the table, my voice shaking a little: 'Uncle, this isn't about replacing a hard drive. There were six months of my work on there — things that may be gone forever. I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to set aside any emotional confrontation and prioritize dealing with the actual loss — the drive itself. I politely but directly push the money back and ask for the drive. I say to my uncle: 'No need for the money — there are some work files on the drive, I need to take it and see if anything can be recovered. Please, make yourself comfortable.' I casually push the stack of bills back onto the coffee table, pick up the drive and the screw, and head to another room to look up data recovery services.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to refuse the money, say my piece briefly, and end this conversation as quickly as possible — without getting into a direct argument with my uncle, but without pretending everything is fine either. I push the stack of money back across the coffee table, my voice flat, and say only one thing: 'Keep the money. What was in there isn't a money matter.' Then I stand up to go get some water, and I don't look at them again.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to make my position clear calmly but firmly — neither accepting the cash nor letting things explode into a scene. I push the wad of money back across the coffee table and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Uncle, I can't take this. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't about the cost of replacing a hard drive. Those files are gone, and that's that. I understand the kid didn't know any better, but I need you to understand how serious this is.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea clearly, but I don't agree to the competition on the spot. I walk through the approach step by step first, and then I tell the teacher that I'd like to go home and organize my thoughts before deciding whether to enter.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to stand up and explain my idea concisely, but politely decline the invitation to join the speech competition. I'll say: 'Teacher, I can walk through this method, but I'd rather not join the speech competition — I don't think it's necessary.' Then I'll lay out the logic of the seesaw weighing method in the most straightforward way I can, without dragging it out, and without caring about the skeptical looks.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to look down and avoid it — deflecting with something vague, neither standing up to speak nor agreeing to enter the competition. I lower my head and say quietly: "Teacher… what I said just now wasn't very clear, I wasn't really thinking that much about it — the competition thing… I'm really not up for it."

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stand up and share my idea in front of everyone; the suggestion about the speech competition is set aside for now, neither accepted nor explicitly refused. I rise from my chair, my voice a little rushed at first but growing steadier as I go: 'It's just… you put an elephant on one end, then you stack people onto the other end one by one, and you count how many people it takes — because you can measure each person's weight ahead of time —'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to speak up before my roommate gets back — to tell him directly what I found, framing it as 'I came across it by accident, and we need to talk,' and to demand that he rewrite it before submission, while making clear that the consequences fall on both of us. I stand up, walk to the door, and the moment it opens I say: 'I accidentally saw what was on your screen just now, and I searched a few of the passages — we need to talk right now, because the deadline is the day after tomorrow and both our names are on this.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to tell my roommate directly, face-to-face — lay out the facts and require him to fix it before the deadline, without any emotional judgment, and without going around him to contact the professor. Once he comes in and sits down, I say calmly: 'I accidentally saw your section just now. I searched a few passages, and they're word-for-word matches with articles online. It's due the day after tomorrow — you need to fix it, or the whole group is done for.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to tell him face-to-face, privately, when he gets back — giving him the chance to rewrite it before submission — rather than staying silent or going straight to the professor. When he pushes the door open, I look up and say: 'I accidentally saw your document just now, and I searched a few passages — they're a perfect match with an article on Zhihu. The deadline is the day after tomorrow; you still have time to fix it, and I can help you.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to prepare myself before my roommate gets back, and to tell him face to face — privately, gently, but directly — that I've discovered the plagiarism, giving him the chance to rewrite before submission, rather than reporting him or staying silent. I close the browser tab, ease his laptop screen back to where it was, take a deep breath, and wait for him to walk in. When he does, I speak: 'There's something I need to talk to you about — sit down for a second. It's about the report. I came across something by accident, and I think we need to talk before it gets submitted.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to be honest as soon as possible, but without framing it as a foregone conclusion. I'll schedule a follow-up appointment first, then bring my family together and tell them: 'I've found something abnormal, and I need us to deal with it right away — together.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep this to myself for now — to build the information framework first, and tell my family once the follow-up results are in. I picked up my phone, opened the calendar, and created a new reminder in tomorrow morning's open slot: 'Contact hospital — schedule follow-up — confirm timeline.' Then I turned the screen face-down on the desk.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold off on telling my family about the abnormal result for the time being, but I take immediate, concrete action to address the risk. I put the phone down, open the hospital app, fill out the follow-up appointment form, and submit it — telling myself: wait for the results, then decide how to have that conversation.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to tell my family as soon as possible, but I'll hold the rhythm steady — I won't frame it as a catastrophe. Tonight I'll bring my partner in, be honest that the doctor has asked for a follow-up and that I'm worried there may be something to it, and we'll go together tomorrow to find out for sure.

options.label: A
options.content: I've decided to reach out directly via private message, pointing out the specific structural risk points in the process — no emotional preamble, no 'just for your reference' disclaimer tacked on at the end. And I'll reply to my colleague: 'I'll say it directly. You don't need to get involved.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to avoid commenting publicly, and instead respond to my colleague through the relatively safer channel of a private message, without directly engaging with the younger colleague's post. Private message reply: 'I saw it too — I'm a little worried, but it doesn't feel right to say something outright. If you think there's a problem, do you want to talk it through first?'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to speak up, but in a private, peer-to-peer way rather than as a public critique. First, I reply to my colleague's message: 'I feel the same way — let's think together about how to bring it up.' Then I send the young colleague a private message: 'Seeing your project update, a few details reminded me of something from my own early career. If you'd ever like to talk it through, I'm happy to share — though of course it's just one perspective; you know your situation far better than I do.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to use my colleague's private message as the entry point — passing along a word of caution from experience indirectly rather than directly, so it doesn't come across as a forceful intervention through a public comment. My reply to my colleague: "I saw it too, and your instinct might be right — if you're open to it, we could talk. I once ran a project with a very similar structure, and there are a few wrong turns that might be worth mentioning."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold my ground on the core issue while proactively offering a middle path — neither full confrontation nor complete retreat. I speak up: 'I hear what you're saying about the timeline and the conditions, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — but the failure points in those cases overlap heavily with our own critical steps, and I can't look the other way. Could you give me one chance — just that one step — to run a round with my control approach and let the data speak? I'll keep pushing forward on everything else according to the original direction.'

options.label: B
options.content: Choose a middle path — neither withdrawing the brief entirely nor pushing back in a direct, forceful confrontation — and instead request a small-scale partial verification at minimal cost. Say aloud: 'I understand your concerns; time and conditions are real constraints. But would you allow me, while moving the existing plan forward, to run a parallel comparison on one very small sub-component — it won't affect the main timeline, and if the data shows no difference, I'll drop this matter completely.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to hold my position in person, not withdraw the materials, but proactively offer a middle path — trying to shift the conversation from confrontation toward joint problem-solving. I say: 'Professor, I understand your concerns, and I'm well aware of the time pressure. But I can't pretend those control data don't exist — I'm not trying to invalidate your framework; I'm asking you to look at where the specific risk points are. Could you give me ten minutes? Let's look together and see whether there's any possibility of making partial adjustments within the current conditions.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to hold my ground and present the core evidence without directly escalating the conflict, while offering a limited compromise — and I do not voluntarily withdraw my materials. I speak up; my voice comes out quieter than I expected, but it doesn't waver: "Professor, I understand the pressure of the timeline, and I'm not trying to overturn the entire existing plan — what I want to ask is: would it be possible, while the main line of work moves forward, to allow me to run an alternative procedure as a controlled comparison at one specific stage, and document the risk point on record? That way, even if the final conclusion doesn't change, there's data showing we considered this issue."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to comply for now and leave, protecting my physical safety rather than confronting them on the spot — but I quietly take note of everything about them, with the intention of pursuing the deposit afterward through legal channels or other means. I bend down and pick the clothes up off the floor, drag my suitcase upright, and say: 'Fine, I'll go — but I'll find somewhere to take this deposit issue up properly.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now, giving up any confrontation in the moment — but I haven't given up on recovering the deposit. I press that thought down into the deepest part of myself, to be dealt with another day. I lower my head, and in a voice that's barely audible, I say: 'I'm leaving now, but this isn't over.' Then I bend down and start picking up the clothes scattered across the floor, hands trembling, eyes never once lifting to look at him.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to appear outwardly compliant for now in order to remove myself from the immediate threat of violence, while quietly initiating evidence collection and legal proceedings in the background — I will not give up the deposit under any circumstances. I say to him: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight — but the contract and the deposit, we're not done with that. You'd better take a good hard look at those contract terms, because I'm going to have someone verify them.' At the same time, I quietly start recording on my phone.

options.label: D
options.content: Refuse to leave in silence — instead, force the confrontation into the open, buying time and drawing witnesses. Say out loud: 'Fine, I'm not going anywhere. You're standing on my stuff while you talk to me about a contract — there are plenty of doors in this hallway, and everyone can hear us right now. I'm calling 12345 this second. You say you know people at the precinct? Perfect — then we'll wait for them together, right here in front of everyone, and sort out this contract and this deposit where the whole world can see.' At the same time, pull out the phone and get ready to dial.

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal on the spot — stating the risk points plainly, not pushing for any particular outcome, just making sure the information got through in full. I said: 'I do have a few thoughts — I put together an alternative proposal, mainly with an eye toward the long-term scaling issues. The document's right here; everyone can take two minutes to look it over, and we can decide after that — no rush.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to casually flag a single risk point without presenting the full alternative plan, leaving myself a way out. I speak up: 'No issue with the direction overall — just something to keep in mind: if the complexity ramps up later, the extensibility piece might get a bit strained, so just worth watching. As for dividing up the work, you all decide.'

options.label: C
options.content: I chose to raise the alternative proposal in this moment, presenting it in a structured, evidence-based way rather than silently accepting the consensus. I turned my screen toward the room and said: 'There are a few other ideas I'd like to share — I've put together a specific proposal with the risk points laid out. I need three minutes. I think saying it now costs less than waiting until something goes wrong later.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to briefly flag the core risk rather than put forward the full alternative plan — leaving my judgment on record in a measured way, without pushing to change the direction. I speak up: 'My alternative proposal is still there. My main concern is that once the complexity ramps up later on, maintenance is going to get pretty painful — I just wanted to flag that risk. However you all want to move forward, I'll follow your lead.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan, accept the loss of a trip that may now never happen, and act on the strongest feeling of the present moment. I pull my phone out of my pocket, open Alipay, and say to him: 'Show me your payment code — I'll transfer it to you right now. You don't need to write me an IOU.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to lend the three thousand yuan, accepting the risk that it may never be returned, and I put the travel plans on hold. I pull out my phone to transfer the money — or dig out the cash — and crouch down beside him: 'Go pay the deposit first. You don't need to write an IOU — we'll sort it out once your father is out.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose not to lend the money, but I give him an alternative direction rather than walking away in silence. I crouch down and gently move his hand from my pant leg, and say calmly: 'I can't lend you anything — but go to the nurses' station right now, tell them the family has no money. The hospital has an emergency payment assistance process. Go ask them. Don't stay here on your knees.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to lend the money, but conditionally — first I do a quick reality check by confirming the situation with the medical staff. I turn to the young man and say: 'Stay right there — I'm going to check with the nurses' station. Once I've confirmed it, I'll go get the money.'

options.label: A
options.content: He chooses to break the silence and reach out rather than suppress it alone or drive home and pretend everything is normal. He dials an old friend's number, his voice a little rough: 'I'm stuck at a red light right now — there's something I've been sitting on for a long time. Do you have a few minutes to talk?'

options.label: B
options.content: I allow myself a brief breakdown at the intersection, but in the end I use self-attack to seal off any genuine questioning, substituting "pretending to accept" for truly facing it. I flip my phone face-down, take a deep breath, and murmur to myself: "You didn't have a choice back then — this is just who you are" — then I put the car in gear and drive home.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to stay at the intersection — refusing to drive straight home and pretend everything is normal, but also refusing to completely fall apart — and instead try to treat these twenty years as a text that needs to be reread rather than a mistake that needs to be negated. I didn't start the engine. I turned my phone face-down on the passenger seat and said quietly to myself: 'This isn't a conclusion. This is just a chapter I haven't finished reading yet.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stay where I am for now — neither falling apart nor forcing myself to calm down — and allow this tearing feeling to exist for a little while. I flip my phone face-down, take a slow deep breath, wait for the traffic light to change one more time, and then ease the car back toward home. Tonight, I go back. Everything else can wait.

options.label: A
options.content: Honestly express how strongly I want this academic opportunity, acknowledge the uncertainty, but make it clear that I want to seriously consider accepting the invitation — and that same evening, reply to my advisor's email with an initial expression of intent. I tell him: "I know this will change a lot of things, and I'm scared too. But when I found that notebook last night, I realized I haven't really been alive for a long time — I want to reply to that email, and we can figure out the rest together."

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to honestly express the longing inside me while acknowledging the uncertainty, and to ask that we have a serious conversation together — without rushing to reply to my advisor. I say to him: 'I really have been reading this email the whole time. Those research directions are something I hadn't thought about in a long time, but never truly let go of. I don't know whether going or not going is the right call — but I want to tell you how I feel first, and then we can think this through together before we decide how to respond to my advisor.'

options.label: C
options.content: I'm honest about my longing for this academic opportunity, while acknowledging that I don't have a concrete plan yet — but I make it clear that I want to try. I say to her: 'I know you're worried, and I don't have all the answers — but that email made me feel something I haven't felt in a very long time. I want to write back to my advisor and tell him I'm interested. Let's just start with a conversation.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to honestly express how deeply I want this academic opportunity, while actively inviting my partner to work through the possibilities together, rather than pushing ahead on my own. I turn toward her, tilt the laptop screen so she can see it, and say: 'I'm not running away — I just haven't felt like this in a really long time. Can you let me tell you what this opportunity means to me? And then we can figure out what to do together.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend and either hand the paper to the teacher or put it back where it came from. I whisper to her, "We can't use this — we'll get caught," and then, while she's not paying attention, I slip the paper back into the crack in the desk or hand it to the teacher, pretending I just happened to find it by accident.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to refuse to use the answer sheet, and I won't let my friend copy it either — I'll return the paper or put it back where it was, and I won't take part in this gamble on luck. I tell my friend: 'I'm not using this, and you shouldn't copy it either — have you actually calculated what it costs if we get caught? I'm putting it back. What you do after that is your call.'

options.label: C
options.content: Choose to refuse copying and return the answer sheet to where it was — neither reporting it nor using it. Say to Xin: 'We can't copy from this — put it back. Getting caught would be way worse than just bombing the test. You head out first, I'll be there once I finish sweeping up.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to refuse my friend's request and decide to hand the answer sheet over to the teacher or put it back where it belongs — I'm not keeping this risk around. I tell my friend: 'I can't copy from this, and you shouldn't either. This belongs to the teacher — I'm turning it in. You should go ahead.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to answer the video call and share the information honestly but precisely, separating the facts from any conclusions. I pick up and say, calmly: 'The report came back. Two markers are elevated — the doctor recommends a follow-up, and I'm already arranging it. There's nothing more to say until those results are in. You don't need to come back.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to answer the call but brush it off vaguely — "everything's fine, nothing major" — and leave it at that until the follow-up results come in. I picked up, keeping my voice as steady as I could: "Nothing to worry about, just a couple of minor indicators a little off — the doctor said to just keep an eye on it. Don't stress, we'll talk when I'm back Friday."

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but I keep things vague for now — I'm not going to drop the worst of it on her yet. Once I pick up, I tell her: two of my markers came back elevated, the doctor wants me to get a follow-up as soon as possible, and the moment those results are in, you'll be the first to know.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to answer the video call, but gloss over the results, planning to tell her everything honestly once the follow-up is done. I press accept, pull together an expression that looks more or less natural, and say: "The results came in — a couple of indicators need a follow-up check, the doctor said it's really common, I'll go back next week to confirm. Don't worry about it — just finish up what you're working on."

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to keep trying to dissuade him — I'll lay out the factual hallmarks of a pyramid scheme structure directly, decline the investment invitation, and not dignify the 'jealousy' accusation with a response. I say: 'I'm not putting money in, and it's not because I'm jealous. This model of yours — recruiting downlines, tiered profit-sharing, returns that depend on the people below you rather than on any actual product — that's the textbook structure of a pyramid scheme. There's nothing I can do about the eight hundred thousand that's already gone, but don't ask me to hand over this hundred and fifty thousand.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to keep trying to talk him out of it, but instead of flat-out rejecting what he's saying, I use self-disclosure and emotional connection to try to get around his defenses. I set down the slides and say quietly: 'I'm not jealous of you. I'm just scared. Scared that three months from now you'll call me to say the money's gone — and that today I said nothing.'

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to state my judgment — gently but without ambiguity. I won't try to drag him out by force, but I'll put what I see on the table and leave the decision with him. I set the PPT down and say, calmly: 'Man, I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're an idiot. But I've been doing this reporting long enough that I recognize this structure — recruit downlines, tiered commissions, guaranteed returns. Put those three things together, and in every case I've ever covered, not one of them ended well. Whether you believe me is up to you. But if I sit here today and say nothing, then this friendship was never worth much to begin with.'

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to speak up directly and urge him to stop — without backing down because of his emotional reaction or the risk to our friendship — using facts and logic as my tools, refusing to trade silence for a surface-level peace. I flip the PowerPoint back to the first page, look him in the eye, and say: 'I'm not jealous of you. I've just seen too many people caught up in this exact structure and watched how it ends. Your income right now comes from the people you recruit, not from any product or service — that's the legal definition of a pyramid scheme, not my opinion, the law's definition. Eight hundred thousand is already gone, but you're still pulling more people in — that's what I'm actually worried about.'

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now, without confronting them head-on in the moment — but inside, I haven't given up on pursuing the deposit. I bend down and quietly gather the clothes scattered across the floor, keeping my voice flat and even: "I'll leave tonight — but I'll deal with the deposit through other means." Then I pick up my suitcase, don't look back at them, and walk out of the hallway.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now, giving up any confrontation in the moment — but I haven't given up on recovering the deposit. I press that thought down into the deepest part of myself, to be dealt with another day. I lower my head, and in a voice that's barely audible, I say: 'I'm leaving now, but this isn't over.' Then I bend down and start picking up the clothes scattered across the floor, hands trembling, eyes never once lifting to look at him.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to comply for now in order to get out of immediate physical danger, but inwardly I resolve not to give up on recovering the deposit — the moment I leave, I'll contact someone who can help me (a tenant rights hotline, legal aid, or a friend). I lower my head and say, voice trembling but as steady as I can manage: 'Fine, I'll go for tonight — but we're not done talking about the deposit. I'll be handling this through the proper channels.' Then I bend down and start gathering the clothes from the floor, and I stop meeting his eyes.

options.label: D
options.content: Refuse to leave in silence — instead, force the confrontation into the open, buying time and drawing witnesses. Say out loud: 'Fine, I'm not going anywhere. You're standing on my stuff while you talk to me about a contract — there are plenty of doors in this hallway, and everyone can hear us right now. I'm calling 12345 this second. You say you know people at the precinct? Perfect — then we'll wait for them together, right here in front of everyone, and sort out this contract and this deposit where the whole world can see.' At the same time, pull out the phone and get ready to dial.

options.label: A
options.content: I refuse to silently accept the arrangement being decided in that group chat — but I choose to put the phone down first and turn to face my partner, to have this conversation between the two of us before anything else, rather than exploding in the chat right now. I press the phone face-down onto the couch, stand up, and walk toward her. 'Let's talk first,' I say. 'Just the two of us.'

options.label: B
options.content: I'm not accepting another round of carrying the bulk of the caregiving alone — but I'm not blowing things up in the open either. I reply in the group chat: both money and caregiving need to be shared on a rotating schedule; I'll cover the medication this time, and tomorrow I'll send out the list and the shift rotation. Then I turn and quietly tell my partner that we need to talk tonight — really talk.

options.label: C
options.content: I refuse to accept this unequal arrangement of trading money for labor, and I make it clear in the group chat that we need to renegotiate how caregiving duties are divided — rather than unilaterally making me carry more. I type in the group: "Let's set the money aside for now. Caregiving isn't one person's job. Next week we sit down face to face and work out exactly how much time and help each household can actually contribute — because the way things are going, none of us can keep this up."

options.label: D
options.content: I refuse to accept this arrangement through silence. I choose to send a real response in the group chat, making it clear that the current division of responsibilities is not sustainable, and demanding that we renegotiate the specifics of how things are shared. I type into the group: "Money isn't the issue — the issue is that this isn't something I can carry alone. We need to sit down and seriously talk about how to divide this, and that means time and people, not just money." Then I put down my phone and turn to walk toward my partner.

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to hold my emotions in check for now — no immediate decision to leave, no immediate forgiveness — and instead ask him to lay everything out. I crouch down and say, in a voice that's quiet but steady: 'Get up first. Then tell me everything — how much is still owed, who these people are, what the situation looks like right now. All of it.'

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to set aside my anger and judgment for now, pull him up off the floor first, and then ask him to walk me through everything in full — the whole picture of the debt, and what he plans to do next. I lean down and reach out my hand: "Get up first — the floor is cold. Then you're going to tell me everything, from the beginning. options.label: D
options.content: I choose to stall and respond vaguely to dodge the camping invitation — neither accepting nor explicitly declining — buying myself some time. I reply: 'It sounds absolutely gorgeous! I just have something on my end this week that I need to check the timing on — I'll let you know for sure in a day or two, you guys go ahead and make your plans in the meantime~ '

options.label: A
options.content: I choose to comply and leave for now, giving up any confrontation in the moment — but I haven't given up on recovering the deposit. I press that thought down into the deepest part of myself, to be dealt with another day. I lower my head, and in a voice that's barely audible, I say: 'I'm leaving now, but this isn't over.' Then I bend down and start picking up the clothes scattered across the floor, hands trembling, eyes never once lifting to look at him.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to appear outwardly compliant for now in order to remove myself from the immediate threat of violence, while quietly initiating evidence collection and legal proceedings in the background — I will not give up the deposit under any circumstances. I say to him: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight — but the contract and the deposit, we're not done with that. You'd better take a good hard look at those contract terms, because I'm going to have someone verify them.' At the same time, I quietly start recording on my phone.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to comply for now and leave without arguing over the deposit on the spot, but I quietly note every key detail for pursuing my rights afterward. I bend down to pick up my ID and phone from the floor, and say calmly: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight. But the matter of the contract — we'll settle that later.' Then I pick up my luggage and walk out.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to comply for now in order to get out of immediate physical danger, but inwardly I resolve not to give up on recovering the deposit — the moment I leave, I'll contact someone who can help me (a tenant rights hotline, legal aid, or a friend). I lower my head and say, voice trembling but as steady as I can manage: 'Fine, I'll go for tonight — but we're not done talking about the deposit. I'll be handling this through the proper channels.' Then I bend down and start gathering the clothes from the floor, and I stop meeting his eyes.

options.label: A
options.content: I chose to submit only the legitimate business travel expenses, slipping the two personal receipts back into my wallet and leaving them off the reimbursement form. I tucked those two receipts back into my wallet, filled out the form for the actual travel amount, got up, and went to print it.

options.label: B
options.content: I choose to submit only the legitimate business travel expenses, leaving those two personal receipts folded in my wallet without turning them in. I fold the Japanese restaurant receipt and the scarf receipt and tuck them back into my wallet, then neatly attach the high-speed rail ticket, the hotel bill, and the work-meal receipt to the reimbursement form, fill in the total — a number more than four hundred yuan short of the ceiling — and push the form to the side, taking a slow, deep breath.

options.label: C
options.content: I choose to submit only the legitimate business travel expenses, tucking the two personal receipts back into my wallet without filing them. I slide the Japanese restaurant bill and the scarf receipt back into my wallet, and attach only the high-speed rail ticket, the hotel invoice, and the work-meal receipt to the expense report, filling in and submitting the actual amount.

options.label: D
options.content: I choose to submit only the genuine business travel expenses, folding the two personal receipts and putting them back in my wallet without filing them. I fold up the sushi receipt and the scarf receipt and tuck them away, then neatly attach only the high-speed rail ticket, the hotel bill, and the work-meal receipt to the reimbursement form.
